# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]
**Define API gravity. A crude oil has a specific gravity of 0.875 at 15.6 °C. Calculate its API gravity.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** API Gravity is an inverse measure of specific gravity, defined by the American Petroleum Institute, such that water has an API of 10°. It is used universally in the petroleum industry to classify crude oil as light, medium, or heavy.

**Calculation:**

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 = \frac{141.5}{0.875} - 131.5$$

$$°API = 161.71 - 131.5 = 30.21 \text{ °API}$$

Since 30.21 lies in the range 22.3 – 31.1, this crude is classified as a **Medium Crude**.

**(3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for correct numerical answer with classification)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]
**List any four physical characteristics of crude oil and state their engineering significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The four physical characteristics are:

1. **Density / API Gravity** — Determines the yield of light vs heavy products; used for cargo settlement and trade pricing.

2. **Viscosity** — Governs pumping power, pipeline sizing, and heat-tracing requirements.

3. **Flash Point** — Decides the hazardous area classification for selecting explosion-proof (Ex d) or intrinsically safe (Ex i) instruments.

4. **Pour Point** — The lowest temperature at which crude flows; critical for subsea pipelines and cold-climate storage.

**(3 Marks: 1 for correct list, 1 for engineering significance, 1 for clarity of presentation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper 1]

**(a)** Explain the major **chemical characteristics of crude oil** with reference to its hydrocarbon composition (SARA analysis) and heteroatom content. Discuss the role of these characteristics in determining refining routes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A crude oil has $SG = 0.91$, $T_{MeABP} = 820 \text{ °F}$, and sulfur content = 1.8 wt%. Calculate:
   - (i) API Gravity
   - (ii) Watson K-factor
   - (iii) Classify the crude based on API and Sulfur content
   - **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Chemical Characteristics of Crude Oil:**

Crude oil is a complex mixture whose chemical characteristics determine the appropriate refining route. The major chemical characteristics are:

**1. Hydrocarbon Composition (SARA Analysis):**
   - **Saturates** (Paraffins + Naphthenes) — Chemically stable; preferred for diesel and jet fuel due to high cetane number and clean burning.
   - **Aromatics** — High octane value; valued for gasoline blending. The presence of benzene, toluene, and xylene (BTX) makes crude a feedstock for petrochemical extraction.
   - **Resins and Asphaltenes** — Heavy, polar molecules that cause fouling in heat exchangers and hydrocracker preheaters.

**2. Sulfur Content:**
   - Sweet crude (< 0.5 wt%) can be processed in simple distillation with no HDS unit.
   - Sour crude (> 0.5 wt%) mandates a **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)** unit to meet product specs (e.g., Euro VI diesel at < 10 ppm S).

**3. Nitrogen Content:**
   - Causes catalyst poisoning in FCC units. Determines the severity of **hydrotreating** required.

**4. Total Acid Number (TAN):**
   - High TAN → Naphthenic acid corrosion in VDU at 220–400 °C. Mandates use of **SS 317L** metallurgy.

**5. Metallic Content (Ni, V):**
   - Ni and V deposit on FCC catalyst, permanently deactivating it. Determines catalyst makeup rate.

**Refining Route Selection:**
The above characteristics drive a refinery to choose between:
- **Hydroskimming** (for light sweet crudes)
- **Conversion refineries** (FCC + hydrocracking for heavy sour crudes)
- **Deep conversion** (coking + residue hydrocracking for extra-heavy crudes)

**(7 Marks: 2 for SARA, 2 for heteroatoms, 1 for TAN/metals, 2 for refining routes)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) API Gravity:**

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{0.91} - 131.5 = 155.49 - 131.5 = 23.99 \text{ °API}$$

**(ii) Watson K-factor:**

Convert to Rankine:
$$T_{°R} = 820 + 459.67 = 1279.67 \text{ °R}$$

$$K = \frac{(1279.67)^{1/3}}{0.91} = \frac{10.87}{0.91} = 11.95 \approx 12.0$$

**(iii) Classification:**
- Since 22.3 < 23.99 < 31.1 → **Medium Crude**
- Since 1.8 wt% > 0.5 wt% → **Sour Crude**
- Watson K ≈ 12 → **Intermediate (mixed) base crude**

**Final Classification: Medium, Sour, Intermediate-base crude** requiring both HDS and moderate conversion units.

**(7 Marks: 2 for API, 2 for K-factor, 1 for classification by API, 1 for sulfur classification, 1 for K classification)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper 1 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of crude oil property measurement** in a refinery. Map each physical and chemical property to its typical analyzer and signal output. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Watson Characterization Factor**. A crude has MeABP = 700 °F and SG = 0.89. Calculate the Watson K-factor and interpret the crude type. A second crude has MeABP = 950 °F and SG = 0.96. Calculate its K-factor and compare. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture of Crude Oil Property Measurement:**

The measurement of crude oil properties in a modern refinery is organized as a **multi-level hierarchy**: Field Level (sensors on pipes/tanks), Control Level (transmitters), and Management Level (DCS / historian).

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Crude Oil Inlet Pipe]
        |
        |--- Density Probe (Vibrating U-tube) --- API calculation
        |--- Viscometer (Capillary) --- μ in cP
        |--- XRF Sulfur Analyzer --- %S
        |--- Capacitance Probe --- %H2O (BS&W)
        |--- Cooled Cell Pour Point --- °C
        |--- Reid Vapor Pressure Bomb --- bar
        |--- Potentiometric Titrator (Lab) --- TAN
        |
[Transmitters] (4-20 mA / HART / Foundation Fieldbus)
        |
[DCS / Control Room]
        |
[Historian / APC / Refinery Planning]
```

Each measurement feeds:
- **Process Control:** Density → mass flow; Viscosity → pump speed control.
- **Product Quality:** Sulfur → diesel blend control; TAN → VDU metallurgy advisory.
- **Safety & Environment:** Flash point/RVP → tank farm interlocks; H2S → gas detection.

**(7 Marks: 2 for block diagram, 2 for property-to-instrument mapping, 2 for control/safety/quality linkage, 1 for neatness)**

---

**(b) Watson K-factor Calculations:**

**Definition:** The Watson Characterization Factor (K) is the ratio of the cube root of the molal average boiling point (in °R) to the specific gravity. It classifies crude as paraffinic, intermediate, or naphthenic.

**Crude 1:**
- MeABP = 700 °F → $T_{°R} = 700 + 459.67 = 1159.67 \text{ °R}$
- $K_1 = \dfrac{(1159.67)^{1/3}}{0.89} = \dfrac{10.52}{0.89} = 11.82$

Since 11 < K₁ < 12, this crude is on the **intermediate/naphthenic border**.

**Crude 2:**
- MeABP = 950 °F → $T_{°R} = 950 + 459.67 = 1409.67 \text{ °R}$
- $K_2 = \dfrac{(1409.67)^{1/3}}{0.96} = \dfrac{11.21}{0.96} = 11.68$

Since K₂ < 12, this crude is **naphthenic base**.

**Comparison:** Although Crude 2 has a higher boiling point (heavier), it is more naphthenic than Crude 1 (slightly lower K). This indicates Crude 2 will yield more lubricating oil base stocks but less high-grade diesel.

**(7 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for Crude 1 calculation, 2 for Crude 2 calculation, 2 for comparison and interpretation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the reference temperature** for API gravity. Always write "at 15.6 °C / 60 °F" — losing this costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing °API and SG** — they are inverse; a high API = low SG = light crude. Many students mistakenly report heavier crude as having higher API.
> 3. **Omitting units** in the K-factor calculation. State T in °R, not °F, in the formula.
> 4. **Sulfur classification cutoff** is **0.5 wt%**, not 1.0% as some students recall. Memorize this exactly.
> 5. **Watson K is dimensionless** — never attach a unit to the K-factor.
> 6. **TAN formula units** — report TAN in **mg KOH/g**, not g KOH/g.
> 7. **Classification mismatch** — students often classify a crude by only one parameter (e.g., by API alone). Always state **all three classifications** (API-based, Sulfur-based, Base-type).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Crude oil is NOT a pure substance** — it is a complex mixture of hydrocarbons and heteroatoms.
- **API Gravity is inverse to specific gravity** — water = 10°API; light crude > 31.1°API.
- **Sweet crude** has < 0.5 wt% S; **Sour crude** has > 0.5 wt% S.
- **SARA Analysis** = Saturates, Aromatics, Resins, Asphaltenes — the chemical fingerprint of any crude.
- **Watson K-factor** classifies crude as paraffinic (K > 12), intermediate (11 < K < 12), or naphthenic (K < 11).
- **Viscosity** is temperature-dependent; always report the reference temperature.
- **Pour point** governs pipeline operability; **Flash point** governs hazardous area classification.
- **TAN** drives material-of-construction (MOC) selection in distillation columns.
- **Density, viscosity, sulfur, TAN, and pour point** are the five most frequently measured properties online in modern refineries.
- **Instrumentation mapping** is a high-yield KTU topic — always link each property to its analyzer and signal.
- **Crude assay** is the complete laboratory report of all these properties and is the foundational document for any refinery design.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** In 14-mark questions, allocate **1 page** for definitions, **2 pages** for derivations/calculations, and **1 page** for diagrams/interpretation. Always box your final numerical answer and label it with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source Rock and Maturation
topics.content:
# SOURCE ROCK AND MATURATION ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Source Rock** is defined as any fine-grained sedimentary rock (typically shale, mudstone, or carbonate) containing sufficient quantities of organic matter (kerogen) capable of generating and expelling economically significant volumes of hydrocarbons (petroleum and natural gas) when subjected to appropriate thermal maturation over geological time. **Kerogen** is the insoluble, high-molecular-weight, dispersed organic matter preserved in sedimentary rocks that, upon progressive burial and heating, transforms into liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons. **Maturation** is the irreversible, temperature- and time-dependent process through which kerogen is progressively converted into crude oil, condensate, and natural gas via thermal cracking of carbon–carbon bonds. This process is the geological engine that transforms dead organic matter into economic petroleum reservoirs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEEIT861 Module 1):** > The chemistry, burial history, and thermal evolution of organic matter inside source rocks form the *geochemical foundation* upon which all upstream petrochemical exploration decisions rest. The maturation index dictates whether a formation will yield oil, gas, or nothing at all. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Model Imagine a **pressure cooker** buried in your kitchen garden for millions of years: | Pressure Cooker Component | Geological Equivalent | |---|---| | Vegetables (carrots, beans) inside the pot | **Kerogen** (organic debris from dead organisms) | | The sealed pot | The impermeable **source rock** (shale) | | The slow-rising heat from below | **Geothermal gradient** (≈25–30 °C/km) | | Steam building inside the pot | Generated **hydrocarbons** (oil and gas) | | Pressure forcing the lid open | **Primary migration** (expulsion) into reservoir rock | Just as a poorly sealed cooker cannot build pressure, a source rock without adequate sealing and organic richness cannot produce hydrocarbons. The "cooking" must be done **slowly** — nature's kitchen takes **millions of years**. ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Quantitative Thresholds) A rock is classified as a **potential source rock** when it meets the following geochemical thresholds: - **Total Organic Carbon (TOC):** $\geq \mathbf{0.5\ \text{wt\%}}$ (poor), $\mathbf{1.0–2.0\ \text{wt\%}}$ (good), $\mathbf{>\ 2.0\ \text{wt\%}}$ (excellent). - **Hydrogen Index (HI):** $\geq \mathbf{150\ \text{mg HC/g TOC}}$ for oil-prone source rocks. - **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro):** $\mathbf{0.6\ \%\ \leq\ R_{o}\ \leq\ 1.3\ \%}$ for the oil window. > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Significance:** > The petrochemical industry is fundamentally built upon the discovery, characterization, and efficient extraction of hydrocarbons generated by source rock maturation. Without mature source rocks, there are no refineries to control, no pipelines to automate, and no cracking units to instrument. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Organic Matter in Source Rocks Organic matter preserved in sediments is categorized into two operational fractions: 1. **Bitumen** — the solvent-extractable fraction (oils, asphaltenes, resins, NSO compounds). 2. **Kerogen** — the insoluble, macromolecular fraction preserved in the rock matrix. Kerogen is the **true mother of petroleum** because it is the precursor from which hydrocarbons are thermally cracked. Kerogen is sub-divided into four types based on its atomic H/C and O/C ratios, originally described by **Durand (1980)** and plotted on the **Van Krevelen diagram**. ### 2.2 The Four Kerogen Types (Van Krevelen Classification) | Type | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Origin | Hydrocarbon Yield | Maceral Analogue | |------|-----------|-----------|--------|-------------------|------------------| | **Type I** (Algal) | > 1.5 | < 0.1 | Lacustrine algae, botryococcus | **Excellent** for oil | Alginite | | **Type II** (Marine) | 1.2 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.2 | Marine planktonic, bacterial | **Very good** for oil & gas | Exinite, Sporinite | | **Type III** (Terrestrial) | < 1.0 | 0.2 – 0.3 | Higher plant debris, woody | **Gas-prone** | Vitrinite, Inertinite | | **Type IV** (Inert) | < 0.5 | < 0.2 | Oxidized/recycled carbon | **None** (dead carbon) | Inertinite | ### 2.3 The Three Stages of Maturation (Tissot & Welte, 1978) The maturation process is divided into three thermogenic stages characterized by increasing temperature and depth: - **Diagenesis (Ro < 0.5 %):** Low-temperature (≤ 60 °C) biochemical alteration. Kerogen loses oxygen (as CO₂ and H₂O). **No significant oil generation.** - **Catagenesis (Ro = 0.5 – 2.0 %):** The principal **oil window** (Ro = 0.6 – 1.3 %) and **wet gas window** (Ro = 1.3 – 2.0 %). Thermal cracking of kerogen liberates liquid hydrocarbons, then wet gas (condensate) as temperature climbs. - **Metagenesis (Ro > 2.0 %):** The **dry gas window**. Only methane and pyrobitumen remain; all liquid hydrocarbons have been cracked to gas. ### 2.4 Critical Maturation Indicators (Rock-Eval Pyrolysis) **Rock-Eval Pyrolysis** (developed by the Institut Français du Pétrole) is the standard laboratory technique used to determine the type, quantity, and maturity of organic matter in a source rock. Four key parameters are measured: - **S1** (mg HC/g rock): Free hydrocarbons already generated and present in the rock. - **S2** (mg HC/g rock): Hydrocarbons generated by thermal cracking of kerogen. - **S3** (mg CO₂/g rock): Oxygen-containing compounds (CO₂) released from kerogen. - **Tmax** (°C): Temperature at which the S2 peak reaches its maximum. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Units | Industrial Significance | |-----------|---------|-------|--------------------------| | Total Organic Carbon | $\text{TOC}$ (measured) | wt % | Quantifies richness of source rock | | Hydrogen Index | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{S_{2}}{\text{TOC}} \times 100$ | mg HC / g TOC | Indicates kerogen type (oil vs gas) | | Oxygen Index | $\text{OI} = \dfrac{S_{3}}{\text{TOC}} \times 100$ | mg CO₂ / g TOC | Indicates degree of oxidation | | Production Index | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{S_{1}}{S_{1} + S_{2}}$ | dimensionless | Measures maturity (PI < 0.1 = immature) | | Transformation Ratio | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{S_{1}}{S_{1} + S_{2}}$ | dimensionless | Same as PI; fraction of kerogen converted | | Vitrinite Reflectance | $R_{o}$ (measured optically) | % | Industry-standard maturity gauge | | Geothermal Gradient | $\dfrac{dT}{dz}$ | °C / km | Determines maturation timing | | Burial Depth | $z = \dfrac{T_{\text{formation}} - T_{\text{surface}}}{\text{Gradient}}$ | km | Required depth for oil window entry | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Examination Point:** > The Van Krevelen diagram (H/C vs O/C plot) is the single most examined visual tool in any KTU examination on this topic. Mastering its four kerogen trajectories is non-negotiable. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Calculating the Hydrogen Index (HI) of a Source Rock **Statement:** A shale sample submitted to Rock-Eval pyrolysis yields the following measurements: - S1 = 1.2 mg HC / g rock - S2 = 5.6 mg HC / g rock - S3 = 0.8 mg CO₂ / g rock - TOC = 3.5 wt % Determine the **Hydrogen Index (HI)**, **Oxygen Index (OI)**, and **Production Index (PI)**. Classify the kerogen type and interpret the maturity. #### Step 1 — Hydrogen Index Calculation The Hydrogen Index quantifies the hydrogen richness of the kerogen and is mathematically defined as the mass of pyrolyzable hydrocarbons (S2) normalized to the mass of total organic carbon present in the rock. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HI} &= \frac{S_{2}}{\text{TOC}} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{HI} &= \frac{5.6\ \text{mg HC/g rock}}{3.5\ \text{wt \%}} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{HI} &= 1.60 \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{HI} &= \mathbf{160\ \text{mg HC / g TOC}} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation logic:** A measured HI of 160 places this kerogen firmly within the **Type II (marine, oil-prone)** envelope. Type I kerogens typically display HI > 300, while Type III is HI < 100. The boundary of 150 mg HC/g TOC is the conventional cutoff for "oil-prone" classification. #### Step 2 — Oxygen Index Calculation The Oxygen Index reflects the oxygen content of the kerogen. A high OI indicates an immature, oxygen-rich (Type III or oxidized) organic matter, while a low OI is characteristic of mature Type I/II kerogens. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{OI} &= \frac{S_{3}}{\text{TOC}} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{OI} &= \frac{0.8\ \text{mg CO}_{2}\text{/g rock}}{3.5\ \text{wt \%}} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{OI} &= 0.2286 \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{OI} &= \mathbf{\approx 23\ \text{mg CO}_{2}\text{ / g TOC}} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation logic:** An OI of 23 is low, consistent with Type II kerogen at moderate maturity. Highly immature Type III kerogens can exhibit OI values exceeding 100. #### Step 3 — Production Index Calculation The Production Index is the ratio of already-generated, free hydrocarbons (S1) to the total hydrocarbon generation potential (S1 + S2). It is a sensitive, direct indicator of thermal maturity. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PI} &= \frac{S_{1}}{S_{1} + S_{2}} \\[6pt] \text{PI} &= \frac{1.2}{1.2 + 5.6} \\[6pt] \text{PI} &= \frac{1.2}{6.8} \\[6pt] \text{PI} &= 0.1765 \\[6pt] \text{PI} &\approx \mathbf{0.18} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation logic:** A PI of 0.18 falls **just above the oil-window onset threshold (0.10)** but **below the peak-oil threshold (0.40)**. This places the rock in the **early-to-peak oil window**, where active hydrocarbon generation is occurring. #### Step 4 — Synthesis: Classification Summary | Parameter | Value | Interpretation | |-----------|-------|----------------| | TOC | 3.5 wt % | **Excellent** source rock richness | | HI | 160 mg HC/g TOC | **Type II** (marine, oil-prone) kerogen | | OI | 23 mg CO₂/g TOC | Low oxygen → moderate maturity | | PI | 0.18 | **Early oil window** — active generation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Decision Rule (Industry-Standard):** > 1. If TOC < 0.5 wt % → **Not a source rock** (do not pursue further analysis). > 2. If HI > 150 and 0.1 < PI < 0.4 → **Active oil-prone source rock** (high-priority exploration target). > 3. If Ro > 1.3 % → Source rock is **over-mature** (only gas potential remains). ### 3.2 Derivation: Maturation Depth from Geothermal Gradient **Statement:** A sedimentary basin exhibits a mean surface temperature of 20 °C and a geothermal gradient of 28 °C/km. Determine the burial depth at which the source rock enters the oil window, assuming a minimum formation temperature of 80 °C is required. #### Step 1 — Establish the Linear Geothermal Equation The temperature in a sedimentary basin increases approximately linearly with depth according to the steady-state conduction model. $$ \begin{aligned} T(z) &= T_{0} + \left(\frac{dT}{dz}\right) \cdot z \end{aligned} $$ Where $T_{0}$ is the surface temperature (°C), $\frac{dT}{dz}$ is the geothermal gradient (°C/km), and $z$ is depth (km). #### Step 2 — Rearrange to Solve for Depth $$ \begin{aligned} z &= \frac{T(z) - T_{0}}{\dfrac{dT}{dz}} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values $$ \begin{aligned} z &= \frac{80\ \text{°C} - 20\ \text{°C}}{28\ \text{°C/km}} \\[6pt] z &= \frac{60\ \text{°C}}{28\ \text{°C/km}} \\[6pt] z &= 2.143\ \text{km} \\[6pt] z &\approx \mathbf{2.14\ km} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4 — Verification and Industrial Context A depth of 2.14 km is geologically realistic for oil-window entry in typical sedimentary basins. For comparison: - **North Sea** basins: oil window entry at ≈ 2.5–3.5 km. - **Gulf of Mexico** deep-water: oil window entry at ≈ 3.0–4.0 km. - **Anadarko Basin** (high gradient, 35–40 °C/km): oil window entry at ≈ 1.8–2.2 km. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Source Rock Maturation Workflow This diagram maps the full geochemical-to-industrial workflow of source rock evaluation, from initial sediment deposition to downstream petrochemical feedstock generation. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SEDIMENT DEPOSITION
Organic Matter Accumulation] --> B[DIAGENESIS STAGE
Ro less than 0.5 percent
Biochemical Alteration] B --> C[TYPE IDENTIFICATION
Rock Eval Pyrolysis
Van Krevelen Plot] C --> D{CATAGENESIS GATE
Ro between 0.5 and 1.3} D -->|Oil Window Reached| E[OIL GENERATION
PI 0.1 to 0.4
HI greater than 150] D -->|Over Mature Ro greater than 1.3| F[WET GAS WINDOW
Condensate Production] F --> G[METAGENESIS STAGE
Ro greater than 2.0 percent
Dry Gas Generation] E --> H[PRIMARY MIGRATION
Expulsion from Source Rock] G --> H H --> I[SECONDARY MIGRATION
Migration to Reservoir Rock] I --> J[RESERVOIR TRAP
Accumulation in Porous Media] J --> K[PETROCHEMICAL FEEDSTOCK
Refinery Input Stream] K --> L[INSTRUMENTATION TARGET
Flow Temperature Pressure Control] L --> M[INTEGRATED PROCESS CONTROL
KTU Module PEEIT861] ``` ### 4.2 Kerogen Classification and Hydrocarbon Yield This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the four kerogen types, their depositional environments, and their associated hydrocarbon generation potential — a high-yield KTU exam diagram. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[KEROGEN CLASSIFICATION] --> B[TYPE I ALGAL] A --> C[TYPE II MARINE] A --> D[TYPE III TERRESTRIAL] A --> E[TYPE IV INERT] B --> B1[Lacustrine Environment] B --> B2[HI greater than 300] B --> B3[EXCELLENT OIL YIELD] C --> C1[Marine Shale] C --> C2[HI 150 to 300] C --> C3[OIL AND GAS YIELD] D --> D1[Coaly Terrestrial] D --> D2[HI less than 100] D --> D3[GAS PRONE ONLY] E --> E1[Oxidized Recycled Carbon] E --> E2[No Generation] E --> E3[DEAD CARBON] ``` ### 4.3 Process Flow — Maturation Window Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Burial Depth 0 km] --> P2[Diagenesis Zone 0 to 1.5 km] P2 --> P3[Oil Window 1.5 to 3.5 km Ro 0.6 to 1.3] P3 --> P4[Wet Gas Window 3.5 to 5.0 km Ro 1.3 to 2.0] P4 --> P5[Dry Gas Window greater than 5.0 km Ro greater than 2.0] P1 --> Q1[Ro less than 0.3 Immature] P2 --> Q2[Ro 0.3 to 0.5 Early Diagenesis] P3 --> Q3[Ro 0.6 to 1.3 Peak Oil Generation] P4 --> Q4[Ro 1.3 to 2.0 Condensate Zone] P5 --> Q5[Ro greater than 2.0 Metagenesis Dry Gas] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** > The maturation window diagram is the most-frequently tested schematic in this KTU module. Students should be able to sketch the Ro progression, the dominant hydrocarbon phase in each window, and the approximate temperature range for each zone from memory. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define "Source Rock" in the context of petroleum geochemistry. List any two essential geochemical parameters used to evaluate a source rock.** **Model Answer:** A *source rock* is a fine-grained sedimentary rock (typically shale, mudstone, or carbonate) that contains sufficient organic matter (kerogen) in the form of Type I, II, or III kerogen to generate and expel commercially significant volumes of hydrocarbons when subjected to appropriate thermal maturation. **Two essential geochemical parameters:** 1. **Total Organic Carbon (TOC):** Expressed in weight percent, this quantifies the total organic richness of the rock. A TOC ≥ 0.5 wt % is the minimum threshold, while values above 2.0 wt % indicate an excellent source rock. 2. **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro):** Expressed as a percentage, this is the optical measure of the reflectance of the maceral vitrinite in polished rock samples. It is the industry-standard indicator of thermal maturity. The oil window is defined by 0.6 % ≤ Ro ≤ 1.3 %. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **What is Kerogen? Differentiate between Type I and Type III Kerogen.** **Model Answer:** **Kerogen** is the insoluble, high-molecular-weight, dispersed organic matter preserved in sedimentary rocks. It is the precursor material from which liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons are generated through thermal maturation (cracking) during burial. **Difference between Type I and Type III Kerogen:** | Feature | Type I (Algal) | Type III (Terrestrial) | |---------|----------------|------------------------| | **Origin** | Lacustrine algae (e.g., *Botryococcus braunii*) | Higher plant debris (woody, coaly) | | **H/C Ratio** | High (> 1.5) | Low (< 1.0) | | **O/C Ratio** | Low (< 0.1) | High (0.2 – 0.3) | | **Hydrogen Index** | > 300 mg HC/g TOC | < 100 mg HC/g TOC | | **Hydrocarbon Yield** | Excellent — predominantly oil | Gas-prone only | | **Depositional Environment** | Freshwater to brackish lakes | Swamps, deltaic, fluvial | --- ### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Module 1, KTU ESE Style `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **(a)** Describe in detail the four types of Kerogen as classified on the Van Krevelen diagram. Discuss their depositional environments and corresponding hydrocarbon generation potentials. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A potential source rock sample yields the following Rock-Eval Pyrolysis results: S1 = 0.8 mg HC/g rock, S2 = 4.0 mg HC/g rock, S3 = 0.5 mg CO₂/g rock, TOC = 2.5 wt %. Compute the Hydrogen Index, Oxygen Index, and Production Index. Interpret the kerogen type and maturity stage of the sample. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a): The **Van Krevelen diagram** is a graphical representation of kerogen evolution, plotting the atomic H/C ratio (ordinate) against the O/C ratio (abscissa) of isolated kerogen. Four primary kerogen types are distinguished: **1. Type I (Algal) Kerogen** - **H/C:** > 1.5, **O/C:** < 0.1. - Originates predominantly from lacustrine (freshwater) algae, especially *Botryococcus* species, deposited in anoxic, stratified lake basins. - Characterized by extremely high hydrogen content and aliphatic (paraffinic) molecular structure. - **Hydrocarbon yield:** Excellent — the highest oil-generation potential of all kerogen types. Examples: Green River Shale (USA), Coorongite deposits. **2. Type II (Marine) Kerogen** - **H/C:** 1.2 – 1.5, **O/C:** 0.1 – 0.2. - Derived from marine planktonic organisms (phytoplankton, zooplankton) and bacterial biomass deposited in anoxic marine shales and carbonates. - The most economically significant kerogen globally, generating both oil and gas. - **Hydrocarbon yield:** Very good — both oil and wet gas. Examples: Kimmeridge Clay (North Sea), Bazhenov Shale (Western Siberia). **3. Type III (Terrestrial) Kerogen** - **H/C:** < 1.0, **O/C:** 0.2 – 0.3. - Derived from higher plant debris, woody tissue, and humic material deposited in deltaic, fluvial, and swamp environments. - Aromatic and oxygen-rich in composition. - **Hydrocarbon yield:** Predominantly gas. Examples: Mahakam Delta coals, North Sea Mandal Formation. **4. Type IV (Inert) Kerogen** - **H/C:** < 0.5, **O/C:** Variable (low). - Composed of oxidized, reworked, or inert carbon residues (inertinite macerals). - **Hydrocarbon yield:** None — "dead carbon" with no economic significance. Acts as a diluent in source rock assessment. As maturation progresses, all four types evolve along **diagonal pathways toward lower H/C and O/C ratios** on the Van Krevelen diagram, ultimately converging at the carbon endpoint (anthracite/meta-anthracite) at Ro > 4.0 %. #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Step 1: Hydrogen Index (HI)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HI} &= \frac{S_{2}}{\text{TOC}} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{HI} &= \frac{4.0}{2.5} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{HI} &= \mathbf{160\ \text{mg HC / g TOC}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Oxygen Index (OI)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{OI} &= \frac{S_{3}}{\text{TOC}} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{OI} &= \frac{0.5}{2.5} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{OI} &= \mathbf{20\ \text{mg CO}_{2}\text{ / g TOC}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Production Index (PI)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PI} &= \frac{S_{1}}{S_{1} + S_{2}} \\[6pt] \text{PI} &= \frac{0.8}{0.8 + 4.0} \\[6pt] \text{PI} &= \frac{0.8}{4.8} \\[6pt] \text{PI} &= \mathbf{0.167 \approx 0.17} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Interpretation** - **TOC = 2.5 wt %:** Indicates a **good-to-excellent** source rock richness. - **HI = 160 mg HC/g TOC:** Falls within the **Type II (marine, oil-prone)** envelope (range 150–300). - **OI = 20 mg CO₂/g TOC:** Low OI is consistent with **moderate maturity** and Type II kerogen origin. - **PI = 0.17:** Sits within the **oil window** (0.10 < PI < 0.40), indicating active hydrocarbon generation. **Conclusion:** The sample is a **Type II (marine), mature, oil-prone source rock currently generating hydrocarbons** — a prime candidate for further exploration and reservoir targeting. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the concept of **Maturation** of source rocks. With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **three stages of thermal maturation** (diagenesis, catagenesis, metagenesis) and indicate the dominant hydrocarbon phase in each stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sedimentary basin has a surface temperature of 25 °C and a geothermal gradient of 30 °C/km. Calculate the depth at which the source rock will reach: (i) the onset of the oil window (T = 90 °C) (ii) the peak gas window (T = 150 °C) State the dominant hydrocarbon phase expected at each depth. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a): **Concept of Maturation:** Maturation refers to the **irreversible, time- and temperature-driven transformation of kerogen into liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons** through the thermal cracking of large, complex organic molecules into smaller, simpler hydrocarbon molecules. This process occurs over geological timescales (millions of years) as the source rock is progressively buried beneath younger sediments. The process is governed by the **Arrhenius relationship**, where reaction rates increase exponentially with temperature. The key controlling variables are: - **Temperature** (dominant variable), - **Time** of exposure to elevated temperature, - **Pressure** (secondary effect, but influences expulsion and migration). **Three Stages of Thermal Maturation:** | Stage | Depth Range (typical) | Temperature | Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro) | Dominant Hydrocarbon Phase | |-------|----------------------|-------------|----------------------------|----------------------------| | **Diagenesis** | 0 – 1.5 km | < 60 °C | < 0.5 % | Methane (biogenic); kerogen dewatering | | **Catagenesis** | 1.5 – 5.0 km | 60 – 175 °C | 0.5 – 2.0 % | **Oil** (Ro 0.6–1.3 %), then **wet gas/condensate** (Ro 1.3–2.0 %) | | **Metagenesis** | > 5.0 km | > 175 °C | > 2.0 % | **Dry gas** (methane) only; pyrobitumen residue | - **Diagenesis:** Biochemical and low-temperature alteration. Kerogen loses oxygen as CO₂ and H₂O. No significant thermogenic oil generation. - **Catagenesis:** Thermal cracking dominates. C–C bonds in kerogen break, releasing liquid hydrocarbons (the **oil window**). With continued burial, liquid hydrocarbons are themselves cracked into **wet gas (condensate)**. - **Metagenesis:** All liquid hydrocarbons have been cracked; only **dry methane gas** survives. Residual carbon forms pyrobitumen and graphite. *(Refer to the Process Flow diagram in SECTION 4.3 for the visual schematic.)* #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Geothermal Equation:** $$ T(z) = T_{0} + \left(\frac{dT}{dz}\right) \cdot z $$ Solving for depth: $$ z = \frac{T(z) - T_{0}}{\dfrac{dT}{dz}} $$ **Given:** $T_{0} = 25\ \text{°C}$, Gradient = 30 °C/km. **Sub-part (i): Depth at onset of oil window (T = 90 °C)** $$ \begin{aligned} z_{1} &= \frac{90 - 25}{30} \\[6pt] z_{1} &= \frac{65}{30} \\[6pt] z_{1} &= 2.167\ \text{km} \\[6pt] z_{1} &\approx \mathbf{2.17\ km} \end{aligned} $$ **Dominant hydrocarbon phase:** **Crude oil** (early oil window; Ro ≈ 0.6 %). **Sub-part (ii): Depth at peak gas window (T = 150 °C)** $$ \begin{aligned} z_{2} &= \frac{150 - 25}{30} \\[6pt] z_{2} &= \frac{125}{30} \\[6pt] z_{2} &= 4.167\ \text{km} \\[6pt] z_{2} &\approx \mathbf{4.17\ km} \end{aligned} $$ **Dominant hydrocarbon phase:** **Wet gas / condensate** transitioning toward **dry gas** (Ro ≈ 1.5 – 2.0 %). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract surface temperature** in the geothermal equation: Many students directly divide the target temperature by the gradient, neglecting $T_{0}$. This yields a *higher* depth value and **costs 1 full mark**. > 2. **Confusing PI and TR:** Both use the same formula ($S_{1}/(S_{1}+S_{2})$), but Transformation Ratio strictly refers to a kerogen-to-hydrocarbon conversion metric, while PI is the broader maturity indicator. Examiners reward explicit terminological correctness. > 3. **Mixing up Type I and Type II kerogen environments:** Type I is **lacustrine (lake)** algae, NOT marine. Type II is marine. This confusion is the single most common cause of lost marks in kerogen classification questions. > 4. **Not stating units explicitly in numerical answers:** Always include km, °C, wt %, and mg HC/g TOC. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction per answer. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - **Source Rock Definition:** Fine-grained, organic-rich sedimentary rock with TOC ≥ 0.5 wt % capable of generating hydrocarbons. - **Kerogen** = insoluble organic precursor; **Bitumen** = soluble fraction. - **Four Kerogen Types:** - **Type I** → Lacustrine algae → **Oil** (HI > 300). - **Type II** → Marine plankton → **Oil + Gas** (HI 150–300). - **Type III** → Higher plants → **Gas** (HI < 100). - **Type IV** → Inert carbon → **None**. - **Three Maturation Stages:** **D**iagenesis (**D**eposition, low T) → **C**atagenesis (**C**racking, oil window) → **M**etagenesis (**M**ethane, dry gas). - **Oil Window:** Ro = 0.6 – 1.3 %; Temperature ≈ 60 – 130 °C; Depth ≈ 1.5 – 3.5 km. - **Gas Window:** Ro = 1.3 – 4.0 %; Temperature > 130 °C. - **Rock-Eval Parameters:** S1 (free HC), S2 (kerogen-derived HC), S3 (CO₂), Tmax. - **Key Formulas (memorize verbatim):** - $\text{HI} = (S_{2}/\text{TOC}) \times 100$ - $\text{OI} = (S_{3}/\text{TOC}) \times 100$ - $\text{PI} = S_{1}/(S_{1}+S_{2})$ - $z = (T_{\text{formation}} - T_{\text{surface}})/(\text{Gradient})$ - **Van Krevelen Diagram:** Plots H/C vs O/C; four diagonal evolution paths; convergence at carbon endpoint. - **TOC Thresholds:** < 0.5 (poor) → 0.5–1.0 (fair) → 1.0–2.0 (good) → > 2.0 (excellent). - **Geothermal Gradient:** Typical crustal value ≈ 25–30 °C/km.

topics.title: Migration of Oil Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Migration of Oil Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Migration of Oil** is the post-generative geochemical and physical process by which hydrocarbons (crude oil and natural gas) are expelled from the fine-grained, low-permeability **source rock** (typically organic-rich shale) and subsequently move through permeable carrier beds and conduits until they are finally trapped in a porous, permeable **reservoir rock** beneath an impermeable **cap rock** (seal).

In the context of the petroleum system, oil migration is broadly classified into three sequential stages:

1. **Primary Migration** — Expulsion of hydrocarbons from the source rock into adjacent carrier beds.
2. **Secondary Migration** — Upward and lateral movement of hydrocarbons through high-permeability carrier rocks toward the trap.
3. **Tertiary Migration** — Re-mobilization of an already-accumulated hydrocarbon pool due to geological disturbances (tilting, faulting, biodegradation, or gas invasion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Examiners frequently test the distinction between *primary* and *secondary* migration, the driving forces involved, and the role of **buoyancy**, **capillary pressure**, and **hydrodynamics** as the principal energy sources.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a wet kitchen sponge (the source rock) soaked with oily sauce (hydrocarbons) sitting inside a large plastic tub (the carrier bed). When you gently tilt the tub, the oil slowly seeps out of the sponge, rises through the watery environment, and floats to the top — eventually gathering under the upside-down lid of a tiffin box (the cap rock). The oil does not "want" to stay trapped in the sponge; the sponge simply had no more room to hold it. The lid (cap rock) prevents the oil from escaping further upward.

In geological reality:
- The **sponge** = source rock (kerogen-saturated, low permeability)
- The **oily sauce** = generated oil and gas
- The **water in the tub** = formation water saturating the carrier/reservoir rock
- The **tiffin lid** = impermeable cap rock (e.g., shale, evaporite)
- The **tilt** = geological tilting / buoyancy force from density contrast

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental driving principle: **oil is lighter than water**, so it naturally tends to rise vertically once freed from the source rock matrix.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Source Rock | Fine-grained, organic-rich rock (shale/marl) where hydrocarbons are generated |
| Carrier Bed | Permeable rock (sandstone/carbonate) through which oil migrates |
| Reservoir Rock | Porous, permeable rock holding hydrocarbons in a trap |
| Cap Rock (Seal) | Impermeable rock layer preventing further vertical migration |
| Kerogen | Solid, insoluble organic matter precursor to hydrocarbons |
| Buoyancy Force | Upward force due to density difference between oil and water |
| Capillary Pressure | Pressure difference across oil–water interface in pores |
| Relative Permeability | Effective permeability of a rock to one fluid in the presence of others |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Petroleum System and Migration Window

Migration can only begin **after** hydrocarbons have been generated. This occurs when the source rock reaches a thermal maturity window (typically **60 °C – 120 °C**, corresponding to the "oil window"). Below this, kerogen remains unconverted; above it, the kerogen is over-cooked into dry gas or graphite.

The migration process is governed by the simultaneous balance of **four fundamental energy terms**:

1. **Buoyancy Energy ($E_b$)** — drives oil upward.
2. **Capillary Resistance ($P_c$)** — resists oil entry into small pores.
3. **Hydrodynamic Force ($F_h$)** — lateral force from moving formation water.
4. **Viscous Drag ($F_v$)** — friction opposing oil flow through pores.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Migration Mechanism

### Step 1: Generation within Source Rock
Kerogen undergoes thermal cracking (catagenesis) producing liquid hydrocarbons and gas. The generated hydrocarbons initially exist as:
- Dissolved in kerogen (adsorbed phase)
- Dissolved in formation water
- Free phase in micro-fractures and pores

### Step 2: Expulsion (Primary Migration)
Because shale has very small pore throats (typically **< 50 nm**), oil cannot pass through unless one of the following occurs:
- **Pressure buildup** exceeds capillary entry pressure (micro-fracturing).
- Oil migrates in **solution** (dissolved in gas or in water at high pressure/temperature).
- **Continuous oil network** forms when saturation exceeds the critical value (~10–20%).

### Step 3: Secondary Migration — The Critical Journey
Once expelled, oil moves **upward** through water-saturated permeable carrier beds. The driving force is **buoyancy** due to the density difference between oil and water.

> [!NOTE]
> The **critical column height** of oil needed to overcome capillary entry pressure of the cap rock is the deciding factor for trap integrity.

### Step 4: Trap Filling and Accumulation
Oil accumulates beneath the cap rock, displacing water from the largest pores first. Migration stops when the trap is full, the seal leaks, or a spill point is reached.

## 2.3 The Four Forces in Detail

### A. Buoyancy Force
The pressure gradient arising from the density difference between hydrocarbons and formation water.

$$ \Delta \rho = \rho_w - \rho_o $$

where $\rho_w$ is formation water density (~**1.00–1.20 g/cm³**) and $\rho_o$ is oil density (~**0.70–0.95 g/cm³**).

### B. Capillary Pressure
Pressure difference across a curved oil–water meniscus in a pore throat of radius $r$.

$$ P_c = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r} $$

where $\sigma$ is the interfacial tension (oil–water), and $\theta$ is the contact angle.

### C. Darcy Velocity of Migration
The volume flux of oil moving through the carrier bed follows **Darcy's Law**.

$$ q_o = -\frac{k \cdot k_{ro}}{\mu_o} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} $$

where $k$ is absolute permeability, $k_{ro}$ is relative permeability to oil, $\mu_o$ is oil viscosity, and $dP/dx$ is the pressure gradient.

### D. Hydrodynamic Force
If formation water is flowing, it exerts a lateral pressure on the oil column, tilting the oil–water contact.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) \cdot g \cdot H$ | Buoyancy force on oil column of height $H$ | Pa |
| 2 | $P_c = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary entry pressure of a pore throat | Pa |
| 3 | $h_c = \dfrac{P_c^{cap}}{(\rho_w - \rho_o)g}$ | Critical oil column height to leak through cap | m |
| 4 | $q_o = -\dfrac{k \cdot k_{ro}}{\mu_o} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Darcy flux of oil through carrier bed | m/s |
| 5 | $S_{or} = f(P_c, k)$ | Residual oil saturation (function of capillary and permeability) | fraction |
| 6 | $v_m = \dfrac{k \cdot k_{ro} \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot g}{\mu_o}$ | Vertical migration velocity (simplified) | m/s |
| 7 | $t_m = \dfrac{D}{v_m}$ | Time taken for migration over distance $D$ | s |
| 8 | $P_{hydro} = \rho_w g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa |
| 9 | $\phi \cdot S_{hc} = V_{oil}/V_{rock}$ | Hydrocarbon pore volume per unit rock volume | m³/m³ |
| 10 | $\phi = \dfrac{V_p}{V_b}$ | Porosity of rock | fraction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **capillary pressure** (force opposing entry) with **buoyancy** (force driving migration). A trap holds oil only when buoyancy $F_b$ is *less than* capillary resistance $P_c$ of the cap rock.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the migration mechanism is foundational for:
- **Exploration Geophysics** — identifying potential traps on seismic sections.
- **Reservoir Engineering** — estimating recoverable reserves and spill points.
- **Drilling Operations** — predicting overpressured zones and seal integrity.
- **Production Optimization** — designing secondary/tertiary recovery (EOR) based on residual oil saturation $S_{or}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Buoyancy Force on an Oil Column

Consider a vertical oil column of height $H$ trapped beneath a cap rock, surrounded by formation water. The pressure at the base of the oil column (in the water leg) is:

$$ P_{base}^{water} = P_{top} + \rho_w g H $$

The pressure at the base of the oil column (in the oil leg) is:

$$ P_{base}^{oil} = P_{top} + \rho_o g H $$

The **buoyancy pressure** (excess pressure at the oil–water contact) is the difference:

$$ \Delta P_b = P_{base}^{water} - P_{base}^{oil} $$

Subtracting the two expressions:

$$ \Delta P_b = \rho_w g H - \rho_o g H $$

Factoring out the common terms $g H$:

$$ \Delta P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g H $$

This is the **buoyancy force per unit area** pushing the oil column upward against the cap rock. The derivation assumes hydrostatic equilibrium and incompressible fluids, which is the standard KTU assumption for board questions.

> [!NOTE]
> The density difference $(\rho_w - \rho_o)$ is the single most important parameter in migration. Typical values are **0.1–0.4 g/cm³** (or 100–400 kg/m³ in SI units).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Critical Oil Column Height for Seal Failure

The cap rock has pore throats of effective radius $r$. The capillary pressure resisting oil entry into the cap rock is:

$$ P_c^{cap} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r} $$

For the trap to hold oil, the buoyancy pressure at the top of the oil column must not exceed this capillary entry pressure:

$$ \Delta P_b \le P_c^{cap} $$

Substituting the buoyancy expression:

$$ (\rho_w - \rho_o) g H_c \le \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r} $$

Solving for the critical column height $H_c$:

$$ H_c = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r (\rho_w - \rho_o) g} $$

This equation is one of the **most frequently tested formulas** in KTU board examinations for petroleum engineering courses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When numerical values are given, students are expected to substitute SI-consistent units: $\sigma$ in N/m, $r$ in m, $\rho$ in kg/m³, and $g = 9.81$ m/s². The answer for $H_c$ will be in meters.

## 3.3 Derivation of Migration Velocity Using Darcy's Law

For a vertical oil column rising through a water-saturated carrier bed, the pressure gradient is the buoyancy gradient:

$$ \frac{dP}{dz} = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g $$

Substituting into Darcy's Law for the oil phase:

$$ v_m = \frac{k \cdot k_{ro}}{\mu_o} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz} $$

Replacing the pressure gradient with the buoyancy expression:

$$ v_m = \frac{k \cdot k_{ro} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_o) g}{\mu_o} $$

This is the **theoretical vertical migration velocity** of oil through the carrier bed. The migration time over a vertical distance $D$ is:

$$ t_m = \frac{D}{v_m} = \frac{D \cdot \mu_o}{k \cdot k_{ro} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_o) g} $$

For typical carrier beds ($k = 100$ mD, $k_{ro} = 0.5$, $\mu_o = 1$ cP, $\Delta\rho = 200$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²), the migration velocity is of the order of **centimeters to meters per year**, consistent with geological timescales of millions of years.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A sandstone carrier bed has permeability $k = 50$ mD, relative permeability to oil $k_{ro} = 0.4$, oil viscosity $\mu_o = 2$ cP, water density $\rho_w = 1050$ kg/m³, oil density $\rho_o = 850$ kg/m³. The vertical distance of migration is $D = 1000$ m. Calculate:
(a) The buoyancy pressure gradient.
(b) The Darcy migration velocity of oil.
(c) The time taken for migration.

**Solution:**

### Part (a): Buoyancy Pressure Gradient
Convert units to SI: $k = 50 \text{ mD} = 50 \times 9.869 \times 10^{-16} \text{ m}^2 = 4.935 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^2$.

The buoyancy pressure gradient is:

$$ \frac{dP}{dz} = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g $$

Substituting values:

$$ \frac{dP}{dz} = (1050 - 850) \times 9.81 $$

$$ \frac{dP}{dz} = 200 \times 9.81 = 1962 \text{ Pa/m} $$

### Part (b): Darcy Migration Velocity
Convert oil viscosity to SI: $\mu_o = 2 \text{ cP} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$.

Using the derived formula:

$$ v_m = \frac{k \cdot k_{ro}}{\mu_o} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz} $$

$$ v_m = \frac{(4.935 \times 10^{-14}) \times 0.4}{(2 \times 10^{-3})} \times 1962 $$

$$ v_m = \frac{1.974 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 1962 $$

$$ v_m = (9.87 \times 10^{-12}) \times 1962 $$

$$ v_m \approx 1.937 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s} $$

Converting to m/year (multiply by $3.154 \times 10^7$):

$$ v_m \approx 0.611 \text{ m/year} $$

### Part (c): Migration Time

$$ t_m = \frac{D}{v_m} = \frac{1000}{0.611} \approx 1636 \text{ years} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** This is a *very* fast migration. In real geological systems with lower permeability and higher viscosity, migration times range from **10⁴ to 10⁶ years**, fitting the geological timescale of petroleum systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Migration Pathway — From Source to Trap

The following Mermaid block illustrates the *block-level functional architecture* of oil migration, mapping the energy flow and physical transitions that occur at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Rock: Organic-Rich Shale] --> B[Kerogen Maturation in Oil Window]
    B --> C[Catagenesis: HC Generation]
    C --> D[Micro-Fracture Buildup in Source]
    D --> E[Primary Migration: Expulsion Phase]
    E --> F[Carrier Bed: Permeable Sandstone]
    F --> G[Buoyancy-Driven Vertical Rise]
    G --> H[Secondary Migration Through Carrier]
    H --> I[Cap Rock: Impermeable Seal]
    I --> J[Trapping and Accumulation]
    J --> K[Reservoir Formation]
    K --> L[Tertiary Migration: Re-mobilization if Faulting or Tilting]

    A1[Buoyancy Force Fb] --> G
    A2[Capillary Resistance Pc] --> I
    A3[Hydrodynamic Pressure Ph] --> G
    A4[Darcy Flow Viscous Drag] --> H
```

## 4.2 Energy Balance Architecture of a Trap

This diagram shows the energy terms acting on an oil column beneath a cap rock and the critical balance that determines whether the trap holds or leaks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TrapSystem[Trap Energy Balance]
        direction TB
        OCOilCol[Oil Column of Height H]
        CapRock[Cap Rock with Pore Throat Radius r]
        WaterLeg[Formation Water Leg Below]
    end

    FbEnergy[Buoyancy: Delta Rho times g times H] --> OCOilCol
    PcEnergy[Capillary Resistance: 2 Sigma Cos Theta over r] --> CapRock
    OCOilCol -->|Fb vs Pc Comparison| Result{Leak or Hold}
    CapRock --> Result
    WaterLeg -->|Hydrostatic support| Result

    Result -->|Fb less than Pc| Hold[Trap Holds: Oil Accumulation]
    Result -->|Fb greater than Pc| Leak[Seal Failure: Oil Escapes Upward]
```

## 4.3 Migration Stage Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Stage0[Hydrocarbon Generation in Source Rock] --> Stage1[Primary Migration: Expulsion]
    Stage1 --> Stage2[Secondary Migration: Upward through Carrier Beds]
    Stage2 --> Stage3[Trap Filling and Reservoir Formation]
    Stage3 --> Stage4[Tertiary Migration: Re-mobilization Event]

    Stage1 -.->|Driving force: Pressure buildup| Note1[Note: Micro-fracturing and solution transport]
    Stage2 -.->|Driving force: Buoyancy + Hydrodynamics| Note2[Note: Darcy flow regime]
    Stage4 -.->|Trigger: Faulting, tilting, gas invasion| Note3[Note: Secondary accumulation possible]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Viva:** When asked to "explain migration with a diagram," always include the **three rock types** (source, carrier, reservoir) and the **cap rock** in your sketch. Label the directions of buoyancy and water displacement.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**
Define **secondary migration** of petroleum. List the two principal forces responsible for it.

**Model Answer:**
Secondary migration is the movement of hydrocarbons through permeable carrier beds from the source rock to the reservoir trap. The two principal forces driving secondary migration are:

1. **Buoyancy force** — arising from the density difference between oil ($\rho_o$) and formation water ($\rho_w$), given by $\Delta \rho g H$.
2. **Hydrodynamic force** — caused by the lateral flow of formation water in the carrier bed, which can tilt the oil column.

Capillary forces act as the *resisting* force, not the driving force. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Similar Pattern]**
Differentiate between **primary migration** and **secondary migration** of hydrocarbons.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Primary Migration | Secondary Migration |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | Expulsion from source rock | Movement through carrier bed to trap |
| Direction | Outward from source | Upward and lateral |
| Dominant Mechanism | Pressure-driven micro-fracturing, solution transport | Buoyancy + Darcy flow |
| Medium | Compacted shale | Sandstone/carbonate carrier beds |
| Driving Force | Compaction + thermal pressure | Buoyancy + hydrodynamics |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Students answer **one full question** from the choice. Each 14-mark question typically has two 7-mark sub-parts.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Predicted Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **mechanism of oil migration** from source rock to reservoir rock. Discuss the **role of buoyancy and capillary pressure** in the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **critical oil column height** $H_c$ that a cap rock can hold before leakage occurs. Given: oil–water interfacial tension $\sigma = 25$ mN/m, contact angle $\theta = 30°$, effective cap rock pore throat radius $r = 50$ nm, $\rho_w = 1050$ kg/m³, $\rho_o = 800$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². Calculate $H_c$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

1. Hydrocarbons are generated from kerogen in the source rock during catagenesis. **(1 mark)**
2. When internal pore pressure exceeds capillary entry pressure, **primary migration** begins — oil is expelled into adjacent carrier beds via micro-fractures. **(1 mark)**
3. In the carrier bed (e.g., sandstone), oil rises vertically under **buoyancy** because oil is less dense than water. **(1 mark)**
4. The upward motion continues until oil encounters an **impermeable cap rock**. **(1 mark)**
5. The cap rock's pore throats have a finite **capillary entry pressure** $P_c = 2\sigma \cos\theta / r$ that opposes the buoyancy pressure. **(1 mark)**
6. If buoyancy $F_b < P_c$, oil accumulates — this is the **trap**. **(1 mark)**
7. If $F_b > P_c$, the seal leaks and oil escapes upward. **(1 mark)**

*(Diagram should show: source rock → carrier bed → reservoir → cap rock, with arrows showing oil rise and water displacement.)*

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Derivation:**
For seal integrity, buoyancy pressure must not exceed capillary entry pressure:

$$ (\rho_w - \rho_o) g H_c = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r} $$

Solving for $H_c$:

$$ H_c = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r (\rho_w - \rho_o) g} $$

**Numerical Substitution:**
- $\sigma = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m
- $\cos 30° = 0.866$
- $r = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- $\rho_w - \rho_o = 1050 - 800 = 250$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

$$ H_c = \frac{2 \times (25 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.866}{(50 \times 10^{-9}) \times 250 \times 9.81} $$

$$ H_c = \frac{4.33 \times 10^{-2}}{1.226 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ H_c \approx 353.2 \text{ m} $$

The trap can hold an oil column of up to **~353 m** before the cap rock fails. **(7 marks — 3 for derivation, 4 for numerical)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **Darcy-based model for oil migration velocity** through a water-saturated carrier bed. Starting from Darcy's Law, derive an expression for the steady-state vertical migration velocity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A petroleum trap has the following parameters: oil column height $H = 80$ m, oil density $\rho_o = 820$ kg/m³, water density $\rho_w = 1040$ kg/m³, carrier bed permeability $k = 200$ mD, relative permeability to oil $k_{ro} = 0.6$, oil viscosity $\mu_o = 3$ cP, migration distance $D = 1500$ m, and $g = 9.81$ m/s². Determine:
   - (i) The buoyancy pressure exerted by the oil column.
   - (ii) The Darcy migration velocity.
   - (iii) The migration time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

Darcy's Law for oil flow in a vertical carrier bed:

$$ v_m = -\frac{k \cdot k_{ro}}{\mu_o} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz} $$

The pressure gradient driving the oil is the **buoyancy gradient**:

$$ \frac{dP}{dz} = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g $$

Substituting:

$$ v_m = \frac{k \cdot k_{ro} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_o) g}{\mu_o} $$

This is the steady-state vertical migration velocity. The migration time over a distance $D$:

$$ t_m = \frac{D \cdot \mu_o}{k \cdot k_{ro} \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_o) g} $$

**Assumptions:** (1) Incompressible fluids, (2) Hydrostatic equilibrium, (3) Constant $k$, $k_{ro}$, $\mu_o$, (4) Vertical flow. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Convert all units to SI:**
- $k = 200$ mD $= 200 \times 9.869 \times 10^{-16} = 1.974 \times 10^{-13}$ m²
- $\mu_o = 3$ cP $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s

**(i) Buoyancy Pressure:**

$$ \Delta P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g H $$

$$ \Delta P_b = (1040 - 820) \times 9.81 \times 80 $$

$$ \Delta P_b = 220 \times 9.81 \times 80 = 172{,}656 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.73 \text{ bar} $$

**(ii) Darcy Migration Velocity:**

$$ v_m = \frac{(1.974 \times 10^{-13}) \times 0.6 \times 220 \times 9.81}{3 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ v_m = \frac{(1.974 \times 10^{-13}) \times 0.6 \times 2158.2}{3 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ v_m = \frac{2.557 \times 10^{-10}}{3 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ v_m \approx 8.52 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s} $$

Converting to m/year:

$$ v_m \approx 8.52 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.154 \times 10^7 \approx 2.69 \text{ m/year} $$

**(iii) Migration Time:**

$$ t_m = \frac{1500}{2.69} \approx 558 \text{ years} $$

The migration would take approximately **558 years**, consistent with rapid geological migration in high-permeability carrier beds. **(7 marks — 2+3+2)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** are the #1 cause of lost marks. Always convert mD to m² (multiply by $9.869 \times 10^{-16}$), cP to Pa·s (multiply by $10^{-3}$), and g/cm³ to kg/m³ (multiply by 1000).
> 2. **Direction of forces:** Many students write buoyancy as "downward" or forget the sign. Always clarify: oil is *less dense*, so buoyancy pushes it *upward*.
> 3. **Capillary pressure is a *resisting* force**, not a driving force. Confusing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing a labeled diagram** when asked to "explain with a diagram" can cost up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Memorizing without understanding** the critical column height derivation — examiners may ask for any step in the derivation, not just the final formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Migration** = movement of hydrocarbons from source rock → trap.
- [x] **Three stages:** Primary (expulsion), Secondary (carrier bed), Tertiary (re-mobilization).
- [x] **Driving forces:** Buoyancy, Hydrodynamics, Compaction pressure.
- [x] **Resisting forces:** Capillary pressure, Viscous drag, Adsorption.
- [x] **Buoyancy formula:** $\Delta P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g H$.
- [x] **Capillary pressure formula:** $P_c = 2\sigma \cos\theta / r$.
- [x] **Critical column height:** $H_c = 2\sigma \cos\theta / [r(\rho_w - \rho_o) g]$.
- [x] **Darcy migration velocity:** $v_m = k k_{ro} (\rho_w - \rho_o) g / \mu_o$.
- [x] **Migration time:** $t_m = D \mu_o / [k k_{ro} (\rho_w - \rho_o) g]$.
- [x] **Typical densities:** $\rho_o \approx 700$–$950$ kg/m³; $\rho_w \approx 1000$–$1200$ kg/m³.
- [x] **Typical interfacial tension (oil–water):** $\sigma \approx 20$–$50$ mN/m.
- [x] **Migration requires:** Source rock maturity + permeable carrier bed + effective seal.
- [x] **KTU exam keyword:** "Explain migration mechanism" — always draw a labeled diagram and name the three rock types.
- [x] **Real-world timescales:** Migration takes $10^3$–$10^6$ years; geologically instantaneous.
- [x] **Trap failure condition:** $F_b > P_c$ → cap rock leaks → oil escapes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reservoir Rock — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reservoir Rock Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> A **Reservoir Rock** is a subsurface, porous and permeable geological formation that contains hydrocarbons (crude oil, natural gas, and condensates) within its inter-connected pore spaces and allows the fluids to flow through it toward a production wellbore under natural or artificial drive mechanisms.

In petroleum engineering, the reservoir rock is the **storage and delivery medium** for hydrocarbons. Without a suitable reservoir rock, even the presence of a mature source rock and a perfect geological trap is meaningless. The rock must satisfy three fundamental physical criteria simultaneously:

1. **Storage Capacity (Porosity, $\phi$):** Must possess interconnected void space to hold hydrocarbons.
2. **Flow Capacity (Permeability, $k$):** Must allow hydrocarbons to flow toward the wellbore.
3. **Wettability and Saturation ($S_o, S_w, S_g$):** Must preferentially hold hydrocarbons over formation water in the productive zone.

## 1.2 Cap Rock — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cap Rock Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> A **Cap Rock** (also known as **Seal Rock** or **Roof Rock**) is an impermeable, ductile, and laterally continuous geological stratum that overlies the reservoir rock and prevents the upward migration (escape) of buoyant hydrocarbons (oil and gas) due to its high capillary entry pressure and near-zero permeability.

The cap rock is the **containment lid** of the petroleum system. It must be mechanically strong enough to withstand the buoyant pressure of the hydrocarbon column accumulated beneath it. If the buoyancy pressure exceeds the capillary entry pressure of the seal, hydrocarbons will leak — a phenomenon known as **seal failure** or **column breach**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — A Real-World Intuitive Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Underground Fuel Cylinder**
> Imagine an inverted, sealed water tank buried underground. The tank's hollow metal shell is the **Reservoir Rock** (it holds the fuel — hydrocarbons — inside tiny pores just like a sponge holds water). The tank's welded steel cap at the top is the **Cap Rock** (it is impermeable and prevents the fuel from escaping upward). If the steel cap has even a single pinhole leak, the entire fuel charge escapes — this is exactly how geological seal failure causes dry holes in exploration!

- **Porosity ($\phi$)** = How much "empty space" the sponge has to hold fuel.
- **Permeability ($k$)** = How easily the fuel can be squeezed out of the sponge.
- **Capillary Entry Pressure ($P_{ce}$)** = The pressure you must apply before fuel can force its way through the tiny pores of the cap rock seal.

## 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU)

- **Porosity ($\phi$):** Expressed as a fraction or **%**, typical range **5% – 30%**.
- **Permeability ($k$):** Measured in **Darcy (D)** or **millidarcy (mD)**; $1 \, D = 1000 \, mD$.
- **Hydrocarbon Saturation ($S_{hc}$):** Expressed as a fraction of pore volume; must exceed $\mathbf{50\%}$ for a commercial reservoir.
- **Capillary Entry Pressure ($P_{ce}$):** Measured in **psi** or **MPa**; for shale seals typically **$1000$ – $5000$ psi**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Reservoir Rocks

Reservoir rocks are broadly classified into two categories based on their geological origin:

### A. Clastic (Detrital / Siliciclastic) Reservoir Rocks
Formed from the compaction and cementation of weathered rock fragments (sediments).

| Sub-Type | Lithology | Typical Porosity (%) | Typical Permeability (mD) |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| **Sandstone** | Quartz grains, well-sorted | 15 – 25 | 100 – 1000 |
| **Siltstone** | Fine quartz/feldspar | 5 – 15 | 0.1 – 10 |
| **Conglomerate** | Gravel + sand matrix | 10 – 20 | 50 – 500 |

### B. Non-Clastic (Chemical / Carbonate) Reservoir Rocks
Formed by chemical or biological precipitation (biogenic origin).

| Sub-Type | Lithology | Typical Porosity (%) | Typical Permeability (mD) |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| **Limestone** | CaCO$_3$ (calcite) | 5 – 25 | 1 – 500 |
| **Dolomite** | CaMg(CO$_3$)$_2$ | 10 – 30 | 5 – 1000 |
| **Chalk** | Microfossil CaCO$_3$ | 25 – 45 | 0.1 – 5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Statistic (High-Yield):** Carbonate reservoirs hold approximately **$\mathbf{60\%}$** of the world's remaining oil and **$\mathbf{40\%}$** of the world's remaining gas reserves, despite clastic (sandstone) reservoirs being more numerous. This is critical for KTU exam questions!

## 2.2 The Four Cardinal Properties of a Reservoir Rock

### Property 1: Porosity ($\phi$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Porosity** is the ratio of the volume of void space (pores, fractures, vugs) in the rock to the total bulk volume of the rock.

$$\phi = \frac{V_{pore}}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{V_{bulk} - V_{grain}}{V_{bulk}}$$

Two sub-types of porosity exist:
- **Primary Porosity ($\phi_1$):** Formed during sediment deposition (intergranular pores).
- **Secondary Porosity ($\phi_2$):** Formed by post-depositional processes like dissolution, fracturing, or dolomitization (vugs, fractures).

$$\phi_{total} = \phi_1 + \phi_2$$

### Property 2: Permeability ($k$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permeability** is the ability of a porous medium to transmit fluids through its interconnected pore network, measured in Darcies (D).

The fundamental **Darcy's Law** for one-dimensional linear flow:

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (cm³/s)
- $k$ = Permeability (Darcy)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area (cm²)
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across length $L$ (atm)
- $\mu$ = Fluid dynamic viscosity (cP)
- $L$ = Length of porous medium (cm)

### Property 3: Hydrocarbon Saturation ($S_{hc}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saturation** is the fraction of pore volume occupied by a particular fluid.

$$S_o + S_w + S_g = 1$$

Where $S_o$, $S_w$, $S_g$ are oil, water, and gas saturations respectively. For a producible reservoir, the **hydrocarbon saturation** must satisfy:

$$S_{hc} = S_o + S_g \geq 0.50$$

### Property 4: Wettability

Wettability is the preference of the solid rock surface for one fluid over another. Reservoir rocks are preferentially:
- **Water-Wet:** Most common (sandstones, limestones in virgin state).
- **Oil-Wet:** Develops over geological time due to polar asphaltene deposition.
- **Mixed-Wet:** Certain pores are water-wet and others oil-wet.

## 2.3 Cap Rock — Sealing Mechanism & Capillary Theory

The seal mechanism of a cap rock is governed by **Capillary Pressure**, which arises from interfacial tension ($\gamma$) between the hydrocarbon phase (non-wetting) and the formation water phase (wetting) within the seal's microscopic pores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capillary Entry Pressure ($P_{ce}$):** The minimum pressure difference across the hydrocarbon-water interface required for hydrocarbons to displace formation water and enter the pore throat of the seal rock.

$$P_{ce} = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma_{hw} \cdot \cos\theta}{r_{throat}}$$

Where:
- $\gamma_{hw}$ = Interfacial tension between hydrocarbon and water (dynes/cm or N/m)
- $\theta$ = Contact angle (degrees), measured through the wetting phase
- $r_{throat}$ = Radius of the largest interconnected pore throat in the seal (cm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $P_{ce} \propto 1/r_{throat}$. This means **finer-grained cap rocks (e.g., shale, evaporite) have exponentially higher sealing capacity** because their pore throats are microscopic. This is why evaporites (salt, anhydrite) are the world's best seals.

## 2.4 Maximum Hydrocarbon Column Height

The **maximum hydrocarbon column height** ($h_{max}$) that a cap rock can hold without leaking is determined by equating the buoyant pressure of the hydrocarbon column to the capillary entry pressure:

$$h_{max} = \frac{P_{ce}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$

Where:
- $h_{max}$ = Maximum sealable hydrocarbon column (m)
- $P_{ce}$ = Capillary entry pressure (Pa)
- $\rho_w$ = Formation water density ($\approx 1.05$ g/cm³)
- $\rho_{hc}$ = Hydrocarbon density ($\approx 0.7$ – $0.9$ g/cm³ for oil)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity ($9.81$ m/s²)

## 2.5 Classification of Cap Rocks

| Cap Rock Type | Lithology | Typical $P_{ce}$ (psi) | Sealing Quality |
|---------------|-----------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Evaporite (best seal)** | Halite (rock salt), Anhydrite | 1000 – 5000+ | Excellent |
| **Shale (clay-rich)** | Compacted clay minerals | 200 – 2000 | Good |
| **Mudstone** | Silty mud, low permeability | 100 – 500 | Moderate |
| **Tight Carbonate** | Micritic limestone (chalk) | 50 – 300 | Marginal |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes all exam-critical equations for this topic:**

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Unit | Used For |
|---|---------|---------------------|------|----------|
| 1 | $\phi = V_{pore} / V_{bulk}$ | Porosity | dimensionless | Storage capacity |
| 2 | $Q = kA\Delta P / (\mu L)$ | Darcy's Law | cm³/s | Flow rate calculation |
| 3 | $S_o + S_w + S_g = 1$ | Fluid saturation balance | dimensionless | Reservoir volumetrics |
| 4 | $P_{ce} = 2\gamma\cos\theta / r_{throat}$ | Capillary entry pressure | Pa or psi | Seal capacity |
| 5 | $h_{max} = P_{ce} / [(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g]$ | Max hydrocarbon column | m | Trap column height |
| 6 | $k = Q\mu L / (A \Delta P)$ | Permeability (rearranged) | Darcy | Lab core analysis |
| 7 | $S_{hc} = (1 - S_w)$ | Hydrocarbon saturation | fraction | Pay zone identification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations form the foundation of **Reservoir Engineering Simulation Software** (e.g., Eclipse, CMG). Every reservoir simulation model in the petrochemical industry is built upon Darcy's Law and capillary pressure relationships. Understanding these formulas is essential for **drilling decision-making, completion design, and production forecasting**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Darcy's Law

**Starting Premise (Empirical Observation by Henry Darcy, 1856):**
For a fluid flowing linearly through a porous medium, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is directly proportional to the pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$ and the cross-sectional area $A$, and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity $\mu$.

### Step 1: Statement of Proportionality

We begin by expressing the proportional relationships as an equation with an unknown constant of proportionality $k$ (which is later defined as permeability):

$$Q \propto A \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{\mu}$$

### Step 2: Introduction of the Constant of Proportionality

Introduce the constant $k$, which encapsulates the geometric and topological properties of the porous medium (pore size, connectivity, tortuosity):

$$Q = k \cdot \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

### Step 3: Dimensional Analysis to Define the Darcy Unit

To make the equation dimensionally consistent, we must define the unit of $k$. Rearranging:

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

### Step 4: Substitute CGS Units to Define "1 Darcy"

In CGS units:
- $Q$ in cm³/s
- $\mu$ in centipoise (cP)
- $L$ in cm
- $A$ in cm²
- $\Delta P$ in atmospheres (atm)

**Definition:** Permeability $k$ equals **1 Darcy** when a fluid of viscosity **1 cP** flows at a rate of **1 cm³/s** through a cross-section of **1 cm²** under a pressure gradient of **1 atm/cm**.

### Step 5: Conversion to Field Units

In oilfield practice, permeability is more commonly reported in **millidarcy (mD)**:

$$1 \, D = 1000 \, mD$$

### Step 6: Final Mathematical Form (Industry Standard)

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}\,}$$

**Engineering Interpretation of Each Term:**
- Numerator ($k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P$): Driving force × cross-section × rock flow capacity.
- Denominator ($\mu \cdot L$): Resistance to flow (viscous drag + path length).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Darcy's Law is valid only for **laminar (viscous) flow** with **Reynolds Number $Re < 1$**. For high-velocity gas flow near the wellbore (non-Darcy flow), the **Forchheimer equation** with inertial correction must be used.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Maximum Hydrocarbon Column Height

### Step 1: Define the Buoyancy Force on a Hydrocarbon Column

The buoyant pressure at the base of a hydrocarbon column of height $h$ in a water-saturated formation is given by the **hydrostatic pressure differential**:

$$P_{buoy} = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$$

Where:
- $\rho_w$ = Density of formation brine (kg/m³)
- $\rho_{hc}$ = Density of hydrocarbon (oil or gas) (kg/m³)
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration ($9.81$ m/s²)
- $h$ = Height of hydrocarbon column (m)

### Step 2: Equilibrium Condition for Seal Integrity

For the cap rock to remain an effective seal, the buoyant pressure at the seal-reservoir interface must NOT exceed the capillary entry pressure of the seal. The condition is:

$$P_{buoy} \leq P_{ce}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Maximum Column Height

Setting the equality condition (limit state):

$$(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h_{max} = P_{ce}$$

Solving for $h_{max}$:

$$h_{max} = \frac{P_{ce}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example:** A sandstone reservoir is sealed by an overlying shale with capillary entry pressure $P_{ce} = 2.0 \, MPa$. Formation water density $\rho_w = 1.05 \, g/cm^3$, oil density $\rho_{oil} = 0.80 \, g/cm^3$. Calculate the maximum oil column height the seal can hold.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta \rho = \rho_w - \rho_{oil} = 1.05 - 0.80 = 0.25 \, g/cm^3$$

Convert to SI: $\Delta \rho = 250 \, kg/m^3$

Convert $P_{ce}$: $2.0 \, MPa = 2 \times 10^6 \, Pa$

$$h_{max} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \, Pa}{250 \, kg/m^3 \times 9.81 \, m/s^2}$$

$$h_{max} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2452.5} \, m$$

$$\boxed{h_{max} \approx 815.5 \, m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The shale cap rock can hold an oil column of up to **815.5 m** before leakage. Any column taller than this would breach the seal. This explains why **giant oil fields** are often found in basins with **evaporite seals** (salt has $P_{ce}$ 5–10× higher than shale).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reservoir vs Cap Rock Properties

| Property | Reservoir Rock | Cap Rock |
|----------|----------------|----------|
| **Porosity** | High (15 – 30%) | Very Low (≤ 5%) |
| **Permeability** | High (10 – 1000 mD) | Near-zero (< 0.001 mD) |
| **Capillary Entry Pressure** | Low (< 50 psi) | Very High (200 – 5000+ psi) |
| **Function** | Stores & transmits hydrocarbons | Prevents upward hydrocarbon escape |
| **Typical Lithology** | Sandstone, Limestone, Dolomite | Shale, Halite, Anhydrite |
| **Mechanical Behavior** | Brittle | Ductile (plastic) |
| **Fluid Saturation** | High $S_{hc}$ (> 50%) | Fully water-saturated |
| **Ductility Requirement** | Low | High (must not fracture under tectonic stress) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Counterintuitive Insight:** The contrast between reservoir and cap rock properties must be **abrupt**. A gradual transition produces a poor trap. This is why structural traps (anticlines, faults) often juxtapose sandstone directly against shale — the property contrast is maximized at the interface.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydrocarbon Trap System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete functional architecture of how reservoir rock, cap rock, and source rock interact in a petroleum system. This addresses topics requiring complex physical drawings by mapping interactions at the functional level.

```mermaid
    N --> P[Mechanical Ductility - No Fracturing]
```


## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — Hydrocarbon Migration and Trapping Process

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SR as Source Rock
    participant CB as Carrier Bed
    participant RR as Reservoir Rock
    participant CR as Cap Rock
    participant WB as Wellbore
    SR->>SR: Kerogen maturation (60-150 deg C)
    SR->>CB: Expelled hydrocarbons migrate upward
    CB->>RR: Enter porous, permeable formation
    RR->>CR: Buoyancy pushes HC upward
    CR-->>RR: Capillary pressure blocks entry
    RR->>RR: Hydrocarbon accumulation begins
    RR->>WB: Well drilled into reservoir
    WB->>RR: Production begins
```

**Q: Define reservoir rock. List any four essential properties that a rock must possess to qualify as a hydrocarbon reservoir rock.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Reservoir Rock Definition:** A reservoir rock is a subsurface, porous and permeable geological formation that contains hydrocarbons within its interconnected pore spaces and allows them to flow toward a wellbore.

**Four Essential Properties:**
1. **Porosity ($\phi$):** Sufficient void space to store hydrocarbons (typically $> 5\%$).
2. **Permeability ($k$):** Sufficient interconnected flow paths to transmit hydrocarbons (typically $> 1$ mD for commercial production).

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: What is a cap rock? Explain the concept of capillary entry pressure in the context of hydrocarbon sealing.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cap Rock Definition:** A cap rock (seal rock) is an impermeable geological stratum that overlies a reservoir rock and prevents the upward migration of hydrocarbons.

**Capillary Entry Pressure ($P_{ce}$):** It is the **minimum pressure differential** across the hydrocarbon-water interface required to force hydrocarbons to displace formation water from the largest interconnected pore throat of the seal rock. It is given by:

$$P_{ce} = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma_{hw} \cdot \cos\theta}{r_{throat}}$$

A **higher $P_{ce}$** indicates a **better seal** because the cap rock can withstand greater buoyant pressures from the underlying hydrocarbon column. Fine-grained seals (shale, halite) have small $r_{throat}$ values, resulting in very high $P_{ce}$ and excellent sealing capacity.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for capillary entry pressure concept = 3 marks)*
