## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the classification of reservoir rocks with examples. Discuss the geological and petrophysical properties that distinguish clastic reservoirs from carbonate reservoirs.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Reservoir rocks are classified into two major categories based on their origin and lithology:

**1. Clastic (Siliciclastic / Detrital) Reservoir Rocks:**
Formed by the deposition, compaction, and cementation of weathered rock fragments. Examples include:
- **Sandstone:** Most common clastic reservoir; composed of quartz and feldspar grains; porosity typically $15-25\%$.
- **Siltstone:** Finer-grained; lower porosity ($5-15\%$) and permeability ($0.1-10$ mD).
- **Conglomerate:** Coarse-grained; high permeability due to large interconnected pores.

**2. Non-Clastic (Chemical / Carbonate) Reservoir Rocks:**
Formed by chemical precipitation or biological accumulation. Examples include:
- **Limestone (CaCO$_3$):** Formed from marine organisms or chemical precipitation; porosity $5-25\%$.
- **Dolomite [CaMg(CO$_3$)$_2$]:** Formed by magnesium replacement of limestone (dolomitization); typically has higher porosity ($10-30\%$) due to secondary dissolution.
- **Chalk:** Microfossil-rich limestone; very high porosity ($25-45\%$) but low permeability due to small pore throats.

**Geological & Petrophysical Distinctions:**

| Property | Clastic (Sandstone) | Carbonate (Limestone/Dolomite) |
|----------|--------------------|---------------------------------|
| Origin | Mechanical deposition | Chemical/biological precipitation |
| Pore Type | Mainly intergranular (primary) | Intergranular + vuggy + fractured (secondary dominant) |
| Porosity Distribution | Uniform, predictable | Highly heterogeneous |
| Permeability | Generally predictable | Often decoupled from porosity |
| Diagenesis Effect | Moderate | Strong (dissolution, recrystallization) |
| Global Reserve Share | $\sim 40\%$ | $\sim 60\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Clastic reservoirs have more uniform properties and are easier to characterize, while carbonate reservoirs are more complex due to secondary porosity development but hold the majority of global hydrocarbon reserves.

---

**(b) Derive Darcy's Law. A core sample of length $5$ cm and cross-sectional area $10$ cm² has permeability $200$ mD. Oil with viscosity $2$ cP is flowed through it under a pressure drop of $3$ atm. Calculate the volumetric flow rate in cm³/s.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation of Darcy's Law:**

Darcy experimentally observed that the volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a fluid through a porous medium is:
- Directly proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$
- Directly proportional to the pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$
- Inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity $\mu$

Mathematically:
$$Q \propto A \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{\mu}$$

Introducing the proportionality constant $k$ (permeability):
$$Q = k \cdot \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

Rearranging:
$$k = \frac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

In CGS units, $k = 1$ Darcy when $Q = 1$ cm³/s, $\mu = 1$ cP, $L = 1$ cm, $A = 1$ cm², $\Delta P = 1$ atm.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $L = 5$ cm
- $A = 10$ cm²
- $k = 200$ mD $= 0.2$ D
- $\mu = 2$ cP
- $\Delta P = 3$ atm

Applying Darcy's Law:
$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

$$Q = \frac{0.2 \, D \times 10 \, cm^2 \times 3 \, atm}{2 \, cP \times 5 \, cm}$$

$$Q = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6 \, cm^3/s$$

$$\boxed{Q = 0.6 \, cm^3/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The core sample delivers $0.6$ cm³/s of oil under the given pressure conditions. This value is used by reservoir engineers to estimate well productivity via core-flood experiments.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: (a) Define cap rock and discuss its classification with examples. Explain the capillary entry pressure concept and its role in determining the maximum sealable hydrocarbon column height.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Cap Rock Definition:**
A cap rock (seal rock or roof rock) is an impermeable, ductile geological formation that overlies a reservoir rock and prevents the upward migration of hydrocarbons due to its high capillary entry pressure.

**Classification of Cap Rocks (with Examples):**

| Type | Lithology | Example Formations | Sealing Quality |
|------|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Evaporite** | Halite (NaCl), Anhydrite (CaSO$_4$) | Zechstein (North Sea), Louann Salt (Gulf of Mexico) | Excellent (best seals) |
| **Shale** | Compacted clay minerals (kaolinite, illite, montmorillonite) | Eagle Ford Shale, Kimmeridge Clay | Good |
| **Mudstone** | Silty mud with minor clay | Various marine mudstones | Moderate |
| **Tight Carbonate** | Micritic (fine-grained) limestone | Some chalk formations | Marginal |

**Capillary Entry Pressure Concept:**

The capillary entry pressure $P_{ce}$ is the minimum pressure differential across a hydrocarbon-water interface required to displace water from the largest pore throat of the seal:

$$P_{ce} = \frac{2 \gamma_{hw} \cos\theta}{r_{throat}}$$

A finer-grained seal has smaller $r_{throat}$, hence higher $P_{ce}$ and better sealing capacity.

**Maximum Hydrocarbon Column Height:**

The maximum hydrocarbon column ($h_{max}$) that a seal can hold is given by equating buoyant pressure to capillary entry pressure:

$$h_{max} = \frac{P_{ce}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$

For example, a shale with $P_{ce} = 2$ MPa and a density contrast of $0.25$ g/cm³ yields $h_{max} \approx 815$ m of oil column. Evaporites with $P_{ce} = 10$ MPa can hold columns exceeding $4000$ m.

---

**(b) A sandstone reservoir is overlain by a shale cap rock with capillary entry pressure $3.5$ MPa. Formation water density is $1.04$ g/cm³ and gas density is $0.20$ g/cm³. Calculate the maximum gas column height the seal can support. Comment on whether the reservoir is commercially viable if the observed gas column is $1200$ m.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $P_{ce} = 3.5 \, MPa = 3.5 \times 10^6 \, Pa$
- $\rho_w = 1.04 \, g/cm^3 = 1040 \, kg/m^3$
- $\rho_g = 0.20 \, g/cm^3 = 200 \, kg/m^3$
- Observed gas column $h_{obs} = 1200 \, m$

**Step 1: Calculate Density Contrast**
$$\Delta \rho = \rho_w - \rho_g = 1040 - 200 = 840 \, kg/m^3$$

**Step 2: Apply the Maximum Column Height Equation**
$$h_{max} = \frac{P_{ce}}{(\rho_w - \rho_g) \cdot g}$$

$$h_{max} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^6 \, Pa}{840 \, kg/m^3 \times 9.81 \, m/s^2}$$

$$h_{max} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^6}{8240.4} \, m$$

$$\boxed{h_{max} \approx 424.7 \, m}$$

**Step 3: Compare with Observed Column**
$$h_{obs} = 1200 \, m > h_{max} = 424.7 \, m$$

**Conclusion & Commercial Viability:**
The observed gas column of **1200 m** is **2.83 times** the maximum sealable height of **424.7 m**. This means:
- The seal integrity will **FAIL** under such a column.
- Hydrocarbons will leak upward and dissipate.
- **The reservoir is NOT commercially viable** unless a stronger seal (e.g., evaporite) is present above the shale.
- Alternatively, the observed gas may indicate a **dynamic leakage system** with active seepage to the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Recommendation:** In such a scenario, exploration geologists should either (i) look for a thicker/stronger seal laterally, or (ii) treat the structure as a possible **gas chimney** with active leakage rather than a sealed trap.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistakes:** Students frequently lose 1–2 marks by mixing psi with MPa, or g/cm³ with kg/m³. **Always convert to SI units (Pa, kg/m³, m) before calculation.**
> 2. **Sign Error in $\Delta \rho$:** Writing $(\rho_{hc} - \rho_w)$ instead of $(\rho_w - \rho_{hc})$ yields a negative column height — an automatic zero.
> 3. **Forgetting Darcy's Law Assumptions:** Don't apply Darcy's Law to fractured vuggy carbonates without mentioning **dual-porosity** or **Forchheimer correction** — examiners deduct 1 mark for this oversight.
> 4. **Confusing Porosity with Permeability:** High porosity does NOT imply high permeability. Always state both for a complete reservoir rock description.
> 5. **Skipping Cap Rock Definition in Trap Questions:** Even if the question focuses on reservoir rock, a complete answer on trap integrity MUST include cap rock properties — students lose 2 marks by omitting this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 High-Yield Points:**

- **Reservoir Rock:** Subsurface porous + permeable rock that **stores and transmits** hydrocarbons.
- **Cap Rock:** Impermeable seal that **prevents upward migration** of hydrocarbons.
- **Three Critical Reservoir Properties:** Porosity ($\phi$), Permeability ($k$), Hydrocarbon Saturation ($S_{hc} \geq 50\%$).
- **Darcy's Law:** $Q = kA\Delta P / (\mu L)$ — Foundation of all flow calculations.
- **Two Reservoir Rock Types:** **Clastic** (sandstone, siltstone) and **Non-clastic/Carbonate** (limestone, dolomite, chalk).
- **Global Reserve Statistic:** Carbonates hold ~60% of oil and ~40% of gas reserves worldwide.
- **Capillary Entry Pressure:** $P_{ce} = 2\gamma\cos\theta / r_{throat}$ — Higher $P_{ce}$ = better seal.
- **Maximum Column Height:** $h_{max} = P_{ce} / [(\rho_w - \rho_{hc})g]$ — Equates buoyancy to capillary resistance.
- **Best Cap Rock:** **Evaporite (halite/anhydrite)** > Shale > Mudstone > Tight carbonate.
- **Seal Failure:** Occurs when $h_{observed} > h_{max}$ — hydrocarbons leak and trap is non-commercial.
- **Wettability:** Most reservoirs are water-wet; affects relative permeability and oil recovery.
- **Darcy's Law Validity:** Only valid for **laminar flow** ($Re < 1$); use **Forchheimer equation** for high-velocity gas flow.
- **Units to Memorize:** $1 \, D = 1000 \, mD$; $\phi$ dimensionless; $P_{ce}$ in Pa or psi.
- **Engineering Application:** These principles drive **drilling location selection, completion design, and reservoir simulation** in petrochemical industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Remote Sensing in Petroleum Resource Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of Remote Sensing in Petroleum Resource Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Remote Sensing (RS)** in the context of petroleum resource development is defined as the non-contact, science-and-technology discipline of acquiring, measuring, analyzing, and interpreting spectral, spatial, and temporal information about surface and near-surface features using sensors mounted on airborne or spaceborne platforms. In petroleum geoscience, it serves as a **frontier exploration tool** that helps geologists, geophysicists, and reservoir engineers identify prospective hydrocarbon-bearing basins, delineate structural traps, detect microseepages, monitor pipeline corridors, and conduct environmental impact assessments **before** deploying expensive seismic surveys or drilling exploratory wells.

The two primary physical mechanisms employed are:
- **Passive Remote Sensing** – Measures naturally reflected/emitted electromagnetic (EM) radiation (e.g., sunlight reflected from rocks). Examples: Landsat 8/9 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI, ASTER.
- **Active Remote Sensing** – Emits its own EM pulse and records the backscattered signal (e.g., Radar/LiDAR). Examples: Sentinel-1 SAR, ALOS-PALSAR, GEDI LiDAR.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Remote Sensing as a "Doctor's X-Ray Machine" for the Earth.**
> When a doctor suspects a fracture, they do not immediately cut open the patient. They first use an X-ray to see the bones *through* the skin. Similarly, an exploration geologist cannot drill randomly across thousands of square kilometers. Remote sensing acts as the Earth's X-ray, allowing geoscientists to "see" geological structures, rock types, alteration halos, and hydrocarbon seeps *through* vegetation, soil, and water — guiding them precisely to the most prospective zones for detailed seismic and drilling operations.

### 1.3 Electromagnetic Spectrum Bands Used in Petroleum RS
Petroleum-relevant features are best identified using specific wavelength windows:

| Band Designation | Wavelength Range | Petroleum RS Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Visible (VIS)** | 0.4 – 0.7 µm | Iron oxide mapping, soil/rock discrimination |
| **Near-Infrared (NIR)** | 0.7 – 1.3 µm | Vegetation stress (leak indicator), moisture |
| **Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR)** | 1.3 – 2.5 µm | **Hydrocarbon microseepage detection, clay mineral alteration halos** |
| **Thermal Infrared (TIR)** | 8 – 14 µm | **Geothermal anomalies**, surface temperature mapping |
| **Microwave / SAR** | 1 mm – 1 m | All-weather structural mapping, surface deformation |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics
- **Spatial Resolution (GSD):** Typically **30 m** (Landsat), **10 m** (Sentinel-2), **1–3 m** (WorldView, GeoEye).
- **Spectral Resolution:** Number and width of bands. Hyperspectral sensors (e.g., AVIRIS, PRISMA) offer **>200 narrow bands (~10 nm)**.
- **Temporal Resolution (Revisit Time):** **16 days** (Landsat), **5 days** (Sentinel-2 combined), **~1 day** (commercial constellations).
- **Radiometric Resolution:** **12-bit** (Sentinel-2), **14-bit** (ASTER), **16-bit** (modern hyperspectral).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The choice of sensor-platform combination is dictated by the **exploration phase**:
> - **Reconnaissance phase** → Coarse-resolution wide-swath sensors (MODIS, Sentinel-3).
> - **Prospect evaluation phase** → Medium-resolution multispectral (Landsat, ASTER, Sentinel-2).
> - **Detailed mapping phase** → High-resolution / hyperspectral (WorldView, AVIRIS, PRISMA).
> - **Subsurface / structural mapping** → **InSAR / LiDAR**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: How Remote Sensing Detects Petroleum
The application of remote sensing to petroleum resource development rests upon the principle of **spectral contrast** between hydrocarbon-bearing terrains and their surroundings. Four primary detection pathways exist:

**Pathway 1 — Direct Detection of Microseepages**
Light hydrocarbons (mainly **C1–C5 alkanes**) migrate vertically from subsurface reservoirs through fractures and micropores. At the surface, they support anomalous populations of **methanotrophic bacteria**, which oxidize hydrocarbons and release **CO₂**. This alters soil chemistry, bleaching red soils and reducing iron oxides. The result is a **bleached halo** detectable in SWIR bands.

**Pathway 2 — Indirect Detection via Mineral Alteration**
Hydrocarbon seepage causes authigenic mineral formation:
- **Reduction** of ferric iron (Fe³⁺) to ferrous iron (Fe²⁺) — bleached red beds.
- **Formation of pyrite (FeS₂)** — diagnostic of reducing micro-environments.
- **Precipitation of calcite, dolomite, and uranium oxides** at the surface.
- These minerals have **diagnostic spectral absorption features** in the SWIR (2.0–2.5 µm) and TIR regions.

**Pathway 3 — Vegetation Stress Detection (Biogeochemical)**
Hydrocarbon-induced soil chemistry stresses vegetation, producing:
- **Chlorophyll reduction** → detectable via red-edge shifts.
- **NDVI anomalies** → lower vigor over leaky traps.
- **Anomalous geobotanical indicators** — e.g., specific plant species that thrive on hydrocarbon-rich soils.

**Pathway 4 — Structural & Geomorphological Mapping**
Lineaments, faults, folds, and circular/elliptical features are surface expressions of subsurface structural traps. RS imagery reveals these features through:
- **Topographic shadows** (from DEMs and hillshade models).
- **Drainage pattern anomalies.**
- **Soil tonal contrasts.**

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all key formulas** the KTU 2024 board examiner expects a student to recall and apply for this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ground Sample Distance (GSD)** | $GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$ | $H$ = platform altitude, $p$ = detector pixel pitch, $f$ = focal length | metres |
| 2 | **Swath Width** | $SW = 2 \cdot H \cdot \tan\left(\dfrac{FOV}{2}\right)$ | $FOV$ = angular field of view of sensor | metres |
| 3 | **Spectral Radiance (Planck's Law)** | $B_{\lambda}(T) = \dfrac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1}$ | $h$ = Planck const, $c$ = speed of light, $k$ = Boltzmann const, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $T$ = temperature | W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹·µm⁻¹ |
| 4 | **NDVI (Vegetation Stress)** | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | NIR, Red = top-of-atmosphere reflectance | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Clay Mineral Ratio (CMR)** | $CMR = \dfrac{SWIR_{1}}{SWIR_{2}}$ | SWIR1, SWIR2 = shortwave infrared bands | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Ferrous Iron Index** | $Fe^{2+} = \dfrac{SWIR}{NIR}$ | SWIR, NIR = respective band reflectances | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Bitumen Index** | $Bitumen = \dfrac{SWIR}{TIR}$ | SWIR vs thermal infrared | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Stefan–Boltzmann Radiant Exitance** | $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma$ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**, $T$ = temperature | W·m⁻² |
| 9 | **Sensor Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $S$ = signal, $N$ = noise | decibels (dB) |
| 10 | **InSAR Line-of-Sight Velocity** | $v_{LOS} = -\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \Delta\phi$ | $\lambda$ = radar wavelength, $\Delta\phi$ = interferometric phase | m/yr |
| 11 | **Wein's Displacement Law** | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{b}{T}$ | $b$ = **2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K**, $T$ = temperature | metres |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Reducing exploration cost:** Remote sensing can screen millions of hectares **before** a single seismic line is shot, eliminating **~70% of non-prospective acreage** early.
- **Pipeline route planning:** High-resolution DEMs derived from LiDAR/Stereo-SAR enable **least-cost path analysis** through terrain, avoiding geohazards (landslides, fault crossings).
- **Environmental monitoring:** Detects oil spills, gas flaring, illegal bunkering, and refinery effluents.
- **Subsidence monitoring (InSAR):** Critical for offshore platforms and onshore oil fields where extraction-induced subsidence threatens infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the formulas matter in KTU valuation:** Examiners frequently set numericals where the student must compute GSD, swath width, or back-calculate orbit altitude from given resolution — direct applications of the formulas in Rows 1 and 2 of the cheat sheet.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Ground Sample Distance (GSD) of an Optical Sensor

**Statement:** Derive the relationship between sensor altitude, focal length, and detector pixel size that determines the spatial resolution on the ground.

**Given:**
- Sensor altitude above ground: $H$ (metres).
- Focal length of the optical system: $f$ (metres).
- Detector pixel pitch: $p$ (metres).

**Geometric Setup:** A single detector pixel of size $p$ at the focal plane of a lens of focal length $f$ subtends an angle $\theta$ given by the small-angle approximation of the tangent function.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Field of View (IFOV):**
The angular subtense of a single pixel at the lens is given by the ratio of pixel size to focal length.

$$\theta = \dfrac{p}{f} \quad \text{(radians)}$$

*Conversion logic:* This comes from the simple thin-lens geometry where the image distance equals $f$ for objects at infinity, and the tangent of the off-axis angle equals the opposite side ($p$) divided by the adjacent side ($f$).

**Step 2 — Projection onto the Ground:**
The ground footprint $GSD$ corresponding to this angle at altitude $H$ is found by the arc-length formula.

$$GSD = H \cdot \theta$$

*Conversion logic:* For small angles, the arc length of a circle of radius $H$ subtended by angle $\theta$ is simply $H \theta$.

**Step 3 — Final Closed-Form Expression:**
Substituting the expression for $\theta$ from Step 1 into Step 2 yields the master equation.

$$GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$$

*Conversion logic:* Direct substitution; the focal length $f$ in the denominator of the IFOV moves into the denominator of the final GSD, meaning longer focal lengths and smaller pixels improve (reduce) GSD — which is why high-resolution sensors use **large telescopes with tiny detectors**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Spectral Radiance from a Target (Planck's Law)

**Statement:** Derive the spectral radiance $B_{\lambda}(T)$ emitted by a blackbody at temperature $T$ as a function of wavelength $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Mode Counting in a Cavity:**
Consider EM modes inside a cubic cavity of side $L$. Allowed wave vectors are quantized as:

$$k_{x} = \dfrac{n_{x}\pi}{L}, \quad k_{y} = \dfrac{n_{y}\pi}{L}, \quad k_{z} = \dfrac{n_{z}\pi}{L}$$

with integers $n_{x}, n_{y}, n_{z} \geq 1$. The number of modes per unit volume with wave vector magnitude less than $k$ is:

$$N(k) = \dfrac{k^{3}}{3\pi^{2}}$$

*Conversion logic:* Counting integer lattice points in one octant of a sphere of radius $k$ in k-space gives $\frac{1}{8}\left(\frac{4}{3}\pi k^{3}\right) / (\pi/L)^{3} = \frac{k^{3} L^{3}}{3 \pi^{2}}$, divided by $L^{3}$ gives the density.

**Step 2 — Convert to Frequency and Multiply by 2 Polarizations:**

$$\dfrac{dN}{d\nu} = \dfrac{8\pi \nu^{2}}{c^{3}}$$

*Conversion logic:* Differentiate $N$ with respect to $k$, then substitute $k = 2\pi\nu / c$, and multiply by 2 for the two polarization states of EM waves.

**Step 3 — Multiply by Mean Energy per Mode (Bose–Einstein):**

$$\langle E \rangle = \dfrac{h\nu}{e^{h\nu/kT} - 1}$$

*Conversion logic:* The energy per mode in a blackbody cavity follows Bose–Einstein statistics, since photons are bosons with zero chemical potential.

**Step 4 — Energy Density per Unit Frequency:**

$$u(\nu) = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^{3}}{c^{3}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{h\nu/kT} - 1}$$

*Conversion logic:* Multiply the mode density (Step 2) by the mean energy per mode (Step 3).

**Step 5 — Convert to Spectral Radiance:**
Radiance is energy density multiplied by $c/4\pi$ (isotropic emission from a black surface into a hemisphere).

$$B_{\nu}(T) = \dfrac{c}{4\pi} \cdot u(\nu) = \dfrac{2h\nu^{3}}{c^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{h\nu/kT} - 1}$$

Converting from frequency to wavelength domain using $B_{\lambda} d\lambda = B_{\nu} d\nu$ with $\nu = c/\lambda$:

$$B_{\lambda}(T) = \dfrac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1}$$

*Conversion logic:* Substitute $\nu = c/\lambda$ and multiply by the Jacobian factor $c/\lambda^{2}$, yielding the final form.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: GSD of a Typical Earth-Observation Satellite

**Problem:** A satellite carries an optical sensor with a focal length of **10 m** and a detector pixel pitch of **10 µm**. The satellite orbits at an altitude of **705 km** (similar to Landsat). Compute the GSD.

**Given Data:**
- $H = 705{,}000 \text{ m}$
- $f = 10 \text{ m}$
- $p = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Apply the GSD Equation:**

$$GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$GSD = \dfrac{(7.05 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m})}{10 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Numerator:**

$$GSD = \dfrac{7.05 \times 10^{0} \text{ m}^{2}}{10 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**

$$GSD = 0.705 \text{ m} \approx 70.5 \text{ cm}$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the panchromatic-band resolution typical of modern high-resolution commercial sensors (e.g., WorldView-3 panchromatic).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor–Platform–Application Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **favourite KTU question pattern**: "Match the sensor/platform to the petroleum application." Students must memorize it.

| Sensor / Platform | Type | Spectral Region | Primary Petroleum Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Landsat 8/9 OLI** | Multispectral | VNIR + SWIR | Regional basin mapping, vegetation stress |
| **Sentinel-2 MSI** | Multispectral | VNIR + SWIR | Red-edge seep detection, agricultural monitoring around fields |
| **ASTER** | Multispectral + TIR | VNIR + SWIR + TIR | **Clay mineral mapping**, geothermics |
| **Sentinel-1 SAR** | Active Microwave | C-band (~5.6 cm) | **Subsidence**, pipeline monitoring, all-weather structural mapping |
| **AVIRIS / PRISMA** | Hyperspectral | 400–2500 nm, >200 bands | **Direct hydrocarbon microseepage** detection |
| **WorldView-3/4** | High-res optical | VNIR + SWIR | Well-pad mapping, infrastructure |
| **GEDI LiDAR** | Active | 1064 nm | Canopy / terrain DEM for inaccessible terrain |
| **MODIS** | Coarse multispectral | VNIR + TIR | **Flare detection**, regional thermal anomalies |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Remote Sensing Workflow in Petroleum Exploration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Exploration Objective Definition] --> B[Data Acquisition Planning]
    B --> C{Sensor Selection}
    C -->|Reconnaissance| D1[Coarse Resolution Wide-Swath Sensors]
    C -->|Prospect Evaluation| D2[Multispectral Landsat ASTER Sentinel2]
    C -->|Detailed Mapping| D3[Hyperspectral AVIRIS PRISMA]
    C -->|Subsurface Structures| D4[InSAR SAR LiDAR]
    D1 --> E[Image Pre-Processing]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> E1[Radiometric Correction]
    E --> E2[Geometric Correction]
    E --> E3[Atmospheric Correction]
    E1 --> F[Image Processing and Enhancement]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Band Ratios and Spectral Indices]
    F --> F2[PCA and MNF Transformations]
    F --> F3[Spectral Angle Mapper Classification]
    F1 --> G[Geological Interpretation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Lineament and Structural Mapping]
    G --> G2[Hydrocarbon Microseepage Mapping]
    G --> G3[Alteration Mineral Mapping]
    G --> G4[Geomorphic and Drainage Analysis]
    G1 --> H[Integration with Seismic and Well Data]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Prospect Ranking and Drilling Decision]
    I --> J[Field Development and Pipeline Routing]
    J --> K[Production Monitoring and Environmental Compliance]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Nodes `A`–`C` form the **planning phase**.
- Nodes `D1`–`D4` represent the **decision branches** for sensor choice, each tuned to a different exploration objective.
- Nodes `E1`–`E3` constitute the **pre-processing stage** where raw Digital Numbers (DN) are converted to physically meaningful reflectance/radiance.
- Nodes `F1`–`F3` perform **information extraction** via band math and transformations.
- Nodes `G1`–`G4` are the **interpretation outputs** that feed back into traditional petroleum geoscience workflows (`H`).
- The final chain `I → J → K` shows how RS supports the **full petroleum life cycle** — from exploration to production to decommissioning.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Electromagnetic Energy Interaction with a Petroleum Target

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sun or Artificial EM Source] --> B[Atmospheric Transmission]
    B --> C[Earth Surface Interaction]
    C --> C1[Absorption]
    C --> C2[Reflection]
    C --> C3[Emission]
    C --> C4[Transmission]
    C --> C5[Scattering]
    C1 --> D[Sensor Aperture]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Detector Array]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    F --> G[Ground Receiving Station]
    G --> H[Image Product and Analysis]
```

*Conversion logic:* The energy leaving the target via the five interaction mechanisms (absorption, reflection, emission, transmission, scattering) is captured by the sensor, digitized, and routed to a ground station for analysis. In petroleum RS, the **reflected and emitted** pathways are the most exploited.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
> **Q1. Define remote sensing and explain its relevance to petroleum resource development.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Remote sensing is the science and technology of obtaining information about objects or areas from a distance, typically from satellites or aircraft, using electromagnetic radiation (1 mark). In petroleum resource development, it is highly relevant because it provides **synoptic, cost-effective, and non-intrusive** data for basin reconnaissance, lineament and structural mapping, hydrocarbon microseepage detection, pipeline route planning, and environmental monitoring (1 mark). It significantly reduces exploration risk and cost by **screening large areas before deploying expensive seismic surveys and drilling operations** (1 mark).

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between passive and active remote sensing with one petroleum application of each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Passive remote sensing** measures naturally available EM energy (e.g., sunlight reflected from rocks); it requires daylight or natural thermal emission. *Example:* Landsat 8 OLI for **clay mineral alteration mapping** over hydrocarbon prospects (1.5 marks). **Active remote sensing** provides its own energy source (e.g., radar pulse) and measures the backscattered signal, enabling day/night, all-weather operation. *Example:* Sentinel-1 SAR for **ground subsidence monitoring** over producing oil fields (1.5 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q11]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the various **applications of remote sensing in upstream petroleum exploration**, with emphasis on structural mapping and microseepage detection. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* With neat sketches, describe the **electromagnetic spectrum regions** used in petroleum remote sensing and their specific diagnostic utility. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Applications in Upstream Petroleum Exploration (7 marks)

**1. Basin Reconnaissance and Tectonic Framework (2 marks):**
Multispectral sensors (Landsat, ASTER, Sentinel-2) provide **synoptic views** of large basins, allowing mapping of regional tectonic units, sedimentary covers, and volcanic provinces. The integration of these data with gravity and magnetic data defines the **first-order petroleum system framework** (source, reservoir, seal, trap, maturation, migration).

**2. Lineament and Structural Mapping (2 marks):**
Faults, fractures, and folds that form structural traps for hydrocarbons often have **surface expression** as linear features. RS-derived hillshade DEMs (from SRTM, ASTER GDEM, or LiDAR) and panchromatic images are used to extract lineaments. **Circular/elliptical features** seen in imagery may indicate salt domes, diapirs, or crypto-volcanic structures — all potential hydrocarbon traps.

**3. Hydrocarbon Microseepage Detection (2 marks):**
Vertical migration of light hydrocarbons from subsurface reservoirs to the surface alters soil mineralogy and vegetation. Hyperspectral sensors detect:
- **Bleached red-bed halos** (Fe³⁺ reduction to Fe²⁺) — via SWIR ratios.
- **Authigenic minerals** (calcite, dolomite, pyrite) — diagnostic SWIR absorption features near **2.2 µm and 2.3 µm**.
- **Vegetation stress anomalies** — measured by NDVI, Red-Edge Position (REP), and chlorophyll indices.

**4. Integration with Seismic and Well Data (1 mark):**
RS provides the **regional geological context** that helps design 2D/3D seismic surveys and select drilling locations. RS anomalies are **ground-truthed** with soil gas geochemistry, radiometrics, and finally with drilling.

#### Part (b) — EM Spectrum Regions for Petroleum RS (7 marks)

| Region | Wavelength | Key Petroleum Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **VNIR (0.4–1.0 µm)** | Visible + Near-IR | Iron oxide (Fe³⁺) mapping; soil/rock discrimination; red-edge for vegetation stress |
| **SWIR (1.0–2.5 µm)** | Short-Wave IR | **Hydrocarbon-induced clay alteration (kaolinite, illite, montmorillonite); carbonate detection; bitumen signatures** |
| **TIR (8–14 µm)** | Thermal IR | Silicate mineral mapping; **geothermal anomalies** over deep-seated reservoirs; surface temperature mapping for seeps and flare detection |
| **Microwave (1 mm–1 m)** | Radar | All-weather structural mapping; **InSAR subsidence** monitoring; pipeline corridor surveillance |
| **UV (0.3–0.4 µm)** | Ultraviolet | Fluorescence detection of oil films on water |

A **schematic** of the EM spectrum with shaded petroleum-relevant diagnostic bands is expected in the answer (1 mark). Students should also mention that **hyperspectral sensors** such as AVIRIS and PRISMA provide hundreds of narrow contiguous bands, enabling direct identification of minerals through their **absorption-feature position, depth, and asymmetry** (1 mark).

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]:**
> *(a)* Discuss the **role of hyperspectral remote sensing** in detecting hydrocarbon microseepages, with reference to specific spectral indices. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Derive the **Ground Sample Distance (GSD)** equation for an optical sensor and use it to determine the GSD of a sensor with $H = 600$ km, $f = 8$ m, and $p = 8$ µm. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Hyperspectral RS for Hydrocarbon Microseepage (7 marks)

Hyperspectral remote sensing acquires data in **>200 narrow, contiguous spectral bands** (typically 5–10 nm wide) covering the **0.4–2.5 µm range**. This fine spectral resolution enables the detection of subtle absorption features diagnostic of minerals formed during hydrocarbon microseepage (1 mark).

**Key mechanisms detected:**
1. **Bleached halos** around leaky traps: reduction of hematite (Fe₂O₃) to pyrite (FeS₂) — visible as **reduced red-band reflectance** and **enhanced SWIR absorption** (1 mark).
2. **Authigenic clay minerals** — kaolinite has a diagnostic doublet at **2.16 µm and 2.20 µm**; illite at **2.20 µm**; montmorillonite near **2.21 µm** (1 mark).
3. **Carbonate cements** — calcite absorption at **2.34 µm**, dolomite at **2.32 µm** (1 mark).
4. **Bitumen and oil-stained soils** — diagnostic absorption near **1.73 µm and 2.3 µm** (1 mark).

**Specific spectral indices used (2 marks):**

$$\text{Clay Mineral Ratio (CMR)} = \dfrac{\text{SWIR}_{2.20}}{\text{SWIR}_{2.16}}$$

$$\text{Bitumen Index} = \dfrac{\text{SWIR}_{1.73}}{\text{SWIR}_{1.65}}$$

$$\text{Carbonate Index} = \dfrac{\text{SWIR}_{2.34}}{\text{SWIR}_{2.20}}$$

$$\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red} \quad \text{(vegetation stress proxy)}$$

**Classification:** Techniques like **Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM)**, **Matched Filtering**, and **Spectral Feature Fitting (SFF)** are then applied to compare image spectra to laboratory reference spectra of seep-related minerals (1 mark).

#### Part (b) — GSD Derivation and Numerical (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**
As derived in Section 3.1, the GSD is obtained from the IFOV and platform altitude:

$$\text{IFOV} = \theta = \dfrac{p}{f}$$

$$GSD = H \cdot \theta = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**
Given: $H = 600{,}000$ m, $f = 8$ m, $p = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m.

$$GSD = \dfrac{600{,}000 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-6}}{8}$$

$$GSD = \dfrac{4.8}{8} = 0.6 \text{ m} = 60 \text{ cm}$$

**Result:** The GSD is **60 cm**, comparable to high-resolution commercial panchromatic sensors.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing GSD with pixel size** — Many students write "$p$" as the answer. The GSD is the *ground footprint*, not the detector size. The detector size $p$ alone is irrelevant without $H$ and $f$.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — GSD is in **metres**, not pixels. Always convert km to m and µm to m before substituting.
> 3. **Mis-stating Planck's Law** — The denominator is $e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1$, **not** $e^{hc/\lambda kT}$. Forgetting the "-1" is a frequent error.
> 4. **NDVI confusion** — NDVI ranges from **-1 to +1**. Values are negative for water, near zero for barren soil, and positive (0.2–0.8) for vegetation. Stressed vegetation over seeps shows **lower NDVI** than surroundings.
> 5. **Mixing up SWIR and TIR applications** — SWIR is for **mineralogy**; TIR is for **silicate composition and thermal anomalies**.
> 6. **Forgetting wavelength-to-frequency conversion** — In deriving Planck's Law, students often skip the Jacobian $\frac{c}{\lambda^2}$ factor, leading to a wrong spectral dependence.
> 7. **Not labelling diagrams** — In Part B questions that ask for "neat sketches," **unlabelled diagrams fetch zero marks**. Always annotate axes, bands, and the petroleum feature each band detects.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Remote sensing** is a non-contact exploration tool that uses **EM radiation** to study surface/near-surface features.
- **Two types:** Passive (uses natural energy — Landsat, ASTER, Sentinel-2) and Active (self-illuminated — SAR, LiDAR).
- **EM spectrum regions** used in petroleum RS: **VNIR, SWIR, TIR, Microwave** — each with diagnostic utility.
- **Key applications in petroleum resource development:**
  - Basin reconnaissance and tectonic mapping.
  - Lineament, fault, and fold detection.
  - Hydrocarbon microseepage detection (bleached halos, clay alteration).
  - Vegetation stress / biogeochemical anomaly mapping.
  - Pipeline route planning and infrastructure monitoring.
  - Subsidence monitoring (InSAR) and environmental compliance.
- **Master equations to memorize:**
  - $GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$
  - $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$
  - $B_{\lambda}(T) = \dfrac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5} (e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1)}$
  - $v_{LOS} = -\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \Delta\phi$
- **Key sensors:** Landsat 8/9 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI, ASTER, AVIRIS, PRISMA, Sentinel-1 SAR, WorldView-3.
- **Hyperspectral superiority:** >200 narrow bands → direct mineral identification via absorption features.
- **Integration principle:** RS data is **complementary** to seismic, gravity, magnetic, and well data — it is **not a replacement** but a **front-end screening tool**.
- **Cost impact:** RS-guided exploration can **eliminate 60–70% of non-prospective acreage** before expensive field operations begin.
- **Environmental role:** Critical for monitoring oil spills, gas flaring, illegal bunkering, and refinery emissions.
- **Numericals to practice:** GSD calculation, swath width, NDVI, Planck's law evaluation, Wein's displacement.
- **Diagrams to master:** EM spectrum with shaded petroleum bands; GSD geometry; RS workflow block diagram.
- **K-T-U quick recall formula for the exam hall:**
  - "**G**round = **H**eight × **P**ixel ÷ **F**ocal length" → $GSD = \dfrac{Hp}{f}$.
  - "**N**o **D**ead **V**egetation **I**ndex" → healthy vegetation has high NDVI; stressed vegetation (over seeps) has low NDVI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basin Exploration Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basin Exploration Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sedimentary Basin** is a regional-scale, long-lived depression in the Earth's crust where significant thicknesses of sedimentary rocks have accumulated over geological time (typically 10⁶ to 10⁸ years). It forms the fundamental *geographic and stratigraphic unit* of petroleum exploration.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEIT861, Module 1)**
> A *Basin Exploration Strategy* is the systematic, multi-stage, and risk-weighted decision-making framework used by E&P (Exploration & Production) companies to identify, evaluate, and rank hydrocarbon-bearing prospects within a sedimentary basin. It integrates **geology, geophysics, geochemistry, and reservoir engineering** under a defined economic and technological envelope.

The strategy is governed by the **Petroleum System Concept** (Magoon & Dow, 1994) and is operationalized through **Play Fairway Analysis (PFA)** and **Sequence Stratigraphy**.

## 1.2 Essential Elements of the Petroleum System

Every basin exploration strategy revolves around identifying and validating the seven (7) critical geological elements. If *any one* of them is missing, the system fails and no commercial accumulation forms.

| # | Element | Definition | Engineering Analogy |
|---|---------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Source Rock** | Organic-rich shale (TOC > 2 wt%) that generates hydrocarbons on maturation. | The "fuel tank" of the system. |
| 2 | **Reservoir Rock** | Porous (φ > 8%) and permeable (k > 1 mD) rock that stores hydrocarbons. | The "warehouse" of the system. |
| 3 | **Seal Rock (Cap Rock)** | Impermeable rock (shale, salt, tight carbonate) that traps hydrocarbons. | The "lid" of the container. |
| 4 | **Trap** | Geometric configuration that localises hydrocarbons (structural / stratigraphic). | The "container" itself. |
| 5 | **Migration Pathway** | Carrier bed / fault network connecting source to reservoir. | The "pipeline" carrying fuel. |
| 6 | **Timing** | Trap formation must predate or coincide with hydrocarbon generation. | The "synchronization clock." |
| 7 | **Preservation** | Post-accumulation integrity against leakage, biodegradation, tectonic destruction. | The "long-term security." |

> [!NOTE] **The "Play" Concept**
> A *Play* is a group of prospects and discovered fields sharing a common geological model: same source, reservoir, seal, and trap style. Basin exploration is fundamentally *play-based* (not prospect-based) at the regional stage.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Detective's Strategy Map"

Imagine a **petroleum exploration geologist as a detective** searching for hidden underground "treasure chests" (oil & gas accumulations) in a vast landscape (the basin).

- **Basin mapping** = Surveying the entire landscape to find regions where treasure *could* be buried (regional geology, gravity-magnetic surveys).
- **Play fairway analysis** = Studying the *type* of hiding spots that worked elsewhere — old river channels, coral reefs, ancient deltas.
- **Seismic imaging** = Using sonar-like sound waves to "see" underground structures without digging (like a 3D ultrasound of the Earth).
- **Risk analysis** = Estimating the probability that a treasure chest actually exists at a given point *before* committing to drill a multi-million dollar well.
- **Volumetric estimation** = Guessing *how much* treasure is in the chest, even before opening it (using rock properties and geometry).

> [!TIP] **Key Insight for B.Tech Students**
> Basin exploration is essentially a **portfolio optimization problem under geological uncertainty**. The explorationist never has 100% data — they must make multi-million dollar drilling decisions with probabilities and statistics.

## 1.4 Why "Basin-Centric" Strategy?

Modern exploration (post-1990s) shifted from "**feature-based**" (drill individual prospects) to "**basin-centered / play-based**" (understand the entire petroleum system). The advantages are:

1. **Reduces dry-hole risk** by 40–60% through systematic de-risking.
2. **Optimizes capital allocation** across multiple prospects of a single play.
3. **Identifies "sweet spots"** — high-productivity zones within a play.
4. **Aligns with license-block / concession strategy** of national oil companies.

## 1.5 Exploration Maturity Stages

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Frequently Tested**
> The basin exploration life cycle is divided into four maturity stages. Each stage has distinct data, cost, and risk profiles.

| Stage | Activity | Data Available | Dry-Hole Risk | Avg. Well Cost |
|-------|----------|----------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Frontier** | New basin entry | Regional only (gravity, magnetic) | > 80% | $50–150 M |
| **Emerging** | Play opening | 2D seismic, sparse wells | 50–70% | $30–80 M |
| **Mature** | Field/appraisal | 3D seismic, multiple wells | 20–40% | $15–40 M |
| **Decline** | Infill / EOR | 4D seismic, production data | < 20% | $5–20 M |

> The **goal of a basin exploration strategy is to migrate a basin from Frontier → Emerging → Mature** by progressively acquiring data and reducing geological uncertainty.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Basin Exploration Workflow

A KTU-aligned exploration strategy is a **funnel-shaped workflow**, where each successive stage is more detailed, expensive, and lower risk.

```
Stage 1: BASIN IDENTIFICATION (Regional)
   ↓  (gravity, magnetic, satellite, tectonic models)
Stage 2: BASIN ANALYSIS (Petroleum System Modeling)
   ↓  (seismic stratigraphy, source rock analysis, burial history)
Stage 3: PLAY FAIRWAY ANALYSIS (PFA)
   ↓  (de-risk source, reservoir, seal, trap elements)
Stage 4: PROSPECT GENERATION & MATURATION
   ↓  (detailed seismic mapping, AVO, inversion)
Stage 5: PROSPECT RANKING & DRILLING DECISION
   (economic cutoff, COPQ analysis, IRR/NPV)
```

> [!NOTE] **Cost vs. Information Trade-off**
> Each stage filters out non-prospective areas *before* committing capital. By the time a well is drilled, ~70% of the original basin area has been eliminated, but the *remaining prospects* are 5–10× more likely to be successful.

## 2.2 Geological Methods in Basin Analysis

### A. Tectonic-Stratigraphic Framework
The basin architecture is reconstructed using the principles of **sequence stratigraphy** (Vail, 1977) and **tectonic subsidence analysis**.

Key parameters derived:
- **Subsidence rate (S)** = dh/dt, in m/Myr
- **Sediment accumulation rate (SAR)** = thickness / duration
- **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)** content of source rocks
- **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro %)** — maturation indicator
- **Heat Flow (q)** — in mW/m², controls hydrocarbon generation

### B. Source Rock Evaluation (Geochemistry)

$$HI = \frac{S_2 \times 100}{TOC} \quad [\text{mg HC / g TOC}]$$

where $S_2$ is the remaining hydrocarbon generation potential from Rock-Eval pyrolysis.

- $HI > 600$ → excellent oil-prone source
- $HI \ 200$–$600$ → gas/condensate-prone
- $HI < 200$ → gas-prone or inert

### C. Maturation Modeling (Lopatin / Arrhenius)

The **Time-Temperature Index (TTI)** of Lopatin (1971) is used to estimate thermal maturation:

$$TTI = \sum_{i} \left( \Delta t_i \cdot r^{n_i} \right)$$

where $\Delta t_i$ is the duration spent in the $i$-th temperature interval and $r = 2$ is a doubling factor for every 10 °C rise in temperature, and $n_i$ is an integer counter.

| TTI Range | Maturation Stage | Hydrocarbon Window |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| < 15 | Immature | None |
| 15 – 75 | Early mature | Oil |
| 75 – 160 | Peak mature | Wet gas / condensate |
| > 160 | Late mature | Dry gas |
| > 1000 | Overmature | Metamorphosed |

## 2.3 Geophysical Methods in Basin Exploration

### A. Gravity & Magnetic Surveys (Regional)
- **Bouguer Anomaly** $\Delta g_B$ in mGal — identifies basin thickness, basement configuration.
- **Magnetic Anomaly** $T$ in nT — locates igneous intrusions, basement highs.

### B. Reflection Seismic (Prospect-Scale)
- **Two-Way Time (TWT)** maps in seconds.
- **Acoustic Impedance (AI)** $= \rho \cdot V_p$ — used in inversion.
- **AVO (Amplitude vs. Offset)** analysis — direct hydrocarbon indicator.

### C. Modern 3D / 4D Seismic
- 3D gives spatial coverage; **4D** (time-lapse) monitors fluid movement in producing fields.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Basin Exploration & Resource Estimation

> [!IMPORTANT] All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear in numerical problems (Part A/B) of the KTU University Exam.

### I. Volumetric Hydrocarbon In-Place Calculations

**Stock Tank Oil Initially In Place (STOIIP)** in stock-tank barrels (STB):

$$STOIIP = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$$

**Gas Initially In Place (GIIP)** in standard cubic feet (SCF):

$$GIIP = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{gi}}$$

**Condensate Initially In Place (CIIP)** in STB:

$$CIIP = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{gi}} \cdot \frac{API_{cond}}{1000 \cdot 5.614}$$

| Symbol | Description | Typical Unit |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| $A$ | Trap area | acres (oil) or ft² (gas) |
| $h$ | Net pay thickness | ft |
| $\phi$ | Porosity | fraction (0–1) |
| $S_w$ | Water saturation | fraction (0–1) |
| $B_{oi}$ | Oil formation volume factor | RB/STB |
| $B_{gi}$ | Gas formation volume factor | RCF/SCF |

### II. Recoverable Reserves

$$RR = STOIIP \cdot RF \quad [\text{STB}]$$

where $RF$ = Recovery Factor (typically 0.20 – 0.50 for oil, 0.60 – 0.90 for gas).

### III. Geological / Posterior Probability of Success (POS)

For a prospect with $n$ independent geological factors, each with probability $P_i$:

$$P_{success} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i = P_{source} \cdot P_{reservoir} \cdot P_{trap} \cdot P_{seal} \cdot P_{timing}$$

> [!TIP] **KTU Pitfall**
> The 7758 constant assumes area in *acres* and thickness in *feet*. If area is in m², use $STOIIP = A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_{oi}$ in m³, then convert using $1\ \text{m}^3 = 6.2898\ \text{bbl}$.

### IV. Expected Monetary Value (EMV) — Drilling Decision

$$EMV = P_s \cdot NPV_{success} \cdot (1 - T) - (1 - P_s) \cdot C_{dry} - C_{seismic}$$

where $P_s$ = POS, $T$ = tax rate, $C_{dry}$ = dry-hole cost, $C_{seismic}$ = sunk cost.

**Decision rule:** Drill if and only if $EMV > 0$.

### V. Basin Thermal Subsidence (McKenzie Model, 1978)

For a basin undergoing uniform stretching with stretch factor $\beta$:

$$S(t) = \frac{(\rho_m - \rho_s) \cdot T_e}{\rho_m \cdot (1 - \alpha T_s)} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right) \right] - C \cdot t$$

where $\tau$ = thermal decay constant, $T_e$ = effective elastic thickness, $\rho_m$ = mantle density, $\rho_s$ = sediment density, $C$ = compaction constant.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Application

| Application Domain | How Basin Exploration Strategy is Used |
|--------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **ONGC / OIL India** (NOCs) | Pre-licensing block evaluation, RSC bidding strategy |
| **Reliance / Cairn (Private)** | KG basin & Barmer basin asset acquisition |
| **Service companies (Schlumberger / Halliburton)** | Targeted tool / service offerings |
| **Government of India (DGH)** | Open Acreage Licensing Policy (OALP) block categorization |
| **International (Shell / BP / Exxon)** | Global portfolio screening, multi-basin risk-balanced investment |

## 2.6 Risk and Uncertainty Quantification

Three levels of risk are evaluated in modern exploration:

1. **Geological Risk (G):** POS = probability of finding hydrocarbons
2. **Commercial Risk (C):** = probability of developing economically
3. **Political / Sovereign Risk (P):** = government stability, contract terms

$$P_{commercial\ success} = P_s \cdot P_c \cdot (1 - P_{political})$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the STOIIP Volumetric Equation

The Stock Tank Oil Initially In Place (STOIIP) is the **fundamental volumetric equation** in petroleum engineering. It is derived from first principles of bulk volume and fluid property conversion.

### Step 1: Define Bulk Rock Volume (BRV)

The bulk rock volume of a reservoir trap is the geometric product of the trap area and the net pay thickness.

$$BRV = A \times h \quad [\text{acre} \cdot \text{ft}]$$

### Step 2: Apply Porosity to Get Pore Volume (PV)

Pore volume is the fraction of bulk rock occupied by pore space (voids):

$$PV = A \times h \times \phi \quad [\text{acre} \cdot \text{ft}]$$

### Step 3: Apply Hydrocarbon Saturation to Get Hydrocarbon Pore Volume (HCPV)

$$HCPV = A \times h \times \phi \times (1 - S_w) \quad [\text{acre} \cdot \text{ft}]$$

### Step 4: Convert to Reservoir Barrels (RB)

The conversion from acre·ft to reservoir barrels is:

$$1\ \text{acre} \cdot \text{ft} = 7758\ \text{RB}$$

So,

$$HCPV = 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) \quad [\text{RB}]$$

### Step 5: Apply Oil Formation Volume Factor $B_{oi}$

The formation volume factor accounts for **shrinkage** of oil when it is brought from reservoir conditions (high P, T) to surface stock-tank conditions.

- $B_{oi} > 1$ always (oil shrinks on production).
- Typical $B_{oi} = 1.2$ to $1.6$ RB/STB.

Dividing HCPV by $B_{oi}$:

$$STOIIP = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}} \quad [\text{STB}]$$

This is the standard industry formula used in KTU numerical problems.

> [!NOTE] **Derivation complete.** The student should memorize the 7758 constant and remember the sequence: Area → Thickness → Porosity → Saturation → $B_{oi}$ conversion.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — STOIIP Calculation (KTU Numerical Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> A prospect has the following data:
> - Trap area: $A = 1500$ acres
> - Net pay: $h = 50$ ft
> - Porosity: $\phi = 0.20$
> - Water saturation: $S_w = 0.30$
> - Oil FVF: $B_{oi} = 1.35$ RB/STB
> - Recovery factor: $RF = 0.35$
>
> Calculate (i) STOIIP in STB, (ii) Recoverable Oil in MMSTB.

### Solution

**Step (i) — STOIIP**

$$STOIIP = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$$

Substituting values:

$$STOIIP = \frac{7758 \times 1500 \times 50 \times 0.20 \times (1 - 0.30)}{1.35}$$

Compute numerator:

$$7758 \times 1500 = 11{,}637{,}000$$

$$11{,}637{,}000 \times 50 = 581{,}850{,}000$$

$$581{,}850{,}000 \times 0.20 = 116{,}370{,}000$$

$$116{,}370{,}000 \times 0.70 = 81{,}459{,}000$$

Divide by $B_{oi} = 1.35$:

$$STOIIP = \frac{81{,}459{,}000}{1.35} = 60{,}340{,}000\ \text{STB}$$

$$STOIIP = 60.34\ \text{MMSTB}$$

**Step (ii) — Recoverable Oil**

$$RR = STOIIP \times RF = 60.34 \times 0.35$$

$$RR = 21.12\ \text{MMSTB}$$

> **Answer:** STOIIP = 60.34 MMSTB, Recoverable Oil = 21.12 MMSTB.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Probability of Success (POS)

The POS is the **single most important variable** in prospect ranking. It is derived from the **chain rule of probability** for independent geological factors.

### Step 1: Identify the Independent Geological Factors

For a prospect to contain commercial hydrocarbons, the following *independent* conditions must all be true:

- (a) Source rock is mature and has generated hydrocarbons.
- (b) Reservoir rock of adequate quality exists.
- (c) Seal rock is present and effective.
- (d) Trap geometry is closed at the time of migration.
- (e) Timing of generation vs. trap formation is correct.

### Step 2: Define Marginal Probabilities

For each factor, the geologist assigns a *marginal probability* $P_i$ (between 0 and 1) based on available data:

- $P_1$ = P(source mature) = 0.9 (well-typed source rock, $Ro = 1.1\%$)
- $P_2$ = P(reservoir quality) = 0.7 (sandstone with $\phi = 18\%$)
- $P_3$ = P(seal effective) = 0.8 (regional shale seal)
- $P_4$ = P(closure) = 0.6 (seismic-defined anticline)
- $P_5$ = P(timing) = 0.9 (burial history analysis)

### Step 3: Apply Chain Rule of Probability

For *independent* events $A$ and $B$:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

For $n$ independent events:

$$P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \dots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i)$$

Therefore:

$$P_{success} = P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3 \cdot P_4 \cdot P_5$$

Substituting:

$$P_{success} = 0.9 \times 0.7 \times 0.8 \times 0.6 \times 0.9$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$0.9 \times 0.7 = 0.63$$

$$0.63 \times 0.8 = 0.504$$

$$0.504 \times 0.6 = 0.3024$$

$$0.3024 \times 0.9 = 0.27216$$

$$P_{success} \approx 0.27 = 27\%$$

> **Interpretation:** This prospect has a 27% chance of finding commercial hydrocarbons. This is *good* by industry standards (typical exploration wells are drilled at 15–25% POS).

> [!TIP] **Independent vs. Conditional**
> The chain rule applies *only* if the events are statistically independent. In reality, source and timing are often *correlated*. KTU accepts the independence assumption for examination purposes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Basin Exploration Decision Tree

This matrix maps *how exploration decisions evolve* as data is acquired through the basin life cycle.

| Stage | Geological Knowledge | Seismic Data | Wells Drilled | Decision Outcome |
|-------|----------------------|--------------|---------------|------------------|
| **1. Frontier** | Plate-tectonic models, regional geology | None (gravity-mag only) | 0 | Identify basin type, license acquisition |
| **2. Play Opening** | Source/reservoir/seal presence | 2D regional seismic | 1–2 (stratigraphic tests) | Define play concept, de-risk $P_1$, $P_2$ |
| **3. Play Extension** | Petroleum system confirmed | 3D seismic, partial AVO | 3–8 | Identify prospects, rank by $P_s$ × volume |
| **4. Prospect Maturation** | Prospect-specific geology | Pre-stack 3D, inversion | 8–15 | Drill-ready prospects, COPQ (Chance of Petroleum Occurrence Quantity) |
| **5. Field Appraisal** | Reservoir heterogeneity, contacts | 4D seismic | 15+ | Reserve booking, development plan |
| **6. Mature Production** | Drainage patterns | Permanent 4D | 50+ | Infill drilling, EOR screening |

---

## 3.5 Basin Classification — Tectonic-Stratigraphic Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield for KTU Descriptive Answers**
> Basins are classified by *genesis* (tectonic origin) — not by geography. The KTU syllabus emphasizes this classification.

| Basin Type | Tectonic Setting | Hydrocarbon Potential | Indian Example |
|------------|------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Rift Basin** | Extensional | Excellent (lacustrine source) | Cambay Basin |
| **Passive Margin** | Stable shelf / continental margin | Excellent (marine source) | Mumbai Offshore |
| **Foreland Basin** | Compression (orogeny) | Good (clastic reservoir) | Assam-Arakan |
| **Intra-cratonic Sag** | Slow subsidence | Variable | Vindhyan Basin |
| **Pull-apart** | Strike-slip | Localized but rich | Gulf of Kutch |
| **Back-arc** | Subduction-related | Good gas potential | Andaman Basin |

---

## 3.6 Numerical — Expected Monetary Value (EMV) of a Prospect

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**
> A prospect has the following data:
> - POS: $P_s = 0.25$
> - Estimated NPV if successful: $NPV_s = \$ 800$ million
> - Tax rate: $T = 0.30$
> - Dry-hole cost: $C_{dry} = \$ 60$ million
> - Seismic sunk cost: $C_{seismic} = \$ 15$ million
>
> Should the company drill this well? Compute the EMV.

### Solution

**Step 1: After-tax NPV in case of success**

$$NPV_{after\ tax} = NPV_s \times (1 - T) = 800 \times 0.70 = 560\ \text{M\$}$$

**Step 2: Expected gain on success**

$$E_{success} = P_s \times NPV_{after\ tax} = 0.25 \times 560 = 140\ \text{M\$}$$

**Step 3: Expected loss on dry-hole**

$$E_{dry} = (1 - P_s) \times C_{dry} = 0.75 \times 60 = 45\ \text{M\$}$$

**Step 4: Subtract sunk cost**

Note: Sunk cost (already spent on seismic) is **not** included in the incremental EMV calculation in the strict corporate-finance sense, but the KTU exam pattern often includes it. We follow the KTU textbook formulation:

$$EMV = P_s \cdot NPV_s \cdot (1 - T) - (1 - P_s) \cdot C_{dry} - C_{seismic}$$

$$EMV = 140 - 45 - 15 = 80\ \text{M\$}$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $EMV = +\$80\ \text{M} > 0$, **the company SHOULD drill this well**.

> [!TIP] **Common KTU Mistake**
> Students often forget to multiply $(1 - T)$ on the success side, OR they add the seismic cost to the success scenario. Drill the exact formula from §2.4 IV.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE] **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied**
> All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `s1`, `d1`) and labels contain no markdown formatting.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Basin Exploration Strategy Funnel

This diagram illustrates the *funnel concept* of basin exploration: a wide regional area progressively narrows down to a single drillable prospect.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
