# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Petroleum System." List its essential elements.**

**Model Answer:**

The *Petroleum System* (Magoon & Dow, 1994) is a natural hydrocarbon system encompassing a pod of active source rock and all related oil and gas accumulations, including the essential geological elements and processes required for their existence.

**Essential Elements (Seven):**

1. Source rock (organic-rich, mature)
2. Reservoir rock (porous, permeable)
3. Seal rock (impermeable cap)
4. Overburden rock (provides pressure and seals the trap)
5. Trap (geological container for accumulation)
6. Migration pathway (carrier beds, faults)
7. Timing (synchronization of generation, migration, and trap formation)

> [!NOTE] **3-mark allocation:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing elements with one-line explanations.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a "Play" in basin exploration? How is it different from a "Prospect"?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Play** is a conceptual group of prospects and discovered fields sharing a *common geological model* — same source, reservoir, seal, and trap style. It represents a *regional* exploration concept valid across an entire fairway.

A **Prospect** is a *specific mappable trap* (defined by seismic or geological mapping) ready to be drilled. A prospect is one *realization* of a play.

| Parameter | Play | Prospect |
|-----------|------|----------|
| Scale | Regional (1000s of km²) | Local (1–50 km²) |
| Status | Conceptual | Mappable, drillable |
| Data | Play-wide analysis | Prospect-specific seismic |
| Example | "Cambay Cambay Cambay Cambay" Cambay Cambay Basin Cambay Cambay Cambay Cambay Cambay Cambay Cambay" Cambay Cambay Cambay" Cambay" | A specific anticlinal closure in Cambay |

> [!TIP] **Examiner's note:** A play is *untested* by drilling; a prospect is *drillable*. A *lead* is even less mature than a prospect.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry internal choice. Two independent alternatives are provided below. **Answer ANY ONE.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Basin Exploration Workflow & Volumetrics**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the systematic basin exploration strategy adopted in upstream petroleum industry. Explain in detail the role of geological, geophysical, and geochemical methods at each stage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A prospect in Mumbai Offshore basin has the following data:
> - Trap area = 2000 acres
> - Net pay thickness = 80 ft
> - Average porosity = 22%
> - Water saturation = 25%
> - Oil FVF = 1.40 RB/STB
> - Recovery factor = 0.40
>
> Compute: (i) STOIIP in MMSTB, (ii) Recoverable oil in MMSTB, (iii) EMV if the prospect is found to be successful with NPV = $1200 M, dry-hole cost = $80 M, tax = 30%, and Probability of Success = 0.30. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Basin Exploration Strategy (7 marks)

A **basin exploration strategy** is a multi-stage, risk-weighted decision framework used to identify and commercialize hydrocarbon accumulations. It progresses through five sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Basin Identification (Regional Geology)**

- **Geological Methods:** Tectonic framework analysis, plate reconstruction, identification of basin-forming mechanisms (rift, sag, foreland).
- **Geophysical Methods:** Gravity and magnetic surveys to map basement depth, basin thickness, and major fault systems.
- **Geochemical Methods:** Not directly applied at this stage — relies on regional literature.

**Stage 2 — Basin Analysis (Petroleum System Modeling)**

- **Geological Methods:** Sequence stratigraphy, depositional environment mapping, identification of source-reservoir-seal triplets.
- **Geophysical Methods:** 2D regional seismic to image basin fill, identify major reflectors and unconformities.
- **Geochemical Methods:** Source rock sampling, TOC measurement, Rock-Eval pyrolysis, vitrinite reflectance ($R_o$) analysis.

**Stage 3 — Play Fairway Analysis (PFA)**

- **Geological Methods:** Regional structural mapping, play concept definition, common risk segment (CRS) maps.
- **Geophysical Methods:** 3D seismic acquisition, attribute analysis, spectral decomposition.
- **Geochemical Methods:** Oil-source rock correlation, biomarker analysis, fluid inclusion studies.

**Stage 4 — Prospect Generation & Maturation**

- **Geological Methods:** Detailed trap mapping, reservoir continuity analysis.
- **Geophysical Methods:** Pre-stack depth migration (PSDM), AVO analysis, seismic inversion for porosity/lithology.
- **Geochemical Methods:** Fluid typing from PVT, basin modeling (1D/2D/3D thermal maturation).

**Stage 5 — Prospect Ranking & Drilling**

- **Geological Methods:** Volumetric estimation of STOIIP, GIIP.
- **Geophysical Methods:** Risk-weighted seismic interpretation.
- **Geochemical Methods:** Final risk register review.
- **Decision Tool:** EMV (Expected Monetary Value) analysis.

> **Key principle:** Each stage is a *gating* decision — failure at any stage halts further investment in that basin/play.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Volumetric & EMV Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**

$A = 2000$ acres, $h = 80$ ft, $\phi = 0.22$, $S_w = 0.25$, $B_{oi} = 1.40$ RB/STB, $RF = 0.40$.

**Part (i) — STOIIP**

$$STOIIP = \frac{7758 \times 2000 \times 80 \times 0.22 \times (1 - 0.25)}{1.40}$$

Numerator computation:

$$7758 \times 2000 = 15{,}516{,}000$$

$$15{,}516{,}000 \times 80 = 1{,}241{,}280{,}000$$

$$1{,}241{,}280{,}000 \times 0.22 = 273{,}081{,}600$$

$$273{,}081{,}600 \times 0.75 = 204{,}811{,}200$$

Divide by $B_{oi}$:

$$STOIIP = \frac{204{,}811{,}200}{1.40} = 146{,}293{,}714\ \text{STB}$$

$$\boxed{STOIIP \approx 146.29\ \text{MMSTB}}$$

**Part (ii) — Recoverable Oil**

$$RR = STOIIP \times RF = 146.29 \times 0.40$$

$$\boxed{RR \approx 58.52\ \text{MMSTB}}$$

**Part (iii) — EMV**

Given: $P_s = 0.30$, $NPV_s = 1200$ M, $C_{dry} = 80$ M, $T = 0.30$.

$$EMV = P_s \cdot NPV_s \cdot (1 - T) - (1 - P_s) \cdot C_{dry}$$

$$EMV = 0.30 \times 1200 \times 0.70 - 0.70 \times 80$$

$$EMV = 252 - 56 = 196\ \text{M\$}$$

$$\boxed{EMV = +\$196\ \text{M} > 0 \rightarrow \text{DRILL}}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> - Many students forget to multiply $NPV$ by $(1 - T)$ → loss of 1 mark.
> - Some use 0.30 (success side) for $(1 - P_s)$ instead of 0.70 → catastrophic error.
> - STOIIP unit confusion: $A$ in *acres*, $h$ in *feet*, gives RB directly. Divide by $B_{oi}$ to get STB.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — Play Concept & Risk**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of "Play Fairway Analysis" (PFA) in basin exploration. How does it differ from traditional prospect-level analysis? What are the key inputs and outputs of a PFA workflow? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A frontier basin has 5 independent geological risk factors with the following probabilities: $P_1 = 0.9$, $P_2 = 0.6$, $P_3 = 0.7$, $P_4 = 0.5$, $P_5 = 0.8$.
>
> Compute:
> (i) Overall Probability of Success (POS).
> (ii) If the volumetric STOIIP estimate is 100 MMSTB, what is the **Expected Value of STOIIP**?
> (iii) If a new 3D seismic survey (cost: $30 M) can improve $P_2$ from 0.6 to 0.85, recompute POS and expected STOIIP. Is the 3D survey economically justified? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Play Fairway Analysis (7 marks)

**Play Fairway Analysis (PFA)** is a regional, multi-disciplinary exploration methodology that systematically maps the spatial distribution of *all essential petroleum system elements* (source, reservoir, seal, trap, timing) over an entire basin. It produces a *risk-segmented map* showing where all elements coexist.

**Differences from Traditional Prospect-Level Analysis:**

| Aspect | Play Fairway Analysis | Traditional Prospect Analysis |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Scale | Regional (basin-wide) | Local (single trap) |
| Focus | Multiple prospects simultaneously | One prospect at a time |
| Data | Integrated geological / geophysical / geochemical | Mainly seismic + offset wells |
| Risk Treatment | CRS (Common Risk Segment) maps | Individual prospect POS |
| Output | Sweet-spot identification | Drill/no-drill decision |

**Key Inputs:**

1. Seismic (2D / 3D) data
2. Well logs, core, and test data
3. Source rock geochemistry (TOC, $R_o$, HI)
4. Reservoir petrophysics ($\phi$, $k$, $S_w$)
5. Structural / tectonic framework

**Key Outputs:**

1. **CRS (Common Risk Segment) maps** — probability-of-presence for each element.
2. **Play Risk Map** — composite product of CRS maps.
3. **Volume maps** — gross rock volume per CRS cell.
4. **Sweet-spot delineation** — high-POS, high-volume fairways.

**Workflow Summary:** (i) Define play concept → (ii) Map each element separately → (iii) Multiply CRS maps → (iv) Identify high-probability fairways → (v) Generate prospects.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Risk & Economic Calculation (7 marks)

**Part (i) — Original POS**

$$P_s = 0.9 \times 0.6 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.8$$

Step by step:

$$0.9 \times 0.6 = 0.54$$

$$0.54 \times 0.7 = 0.378$$

$$0.378 \times 0.5 = 0.189$$

$$0.189 \times 0.8 = 0.1512$$

$$\boxed{P_s = 0.1512 \approx 15.12\%}$$

**Part (ii) — Expected STOIIP**

$$E[STOIIP] = P_s \times STOIIP = 0.1512 \times 100 = 15.12\ \text{MMSTB}$$

**Part (iii) — POS after 3D seismic de-risking $P_2$**

Updated $P_2 = 0.85$:

$$P_s^{new} = 0.9 \times 0.85 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 0.8$$

$$= 0.9 \times 0.85 = 0.765$$

$$\times 0.7 = 0.5355$$

$$\times 0.5 = 0.26775$$

$$\times 0.8 = 0.2142$$

$$\boxed{P_s^{new} = 0.2142 \approx 21.42\%}$$

**New Expected STOIIP:**

$$E[STOIIP]^{new} = 0.2142 \times 100 = 21.42\ \text{MMSTB}$$

**Increment in Expected STOIIP:**

$$\Delta E[STOIIP] = 21.42 - 15.12 = 6.30\ \text{MMSTB}$$

**Economic Justification:**

Cost of 3D survey = $30 M. The increment of 6.30 MMSTB must be valued at the *expected netback price per barrel*. If netback = $30/bbl, the value uplift = 6.30 × 10⁶ × 30 = $189 M. Since $189 M ≫ $30 M, **the 3D survey is economically justified**.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> - Q.B(b) tests both *probabilistic reasoning* AND *economic judgment*. Don't stop at computing POS — you must make a **justified go/no-go decision**.
> - Note: The exact break-even netback = $30 M / 6.30 MMSTB = **$4.76/bbl**. Since realistic netbacks are $20–60/bbl, the survey is justified in virtually all scenarios.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Print this checklist on your last revision day before the KTU exam.**

**Core Concepts**

- A **basin** is a depression filled with sedimentary rocks; it is the *fundamental unit* of petroleum exploration.
- The **Petroleum System** has 7 elements: source, reservoir, seal, overburden, trap, migration, timing.
- A **Play** is regional and conceptual; a **Prospect** is local and drillable; a **Lead** is even less mature.

**Workflow Stages**

- Five stages: Basin Identification → Basin Analysis → Play Fairway → Prospect Generation → Drilling Decision.
- Each stage is a *gating decision* — no commitment of capital without data.

**Key Formulas (Memorize!)**

- $STOIIP = 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_{oi}$ — units: acres, ft, RB/STB.
- $GIIP = 43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_{gi}$ — units: ft², ft, RCF/SCF.
- $P_s = \prod P_i$ — product of marginal probabilities (independent factors).
- $EMV = P_s \cdot NPV_s \cdot (1 - T) - (1 - P_s) \cdot C_{dry} - C_{seismic}$.
- $HI = 100 \cdot S_2 / TOC$ — source rock quality indicator.
- $TTI$ from Lopatin method — maturation index (oil: 15–75, gas: 75–160, dry gas: >160).

**Basin Classification (Tectonic-Stratigraphic)**

- **Rift** — Cambay, Rhine; **Passive Margin** — Mumbai Offshore, North Sea; **Foreland** — Assam-Arakan, Rockies; **Intra-cratonic** — Vindhyan; **Pull-apart** — Kutch; **Back-arc** — Andaman.

**Exploration Maturity**

- Frontier (POS < 20%) → Emerging (20–40%) → Mature (40–70%) → Declining (> 70%).
- Cost and risk decrease monotonically with maturity.

**Geophysical Tools**

- Gravity-magnetic (regional), 2D seismic (basin), 3D seismic (play), AVO/inversion (prospect), 4D seismic (reservoir monitoring).

**Geochemical Tools**

- TOC, $R_o$, $S_1$, $S_2$, $S_3$, HI, OI, Tmax, biomarker analysis.

**Decision Rules**

- Drill if $EMV > 0$.
- Drill if $P_s \times NPV_{success} > (1 - P_s) \cdot C_{dry}$.

**Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)**

- Mixing area units (acres vs m²) in STOIIP formula.
- Forgetting the $(1 - S_w)$ term.
- Forgetting the $(1 - T)$ tax factor in EMV.
- Using *conditional* probability formula when *independent* probability applies (or vice versa).
- Conflating **Lead → Prospect → Play** terminology.

> [!WARNING] **Final KTU Examiner's Warning**
> In KTU Module 1 (Basin Exploration Strategies), the most common *mark-losing* errors are: (1) forgetting $B_{oi}$ in STOIIP, (2) using absolute units instead of fractional for $\phi$ and $S_w$, and (3) not stating the **decision criterion** in EMV problems. Always **end a numerical answer with a clear "go / no-go" decision statement.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entrapment of Oil-type Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Entrapment of Oil-type Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Entrapment of Oil-type Mechanisms** refers to the physical, chemical, and surface-tension-based phenomena by which hydrocarbon (oil) phases become immobilized within the pore network of a reservoir rock or within process equipment surfaces, resisting displacement by water, gas, or other drive fluids. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these mechanisms are studied under the broader umbrella of **reservoir wettability, capillary physics, and multiphase flow instrumentation** because they directly dictate the design of **Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) systems**, **downhole sensors**, and **separation control loops** in petrochemical plants.

The principal mechanisms are:

1. **Capillary (Jamin) Entrapment** – oil blobs snap off in pore throats.
2. **Adsorptive / Wettability Entrapment** – oil films adhere to rock surfaces.
3. **Geometric / Pore-throat Entrapment** – oil is mechanically stranded in dead-end pores.
4. **Viscous Entrapment** – high-mobility contrasts immobilize oil ganglia.
5. **Mineralogical / Clay-mediated Entrapment** – polar compounds bind to clay surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PEEIT861 emphasizes how the *measurement* of entrapped oil (using resistivity logs, NMR tools, and distributed fiber-optic sensors) depends on the *mechanism* of entrapment. Therefore, understanding the mechanism is a prerequisite to selecting the correct instrument.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **wet kitchen sponge placed under a faucet**. When you turn off the tap, the sponge still holds water. You have to *squeeze* it (capillary pressure drop) or *heat* it (lowering viscosity) to release that water. The sponge's tiny holes are like the **pore throats** of reservoir rock, and the water left behind is like **entrapped oil**. The "stickiness" of the sponge walls corresponds to **wettability**, and the size of the pores controls **capillary pressure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric (bolded for emphasis):** Typical residual oil saturation ($S_{or}$) in water-wet sandstones ranges from **0.20 to 0.40** (i.e., 20–40 % of pore volume), while in strongly oil-wet carbonates it can exceed **0.50**. This single number drives billions of dollars in EOR investment globally.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism-by-Mechanism Logical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Capillary (Jamin) Entrapment
- **Why:** When oil is displaced by water through a constricted pore throat, the oil–water interface must deform. Surface tension resists this deformation.
- **How:** Once the capillary number exceeds a critical threshold, the oil ganglion snaps off and is stranded downstream. This is governed by the **capillary pressure** of the pore throat.
- **Engineering utility:** Dictates the minimum pressure differential an injection pump must overcome (an instrumentation/control setpoint).

#### 2.1.2 Adsorptive / Wettability Entrapment
- **Why:** Polar hydrocarbons (asphaltenes, resins) and organic acids form a **tenacious adsorbed film** on mineral surfaces, especially clays (kaolinite, illite, montmorillonite).
- **How:** This film alters contact angle $\theta$ from water-wet ($\theta < 90^\circ$) to oil-wet ($\theta > 90^\circ$). The adhesion force per unit area is $F = \sigma_{ow} (1 + \cos\theta)$.
- **Engineering utility:** Wettability is the primary input to **Amott-Harvey** and **USBM** wettability indices, which inform chemical EOR dosing.

#### 2.1.3 Geometric / Pore-throat Entrapment
- **Why:** Reservoir rock has a wide distribution of pore-body-to-pore-throat ratios.
- **How:** Oil enters a large pore body but cannot exit a narrower throat unless the local capillary pressure is overcome. This produces isolated **oil ganglia**.
- **Engineering utility:** Determines the optimal **pore-size distribution** model (e.g., Brooks-Corey or Leverett J-function) used in log interpretation.

#### 2.1.4 Viscous Entrapment
- **Why:** When the displacing fluid is much less viscous than oil, viscous fingering bypasses oil zones.
- **How:** Quantified by the **mobility ratio** $M = (k_{rw}/\mu_w)/(k_{ro}/\mu_o)$. For $M > 1$, the displacement is unstable.
- **Engineering utility:** Polymer flooding instrumentation aims to keep $M \approx 1$ by injecting viscosifiers.

#### 2.1.5 Mineralogical (Clay-mediated) Entrapment
- **Why:** Clays have high specific surface area (10–100 m²/g) and cation exchange capacity.
- **How:** They adsorb polar oil components, effectively "locking" oil at the nano-scale.
- **Engineering utility:** Justifies why low-salinity water injection (LoSal™) is a controlled-injection technique in modern plants.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $P_c = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure | Pa | Jamin snap-off threshold |
| 2 | $J(S_w) = \dfrac{P_c}{\sigma \cos\theta}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\phi}}$ | Leverett J-function | dimensionless | Scaling $P_c$ across rocks |
| 3 | $N_c = \dfrac{v \mu_w}{\sigma_{ow}}$ | Capillary number | dimensionless | Viscous vs. capillary forces |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{k_{rw}/\mu_w}{k_{ro}/\mu_o}$ | Mobility ratio | dimensionless | Displacement stability |
| 5 | $F = \sigma_{ow}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Adhesion force per unit length | N/m | Wettability work of adhesion |
| 6 | $k_{ro} = k_{ro}^{\max}\left(1 - \dfrac{S_w - S_{wr}}{1 - S_{or} - S_{wr}}\right)^{n_o}$ | Corey relative permeability | dimensionless | Two-phase flow models |
| 7 | $I_{AH} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta S_{w1}}{\Delta S_{w1}+\Delta S_{o1}} + \dfrac{\Delta S_{o2}}{\Delta S_{o2}+\Delta S_{w2}}\right)$ | Amott-Harvey Index | dimensionless ($-1$ to $+1$) | Wettability quantification |
| 8 | $\Delta S = \dfrac{\phi (S_{wi} - S_{or})}{B_o}$ | Recoverable oil volume per bulk volume | fraction | Reserve estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU valuation, examiners expect students to state the **units** of every symbol in the formula. Memorize the units of $\sigma$ (mN/m), $\mu$ (cP or Pa·s), $k$ (mD or m²), and $\phi$ (fraction or %).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Capillary Pressure from Young–Laplace (Jamin Threshold)

Starting point: a single oil droplet lodged in a cylindrical pore throat of radius $r$, with oil–water interfacial tension $\sigma_{ow}$ and contact angle $\theta$ measured through the water phase.

**Step 1 — Identify the two principal radii of curvature.**
For a spherical-cap interface in a circular pore, the two principal radii are equal: $R_1 = R_2 = R$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Young–Laplace equation.**

$$
P_c = P_{non-wetting} - P_{wetting} = \sigma_{ow}\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R_1 = R_2 = R$.**

$$
P_c = \sigma_{ow} \cdot \dfrac{2}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate $R$ to the pore-throat radius $r$.**
Geometrically, the meniscus radius $R$ relates to $r$ by $R = r/\cos\theta$. Therefore:

$$
P_c = \sigma_{ow} \cdot \dfrac{2 \cos\theta}{r}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the sign convention.**
- If $\theta < 90^\circ$ (water-wet), $\cos\theta > 0$, so $P_c > 0$ and oil (non-wetting) is at higher pressure than water.
- If $\theta > 90^\circ$ (oil-wet), $\cos\theta < 0$, so $P_c < 0$ and the capillary forces *favor* oil entry.

> **Conversion logic:** This single equation is the **Rosette stone** of all multiphase-flow instrumentation because resistivity logs, capillary-pressure curves, and centrifuge measurements all trace back to it.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Critical Capillary Number for Snap-off

The capillary number is defined as the ratio of viscous to capillary forces:

$$
N_c = \dfrac{v \mu_w}{\sigma_{ow}}
$$

Empirically (from micro-model experiments of Morrow & Song, 1990s):

$$
N_{c,\text{crit}} \approx 10^{-5} \quad \text{(water-wet)}, \qquad N_{c,\text{crit}} \approx 10^{-3} \quad \text{(oil-wet)}
$$

**Step 1 — Darcy's velocity for the water phase:**

$$
v = \dfrac{k_{rw}\, \Delta P}{\mu_w L}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $N_c$:**

$$
N_c = \dfrac{k_{rw}\, \Delta P}{\sigma_{ow}\, L}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the minimum pressure gradient to mobilize a trapped ganglion:**

$$
\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{\min} = \dfrac{N_{c,\text{crit}}\, \sigma_{ow}}{k_{rw}}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** A KTU instrumentation problem often asks, *"What injection pressure is required to mobilize residual oil in a sandstone with $k_{rw} = 0.2$, $\sigma_{ow} = 30$ mN/m?"* The above equation is the direct answer template.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Given:** Water viscosity $\mu_w = 1.0$ cP, oil–water interfacial tension $\sigma_{ow} = 25$ mN/m, pore-throat radius $r = 5 \,\mu$m, contact angle $\theta = 60^\circ$, water-wet relative permeability $k_{rw} = 0.15$, length $L = 1$ m, critical capillary number $N_{c,\text{crit}} = 10^{-5}$.

**Step 1 — Compute capillary pressure.**

$$
P_c = \dfrac{2 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times \cos 60^\circ}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.025 \times 0.5}{5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
P_c = \dfrac{0.025}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 5{,}000 \ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Darcy velocity threshold for mobilization.**

$$
v_{\min} = \dfrac{N_{c,\text{crit}}\,\sigma_{ow}}{\mu_w} = \dfrac{10^{-5} \times 25 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute required pressure gradient.**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} \times \dfrac{1}{?}
$$

Re-deriving cleanly:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{v_{\min}\, \mu_w}{k_{rw}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} = 1.67 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{Pa/m}
$$

> **Note:** The very small gradient reflects the need to exceed capillary forces only marginally; in field-scale practice, gradients of 10–100 kPa/m are common to compensate for heterogeneity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Entrapment Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir Pore Network] --> B{Pore Throat Geometry}
    A --> C{Mineral Surface Chemistry}
    A --> D{Fluid Viscosity Contrast}

    B --> B1[Capillary Jamin Snap-off]
    B --> B2[Geometric Stranding]

    C --> C1[Adsorptive Film Formation]
    C --> C2[Wettability Alteration]
    C2 --> C3[Clay Mediated Binding]

    D --> D1[Viscous Fingering]
    D --> D2[Mobility Ratio M greater than 1]

    B1 --> E[Oil Ganglion Formation]
    B2 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E

    E --> F[Residual Oil Saturation Sor]
    F --> G[Instrumentation Responses]
    G --> G1[Resistivity Log Decrease]
    G --> G2[NMR T2 Shift]
    G --> G3[Capillary Pressure Curve Hysteresis]
    G --> G4[Distributed Fiber Optic DAS]

    G1 --> H[Reservoir Model Update]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[EOR Process Control Decision]
```

### 4.2 Inter-Matrix Mapping: Mechanism → Controlling Parameter → Sensor Type

| Mechanism Block | Dominant Physical Parameter | Typical KTU-relevant Sensor |
|-----------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Capillary Jamin | Interfacial tension $\sigma_{ow}$ | Spinning-drop tensiometer |
| Adsorptive Film | Contact angle $\theta$ | Wilhelmy plate / Sessile drop |
| Geometric Stranding | Pore-throat radius $r$ | Mercury injection capillary pressure (MICP) |
| Viscous Fingering | Mobility ratio $M$ | Differential pressure transducer |
| Clay Binding | Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC) | XRD / SEM-EDS on cuttings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow represents a causal flow. The convergence at the **Oil Ganglion Formation** block is the conceptual heart of the topic — every entrapment mechanism ultimately produces the same observable: an *isolated oil ganglion* that downstream instrumentation must detect and quantify.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define Jamin effect and state the equation governing the snap-off of an oil droplet in a pore throat.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Jamin effect** is the resistance to flow offered by alternating oil and water slugs in a capillary due to differential deformation of the oil–water interface at the pore throat. Snap-off occurs when the local capillary pressure is exceeded by the viscous pressure drop. The governing equation is:

$$
P_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{ow} \cos\theta}{r}
$$

where $\sigma_{ow}$ is oil–water interfacial tension (N/m), $\theta$ is the contact angle through the water phase (degrees), and $r$ is the pore-throat radius (m). **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for unit/symbol clarity.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
*List any three mechanisms of oil entrapment in reservoir rock and state the physical parameter that controls each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Capillary (Jamin) entrapment** — controlled by interfacial tension $\sigma_{ow}$ and pore-throat radius $r$. **(1 mark)**
2. **Adsorptive / wettability entrapment** — controlled by contact angle $\theta$ and surface chemistry. **(1 mark)**
3. **Viscous entrapment** — controlled by the mobility ratio $M$ and capillary number $N_c$. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the Young–Laplace capillary pressure equation for an oil–water meniscus in a cylindrical pore throat. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water-wet sandstone has an average pore-throat radius of $4 \,\mu$m, oil–water interfacial tension of $28$ mN/m, and a contact angle of $45^\circ$. Compute (i) the capillary pressure, and (ii) the minimum Darcy velocity of water required to mobilize the trapped oil ganglion, given $\mu_w = 1.0$ cP and $N_{c,\text{crit}} = 10^{-5}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
- State Young–Laplace: $P_c = \sigma(1/R_1 + 1/R_2)$. **(1 mark)**
- Assume spherical interface in cylindrical pore: $R_1 = R_2 = R$. **(1 mark)**
- Substituting gives $P_c = 2\sigma/R$. **(1 mark)**
- Geometric relation $R = r/\cos\theta$. **(1 mark)**
- Final result: $P_c = 2\sigma_{ow}\cos\theta/r$. **(1 mark)**
- Assumptions: (i) quasi-static interface, (ii) uniform pore geometry, (iii) no-slip at the wall, (iv) Newtonian fluids. **(2 marks — 0.5 each)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**
**(i) Capillary pressure:**

$$
P_c = \dfrac{2 \times 28 \times 10^{-3} \times \cos 45^\circ}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.028 \times 0.7071}{4 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
P_c = \dfrac{0.03960}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 9{,}900 \ \text{Pa} \approx 9.9 \ \text{kPa}
$$

**(1.5 marks for substitution, 1 mark for numerical answer, 0.5 mark for unit.)**

**(ii) Minimum Darcy velocity:**

$$
v_{\min} = \dfrac{N_{c,\text{crit}} \cdot \sigma_{ow}}{\mu_w} = \dfrac{10^{-5} \times 28 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.8 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m/s}
$$

**(2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for numerical answer, 0.5 mark for unit.)**

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The capillary pressure of **9.9 kPa** must be exceeded to mobilize the oil ganglion, requiring a Darcy velocity of at least **$2.8 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s**.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **five mechanisms of oil entrapment** in a reservoir. State the role of wettability in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Amott-Harvey wettability index**. A core sample shows $\Delta S_{w1} = 0.18$, $\Delta S_{o1} = 0.05$, $\Delta S_{o2} = 0.10$, $\Delta S_{w2} = 0.22$. Compute the index and interpret the wettability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five mechanisms (7 marks — 1 mark each for naming, 0.4 for wettability role):**
1. **Capillary (Jamin) snap-off** — wettability sets the sign of $\cos\theta$ and the magnitude of $P_c$.
2. **Adsorptive film entrapment** — wettability controls film thickness via $\cos\theta$.
3. **Geometric stranding** — wettability influences the *drainage* vs. *imbibition* curve.
4. **Viscous entrapment** — wettability alters relative permeability curves $k_{rw}$ and $k_{ro}$.
5. **Clay-mediated binding** — wettability is the macroscopic manifestation of clay polarity.

**(b) Amott–Harvey Index (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**

$$
I_{AH} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta S_{w1}}{\Delta S_{w1} + \Delta S_{o1}} + \dfrac{\Delta S_{o2}}{\Delta S_{o2} + \Delta S_{w2}}\right)
$$

where $\Delta S_{w1}$ is spontaneous imbibition of water, $\Delta S_{o1}$ is forced imbibition of water, $\Delta S_{o2}$ is spontaneous drainage of oil, and $\Delta S_{w2}$ is forced drainage of oil.

**Substitution (2.5 marks):**

$$
I_{AH} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{0.18}{0.18+0.05} + \dfrac{0.10}{0.10+0.22}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{0.18}{0.23} + \dfrac{0.10}{0.32}\right)
$$

$$
I_{AH} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(0.7826 + 0.3125\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1.0951\right) = 0.5476
$$

**Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
- $I_{AH} > 0$ ⇒ **water-wet**.
- $I_{AH} = 0.5476$ ⇒ **strongly water-wet** (range $+0.3$ to $+1.0$).
- Engineering implication: chemical EOR with surfactants is favored, and waterflooding is efficient. **(1 mark for engineering link.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in the Amott–Harvey equation is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in valuation. Always write the equation fully before substituting.
> 2. **Sign convention mistakes** in capillary pressure (oil-wet giving $P_c < 0$) cost 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Unit omission** in numerical answers costs 0.5 mark per sub-part. Always write Pa, mN/m, m/s, etc.
> 4. **Confusing the Leverett J-function with the Jamin effect** — they sound identical but are completely different (J-function is dimensionless scaling; Jamin is the snap-off phenomenon).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Entrapment ≠ Trapping Permanence** — oil ganglia can be remobilized if $N_c$ exceeds a critical threshold (~$10^{-5}$ water-wet, ~$10^{-3}$ oil-wet).
- The **capillary pressure equation** $P_c = 2\sigma\cos\theta/r$ is the single most important formula; memorize it with units.
- The **Leverett J-function** is the dimensionless scaling tool used to compare $P_c$ curves across heterogeneous rocks.
- **Wettability** is the master variable: it enters $\cos\theta$, $k_{ro}$, $k_{rw}$, and the Amott–Harvey index.
- **Mobility ratio** $M$ dictates displacement stability; aim for $M \le 1$ in injection design.
- **Five mechanisms** to recall in any descriptive answer: Jamin (capillary), Adsorptive, Geometric, Viscous, Clay-mediated.
- **Instrumentation link:** resistivity logs, NMR, MICP, and DAS/DTS fiber-optic sensors all *measure the consequence* of these mechanisms — the question in KTU papers is often to link mechanism to sensor.
- **Always** state the boundary condition (water-wet vs. oil-wet) before writing a wettability-based answer.
- **Sorption order** in clay binding: kaolinite < illite < montmorillonite (low to high CEC, hence stronger oil binding).
- **Units quick-reference:** $\sigma$ in mN/m, $\mu$ in cP, $k$ in mD, $r$ in $\mu$m or m, $P_c$ in Pa or kPa, $N_c$ dimensionless.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refinery Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Refinery Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Refinery Products** are the purified, value-added hydrocarbon fractions obtained by processing **crude petroleum oil** through a series of physical separation (distillation) and chemical conversion (cracking, reforming, alkylation, isomerization, hydrotreating) operations in an oil refinery. These products are classified according to their **boiling range**, **carbon number distribution**, **molecular structure**, and end-use application.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme (PEEIT861 – Module 1), refinery products are categorized into four broad families:

1. **Gaseous products** (LPG – Liquefied Petroleum Gas, refinery off-gases).
2. **Light distillates** (Naphtha, Gasoline/Petrol).
3. **Middle distillates** (Kerosene, Aviation Turbine Fuel – ATF, Diesel, Light Fuel Oil).
4. **Heavy distillates & residues** (Heavy Fuel Oil, Lubricating oils, Waxes, Bitumen/Asphalt, Petroleum Coke).

Each fraction corresponds to a specific **carbon number (C₁ to C₅₀+)** and **boiling point window**, enabling downstream petrochemical and energy applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1.1):** Students must be able to identify the boiling range, typical carbon number, and primary application of at least the **eight major refinery products** — LPG, Naphtha, Gasoline, Kerosene/ATF, Diesel, Fuel Oil, Lubricating Oil, and Bitumen.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **crude oil as a large bowl of mixed fruit juice** containing strawberries, oranges, grapes, and mangoes all crushed together. The fruit pieces (hydrocarbon molecules of different sizes) cannot be separated by simply pouring them out — they must be sorted by **size and volatility** (how easily each evaporates).

A refinery is essentially a **giant industrial "juice sorter"**:
- **Heat** the crude oil gently in a distillation column (like warming the juice to release different vapors).
- **Lighter, smaller molecules** (LPG, gasoline) rise to the top of the column and condense at cooler trays.
- **Heavier, larger molecules** (diesel, fuel oil) stay lower in the column.
- **Chemical converters** (crackers, reformers) act like a "blender" that breaks oversized molecules into medium-sized ones to maximize valuable output.

The end products are the cleanly separated, purified streams that fuel cars, planes, stoves, and power plants — or serve as feedstocks for petrochemicals (plastics, fertilizers, synthetic rubber).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Approximately **1 barrel (≈ 159 L) of crude oil** yields:
> - **~ 45% gasoline**
> - **~ 25% diesel & heating oil**
> - **~ 9% kerosene/jet fuel**
> - **~ 5% LPG**
> - **~ 6% heavy fuel oil**
> - **~ 10% other products** (lubricants, asphalt, petrochemicals)

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The following standard units are universally used to characterize refinery products:

- **API Gravity (°API):** Measure of density; higher value ⇒ lighter liquid.
- **Specific Gravity (S):** Ratio of liquid density to water density at 60 °F.
- **Sulfur Content (wt% or ppm):** Critical for environmental compliance (Euro VI, BS-VI norms).
- **Octane Number (RON/MON):** Knock resistance of gasoline.
- **Cetane Number (CN):** Ignition quality of diesel.
- **Flash Point (°C):** Lowest temperature at which vapors ignite.
- **Pour Point (°C):** Lowest temperature at which liquid flows.
- **Viscosity (cSt @ 40 °C or 100 °C):** Flow resistance.
- **Calorific Value (MJ/kg or BTU/lb):** Energy content on combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> The **API-to-Specific Gravity** relation is given by:
> $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{S} - 131.5$$
> where **S** is the specific gravity at 60 °F (15.6 °C). Lower S ⇒ higher °API ⇒ lighter crude/product.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic – The "Why" Behind the Fractions

Crude oil is a complex mixture of **alkanes (paraffins), cycloalkanes (naphthenes), aromatics, and asphaltic/resinic compounds**, with carbon numbers ranging from **C₁ (methane)** to **C₅₀⁺ (asphaltenes)**. The refinery exploits the principle that **boiling point increases with carbon number and molecular weight**, enabling fractionation in a distillation column.

### 2.1.1 Sequential Fractions (Top → Bottom of Atmospheric Distillation Column)

| # | Cut Name | Carbon No. | Boiling Range (°C) | Primary Use |
|---|----------|------------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Refinery Gas (C₁–C₂) | C1–C2 | < 0 | Refinery fuel gas |
| 2 | LPG (C₃–C₄) | C3–C4 | –42 to +1 | Domestic/cooking fuel, autogas |
| 3 | Light Naphtha | C5–C6 | 30 – 90 | Petrochemical feedstock |
| 4 | Heavy Naphtha | C6–C10 | 90 – 200 | Gasoline blending, reformer feed |
| 5 | Gasoline (Motor Spirit) | C5–C11 | 40 – 200 | Automotive fuel |
| 6 | Kerosene / ATF | C10–C16 | 150 – 275 | Domestic fuel, jet fuel |
| 7 | Diesel (HSD / Gasoil) | C12–C20 | 200 – 370 | Automotive diesel, LDO |
| 8 | Light Fuel Oil (LFO) | C14–C20 | 250 – 380 | Industrial burner fuel |
| 9 | Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) | C20–C50 | 350 – 600+ | Marine fuel, power plants |
| 10 | Vacuum Residue | C40+ | > 565 (under vacuum) | Bitumen, coke, lubes |
| 11 | Bitumen / Asphalt | C30+ | Solid at 25 °C | Road construction, roofing |
| 12 | Petroleum Coke | C70+ | Solid | Electrodes, fuel |

## 2.2 The "How" — Production Pathways

### 2.2.1 Atmospheric Distillation (Primary Fractionation)
- Crude preheated to **~ 370 °C** in a furnace, then flashed into the **Atmospheric Distillation Column (ADC)**.
- Products withdrawn as **overhead vapor (Naphtha + gases)**, **side draws (kerosene, diesel)**, and **bottom residue (Reduced Crude Oil – RCO)**.

### 2.2.2 Vacuum Distillation
- Bottom RCO is fed to a **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)** at **30 – 50 mmHg absolute** to avoid thermal cracking.
- Yields **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)** and **Vacuum Residue (VR)**.

### 2.2.3 Conversion Processes
- **Catalytic Cracking (FCC – Fluid Catalytic Cracking):** Converts heavy VGO into gasoline + LPG.
- **Hydrocracking:** H₂ + catalyst ⇒ high-quality diesel/jet fuel.
- **Steam Cracking:** Produces **olefins** (ethylene, propylene) for petrochemicals.
- **Catalytic Reforming:** Converts low-octane naphthenes to **high-octane aromatics** (benzene, toluene, xylene).
- **Alkylation:** C₃/C₄ olefins + isobutane ⇒ high-octane alkylate gasoline.
- **Isomerization:** Straight-chain C₅/C₆ ⇒ branched isomers (higher octane).
- **Hydrotreating (HDS – Hydrodesulfurization):** Removes **sulfur, nitrogen, metals** to meet BS-VI norms.

### 2.2.4 Blending
Final products are blended from multiple streams in **on-line blend headers** to meet specifications (e.g., RON 91 for petrol, CN 51 for diesel).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{S} - 131.5$ | API gravity ↔ Specific gravity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\rho_{15.6^\circ C} = S \times 999 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Liquid density from S | kg/m³ |
| 3 | $\text{Vol\%} = \dfrac{100 \times V_{\text{product}}}{V_{\text{crude}}}$ | Volume yield of a fraction | % |
| 4 | $\text{Wt\%} = \dfrac{100 \times m_{\text{product}}}{m_{\text{crude}}}$ | Mass yield of a fraction | % |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\text{Moles of H}_2 \text{ formed}}{\text{Moles of oil fed}}$ | Hydrogen balance in hydrocracker | – |
| 6 | $E_f = \dfrac{\text{Observed conversion}}{\text{Equilibrium conversion}}$ | Catalyst efficiency factor | – |
| 7 | $\text{Carbon number} \approx \dfrac{T_b\,(^{\circ}C) + 200}{30}$ | Approx. Cn from boiling point | – |
| 8 | $CV\,(\text{MJ/kg}) = 51.91 - 0.00455\,\rho_{15} - 0.0095\,S_{wt}$ | Net calorific value (crude) | MJ/kg |
| 9 | $\text{RVP} \approx 0.18\,T_{10\%} - 0.005\,(T_{50\%})^2$ | Reid Vapor Pressure (gasoline) | psi |
| 10 | $\text{TBP}_{\text{ASTM}} \Rightarrow \text{EFV}$ via empirical correlation | Distillation curve conversion | °C |
| 11 | $\text{Loss\%} = \dfrac{W_{\text{feed}} - (W_{\text{distillate}} + W_{\text{bottom}})}{W_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | ASTM D86 distillation loss | % |
| 12 | $L = \dfrac{V}{D}$ | Reflux ratio (L) in distillation | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions (Fractions):**
> - **Gasoline:** RON ≥ 91, S ≤ 10 ppm (BS-VI), RVP ≤ 0.6 bar.
> - **Diesel (HSD):** CN ≥ 51, S ≤ 10 ppm, Flash Point ≥ 38 °C, 95% recovery at 360 °C.
> - **ATF (Jet A-1):** Flash Point ≥ 38 °C, Freezing Point ≤ –47 °C, Aromatics ≤ 25%.
> - **LPG:** 100% vaporizes at 40 °C, propane/propylene-rich, vapor pressure ≤ 1430 kPa @ 40 °C.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery economics** depend on maximizing yields of **transportation fuels** (gasoline, diesel, jet) because they command the highest price per barrel.
- **Instrumentation relevance:** Product quality is monitored using online analyzers — **NIR (Near-Infrared) for RON/CN**, **XRF for sulfur**, **density meters**, **flash point testers**, and **ASTM D86 distillation rigs**.
- **Control loops** in blending ensure **RON, RVP, distillation 90% point** remain within tight specifications, where every **1 octane deviation** can change revenue by **$0.5 – 2 per barrel**.
- **Sustainability:** Modern refineries integrate **bio-feed co-processing** and **carbon capture** to produce "low-carbon" refinery products.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — API Gravity of a Product Given Its Specific Gravity

**Given:** Specific gravity of a kerosene sample **S = 0.820** at 60 °F (15.6 °C).
**Find:** API gravity and density in kg/m³.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the relation between API gravity and specific gravity.

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{S} - 131.5$$

**Step 2:** Substitute S = 0.820.

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.820} - 131.5$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the division 141.5 / 0.820.

$$\frac{141.5}{0.820} = 172.5609...$$

**Step 4:** Subtract 131.5.

$$^{\circ}\text{API} = 172.5609 - 131.5 = 41.0609$$

**Step 5:** Round to typical industry precision (1 decimal place).

$$^{\circ}\text{API} \approx 41.1$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the density at 15.6 °C using water density ≈ 999 kg/m³.

$$\rho = S \times 999 = 0.820 \times 999 = 819.18 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 7:** Final answers.

$$\boxed{{^{\circ}\text{API} \approx 41.1, \quad \rho \approx 819.2 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

> **Interpretation:** A kerosene with °API ≈ 41 is typical for aviation turbine fuel, confirming it is a **light middle-distillate fraction**.

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Volume Yield Calculation of a Refinery Product

**Given:** A refinery processes **100,000 barrels per day (BPD)** of crude oil. The diesel fraction recovered is **28,500 BPD**.
**Find:** Volume % yield of diesel.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Use the volume % formula.

$$\text{Vol\%} = \frac{100 \times V_{\text{product}}}{V_{\text{crude}}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$\text{Vol\%} = \frac{100 \times 28{,}500}{100{,}000}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.

$$\text{Vol\%} = 28.5\%$$

**Step 4:** Final answer.

$$\boxed{\text{Diesel yield} = 28.5\text{ vol\%}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A diesel yield of ~28% is **typical for a mid-sized refinery optimized for middle distillates**, especially in markets with high diesel demand (e.g., India's transportation sector).

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Calorific Value of a Crude Sample

**Given:** A crude oil has density $\rho_{15} = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and sulfur content **S_wt = 1.5 wt%**.
**Find:** Net calorific value (CV) in MJ/kg using:

$$CV = 51.91 - 0.00455\,\rho_{15} - 0.0095\,S_{wt}$$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Substitute $\rho_{15} = 870$ kg/m³ and $S_{wt} = 1.5$.

$$CV = 51.91 - 0.00455 \times 870 - 0.0095 \times 1.5$$

**Step 2:** Compute term 1.

$$0.00455 \times 870 = 3.9585$$

**Step 3:** Compute term 2.

$$0.0095 \times 1.5 = 0.01425$$

**Step 4:** Subtract both terms from 51.91.

$$CV = 51.91 - 3.9585 - 0.01425 = 47.93725$$

**Step 5:** Round to 2 decimals.

$$\boxed{CV \approx 47.94 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A calorific value of ~48 MJ/kg is **standard for medium-sour crude oil** (cf. ~42 MJ/kg for diesel, ~46 MJ/kg for gasoline), confirming the formula's validity.

## 3.4 Problem 4 — ASTM D86 Distillation Loss Analysis

**Given:** ASTM D86 distillation of a gasoline sample:
- Charge = 100 mL
- Distillate recovered = 97.5 mL
- Bottom residue = 2.0 mL

**Find:** Distillation loss %.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply loss formula.

$$\text{Loss\%} = \frac{W_{\text{feed}} - (W_{\text{distillate}} + W_{\text{bottom}})}{W_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$\text{Loss\%} = \frac{100 - (97.5 + 2.0)}{100} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Sum the right side numerator.

$$\text{Loss\%} = \frac{100 - 99.5}{100} \times 100 = \frac{0.5}{100} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$\text{Loss\%} = 0.5\%$$

**Step 5:** Final answer.

$$\boxed{\text{Distillation loss} = 0.5\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** ASTM D86 specifications require **loss < 1.5% for gasoline** and **< 1.0% for kerosene/ATF**. A loss of 0.5% confirms **excellent fractionation quality** and minimal carryover of light ends.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Key Refinery Products

| Property | LPG | Gasoline | Kerosene (ATF) | Diesel (HSD) | Fuel Oil (HFO) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Boiling range (°C) | –42 to +1 | 40 – 200 | 150 – 275 | 200 – 370 | 350 – 600+ |
| Carbon number | C3–C4 | C5–C11 | C10–C16 | C12–C20 | C20–C50+ |
| °API (typical) | — | 55 – 70 | 40 – 50 | 30 – 40 | 7 – 22 |
| Flash point (°C) | < –50 | –43 | ≥ 38 | ≥ 38 | ≥ 60 |
| Key quality index | Vapor pressure | RON ≥ 91 | Aromatics ≤ 25% | Cetane ≥ 51 | Viscosity ≤ 380 cSt @ 50 °C |
| Density (kg/m³) | Gas | 720 – 780 | 780 – 840 | 830 – 880 | 940 – 1010 |
| Primary test | ASTM D1267 | ASTM D86, D2699 | ASTM D86, D1319 | ASTM D86, D613 | ASTM D445, D1298 |
| Sulfur limit (BS-VI) | — | ≤ 10 ppm | ≤ 30 ppm | ≤ 10 ppm | ≤ 3.5 wt% (FO for power) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Crude-to-Product Refinery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    crudeoil[A1 CRUDE OIL STORAGE TANK] --> desalination[A2 DESALTING UNIT]
    desalination --> furnace[A3 CRUDE FURNACE 370 C]
    furnace --> adc[A4 ATMOSPHERIC DISTILLATION COLUMN]
    adc --> ovhd[A5 OVERHEAD VAPOUR NAPHTHA AND GASES]
    ovhd --> degasser[A6 NAPHTHA STABILISER DEGASSER]
    degasser --> lpgA[A7 LPG FRACTION C3 C4]
    degasser --> naphthaA[A8 NAPHTHA C5 C10]
    adc --> keroDraw[A9 KEROSENE SIDE DRAW 150 275 C]
    adc --> dieselDraw[A10 DIESEL SIDE DRAW 200 370 C]
    adc --> rco[A11 REDUCED CRUDE OIL BOTTOM]
    rco --> vdu[A12 VACUUM DISTILLATION UNIT]
    vdu --> vgo[A13 VACUUM GAS OIL VGO]
    rco --> residueA[A14 VACUUM RESIDUE]
    vgo --> fcc[A15 FCC UNIT CATALYTIC CRACKER]
    fcc --> gasolineA[A16 GASOLINE POOL]
    fcc --> lpgFCC[A17 LPG FROM FCC]
    naphthaA --> reformer[A18 CATALYTIC REFORMER]
    reformer --> gasolineA
    reformer --> aromatics[A19 BTX AROMATICS PETROCHEM]
    naphthaA --> isomer[A20 ISOMERISATION UNIT]
    isomer --> gasolineA
    gasolineA --> blender[A21 GASOLINE BLENDING HEADER]
    blender --> tankfarmA[A22 GASOLINE PRODUCT TANK]
    dieselDraw --> hds[A23 HDS HYDRODESULFURISATION]
    hds --> tankfarmB[A24 DIESEL PRODUCT TANK]
    keroDraw --> tankfarmC[A25 KEROSENE ATF TANK]
    residueA --> bitumen[A26 BITUMEN AIR BLOWING]
    residueA --> coker[A27 DELAYED COKER]
    coker --> petcoke[A28 PETROLEUM COKE]
    lpgA --> lpgStorage[A29 LPG STORAGE BULLET]
    lpgFCC --> lpgStorage
    lpgStorage --> bottling[A30 LPG CYLINDER BOTTLING]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Product Specification & Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    crudeStream[AA1 CRUDE INLET] --> distCol[AA2 DISTILLATION COLUMN]
    distCol --> sideDraw[AA3 SIDE DRAW OFF TAKE]
    sideDraw --> inlAnalyzer[AA4 ONLINE NIR ANALYSER]
    inlAnalyzer --> dcs[AA5 DCS CONTROLLER]
    dcs --> valve[AA6 PRODUCT DRAW VALVE]
    dcs --> refluxPump[AA7 REFLUX PUMP VFD]
    inlAnalyzer --> octaneSensor[AA8 RON MON PREDICTED]
    inlAnalyzer --> cetaneSensor[AA9 CETANE NUMBER PREDICTED]
    inlAnalyzer --> sulfurSensor[AA10 XRF SULFUR PPM]
    inlAnalyzer --> densityMeter[AA11 DENSITY METER API]
    octaneSensor --> specCheck[AA12 SPEC CHECK LOGIC]
    sulfurSensor --> specCheck
    specCheck --> alarmHH[AA13 HIGH HIGH ALARM TO OPERATOR]
    specCheck --> trimValve[AA14 BLENDING TRIM VALVE]
    trimValve --> productTank[AA15 FINAL PRODUCT TANK]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Conversion Process Mapping (Block-Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedNaphtha[B1 NAPHTHA FEED C5 C10] --> reformerB[B2 CATALYTIC REFORMER]
    reformerB --> reformerOut[B3 HIGH OCTANE REFORMATE RON 98]
    feedNaphtha --> isoUnit[B4 ISOMERISATION UNIT]
    isoUnit --> isomerate[B5 ISOMERATE RON 88]
    feedVGO[B6 VGO FEED C20 C50] --> fccUnit[B7 FCC UNIT]
    fccUnit --> gasolineFCC[B8 FCC GASOLINE RON 92]
    fccUnit --> lpgFCC2[B9 LPG C3 C4]
    feedVGO --> hydrocrack[B10 HYDROCRACKER]
    hydrocrack --> jetFuel[B11 JET FUEL KEROSENE]
    hydrocrack --> dieselHC[B12 ULTRA LOW SULFUR DIESEL]
    feedResid[B13 VACUUM RESIDUE] --> cokerB[B14 DELAYED COKER]
    cokerB --> greenCoke[B15 GREEN PETROLEUM COKE]
    feedResid --> bitumenB[B16 BITUMEN BLOWING]
    bitumenB --> asphalt[B17 ROAD GRADE ASPHALT]
    reformerOut --> blendB[B18 GASOLINE BLEND HEADER]
    isomerate --> blendB
    gasolineFCC --> blendB
    blendB --> product[B19 RON 91 95 GASOLINE TANK]
    jetFuel --> atfTank[B20 ATF TANK]
    dieselHC --> hsdTank[B21 HSD TANK]
    lpgFCC2 --> lpgTank[B22 LPG BULLET]
    greenCoke --> calciner[B23 CALCINER]
    calciner --> anodeCoke[B24 ANODE GRADE COKE]
```

> **Reading Tip:** Each block above represents a process unit in a real refinery. The arrows denote **mass flow streams**; the DCS/Analyzer block in Section 4.2 shows how **online instrumentation** (NIR, XRF, density) is used in **closed-loop control** to maintain product specifications.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List any **four major refinery products** with their typical **boiling range** and **carbon number** range.

**Model Answer (4 marks layout):**

1. **LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas):** Boiling range **–42 °C to +1 °C**, Carbon number **C3 – C4**. Used as domestic cooking fuel and automotive LPG.
2. **Naphtha:** Boiling range **30 – 200 °C**, Carbon number **C5 – C10**. Used as petrochemical feedstock (steam cracker) and gasoline blending stock.
3. **Kerosene / Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF):** Boiling range **150 – 275 °C**, Carbon number **C10 – C16**. Used as jet fuel and domestic illuminating fuel.
4. **Diesel (High-Speed Diesel – HSD):** Boiling range **200 – 370 °C**, Carbon number **C12 – C20**. Used as automotive diesel engine fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Product name (0.5) + Boiling range (0.5) + Carbon no. (0.5) + Use (0.5) = 2 marks per product × 4 = 8 marks but constrained to 3-mark question ⇒ student should pick 4 products but write briefly; here full credit is given for any 4 correctly listed with one-line description.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between **atmospheric distillation** and **vacuum distillation** of crude oil.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Atmospheric Distillation | Vacuum Distillation |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure | 1 atm (101.3 kPa) | 30 – 50 mmHg (vacuum) |
| Max bottom temp | ~ 370 °C | ~ 400 °C equivalent |
| Feed | Desalted crude | RCO (Reduced Crude Oil) |
| Products | LPG, Naphtha, Kero, Diesel, RCO | VGO, Vacuum Residue |
| Purpose | Initial primary fractionation | Prevent thermal cracking of heavy residue |

> **Mark split:** Definition of each (1) + distinguishing features (1) + key product difference (1) = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either Question A or Question B** as the internal choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

#### **Q.A.(a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024] With a neat **schematic flow diagram**, explain the **atmospheric distillation** of crude oil. List the **main products** obtained with their **boiling ranges**.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Schematic Flow:** (Ref. Section 4.1 — Atmospheric Distillation Block)

**Process Steps:**

1. **Desalting:** Crude oil is heated to **120 °C** and mixed with water to remove salts (NaCl, CaCl₂) — corrosion prevention.
2. **Preheating Train:** Crude passes through **heat exchangers** using hot product streams (kerosene, diesel) to recover heat.
3. **Crude Furnace:** Final heating to **~ 370 °C** in a fired heater.
4. **Flash Zone Injection:** Hot crude enters ADC at the **flash zone**; vapors rise, liquids fall.
5. **Fractionation:** Reflux and side draws withdraw fractions at appropriate trays:
   - **Overhead (≤ 200 °C):** Naphtha + Gases
   - **Side Draw 1 (150 – 275 °C):** Kerosene
   - **Side Draw 2 (200 – 370 °C):** Diesel
   - **Side Draw 3 (250 – 380 °C):** Light Gas Oil
   - **Bottom (> 370 °C):** Reduced Crude Oil (RCO)

**2. Main Products & Boiling Ranges:**

| Product | Boiling Range (°C) |
|---|---|
| Refinery Gas | < 0 |
| LPG | –42 to +1 |
| Naphtha | 30 – 200 |
| Kerosene | 150 – 275 |
| Diesel | 200 – 370 |
| LGO (Light Gas Oil) | 250 – 380 |
| RCO (Residue) | > 370 |

> **Mark split:** Schematic (3 marks) + process steps (2 marks) + product table (2 marks) = 7 marks.

#### **Q.A.(b)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Explain the **catalytic cracking (FCC)** process. Discuss the **products** obtained and how they differ from those of **steam cracking**.

**Model Answer:**

**1. FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking):**
- Feed: VGO (Vacuum Gas Oil), C20 – C50, boiling 370 – 570 °C.
- Catalyst: **Zeolite-based (ZSM-5, USY)** in finely divided powder form.
- Reactor: **Riser reactor** at **500 – 540 °C**, near atmospheric pressure.
- Regenerator: Coke is burned off catalyst with air, catalyst is recycled.

**2. Reactions (carbonium ion mechanism):**
- **Cracking:** C–C bond cleavage of heavy paraffins ⇒ smaller olefins + paraffins.
- **Isomerization:** Normal olefins ⇒ iso-olefins.
- **Aromatic alkylation & dehydrogenation** form gasoline-range aromatics.

**3. FCC Products (typical yields):**
- **Gasoline (40–45 vol%):** RON 88 – 92 (high-octane due to branched alkanes & aromatics).
- **LPG (15–20 vol%):** C3 / C4 olefins-rich ⇒ alkylation feedstock.
- **Light Cycle Oil (LCO, 20 vol%):** Blended into diesel.
- **Heavy Cycle Oil (HCO):** Recycle to extinction or fuel oil.
- **Coke (5 wt%):** Burned in regenerator to supply process heat.

**4. Difference from Steam Cracking:**

| Feature | FCC | Steam Cracking |
|---|---|---|
| Feed | VGO, atmospheric gasoil | Naphtha, ethane, propane |
| Mechanism | Catalytic (zeolite) | Thermal (free radical) |
| Temperature | 500 – 540 °C | 750 – 900 °C |
| Pressure | ~ 1 atm | Slight vacuum |
| Diluent | None (catalyst) | Steam (1:0.3 to 1:1) |
| Primary products | Gasoline + LPG | Ethylene, propylene, butadiene (olefins) |
| Application | Fuel production | Petrochemical feedstock |

> **Mark split:** FCC process explanation (3) + products (2) + comparison with steam cracking (2) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

#### **Q.B.(a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Discuss the **various conversion processes** used in a refinery. With a **flow diagram**, show how **naphtha** is converted to **high-octane gasoline**.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Conversion Processes:**

- **Catalytic Cracking (FCC):** Heavy VGO → Gasoline + LPG (high RON).
- **Hydrocracking:** Heavy feed + H₂ → Jet fuel, diesel (low S, high CN).
- **Catalytic Reforming:** Low-octane naphtha → High-octane reformate (RON 96 – 100).
- **Isomerization:** n-C5/n-C6 → iso-C5/iso-C6 (RON boost by 10 – 12).
- **Alkylation:** C3/C4 olefins + isobutane → Alkylate (RON 92 – 96).
- **Hydrotreating (HDS, HDN, HDM):** Removal of S, N, metals using H₂.

**2. Naphtha-to-Gasoline Flow Diagram:**

> (Ref. Section 4.1: Naphtha → Isomerisation Unit + Catalytic Reformer → Gasoline Blending)

**Pathways:**
- **Heavy Naphtha (C7–C10) → Reformer → Reformate (RON 96+) → Gasoline Pool.**
- **Light Naphtha (C5–C6) → Isomerization Unit → Isomerate (RON 85–88) → Gasoline Pool.**

**3. Reactions in Reformer:**
- Naphthenes → Aromatics (dehydrogenation): $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12} \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + 3\text{H}_2$
- Paraffins → Isoparaffins (rearrangement).
- Paraffins → Aromatics (dehydrocyclization): $\text{n-C}_7\text{H}_{16} \rightarrow \text{C}_7\text{H}_8 + 4\text{H}_2$

> **Mark split:** Conversion processes overview (3) + naphtha flow diagram (2) + reformer reactions (2) = 7 marks.

#### **Q.B.(b)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Explain the **properties and specifications of gasoline and diesel** as per **BS-VI (Euro-VI equivalent)** norms. Highlight the role of **online analyzers** in ensuring these specifications.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Gasoline (BS-VI Equivalent):**

| Specification | Limit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Research Octane Number (RON) | ≥ 91 (regular), 95 (premium) | Knock resistance |
| Motor Octane Number (MON) | ≥ 81 – 85 | High-speed knock resistance |
| Sulfur content | ≤ 10 ppm (down from 50 ppm in BS-IV) | Emission control (SOx) |
| RVP (Reid Vapor Pressure) | 0.45 – 0.6 bar | Hot-start vs. vapor lock trade-off |
| Distillation 90% recovered | ≤ 210 °C | Engine warm-up behaviour |
| Aromatics | ≤ 35% | Reduce engine deposits |
| Olefins | ≤ 18% | Photoreactivity, ozone |
| Benzene | ≤ 1% | Carcinogen control |
| Lead | 0 g/L | Toxicity |
| Density | 720 – 775 kg/m³ | Energy content |

**2. Diesel (HSD – BS-VI):**

| Specification | Limit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Cetane Number (CN) | ≥ 51 | Ignition quality |
| Sulfur content | ≤ 10 ppm | Particulate emission |
| Flash Point | ≥ 38 °C | Safety |
| Pour Point | ≤ 6 °C (winter grade) | Cold flow |
| CFPP (Cold Filter Plugging Pt) | ≤ 6 °C | Operability |
| Lubricity (HFRR) | ≤ 460 µm | Pump wear |
| Distillation 95% recovery | ≤ 360 °C | Engine efficiency |
| Density | 820 – 860 kg/m³ | Energy per litre |
| PAH (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons) | ≤ 11% | Emission of PAHs |
| FAME content | ≤ 7% v/v (B7 blend) | Bio-diesel blending |

**3. Role of Online Analyzers:**

- **NIR (Near-Infrared) Analyzers** — Predict RON, MON, CN, density, distillation points in **< 60 seconds**.
- **XRF (X-Ray Fluorescence) Analyzers** — Measure **S, Pb, P, Cl** at ppm level in real time.
- **Density Meters (Coriolis/Gamma)** — Continuous density → °API, S calculations.
- **Gas Chromatographs (Process GC)** — Detailed hydrocarbon speciation (C1 – C12 split, aromatics, olefins).
- **RVP Analyzers (Mini-method)** — Inline Reid Vapor Pressure for gasoline.
- **Flash Point Analyzers (Continuously Closed Cup)** — At-line flash point for diesel/ATF.

**4. Control Strategy:**
- Analyzers feed **DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEM (DCS)** in real time.
- Closed-loop control of **blending header valves** trims stream ratios to maintain RON, CN, S, and 90% distillation targets.
- Any deviation triggers **HH (High-High) alarm** and **automatic diversion** to reprocessing.

> **Mark split:** Gasoline specs (2) + Diesel specs (2) + Online analyzers (2) + Control role (1) = 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TBP and ASTM D86 curves** — Many students use them interchangeably; in reality, ASTM D86 is **batch atmospheric** while TBP is **continuous equilibrium** and EFV is **flash vaporization**.
> 2. **Forgetting that kerosene and ATF are not identical** — ATF (Jet A-1) has stricter freeze point (–47 °C), aromatics (≤ 25%), and is the higher-grade version of kerosene.
> 3. **Wrong unit on °API** — Always report as "°API" not just a number; mixing it up with specific gravity loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Saying LPG boils at 1 °C** — Actually, **pure propane boils at –42 °C** and pure butane at –0.5 °C; LPG is a **mixture** of C3/C4.
> 5. **Skipping the role of HDS** — In BS-VI era, HDS is **mandatory** to achieve 10 ppm sulfur; missing it in flow diagrams costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Octane vs. Cetane confusion** — Octane applies to **gasoline (spark ignition)**, Cetane to **diesel (compression ignition)**. Reversing these is a guaranteed mark loser.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⛽ **Refinery products** are obtained by **physical separation (distillation)** followed by **chemical conversion (cracking, reforming, alkylation)** of crude oil.
- 🔢 **Carbon number ↔ Boiling point**: C1–C2 (gas), C3–C4 (LPG), C5–C10 (naphtha), C10–C16 (kero/ATF), C12–C20 (diesel), C20+ (fuel oil, bitumen).
- 🌡️ **Atmospheric distillation** gives light & middle distillates up to 370 °C; **vacuum distillation** processes the residue at < 50 mmHg to avoid cracking.
- 🔄 **Conversion units**: FCC (VGO → gasoline/LPG), Reformer (naphtha → high-octane reformate), Alkylation (C3/C4 → alkylate), Isomerization (n-C5/C6 → iso), Hydrocracker (heavy → diesel/jet), HDS (S removal).
- 🧪 **Key quality indices**: RON, MON, CN, Flash Point, Pour Point, RVP, °API, Sulfur ppm, Aromatics %.
- 📏 **°API = 141.5/S − 131.5**; **density = S × 999 kg/m³** at 15.6 °C.
- 🏭 **ASTM D86** distillation defines product cut points; **NIR/XRF** analyzers ensure **BS-VI** compliance in real time.
- 🟢 **BS-VI limits**: Gasoline RON ≥ 91, S ≤ 10 ppm; Diesel CN ≥ 51, S ≤ 10 ppm, Flash ≥ 38 °C, 95% recovery ≤ 360 °C.
- 📊 **Yields (typical Indian refinery)**: ~ 45% gasoline, ~ 25% diesel, ~ 9% kerosene/jet, ~ 5% LPG, ~ 16% others.
- 🛢️ **Bottom of the barrel**: Bitumen (road), Petroleum coke (electrodes/cement), Vacuum residue (further cracked).
- ⚙️ **Instrumentation is the brain**: Every product stream is monitored by **density meters, NIR, XRF, GC, flash-point analyzers** feeding the **DCS** for closed-loop control of product quality and refinery economics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refinery Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Refinery Steps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Refinery Steps** refer to the systematic, sequentially engineered sequence of physical separation, chemical conversion, and finishing operations employed in a petroleum refinery to transform **crude oil** (a complex, heterogeneous hydrocarbon mixture) into a spectrum of value-added commercial products such as **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)**, **gasoline (petrol)**, **kerosene**, **diesel**, **jet fuel**, **lubricating oils**, **bitumen**, and **petrochemical feedstocks (naphtha)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, refinery steps are classified under three umbrella categories:

1. **Physical Separation Processes** (e.g., Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation, Desalting).
2. **Chemical Conversion Processes** (e.g., Catalytic Cracking, Hydrocracking, Reforming, Alkylation, Coking).
3. **Finishing / Treatment Processes** (e.g., Hydrotreating, Sweetening, Blending).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must understand each refinery step in terms of (a) its **process objective**, (b) the **instrumentation loop** used to monitor and control it, and (c) the **typical control variables** (Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Level) and **analyzer variables** (sulfur content, octane number, RVP, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Refinery as a Kitchen" Analogy**
>
> Imagine crude oil as a **raw mixed fruit basket** (containing mangoes, grapes, apples, oranges, and berries all jumbled together). You cannot serve this directly as a "fruit salad" of a specific flavor. You must:
> 1. **Wash the fruits** (this is **Desalting** — removing impurities/salt).
> 2. **Sort them by size/weight** (this is **Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation** — separating hydrocarbons by boiling point/molecular weight).
> 3. **Cook/process the unsuitable ones** (this is **Conversion** — breaking heavy molecules into lighter gasoline-range ones, or rearranging molecules to improve quality).
> 4. **Polish/clean them** (this is **Treatment/Hydrotreating** — removing sulfur, nitrogen, and other contaminants).
> 5. **Mix the right proportions** to make a final dish (this is **Blending** — mixing refinery streams to meet market-grade product specifications).

The **instrumentation and control engineer** in this analogy is like the **master chef's assistant** who constantly checks the oven temperature (Temperature Transmitter), the gas flow (Flow Transmitter), the pressure inside the cooker (Pressure Transmitter), and the dish's doneness (Online Analyzers), and adjusts the knobs (Control Valves) accordingly — all coordinated through a **Digital Control System (DCS)**.

## 1.3 Crude Oil — The Feedstock Standard

- **API Gravity** of crude oil: typically **10° to 45°** (lower API = heavier crude, higher API = lighter crude).
- **Sulfur content**: classified as **Sweet** (< **0.5 wt%**) or **Sour** (> **0.5 wt%**).
- **Pour point, viscosity, and TAN (Total Acid Number)** are critical feed quality metrics monitored at the **crude receipt battery limit**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Refinery throughput is universally measured in **barrels per day (BPD)** where 1 barrel = **42 US gallons ≈ 159 liters**.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sequential Logic of Refinery Operations

A modern petroleum refinery follows a **strictly ordered** operational sequence. Each step's output becomes the next step's input. This is a **continuous-flow, mass-balance-driven** system.

### Step 1 — Crude Oil Receiving & Storage

- **Objective:** Receive crude from pipelines/tankers and store in **floating roof tanks** to prevent evaporation losses.
- **Instrumentation:** Level transmitters (servo, radar, guided wave radar), temperature probes, and **mass flow meters** (Coriolis) for custody transfer.
- **Control Loop:** Level control via pump speed; temperature control via heat tracing.

### Step 2 — Desalting (Atmospheric or Two-Stage)

- **Objective:** Remove dissolved salts (NaCl, MgCl₂, CaCl₂) and suspended solids (sand, rust) to prevent **fouling and corrosion** in downstream heat exchangers and furnace tubes.
- **Process:** Crude is mixed with **wash water** (5–10 vol%) and demulsifier, then subjected to a high-voltage **electrostatic field** (15–35 kV) inside a desalter vessel to coalesce water droplets.
- **Instrumentation:** Differential Pressure (dP) transmitter across the water/crude interface; conductivity analyzers for wash water quality; **BS&W (Basic Sediment & Water) analyzer**.

### Step 3 — Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU / Crude Distillation Tower - CDU)

- **Objective:** Separate crude into fractions by boiling point at **near-atmospheric pressure** (slightly above atmospheric, ~ 1.05–1.5 kg/cm²g).
- **Furnace:** The desalted crude is heated to **350–400 °C** in a **fired heater** (pipe still furnace).
- **Column Internals:** Sieve/valve trays (40–50 trays typical).
- **Side Draws (from top to bottom):**
  - **Overhead:** Unstabilized Naphtha (C₅–C₆)
  - **Side cut 1:** Kerosene/ATF (Aviation Turbine Fuel)
  - **Side cut 2:** Diesel (Light Gas Oil - LGO)
  - **Side cut 3:** Heavy Gas Oil (HGO) — feed to FCC/HC
  - **Bottom:** Atmospheric Residue / Reduced Crude Oil (RCO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instrumentation Critical Points in CDU:**
> - **Furnace outlet temperature** control (cascade to fuel gas flow) — prevents coking.
> - **Column top temperature & pressure** control (reflux drum).
> - **Side draw product specifications** controlled via online analyzers (e.g., ASTM D86 distillation cut points via NIR/FT-NIR analyzers).

### Step 4 — Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)

- **Objective:** Further separate **Atmospheric Residue** into heavy distillates (Lube base stocks, heavy gas oil feed) **without thermal cracking**.
- **Key Principle:** Operate at **very low absolute pressure** (10–50 mmHg) so that heavy hydrocarbons vaporize at temperatures below their cracking temperature (< 420 °C).
- **Vacuum Source:** Steam ejectors or liquid ring vacuum pumps.
- **Instrumentation:** Absolute pressure transmitters on the column top; steam ejector motive steam flow control; bottom level control.

### Step 5 — Conversion Processes

These **break, rearrange, or combine** hydrocarbon molecules to maximize high-value product yield.

#### 5.1 Catalytic Cracking (FCC — Fluid Catalytic Catalytic Cracking)
- **Objective:** Convert heavy gas oils (HGO/VGO) into gasoline and LPG using a **zeolite-based catalyst** (e.g., ZSM-5, USY) at **480–540 °C**.
- **Reactor-Regenerator System:** The catalyst is continuously circulated between the **riser reactor** and the **regenerator** (where coke is burned off with air).
- **Instrumentation:** Slide valve position control on spent/regenerated catalyst lines; oxygen analyzer on regenerator flue gas; cyclone differential pressure.

#### 5.2 Hydrocracking
- **Objective:** Convert heavy feeds into middle distillates (jet, diesel) and naphtha using **hydrogen + bifunctional catalyst** at high pressure (**70–200 bar**).
- **Advantage:** Produces **low-sulfur, high-cetane** products directly.

#### 5.3 Coking (Delayed Coking & Fluid Coking)
- **Objective:** Convert vacuum residue into **petroleum coke**, naphtha, and gas oil via **severe thermal cracking** at **480–510 °C**.
- **Delayed Coking:** Coke accumulates in drums cyclically (24-hour cycle); switched between two drums.

#### 5.4 Catalytic Reforming
- **Objective:** Convert low-octane naphtha into **high-octane reformate** (RON 95–100) by **dehydrocyclization, isomerization, and dehydrogenation** on **platinum-rhenium / platinum-tin catalyst** at **450–520 °C** and **5–30 bar** with hydrogen.
- **Critical Variable:** **Research Octane Number (RON)** — measured online via NIR analyzers.

#### 5.5 Alkylation
- **Objective:** Combine **C3–C4 olefins** (from FCC) with **isobutane** in the presence of **sulfuric acid** or **HF** catalyst to produce **alkylate** — a premium, high-octane gasoline blendstock (RON 92–96).

#### 5.6 Isomerization
- **Objective:** Convert **n-butane / n-pentane / n-hexane** into their **iso-isomers** to boost octane number.

### Step 6 — Treatment Processes (Hydrotreating / Desulfurization)

- **Objective:** Remove **sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, and metals** from product streams by reacting with **hydrogen** over **Co-Mo / Ni-Mo / Ni-W** catalysts.
- **Typical Operating Conditions:** 200–400 °C, 10–80 bar H₂ pressure.
- **Key Specification Variable:** **Sulfur content in ppm** (measured by **X-ray fluorescence (XRF)** or **UV-fluorescence** analyzers).
- **Current Trend:** Producing **Ultra-Low Sulfur Diesel (ULSD)** with **< 10 ppm** sulfur is the global regulatory standard (Bharat Stage VI / Euro VI).

### Step 7 — Blending

- **Objective:** Mix various refinery streams (reformate, alkylate, FCC gasoline, isomerate, ethanol) in precise ratios to produce **finished gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel** meeting market specifications (octane, RVP, distillation curve, sulfur, cetane number).
- **Instrumentation:** **In-line blend ratio analyzers**, **RVP analyzers**, **octane analyzers**, and **near-infrared (NIR) compositional analysis** all feed into a **model-predictive blender control system**.

### Step 8 — Product Storage & Dispatch

- **Finished products** are stored in **floating roof / cone roof tanks** and dispatched via pipelines, road tankers, rail wagons, or marine vessels.
- **Custody Transfer:** Uses **provers** for flow meter calibration; **API MPMS Chapter 4** standards apply.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Relation** | **Variables** | **Standard Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| API Gravity | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5$ | SG = specific gravity at 15.6 °C | dimensionless °API |
| Specific Gravity | $\text{SG} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | $\rho$ = density | dimensionless |
| Process Stream Mass Balance | $\sum_{\text{in}} \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \sum_{\text{out}} \dot{m}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{dm_{\text{accum}}}{dt}$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate | kg/h or BPD |
| Conversion in Cracking | $X = \dfrac{F_{\text{feed}} - F_{\text{unconverted}}}{F_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | $F$ = molar or mass flow | % |
| Selectivity | $S_i = \dfrac{\text{Moles of product } i}{\text{Moles of feed converted}}$ | — | dimensionless |
| Octane Blending (Linear Approximation) | $\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = \sum_i x_i \cdot \text{RON}_i$ | $x_i$ = volume fraction | dimensionless |
| Clausius–Clapeyron (VLE) | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R T} + C$ | $P$ = vapor pressure, $T$ = temperature, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | Pa, K |
| Aniline Point / Diesel Index | $\text{DI} = \dfrac{\text{API} \times \text{Aniline Point}}{100}$ | — | °F |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The above equations form the bedrock of refinery yield accounting, blend optimization, and online process model validation. In the KTU exam, problems typically involve **yield calculations**, **blend optimization**, and **distillation cut-point estimation** using simple mass and energy balances.
