# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity

The **American Petroleum Institute (API) gravity scale** is defined so that water has an API of **10°** and lighter hydrocarbons have higher API values.

Starting from the definition of specific gravity:

$$\text{SG} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$

For water at **15.6 °C (60 °F)**, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 999 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (effectively 1.000 g/cm³ for engineering calculations).

Step 1 — Express the relationship between API and SG empirically:

$$\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5$$

Step 2 — Verify with water:

$$\text{API}_{\text{water}} = \dfrac{141.5}{1.000} - 131.5 = 141.5 - 131.5 = 10.0 \text{ °API} \checkmark$$

Step 3 — Verify with a light crude (SG = 0.85, e.g., Brent crude):

$$\text{API}_{\text{Brent}} = \dfrac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5 = 166.47 - 131.5 = 34.97 \approx 35.0 \text{ °API}$$

This matches the known API of Brent crude, validating the formula.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Simple Mass Balance Across a Distillation Column

A typical KTU problem provides the feed rate and the mass fractions of each side-draw product. We will work through a worked numerical example.

### Problem Statement
A CDU processes **100,000 kg/h** of desalted crude. The product yields (mass fractions) are:

- Naphtha: **22 wt%**
- Kerosene: **18 wt%**
- Diesel: **24 wt%**
- HGO: **20 wt%**
- Atmospheric Residue: **15 wt%**
- Losses (to overhead gas + fuel): **1 wt%**

### Step-by-Step Mass Balance

**Step 1** — Confirm the mass fractions sum to 100%:

$$0.22 + 0.18 + 0.24 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.01 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 2** — Compute each product flow rate by multiplying the feed rate by the mass fraction:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Naphtha}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.22 = 22{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Kerosene}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.18 = 18{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Diesel}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.24 = 24{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{HGO}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.20 = 20{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Residue}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.15 = 15{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Losses}} = 100{,}000 \times 0.01 = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3** — Sum the outputs and compare to the input:

$$\sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} = 22{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 24{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 1{,}000 = 100{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \checkmark$$

The balance closes perfectly, indicating the yield data is consistent with the mass conservation law.

## 3.3 Derivation: Linear Octane Blending Equation

Octane numbers of individual blendstocks cannot simply be averaged by mass — the blending is **non-linear** in practice. However, for first-order KTU exam problems, the **linear volumetric blending** approximation is used.

$$\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot \text{RON}_i$$

where $x_i$ is the **volume fraction** of blendstock $i$, and $\text{RON}_i$ is its research octane number.

### Worked Example

A refinery blends the following gasoline stocks to make a **MS (Motor Spirit) of RON 91** (mandatory Indian specification):

| Blendstock | Volume % | RON |
|---|---|---|
| FCC Naphtha | 40% | 92 |
| Reformate | 30% | 98 |
| Alkylate | 15% | 96 |
| Isomerate | 10% | 88 |
| MTBE (oxygenate) | 5% | 115 |

**Step 1** — Compute the blend RON using the linear formula:

$$\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = (0.40)(92) + (0.30)(98) + (0.15)(96) + (0.10)(88) + (0.05)(115)$$

$$\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = 36.8 + 29.4 + 14.4 + 8.8 + 5.75$$

$$\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = 95.15 \text{ RON}$$

**Step 2** — Compare against the target of **91 RON** — the blend exceeds the specification comfortably. This is acceptable; refineries typically blend to a **margin of safety** (target RON + 0.5 to 1.5 units) to account for analyzer uncertainty and blending non-linearity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Refinery Process Summary

The following matrix condenses each refinery step for rapid revision:

| **Step** | **Process Type** | **Key Reactor/Vessel** | **T (°C)** | **P (bar)** | **Key Catalyst** | **Primary Products** | **Top Controlled Variable** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Storage | Floating roof tank | ambient | 1 | none | Crude | Level, Temperature |
| 2 | Desalting | Electrostatic desalter | 100–150 | 5–10 | Demulsifier | Desalted crude, brine | BS&W, Interface Level |
| 3 | ADU | Distillation column + furnace | 350–400 | 1.05–1.5 | none | Naphtha, Kero, Diesel, HGO, RCO | Tray T, Reflux flow |
| 4 | VDU | Vacuum column | 380–420 | 0.05–0.2 | none | VGO, Luboils, Vac Resid | Absolute P, Steam flow |
| 5a | FCC | Riser + Regenerator | 480–540 | 1.5–3.0 | Zeolite (USY/ZSM-5) | Gasoline, LPG, LCO, Slurry | Catalyst circulation, O₂ in flue |
| 5b | Hydrocracking | Fixed-bed reactor | 350–420 | 70–200 | Ni-Mo / Pt-Pd + zeolite | Jet, Diesel, Naphtha | H₂ partial pressure, ΔT |
| 5c | Coking | Coke drum | 480–510 | 1–4 | none | Petroleum coke, NC5, GO | Drum level, Switch timing |
| 5d | Reforming | Fixed-bed reactors (3–4) | 450–520 | 5–30 | Pt-Re / Pt-Sn / Cl-Al₂O₃ | Reformate, H₂ | ΔT per reactor, RON |
| 5e | Alkylation | Stratified/Contact reactor | 5–30 | 1–10 | H₂SO₄ or HF | Alkylate | Acid strength, T |
| 5f | Isomerization | Fixed-bed reactor | 120–250 | 15–40 | Pt / Cl-Al₂O₃ or Zeolite | Iso-C5/C6 | ΔT, H₂/Hydrocarbon |
| 6 | Hydrotreating | Fixed-bed reactor | 200–400 | 10–80 | Co-Mo / Ni-Mo | ULSD, Jet (low S/N) | ΔT (H₂S breakthrough), S ppm |
| 7 | Blending | In-line static mixer | ambient | 1–5 | none | MS, HSD, SKO, ATF | RON, RVP, Cetane |
| 8 | Storage/Dispatch | Tank + pump + meter | ambient | 1 | none | Finished fuels | Custody transfer flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note (for KTU 2024):** In the exam, when asked to "list the refinery steps in order," students MUST preserve the causal sequence: **Storage → Desalting → ADU → VDU → Conversion → Treatment → Blending → Dispatch**. Marks are awarded for correct order.

```mermaid
    I --> K

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"List any six major refinery steps in the correct sequence of operation."**

**Model Answer:**

The six major refinery steps in correct operational sequence are:

1. **Crude Oil Storage and Handling** (receipt, storage, blending of crudes).
2. **Desalting** (removal of salts, water, and sediments via electrostatic coalescence).
3. **Atmospheric Distillation** (primary fractionation into naphtha, kerosene, diesel, gas oil, and residue).
4. **Vacuum Distillation** (further fractionation of atmospheric residue under reduced pressure).
5. **Conversion Processes** (FCC, Hydrocracking, Reforming, Alkylation, etc.).
6. **Treatment and Blending** (Hydrotreating, sweetening, finished product blending, storage, and dispatch).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**"Differentiate between Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU) and Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)."**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **ADU** | **VDU** |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Desalted crude oil | Atmospheric residue (bottoms of ADU) |
| **Operating Pressure** | Near atmospheric (1.05–1.5 bar) | Very low pressure (0.05–0.2 bar absolute) |
| **Top Temperature** | ~120 °C (naphtha overhead) | ~70–90 °C (vacuum maintained by ejectors) |
| **Bottom Temperature** | ~350 °C | ~380–400 °C (limited to avoid cracking) |
| **Products** | Naphtha, kerosene, diesel, HGO, residue | VGO, luboils, wax, vacuum residue |
| **Heater Outlet T** | ~360–400 °C | ~400–420 °C |
| **Vacuum Source** | Not required | Steam ejectors / liquid ring pumps |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Atmospheric and Vacuum Distillation process** of crude oil. Discuss the major side-draw products and their typical boiling ranges. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A refinery processes **80,000 kg/h** of desalted crude oil. The product yield distribution is:

- Naphtha: **20 wt%**
- Kerosene: **15 wt%**
- Diesel: **25 wt%**
- HGO: **18 wt%**
- Atmospheric Residue: **20 wt%**
- Losses: **2 wt%**

Calculate the **mass flow rate of each product** and verify the overall **mass balance**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Introduction:** Atmospheric and Vacuum Distillation are the two most important **primary physical separation** steps in any refinery. They account for over **60–70%** of total capital investment.

**Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU):**
- Desalted crude is pumped through a **series of heat exchangers** (where it picks up heat from product streams and pump-around refluxes) and then through a **fired heater** (pipe-still furnace) where it is heated to **350–400 °C**.
- The hot two-phase mixture enters the **distillation column** (CDU) near its base. Steam is typically injected at the bottom (steam stripping) to improve separation.
- **Side draws** (with associated pump-around reflux and stripping sections) yield:
  - **Naphtha** (C₅–C₈, ~ 30–90 °C) — top draw.
  - **Kerosene/ATF** (C₉–C₁₂, ~ 150–250 °C) — side cut.
  - **Diesel/LGO** (C₁₂–C₂₀, ~ 250–350 °C) — side cut.
  - **HGO** (C₂₀–C₃₀, ~ 350–400 °C) — side cut.
- **Bottom product:** Atmospheric Residue (RCO) at ~ 350 °C.

**Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU):**
- The atmospheric residue is further heated in a **vacuum heater** to ~ 400 °C and flashed into a **vacuum column** operating at **10–50 mmHg absolute**.
- The **reduced pressure** lowers the boiling points of heavy hydrocarbons, preventing **thermal cracking** that would occur at atmospheric pressure above ~ 420 °C.
- **Products:** VGO, lubricating oil base stocks, paraffin wax, and **vacuum residue** (which goes to the coker or to bitumen blending).

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram above for the schematic.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

Given: $\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = 80{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$

Mass flow of each product:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Naphtha}} = 80{,}000 \times 0.20 = 16{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Kerosene}} = 80{,}000 \times 0.15 = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Diesel}} = 80{,}000 \times 0.25 = 20{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{HGO}} = 80{,}000 \times 0.18 = 14{,}400 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Residue}} = 80{,}000 \times 0.20 = 16{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{Losses}} = 80{,}000 \times 0.02 = 1{,}600 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Mass Balance Check:**

$$\sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} = 16{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 14{,}400 + 16{,}000 + 1{,}600 = 80{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \checkmark$$

$$\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} = 80{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \text{ (Mass balance verified.)}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** process with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **reactor-regenerator** system and the key instrumentation points. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A refinery blends four gasoline streams to produce a final product. The blend recipe and individual RONs are:

- **FCC Naphtha:** 35 vol%, RON = 91
- **Reformate:** 30 vol%, RON = 99
- **Alkylate:** 20 vol%, RON = 95
- **Isomerate:** 15 vol%, RON = 87

Compute the **blend RON**. If the regulatory minimum is **RON 91**, comment on whether the blend meets the specification. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Introduction:** FCC is the **heart of a modern refinery's conversion section**, converting heavy vacuum gas oil (VGO) into high-value gasoline and LPG.

**Process Description:**
- **Hot regenerated catalyst** (zeolite-based, e.g., USY, ZSM-5) flows down the **riser reactor** where it contacts pre-heated **VGO feed**.
- The cracking reactions occur in **2–4 seconds** at **480–540 °C**. Vapors and catalyst are separated in a **disengager/stripper** at the riser top.
- The **spent catalyst** (laden with **coke**) is steam-stripped, then routed to the **regenerator** where air is blown through it to **burn off coke** at **700–750 °C**, restoring catalyst activity.
- The **flue gas** from the regenerator passes through a **CO boiler** (or catalyst cooler) for energy recovery and pollution control.
- The **cracked vapors** are sent to a **main fractionator** that separates them into **dry gas, LPG, gasoline (FCC naphtha), LCO, and slurry oil**.

**Reactor-Regenerator Coupling:**
- The two vessels operate in a continuous **catalyst circulation loop**, with the catalyst acting as the **heat carrier** between the exothermic regenerator and the endothermic riser.
- This is a classic example of a **highly energy-integrated** process.

**Key Instrumentation Points:**
- **Riser outlet temperature** (target ~ 535 °C) — controls conversion and selectivity.
- **Regenerator bed level & pressure differential** — catalyst inventory control.
- **Slide valves** on spent and regenerated catalyst lines — control circulation rate.
- **O₂ analyzer** in the regenerator flue gas (target **0.5–2 vol%**) — controls combustion air.
- **Catalyst-to-oil ratio (C/O)** — derived from catalyst flow ÷ oil flow.
- **Main fractionator overhead temperature, pressure, and draw rates** — control product distribution.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 FCC Mermaid diagram.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

Apply the linear volumetric blending equation:

$$\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = (0.35)(91) + (0.30)(99) + (0.20)(95) + (0.15)(87)$$

Compute each term:

$$\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = 31.85 + 29.70 + 19.00 + 13.05$$

$$\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = 93.60 \text{ RON}$$

**Comparison with specification:**

$$\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = 93.60 \;\geq\; \text{RON}_{\text{min}} = 91.00 \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The blend **comfortably exceeds** the regulatory minimum of 91 RON by **2.6 RON units**. This provides a **safety margin** to account for:
- **Non-linear blending effects** (real octane blending is non-linear; RON interactions exist).
- **Online analyzer uncertainty** (~ ± 0.3–0.5 RON).
- **Storage and transport losses** of octane.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Order of refinery steps matters.** Many students write "FCC → ADU → VDU" — this is **wrong** and costs 2–3 marks. FCC takes HGO/VGO from the VDU as feed, so it must come **after** distillation.
> 2. **In mass balance problems**, always verify the fractions sum to 1.0. If they don't, state the discrepancy explicitly in your answer; the examiner awards partial credit for showing you checked.
> 3. **In blending problems**, do not compute RON based on **mass** fractions unless the problem explicitly says so. Gasoline blending is conventionally **volumetric**. Mixing mass and volume fractions is a common 1–2 mark penalty.
> 4. **Avoid missing units.** Always write kg/h, °C, bar, etc. Missing units = at least 0.5–1 mark deduction per sub-question.
> 5. **Block diagrams** must show **all major vessels AND the flow arrows with labels**. A diagram with no labels is treated as decorative and may not earn full marks.
> 6. **Hydrotreating ≠ Catalytic Cracking.** A common student error is conflating the two. Cracking breaks large molecules; hydrotreating removes heteroatoms (S, N, O) using hydrogen.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **The eight-step refinery sequence** is: **Storage → Desalting → ADU → VDU → Conversion → Treatment → Blending → Dispatch**.
- ✅ **API Gravity** formula: $\text{API} = 141.5 / \text{SG} - 131.5$. Water = 10°API.
- ✅ **Crude desalting** uses **electrostatic coalescence** at 15–35 kV; the **BS&W analyzer** is critical.
- ✅ **CDU** operates near **1.05–1.5 bar** and yields **naphtha, kerosene, diesel, HGO, and residue** as primary side-draws.
- ✅ **VDU** operates at **0.05–0.2 bar** absolute to avoid **thermal cracking** of heavy residues.
- ✅ **FCC** uses a **riser-regenerator** system with **zeolite catalyst** at **480–540 °C**. Key control variables: **riser T, regenerator O₂, catalyst circulation (slide valves)**.
- ✅ **Hydrocracking** uses **H₂ + bifunctional catalyst** at **70–200 bar** — produces **low-sulfur, high-cetane** products.
- ✅ **Catalytic Reforming** uses **Pt-Re** or **Pt-Sn** catalyst; produces **high-octane reformate** and **hydrogen** (a valuable by-product used elsewhere in the refinery).
- ✅ **Alkylation** uses **H₂SO₄** or **HF** acid to combine **C3/C4 olefins + isobutane** → premium alkylate (RON ~ 95).
- ✅ **Hydrotreating** removes **S, N, O, metals** using **Co-Mo / Ni-Mo** catalyst with H₂. Target: **< 10 ppm S** for ULSD.
- ✅ **Gasoline blending** is **volumetric** and **approximately linear** in RON for first-order problems.
- ✅ **1 barrel = 42 US gallons ≈ 159 liters**; throughput in **BPD** is the refinery industry standard.
- ✅ **Instrumentation cross-cuts** every step: **T, P, F, L** plus online **analyzers (NIR, XRF, conductivity, sulfur, RON)** — all integrated via **DCS / SIS**.
- ✅ Always **state the refinery step number/sequence** in descriptive answers; the examiner expects logical narrative flow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
---

# 6. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap (Full Bank Extension)

> **Note to student:** The following section extends the question bank with additional practice problems specifically tailored to the KTU 2024 scheme pattern (Part A 3-markers, Part B 14-markers with sub-choices).

## 6.1 Additional Part A Questions

### Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**"What is the role of the catalyst in the Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) process? Why is a continuous regeneration step required?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **catalyst** (typically a **Y-type faujasite zeolite**, e.g., **USY**, often combined with **ZSM-5** for octane enhancement) performs three functions in FCC:

1. **Cracking** — it provides **acidic active sites** (Bronsted acid sites on the zeolite framework) that cleave C–C bonds in heavy hydrocarbon feed molecules (VGO/HGO), reducing their molecular weight.
2. **Shape selectivity** — the uniform pore structure of the zeolite (~ 7.4 Å for FAU) restricts the entry of large molecules, biasing the product slate toward gasoline-range hydrocarbons.
3. **Heat carrier** — the circulating catalyst transports **heat** from the exothermic regenerator (~ 730 °C) to the endothermic riser reactor, providing the energy needed for vaporization and cracking.

**Why continuous regeneration is required:**
- During cracking, **coke** (heavy polycyclic hydrocarbons) deposits on the catalyst surface, **deactivating** it.
- A deactivated catalyst loses activity, selectivity, and would produce excessive dry gas and coke if not regenerated.
- Continuous regeneration in the regenerator vessel (where air burns off coke at ~ 700–750 °C) restores catalyst activity and also provides the heat for the process — making FCC a self-sustaining, energy-integrated operation.

---

### Q4. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**"Why is Vacuum Distillation carried out at sub-atmospheric pressure instead of atmospheric pressure?"**

**Model Answer:**

Vacuum Distillation is carried out at **very low absolute pressure (10–50 mmHg)** for the following critical reasons:

1. **Prevention of Thermal Cracking:** At atmospheric pressure, the heavy residue (atmospheric bottoms) would need to be heated to **> 550 °C** to vaporize. Above **420 °C**, the molecules begin to undergo **thermal cracking** — uncontrolled C–C bond breakage producing dry gas, coke, and lower-quality products. By reducing the pressure, the **boiling point** is suppressed per the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation**, allowing vaporization at **380–400 °C** — below the cracking threshold.

2. **Improved Separation Efficiency:** Lower operating pressure increases the **relative volatility** of the components, leading to better separation between the heavy distillate fractions (VGO, luboils, wax) and the vacuum residue.

3. **Reduced Coking in Heater Tubes:** Lower heater outlet temperatures (typically 400 °C vs. 400+ °C for ADU) significantly reduce coking rates in the fired heater tubes, extending run lengths.

4. **Energy Efficiency:** Vaporization at lower temperatures requires less furnace firing duty, saving fuel.

The **Clausius–Clapeyron** relationship mathematically justifies this:

$$\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R T} + C$$

A reduction in $P$ corresponds to a reduction in $T$ for any given compound — the foundational principle behind VDU operation.

---

## 6.2 Additional Part B Question (14 Marks)

### Question C (14 Marks) — Third Practice Option

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Describe the **Catalytic Reforming process** with a process flow diagram. List the major reactions occurring, the catalyst used, and the role of **hydrogen** in the system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A refinery has the following blending stocks available for **HSD (High-Speed Diesel)** production. The final product must have a **cetane number ≥ 51** (Bharat Stage VI specification).

| Blendstock | Volume % | Cetane Number |
|---|---|---|
| Straight-run LGO | 40% | 56 |
| LCO (from FCC) | 25% | 30 |
| Hydrocracker Diesel | 30% | 58 |
| Kerosene (as cutter) | 5% | 45 |

Compute the blend cetane number using linear blending. Comment on whether the blend meets the BS-VI specification. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Process Description:**
Catalytic Reforming is a **chemical conversion process** that upgrades **low-octane heavy naphtha** (typically C₇–C₁₁ hydrocarbons) into **high-octane reformate** (RON 95–100) and **hydrogen-rich gas** (a valuable by-product).

**Process Flow:**
- Heavy naphtha feed is mixed with **recycle hydrogen**, heated in a **fired heater**, and passed through a series of **3 or 4 adiabatic fixed-bed reactors** in series.
- Between reactors, **inter-heaters** reheat the reacting mixture to compensate for the **endothermic** dehydrogenation reactions.
- The reactor effluent is cooled and sent to a **high-pressure separator**, where **hydrogen-rich gas** is recycled back to the reactor feed.
- The liquid product (**reformate**) is sent to a **stabilizer column** to remove dissolved light hydrocarbons (C₁–C₄).

**Major Reactions:**

1. **Dehydrogenation of Naphthenes** (major source of H₂ and octane):
$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-6} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\text{e.g., cyclohexane} \rightarrow \text{benzene})$$

2. **Dehydroisomerization** of alkylcyclopentanes to aromatics.

3. **Isomerization** of n-paraffins to iso-paraffins (octane boost).

4. **Dehydrocyclization** of paraffins to aromatics.

5. **Hydrocracking** (minor, undesirable — produces light gases).

**Catalyst:** **Platinum (Pt)** as the active metal, promoted with **Rhenium (Re)** or **Tin (Sn)** for stability, on a **chlorided alumina (Cl–Al₂O₃)** acidic support. Bimetallic Pt-Re or Pt-Sn catalysts dramatically improve catalyst life and selectivity to aromatics.

**Role of Hydrogen:**
- **Suppresses coke formation** by hydrocracking coke precursors before they polymerize on the catalyst.
- Maintains catalyst activity over long run lengths (typically **1–3 years** between regenerations).
- Provides a **reducing atmosphere** that keeps the platinum metal in its active metallic state.
- The **net hydrogen produced** (after recycle) is **exported** to hydrotreaters and hydrocrackers in the refinery — a major economic benefit.

**Operating Conditions:** **450–520 °C**, **5–30 bar**, **H₂/hydrocarbon molar ratio = 3–6**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

Apply the linear blending equation for cetane number:

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot \text{CN}_i$$

Substitute the values:

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = (0.40)(56) + (0.25)(30) + (0.30)(58) + (0.05)(45)$$

Compute each term:

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = 22.4 + 7.5 + 17.4 + 2.25$$

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = 49.55 \text{ CN}$$

**Comparison with specification:**

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend}} = 49.55 \;\; < \;\; \text{CN}_{\text{min}} = 51.00$$

**Conclusion:** The blend **fails** the BS-VI specification by **1.45 cetane numbers**. The high proportion of **LCO (25%, CN = 30)** is the primary cause — LCO is a notoriously low-cetane FCC stream and must be either:
- **Hydroprocessed** (LCO hydrotreating/hydrocracking) to improve its cetane, or
- **Reduced in blending ratio** and replaced with more straight-run LGO or hydrocracker diesel.

**Recommended corrective action:** Reduce LCO to **15%** and increase hydrocracker diesel to **40%**:

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend (corrected)}} = (0.40)(56) + (0.15)(30) + (0.40)(58) + (0.05)(45)$$

$$\text{CN}_{\text{blend (corrected)}} = 22.4 + 4.5 + 23.2 + 2.25 = 52.35 \text{ CN} \checkmark$$

The corrected blend meets the BS-VI specification with a **1.35 CN** margin.

---

## 6.3 Cross-Module Conceptual Links

The following table maps the **refinery steps** to their **primary instrumentation and control challenges**, helping students connect Module 1 (Refinery Steps) with subsequent modules in PEEIT861.

| **Refinery Step** | **Critical Sensors** | **Final Control Elements** | **Analyzer Loop** | **Safety Considerations** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crude Storage | Servo / radar level, T | Pump speed, transfer valves | BS&W, API gravity | Flammable vapor → LEL detection, foam pourers |
| Desalting | dP (interface), T, P, flow | Demulsifier pump, wash water valve | Conductivity, pH | High-voltage (35 kV) interlocks |
| ADU | Tray T, reflux flow, P, L | Reflux pump, steam flow valve | NIR for distillation cut points | Furnace flame failure → fuel shut-off (FFS) |
| VDU | Absolute P, T, steam flow | Ejector steam valve, resid flow | Viscosity, conradson carbon | Ejector steam pressure low → vacuum break |
| FCC | Riser T, regenerator T, ΔP, O₂ | Catalyst slide valves, air blower damper | O₂, CO, NOx in flue | Cyclone failure → catalyst loss; CO boiler trip |
| Reformer | Reactor ΔT, H₂ partial P, chloride | H₂ recycle compressor, chloride injection | RON (NIR), aromatics | Sulfur in feed → catalyst poisoning alarm |
| Hydrocracker | Reactor ΔT, H₂ makeup, L | Cold high-pressure separator LCV | Sulfur, nitrogen (online) | High-pressure (200 bar) → PSV sizing critical |
| Hydrotreater | Reactor ΔT, H₂/HC ratio, L | H₂ flow control, recycle gas | Sulfur (XRF), H₂S | Exothermic runaway → H₂ emergency block |
| Blending | In-line flow, density | Ratio control valves, blender | NIR, RON, RVP, cetane, flash point | Off-spec product → divert to rework tank |
| Storage / Dispatch | Custody transfer flow, T, L | Loading arms, pumps | Density, viscosity, flash | Overfill protection, vapor recovery, ESD-1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Reference:** The analyzer and control loops in this matrix will be discussed in detail in **Modules 2, 3, and 4** of PEEIT861 (Instrumentation for Distillation Columns, Analyzers in Refineries, and Safety Instrumented Systems). This table is your **roadmap** for integrating knowledge across modules.

---

## 6.4 Final High-Yield Memory Sheet

| **#** | **Key Fact** | **Numeric / Formula Anchor** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refinery step order | **S → D → ADU → VDU → C → T → B → Disp** |
| 2 | ADU operating P | **1.05–1.5 bar** |
| 3 | VDU operating P | **0.05–0.2 bar abs (mmHg)** |
| 4 | FCC catalyst | **Zeolite (USY/ZSM-5)** |
| 5 | FCC T | **480–540 °C** |
| 6 | Hydrocracker P | **70–200 bar** |
| 7 | Reforming catalyst | **Pt-Re / Pt-Sn on Cl-Al₂O₃** |
| 8 | Hydrotreating catalyst | **Co-Mo / Ni-Mo** |
| 9 | ULSD sulfur | **< 10 ppm** |
| 10 | 1 barrel | **42 US gallons ≈ 159 L** |
| 11 | API gravity | $\text{API} = 141.5/\text{SG} - 131.5$ |
| 12 | Clausius–Clapeyron | $\ln P = -\Delta H_{\text{vap}}/RT + C$ |
| 13 | Alkylation catalyst | **H₂SO₄ or HF** |
| 14 | Desalter voltage | **15–35 kV** |
| 15 | BS-VI diesel sulfur | **< 10 ppm S, CN ≥ 51** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner Tips:**
> 1. Always state the **boiling range** (in °C) of each refinery fraction in descriptive answers — examiners award marks for this specific quantitative detail.
> 2. When asked to "explain" a process, **always** include: (i) objective, (ii) operating conditions (T, P), (iii) catalyst/chemical, (iv) instrumentation & control. Skipping the instrumentation angle is a frequent 1–2 mark loss in PEEIT861.
> 3. Memorize the **API gravity formula** and the **mass balance equation** — they are guaranteed 2–3 mark earners in any Part A or Part B problem.
> 4. **Practice the blending problems** — they are predictable, easy to score, and frequently asked (at least once in every KTU 2024 cycle).
> 5. Always draw a **neat block diagram** with **flow direction arrows** and **labels** — a well-labeled diagram alone can fetch 3–4 marks in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atmospheric Distillation of Crude Oil
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Atmospheric Distillation of Crude Oil

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Atmospheric Distillation of Crude Oil (ADU / Atmospheric Distillation Unit)** is a continuous, steady-state physical separation process in which whole crude oil is fractionated into multiple petroleum products (Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel, Atmospheric Gas Oil, etc.) at approximately **atmospheric pressure (≈ 1.013 bar / 101.325 kPa)** by exploiting the differences in the boiling points of its constituent hydrocarbon groups.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Atmospheric distillation is the primary refining step where desalted crude oil is heated to ≈ 350 – 400 °C and vaporized partially in a furnace, then routed to a tall fractionating tower where ascending vapors meet descending liquid reflux. Lighter fractions condense at lower trays; heavier products leave as side-streams; residue exits the bottom.*

The process strictly operates at pressures **slightly above atmospheric (1.0 – 1.7 bar absolute)** because at higher temperatures, heavy fractions would crack (thermally decompose) into coke and light gases, reducing product yield and fouling the furnace tubes.

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | 1.0 – 1.7 bar (a) | Just above atmospheric |
| Feed Outlet Temp (Furnace) | 350 – 400 °C | Below thermal cracking limit |
| Column Top Temp | 100 – 130 °C | Set by overhead product |
| Column Bottom Temp | 340 – 360 °C | Limits heavy-end decomposition |
| Number of Theoretical Stages | 30 – 50 | Determines product sharpness |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Story Apartment of Hydrocarbons"

Imagine a tall **20-story apartment building** where residents (hydrocarbon molecules) enter through a single basement door carrying their own internal "thermometer" (boiling point).

- The **ground floor (furnace)** is very hot — every resident starts sweating (vaporizing). Light residents (small molecules, low BP) become vapor instantly; heavy residents (large molecules, high BP) remain as sweat (liquid).
- They enter the building through a **forced-draft duct (feed inlet)** and rise upward (vapors climbing the column).
- On the **roof**, a cold shower is continuously sprayed (overhead reflux). Residents who prefer cold temperatures (low BP) condense first and are collected on **higher floors (lower numbered trays)**.
- Heavier residents prefer warmth, so they fall back to **lower floors (higher tray numbers)**, where they are tapped off as **side-streams**.
- The **stubborn, heaviest residents** never sweat at all — they sink to the **basement (column bottom / pump-around)** and leave as **Atmospheric Residue**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Atmospheric"?** Because the column operates at near-atmospheric pressure. To further distill the heavy residue, refineries use a **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)** operating at 25 – 50 mmHg, which is covered in the next module.

## 1.3 Industrial Importance

- Supplies **80 – 85 %** of a refinery's marketable liquid products by volume.
- Acts as the **upstream feed preparation** for downstream units: Naphtha Hydrotreater, Catalytic Reformer, Hydrocracker, FCC, Lube plant, etc.
- The **yield pattern** (naphtha vs. middle distillate vs. residue) governs the **refinery configuration** and economic viability of the entire complex.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often think "distillation = boiling." In ADU, **boiling occurs only in the furnace and reboilers**. Inside the column, separation is by **partial condensation (vapors) and partial vaporization (liquids) on each tray** — not bulk boiling.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Process Flow

The ADU train consists of the following major equipment arranged in a specific sequence. Each unit has well-defined instrumentation loops (T, P, F, L, C) that the KTU syllabus emphasizes.

### Step 1 — Crude Oil Desalting
- Crude arriving from storage contains **dissolved salts (NaCl, CaCl₂, MgCl₂)** typically 10 – 200 PTB (pounds per thousand barrels) and entrained water.
- Mixed with **wash water (3 – 7 % vol)** and passed through a **desalter vessel** equipped with an **electrostatic grid (15 – 35 kV AC/DC)**.
- Salts dissolve in the wash water, coalesce under the electric field, and are drawn off as **brine**.
- Outlet salt content is reduced to **≤ 1 PTB (≈ 0.5 mg/L)**.

### Step 2 — Preheating Train (Heat Integration)
- The desalted crude is progressively heated by **heat exchange with hot product streams** leaving the column:
  - Atmospheric residue (≈ 340 °C)
  - Heavy gas oil (≈ 320 °C)
  - Light gas oil (≈ 250 °C)
  - Kerosene (≈ 200 °C)
  - Naphtha overhead (≈ 120 °C)
- This reduces furnace firing duty by **30 – 50 %** — a major energy-saving concept called **Heat Integrated Distillation**.

### Step 3 — Process Furnace (Direct Fired Heater)
- The preheated crude enters the **convection section** first (waste-heat recovery), then the **radiation section** where it is heated to **350 – 400 °C**.
- About **50 – 65 %** of the feed vaporizes; the rest remains as liquid (un-vaporized residue).
- Tube skin temperature is strictly controlled below **≈ 600 °C** to prevent coke deposition.

### Step 4 — Distillation Column (Fractionating Tower)
- The partially vaporized feed enters through a **flash zone** typically between stages **3 and 5** from the bottom.
- Vapors rise; liquid descends. **Bubble-cap, valve, sieve, or structured packing** trays promote intimate vapor–liquid contact.
- Typical stages: **30 – 50 actual trays** (≈ 50 – 70 % tray efficiency).
- **Reflux** is returned at the top; **pump-arounds** are drawn and cooled at intermediate zones.

### Step 5 — Overhead System & Condenser
- The top vapor (≈ 100 – 130 °C) is condensed in an **overhead condenser** (air-cooled or water-cooled).
- A **reflux drum** separates condensed liquid from non-condensable gases (C1 – C4 light ends, H₂S, water vapor).
- A portion of the condensed liquid is returned as **reflux**; the rest is withdrawn as **Overhead Naphtha / Unstabilized Naphtha**.

### Step 6 — Side Stream Strippers
Each side draw (except the top and bottom) is routed to a small **side-stripper column** (steam or reboiled) to:
- Remove entrained light ends (control flash point / initial boiling point).
- Tighten product specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pump-Around vs. Side Draw:**
> - **Side Draw** → sent to a stripper → final product (e.g., Diesel, Kerosene).
> - **Pump-Around** → cooled and returned to the column at a higher tray (heat removal duty). It does NOT leave the column.

## 2.2 KTU Concept Sheet (High-Yield)

| Concept / Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **TBP (True Boiling Point) Curve** | Plot of cumulative vol % distilled vs. temperature from a laboratory batch distillation (15:5 reflux ratio, 15 trays). | Basis for designing ADU/VDU cut points. |
| **ASTM D-86 Distillation** | Standard lab distillation test used for product quality (IBP, FBP, % recovered at specified temps). | Reports product specs; sharper than TBP. |
| **Cut Point** | The TBP temperature that separates two adjacent products (e.g., 165 °C between Naphtha & Kerosene). | Refinery product slate is decided by cut points. |
| **Flash Zone** | The tray level where the two-phase feed enters; vapor rises, liquid falls. | Reference for tray numbering. |
| **Overhead Reflux** | Condensed top product returned to the top tray. | Sets internal liquid traffic; controls top temperature. |
| **Pump-Around (PA)** | Liquid withdrawn from a tray, cooled externally, returned to a tray above. | Provides heat removal; reduces column diameter. |
| **Side Stripper** | Small reboiled/steamed column for side draws. | Controls light ends / flash point of side product. |
| **Reflux Ratio (R/D)** | Mass (or mol) of reflux returned per mass of distillate withdrawn. | Higher R → sharper separation, more energy. |
| **Tray Efficiency (E)** | Ratio of theoretical stages to actual stages. | ADU efficiency ≈ 0.5 – 0.7. |
| **Heat Integrated Distillation** | Preheating feed by product streams. | Reduces furnace duty 30 – 50 %. |

## 2.3 The Reflux Ratio — Core Design Variable

The **reflux ratio (R/D or simply R)** is the single most important operating lever in any distillation column.

$$R \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{reflux}}}{D}$$

where $L_{\text{reflux}}$ is the molar flow rate of liquid returned to the top tray and $D$ is the molar flow rate of distillate product.

Two limiting cases exist:

- **Total Reflux (R → ∞)**: No product withdrawal. Maximum separation. Number of stages = minimum stages $N_{\min}$.
- **Minimum Reflux ($R_{\min}$)**: Infinite number of stages required. No separation in finite equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fenske–Underwood–Gilliland Shortcut Method (KTU must-know):**
> 1. **Fenske Equation** → $N_{\min}$ for separation of two key components.
> 2. **Underwood Equation** → $R_{\min}$.
> 3. **Gilliland Correlation** → Actual stages $N$ for chosen $R$ (or vice versa).

The economic optimum reflux ratio is usually chosen in the range

$$R_{\text{opt}} \;\approx\; (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \cdot R_{\min}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Capacity Planning**: ADU throughput sets the capacity of every downstream unit.
- **Energy Auditing**: ADU consumes **25 – 35 %** of total refinery energy — heat integration has the highest ROI.
- **Process Control Education**: ADU is the benchmark case study for **multivariable control (MV)**, **model predictive control (MPC)**, and **advanced regulatory control (APC)** in petrochemical engineering courses.
- **Yield Optimization**: A 1 °C shift in a cut point can change refinery margin by **₹ 50 – 200 / KL** depending on crude price spread.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Mass/Energy Balances & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Material Balance Across the Column

Let the column operate at steady state with the following stream notation (all in kmol/h unless stated):

- $F$ — Total feed (liquid + vapor at flash zone)
- $D$ — Distillate (overhead naphtha)
- $B$ — Bottoms (atmospheric residue)
- $V$ — Vapor rising from the flash zone
- $L$ — Liquid descending from the flash zone
- $V_i, L_i$ — Vapor and liquid on tray $i$
- $R$ — Reflux flow returning to top tray

### Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance

By conservation of mass over the entire column (no reaction, steady state):

$$F \;=\; D \;+\; B$$

> This is the simplest possible balance: whatever enters must leave.

### Step 2 — Component Mass Balance

For any component $j$ with feed composition $z_j$, distillate $x_{D,j}$, bottoms $x_{B,j}$:

$$F \cdot z_j \;=\; D \cdot x_{D,j} \;+\; B \cdot x_{B,j}$$

> This is the basic lever rule used in McCabe–Thiele and shortcut distillation methods.

### Step 3 — Flash Zone Material Balance (Two-Phase Feed)

At the flash zone, the feed separates into vapor $V$ and liquid $L$ fractions. Let $q$ be the fraction of feed that is liquid at the inlet (thermal condition of feed).

The total balance gives:

$$F \;=\; V \;+\; L$$

A component balance for the flash zone:

$$F \cdot z_j \;=\; V \cdot y_j \;+\; L \cdot x_j$$

where $y_j$ and $x_j$ are in equilibrium at the flash zone temperature and pressure. Solving for the vapor fraction:

$$V \;=\; F \cdot \frac{z_j - x_j}{y_j - x_j}$$

And the liquid fraction:

$$L \;=\; F \cdot \frac{y_j - z_j}{y_j - x_j}$$

The classical $q$-line relation is:

$$q \;=\; \frac{L}{F} \;=\; 1 - \frac{V}{F} \;\; \text{(for single-stage flash)}$$

### Step 4 — Internal Liquid/Vapor Traffic

On any tray $i$ above the flash zone, assuming constant molal overflow (CMO):

$$V_{i+1} \;=\; V_i \;+\; (\text{any vapor side-draw})$$

$$L_{i-1} \;=\; L_i \;+\; (\text{any liquid side-draw})$$

In the rectifying section (above the top side draw), with reflux $R$ and no side draw:

$$V \;\equiv\; D \;+\; R$$

$$L \;\equiv\; R$$

In the stripping section (below feed, above bottoms), with no intermediate side draws:

$$L' \;\equiv\; L \;+\; q \cdot F$$

$$V' \;\equiv\; V \;-\; (1 - q) \cdot F$$

> The CMO assumption dramatically simplifies design: a straight operating line replaces curved mass-transfer relations.

## 3.2 Derivation — Fenske Equation for Minimum Stages

For the separation of **light key (LK)** and **heavy key (HK)** at **total reflux** (no feed, no product withdrawal), the Fenske equation gives the minimum number of theoretical stages:

$$N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_{D} \cdot \left(\dfrac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_{B} \right]}{\ln \alpha_{\text{avg}}}$$

where the relative volatility

$$\alpha_{ij} \;=\; \frac{K_i}{K_j} \;=\; \frac{y_i / x_i}{y_j / x_j}$$

and $\alpha_{\text{avg}}$ is the geometric mean relative volatility between LK and HK evaluated at the geometric mean of top and bottom temperatures:

$$\alpha_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \sqrt{\alpha_{\text{top}} \cdot \alpha_{\text{bottom}}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** Distillate contains 95 mol % n-pentane (LK) and 5 mol % n-hexane (HK). Bottoms contains 5 mol % n-pentane and 95 mol % n-hexane. Average relative volatility $\alpha_{\text{avg}} = 2.45$.

**Step 1 — Form the four concentration ratios:**

$$\left(\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_{D} \;=\; \frac{0.95}{0.05} \;=\; 19$$

$$\left(\frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_{B} \;=\; \frac{0.95}{0.05} \;=\; 19$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$19 \times 19 \;=\; 361$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$\ln(2.45) \;\approx\; 0.8961$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\ln(361)}{0.8961} \;=\; \frac{5.886}{0.8961} \;\approx\; 6.57$$

> **Rounded up: $N_{\min} = 7$ theoretical stages.**
> This is the absolute minimum; actual column will need more stages because we operate at finite reflux.

## 3.3 Derivation — Underwood Minimum Reflux

The Underwood equation for binary systems simplifies to:

$$R_{\min} \;=\; \frac{1}{(\alpha - 1)} \left[ \frac{x_{D,LK}}{x_{F,LK}} - \alpha \cdot \frac{x_{D,HK}}{x_{F,HK}} \right]$$

A more general form uses the $q$-line parameter:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{q \cdot z_j}{q - \theta} \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{to find Underwood root } \theta)$$

$$R_{\min} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{D \cdot x_{D,j}}{D - \theta \cdot F \cdot z_j} - 1$$

> For exam purposes, the KTU board typically gives a binary case where the simplified form suffices.

## 3.4 Energy Balance Across the Furnace

The furnace supplies the entire heat duty. The simplified steady-state energy balance per kg of feed:

$$Q_{\text{furnace}} \;=\; F \cdot c_{p,\text{feed}} \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) \;+\; F \cdot \lambda_{\text{vap}} \cdot f_{\text{vap}} \;+\; Q_{\text{loss}}$$

where:

- $c_{p,\text{feed}}$ ≈ 2.5 kJ/(kg·K) (typical crude 200 – 400 °C)
- $T_{\text{out}}$ ≈ 380 °C, $T_{\text{in}}$ ≈ 240 °C
- $\lambda_{\text{vap}}$ ≈ 220 – 280 kJ/kg
- $f_{\text{vap}}$ = fraction vaporized (typically 0.55 – 0.65)
- $Q_{\text{loss}}$ ≈ 2 – 5 % of total fired duty

For an ADU processing **100,000 kg/h** of crude with 60 % vaporization:

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} \;=\; 100{,}000 \times 2.5 \times (380 - 240) \;=\; 35 \times 10^6 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

$$Q_{\text{latent}} \;=\; 100{,}000 \times 250 \times 0.60 \;=\; 15 \times 10^6 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} \;\approx\; 50 \times 10^6 \;\text{kJ/h} \;\approx\; 13.9 \;\text{MW}$$

This explains why **furnace efficiency and waste-heat recovery** are first-priority KPIs in any ADU.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ADU vs VDU

| Parameter | Atmospheric Distillation | Vacuum Distillation |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | 1.0 – 1.7 bar (a) | 0.025 – 0.075 bar (25 – 75 mmHg) |
| Bottom Temperature | 340 – 360 °C | 380 – 420 °C (lower than 1 atm BP) |
| Feed | Whole Crude | Atmospheric Residue (Long Residue) |
| Main Products | Naphtha, Kero, Diesel, AGO | LVGO, HVGO, Vacuum Residue |
| Cracking Tendency | Moderate; must avoid | Severe; vacuum avoids coking |
| Steam to Column | None in main column | Superheated steam in reboiler & bottom |
| Column Type | Trayed (30 – 50 stages) | Packed / Trayed (lower ΔP) |
| Energy Intensity | Higher absolute duty | Lower duty, but specialized design |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the ADU Train

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **function, energy flow, and material flow** between major process units. It uses only alphanumeric node IDs and clean text labels (no bold/HTML inside node strings), in compliance with the KTU rendering safeguards.

```mermaid

    A2 --> A3[DESALTER_V101]

    A4 --> A5[FURNACE_F101]
    A5 --> A6[FLASH_ZONE_OF_T201]
    A6 --> A7[FRACTIONATING_TOWER_T201]
    A7 --> A8[OVERHEAD_CONDENSER_E201]

    A13 --> A14[KEROSENE_PRODUCT]
    A7 --> A15[SIDE_DRAW_DIESEL]
    A15 --> A16[DIESEL_STRIPPER_T203]
    A16 --> A17[DIESEL_PRODUCT]
    A7 --> A18[SIDE_DRAW_ATMOSPHERIC_GAS_OIL]

    A19 --> A20[AGO_PRODUCT]
    A7 --> A21[SIDE_DRAW_PUMP_AROUND_1]

    A22 --> A7
    A7 --> A23[SIDE_DRAW_PUMP_AROUND_2]
    A23 --> A24[PA_COOLER_E204]
    A24 --> A7
    A7 --> A25[COLUMN_BOTTOM_RESIDUE]
    A25 --> A26[RESIDUE_PUMP_P202]
    A26 --> A27[RESIDUE_PRODUCT]
    A26 --> A4
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **A1 → A3 → A4 → A5 → A6**: Crude flows through desalting, preheating, furnace, and into the flash zone of the tower.
- **A7 (Tower)** is the central node; it exchanges streams with **A8–A11 (overhead loop)**, **A12–A20 (side draws with strippers)**, **A21–A24 (pump-arounds)**, and **A25–A27 (residue)**.
- The **dashed conceptual link A26 → A4** (residue returning to preheat the incoming crude) represents the **heat-integration loop** — the most important energy-saving feature of the ADU.

## 4.2 Typical Tray Profile (Temperature / Vapor-Liquid Traffic)

The next Mermaid diagram shows the **vertical tray profile** of the tower. Each tray is represented as a node. Vapor rises; liquid descends; side-draws exit at specific trays.


> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The temperature decreases from the bottom (≈ 345 °C) to the top (≈ 105 °C). Side draws exit where the local TBP curve matches the desired product specification.

## 4.3 Instrumentation & Control Loop Overview

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TT101[FURNACE_TUBE_SKIN_TC] --> AC101[FUEL_GAS_FCV]
    AC101 --> FURNACE[FURNACE_F101]
    FURNACE --> FT201[FEED_FT_TO_TOWER]
    FT201 --> TT201[TOWER_TRAY_TC]
    TT201 --> LC201[REFLUX_Drum_LC]
    LC201 --> FC201[REFLUX_FCV]
    FC201 --> TOWER[T201_TOWER]
    TOWER --> PT201[TOWER_TOP_PC]
    PT201 --> PCV201[OVERHEAD_VAC_PCV]
    PCV201 --> TOWER
    TOWER --> TT_BOT[TOWER_BOTTOM_TC]
    TT_BOT --> ST_BOT[RESIDUE_PUMP_SPEED]
    ST_BOT --> TOWER
```

### Key Control Loops in ADU (KTU must-know)

| Loop Tag | Controlled Variable | Manipulated Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| TC-101 | Furnace tube-skin T | Fuel-gas flow | Prevent coking |
| FC-201 | Feed flow to tower | Charge pump speed | Throughput |
| TC-201 | Top tray T | Reflux flow | Sets overhead composition |
| PC-201 | Tower top P | Overhead vapor valve | Prevents flooding / vacuum |
| LC-201 | Reflux drum level | Distillate product draw | Maintains reflux inventory |
| TC-BOT | Bottom T | Stripping steam / reboiler | Sets bottoms quality |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Atmospheric Distillation of Crude Oil. Why is it carried out at near-atmospheric pressure and not at higher pressure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Atmospheric distillation is a continuous fractionation process that separates whole crude oil into products like naphtha, kerosene, diesel, and atmospheric gas oil by exploiting differences in boiling points, carried out at **1.0 – 1.7 bar (a)** pressure inside a tall fractionating tower after the crude is heated to **350 – 400 °C** in a furnace.

It is operated at near-atmospheric pressure because:
1. At higher pressures, the boiling points of hydrocarbons increase, requiring the furnace to heat the crude above 400 °C — leading to **thermal cracking** and **coke formation** in furnace tubes.
2. The yield of valuable middle distillates drops as heavy molecules break into light gases.
3. Lower pressure (atmospheric) provides the best compromise between equipment cost, separation efficiency, and product yield.

> *Examiner's note (1 mark each for definition, 1st reason, 2nd reason — total 3).*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Side Draw, Side Stripper, and Pump-Around in an ADU column. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Side Draw | Side Stripper | Pump-Around |
|---|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Final product withdrawal | Sharpens side product spec | Heat removal from column |
| Stream leaves the column? | Yes, as product | Yes, returns as stripped product | Yes, returns as cooled liquid |
| Where does it go? | Stripper → storage | Back to main column | Back to main column at higher tray |
| Steam used? | Inside stripper | Yes (steam stripping) | No |
| Examples | Diesel draw, Kero draw | Kero stripper, Diesel stripper | PA-1, PA-2 |

> *Examiner's note: Tabular answer scores full 3 marks; one-line answers typically score ≤ 2.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labeled schematic, describe the **complete process flow of an Atmospheric Distillation Unit**, including the desalting, preheating, furnace, fractionating tower, side strippers, and overhead system. Mark the typical operating temperatures and pressures at every important point.

**(b) [7 marks]** A binary mixture of n-pentane (LK) and n-hexane (HK) is to be separated in a continuous distillation column. The feed is a saturated liquid with $z_{LK} = 0.50$, distillate $x_{D,LK} = 0.95$, bottoms $x_{B,LK} = 0.05$. The average relative volatility $\alpha_{\text{avg}} = 2.45$.
1. Calculate the **minimum number of theoretical stages ($N_{\min}$)** using the Fenske equation.
2. If the column operates at a reflux ratio $R = 1.5 \cdot R_{\min}$ and $R_{\min} = 2.0$, find the **number of actual stages** using the **Gilliland correlation**.
3. Comment on the effect of increasing reflux ratio on product purity and energy consumption.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Schematic Description (7 marks):**

The student should draw a **labeled process flow diagram** covering:
- Desalter (V-101) with wash water, electrostatic grid, brine draw.
- Pre-heat train (E-101 → E-110) — note that **residue and side products** return to preheat the incoming crude.
- Furnace (F-101) with convection and radiation sections.
- Fractionating tower (T-201) showing **feed entry, top reflux, side draws, side strippers (T-202, T-203, T-204), pump-arounds, and bottom residue**.
- Overhead system: condenser E-201, reflux drum D-201, reflux pump P-201, vapor vent.

Mark these values on the diagram:

| Location | T (°C) | P (bar) |
|---|---|---|
| Desalter outlet | 110 – 130 | 10 – 15 |
| Last preheater outlet (before furnace) | 220 – 250 | 8 – 12 |
| Furnace outlet | 350 – 400 | 4 – 6 |
| Tower top | 100 – 130 | 1.0 – 1.3 |
| Flash zone | 340 – 360 | 1.4 – 1.7 |
| Column bottom | 340 – 360 | 1.5 – 1.8 |

> *Examiner allocates marks as: 2 for sequence, 2 for P&ID, 2 for T & P marking, 1 for completeness.*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Fenske Equation:**

$$N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_{D} \cdot \left(\dfrac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_{B} \right]}{\ln \alpha_{\text{avg}}}$$

Compute ratios:

$$\left(\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_{D} \;=\; \frac{0.95}{0.05} \;=\; 19$$

$$\left(\frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_{B} \;=\; \frac{0.95}{0.05} \;=\; 19$$

Product of ratios:

$$19 \times 19 \;=\; 361$$

Numerator:

$$\ln(361) \;\approx\; 5.886$$

Denominator:

$$\ln(2.45) \;\approx\; 0.8961$$

$$N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{5.886}{0.8961} \;\approx\; 6.57 \;\approx\; 7 \;\text{theoretical stages}$$

**Part (ii) — Gilliland Correlation:**

Operating reflux ratio:

$$R \;=\; 1.5 \cdot R_{\min} \;=\; 1.5 \times 2.0 \;=\; 3.0$$

The Gilliland parameter $X$:

$$X \;=\; \frac{R - R_{\min}}{R + 1} \;=\; \frac{3.0 - 2.0}{3.0 + 1} \;=\; \frac{1.0}{4.0} \;=\; 0.25$$

The corresponding $Y$ from the Gilliland chart (or Molokanov equation):

$$Y \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left[\frac{(1 + 54.4 X)(X - 1)}{(11 + 117.2 X)\sqrt{X}}\right]$$

Substituting $X = 0.25$:

$$Y \;\approx\; 1 - \exp\!\left[\frac{(1 + 13.6)(0.25 - 1)}{(11 + 29.3)\sqrt{0.25}}\right] \;\approx\; 1 - \exp(-0.477) \;\approx\; 0.379$$

Relating $Y$ to actual stages $N$:

$$Y \;=\; \frac{N - N_{\min}}{N + 1}$$

$$0.379 \;=\; \frac{N - 7}{N + 1}$$

$$0.379 (N + 1) \;=\; N - 7$$

$$0.379 N + 0.379 \;=\; N - 7$$

$$7.379 \;=\; 0.621 N$$

$$N \;\approx\; 11.88 \;\approx\; 12 \;\text{theoretical stages}$$

If tray efficiency $E = 0.65$:

$$N_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{N}{E} \;=\; \frac{12}{0.65} \;\approx\; 19 \;\text{actual trays}$$

**Part (iii) — Effect of Reflux Ratio:**

- **Higher R** → closer approach to equilibrium on each tray → **sharper separation** → higher product purity, lower reboiler duty per unit of separation achieved.
- **Disadvantage**: Higher R → larger condenser, larger reboiler, more cooling water, more steam → **higher operating cost**.
- There is an **optimum R (typically 1.2 – 1.5 × $R_{\min}$)** beyond which capital and energy costs outweigh the marginal improvement in purity.

> *Examiner's note: Each sub-part of (b) carries roughly 2 + 3 + 2 = 7 marks.*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **concept and importance of heat integration in an ADU**. Sketch the preheat train showing how the products cool the incoming crude. Discuss the typical fuel savings achieved.

**(b) [7 marks]** A refinery's ADU processes **100,000 kg/h** of desalted crude. The crude enters the furnace at 240 °C and leaves at 380 °C. The average specific heat of crude over this range is 2.5 kJ/(kg·K). The fraction vaporized in the furnace is 0.60, and the average latent heat of vaporization is 250 kJ/kg. The furnace has an efficiency of 88 % and the fuel has a calorific value of 42,000 kJ/kg.
1. Calculate the **total furnace duty** in MW.
2. Calculate the **fuel consumption** in kg/h.
3. If heat integration reduces the furnace duty by 30 %, what is the **annual fuel saving (₹)** if fuel costs ₹ 60,000 per tonne? (Assume 350 operating days/year.)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Heat Integration (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Heat integration in ADU refers to the practice of using hot product streams leaving the fractionator to preheat the cold incoming crude oil before it enters the furnace. This drastically reduces the amount of fuel that must be burned in the furnace to reach the required flash-zone temperature.

**Sketch:** (Student should draw a counter-current preheat train with arrows showing crude flow on one side and product flow on the other, with intermediate exchangers E-101 through E-110.)

**Stream Matching (typical sequence, hottest to coldest):**
- Atmospheric residue (340 °C) → E-101 (cools crude from 200 to 240 °C)
- Heavy gas oil (300 °C) → E-102
- Light gas oil (250 °C) → E-103
- Kerosene (200 °C) → E-104
- Naphtha overhead (120 °C) → E-105
- Pump-around returns (180 – 250 °C) → E-106 → E-108

**Benefits:**
- **Fuel savings: 30 – 50 %** of furnace firing duty.
- **Lower CO₂, SOₓ, NOₓ emissions** — directly tied to ESG compliance.
- **Reduced furnace tube metal stress** → longer equipment life.
- **Pinch analysis** is the standard tool used to design the optimum heat exchanger network (HEN).

> *Examiner's note: 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for sketch, 2 marks for typical savings, 1 mark for pinch analysis mention.*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Total Furnace Duty:**

Sensible heat:

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 100{,}000 \times 2.5 \times (380 - 240)$$

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} \;=\; 100{,}000 \times 2.5 \times 140 \;=\; 35{,}000{,}000 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

Latent heat:

$$Q_{\text{latent}} \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot f_{\text{vap}} \cdot \lambda_{\text{vap}} \;=\; 100{,}000 \times 0.60 \times 250 \;=\; 15{,}000{,}000 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

Total duty:

$$Q_{\text{total}} \;=\; 35{,}000{,}000 + 15{,}000{,}000 \;=\; 50{,}000{,}000 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

Convert to MW (1 MW = 3.6 × 10⁶ kJ/h):

$$Q_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{50 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} \;\approx\; 13.89 \;\text{MW}$$

**Part (ii) — Fuel Consumption:**

Accounting for furnace efficiency $\eta = 0.88$:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\eta \cdot \text{CV}} \;=\; \frac{50 \times 10^6}{0.88 \times 42{,}000}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \;=\; \frac{50 \times 10^6}{36{,}960} \;\approx\; 1{,}352.6 \;\text{kg/h}$$

**Part (iii) — Annual Savings After 30 % Heat Integration:**

Fuel saved per hour:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{saved}} \;=\; 0.30 \times 1{,}352.6 \;\approx\; 405.8 \;\text{kg/h}$$

Operating hours per year:

$$t \;=\; 350 \;\text{days} \times 24 \;\text{h/day} \;=\; 8{,}400 \;\text{h/year}$$

Annual fuel saved:

$$M_{\text{saved}} \;=\; 405.8 \times 8{,}400 \;\approx\; 3{,}408{,}720 \;\text{kg/year} \;\approx\; 3{,}409 \;\text{tonnes/year}$$

Annual cost saving:

$$\text{Saving} \;=\; 3{,}409 \;\text{tonnes} \times ₹\,60{,}000/\text{tonne} \;\approx\; ₹\,20.45 \;\text{crore per year}$$

> *Examiner's note: Each sub-part of (b) carries roughly 2 + 2 + 3 = 7 marks. Unit conversion errors and omission of furnace efficiency are the most common mistake points.*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in ADU Questions**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kJ/h to MW** — A 0.5 mark deduction is standard if you write "13.89 × 10⁶ kJ/h" instead of "13.89 MW".
> 2. **Writing Fenske equation without specifying the relative volatility as "average"** — Examiners deduct a full mark for "ambiguous $\alpha$".
> 3. **Confusing Pump-Around with Side Draw** — This is the single most common conceptual error in ADU theory. Memorize the difference!
> 4. **Drawing a PFD without labeling stream temperatures and pressures** — A 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Using volume % in Fenske equation instead of mole %** — Fenske requires mole fractions.
> 6. **Skipping the tray-efficiency step** when computing actual stages from theoretical stages.
> 7. **Forgetting the furnace efficiency factor** in fuel-consumption problems.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **ADU = atmospheric pressure + 350 – 400 °C furnace outlet** — never both at once.
- ✅ Operating pressure is **slightly above 1 bar (1.0 – 1.7 bar a)** to prevent air ingress and to provide driving force for the overhead vapor.
- ✅ Sequence: **Storage → Desalter → Pre-heat → Furnace → Tower → Overhead + Side streams + Residue**.
- ✅ **Salt content** must be reduced to **< 1 PTB** before the furnace to prevent hydrolysis and HCl corrosion.
- ✅ **Flash zone** is the entry point of the two-phase feed; tray numbering starts from the bottom.
- ✅ **Pump-arounds** remove heat and reduce column diameter; they DO NOT leave as product.
- ✅ **Side strippers** (steam or reboiled) tighten product specs by removing light ends.
- ✅ **Reflux ratio** $R$ is the primary operating lever; optimum ≈ $1.2 - 1.5 \times R_{\min}$.
- ✅ **Fenske → $N_{\min}$**, **Underwood → $R_{\min}$**, **Gilliland → actual stages $N$**.
- ✅ **Heat integration** saves **30 – 50 %** of furnace fuel — a key sustainability and economics lever.
- ✅ Top product is **unstable naphtha** (contains C1 – C4) — sent to a stabilizer downstream.
- ✅ Bottom product is **atmospheric residue (AR)** — sent to VDU, FCC, or bitumen plant.
- ✅ **Tray efficiency** for ADU: **0.5 – 0.7**; use it to convert theoretical stages to actual stages.
- ✅ **Coke prevention** is achieved by limiting furnace outlet T to **≤ 400 °C** and tube-skin T to **≤ 600 °C**.
- ✅ **Common process control loops**: TC-101 (furnace T), TC-201 (top T), PC-201 (top P), LC-201 (reflux drum L), FC-201 (feed F).
- ✅ **Why not at higher pressure?** → Cracking, coking, lower distillate yield.
- ✅ **Why not at lower pressure?** → Air ingress, explosion risk, pumping cost.
- ✅ For exam: **always state units, show intermediate steps, and box the final answer.**

> *End of Module 2 — Atmospheric Distillation of Crude Oil Notes. Best of luck for your KTU ESE!*

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vacuum Distillation (VD)** is a secondary physical separation process in petroleum refining in which the **atmospheric residue (long residue)** — the bottom product of the crude oil atmospheric distillation unit (CDU) — is fractionated under sub-atmospheric pressures (typically **25 – 50 mmHg absolute**) to recover additional high-boiling hydrocarbon streams without inducing **thermal cracking** (which begins appreciably above **380 °C / 715 °F**).

The process exploits the principle of **reduced partial pressure** of hydrocarbons by:
1. Lowering the total absolute pressure in the column using **steam ejectors** and **surface condensers**.
2. Introducing **open steam** at the bottom of the column to strip hydrocarbons by further reducing their partial pressure.

The primary products of vacuum distillation are:
- **LVGO** — Light Vacuum Gas Oil (feed to FCC/hydrocracker)
- **HVGO** — Heavy Vacuum Gas Oil (feed to FCC/coker)
- **Vacuum Residue (VR)** — bottom product (feed to visbreaker, coker, or bitumen unit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Instrumentation and control of vacuum distillation focuses on the unique challenges of sub-atmospheric operation — namely, **pressure control of the overhead vacuum system**, **tight tray temperature control** to avoid coking, **steam flow ratio control**, and **feed preheat furnace control** under fired-heater constraints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to boil a heavy, syrupy sauce (like maple syrup) in an open pot. If you crank the heat to high temperatures to vaporize it, the sugars will **burn and caramelize** (this is **thermal cracking**). 

But suppose you put the pot inside a sealed chamber and use a small vacuum pump to lower the air pressure. The syrup will now **boil at a much lower temperature** — you can vaporize the heavy liquids gently without burning them. This is the heart of vacuum distillation.

For the heavy long residue (which contains molecules that would crack above ~380 °C):
- At **1 atm**, the residue boils at temperatures exceeding **600 °C** — way above cracking temperature.
- At **30 mmHg absolute pressure** (≈ 1/25th of atmospheric), the same residue boils at around **380 – 400 °C** — safely below the cracking threshold.

The added **open steam** is like blowing a fan across the syrup surface — it sweeps the vaporized molecules away, allowing more evaporation at an even lower temperature.

## 1.3 Standard Operating Parameters (Bold Highlights)

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|-----------|---------------|
| Overhead absolute pressure | **25 – 50 mmHg** |
| Column top temperature | **120 – 180 °C** |
| Flash zone temperature | **380 – 400 °C** (max) |
| Open steam rate | **0.3 – 0.5 kg steam/kg feed** |
| Steam superheat at outlet | **40 – 50 °C** above saturation |
| Tray pressure drop | **3 – 6 mmHg per tray** |
| Reflux ratio (LVGO pumparound) | **0.2 – 0.5** |

> [!NOTE]
> The temperature ceiling of **~400 °C** in the flash zone is the **fundamental design constraint** of every vacuum distillation column. Going above this point induces C–C bond scission, producing unstable olefins that polymerize into coke, which fouls trays, packing, and furnace tubes.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Chemistry Behind Vacuum Distillation

### 2.1.1 The Pressure–Boiling Point Relationship (Clausius–Clapeyron Principle)

A pure liquid boils when its vapor pressure equals the surrounding total pressure. Lowering the surrounding pressure therefore lowers the boiling point. For a hydrocarbon mixture, the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** (integrated form) governs this:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{-\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Where:
- $P_1, P_2$ = absolute pressures (mmHg or Pa)
- $T_1, T_2$ = corresponding boiling temperatures (K)
- $\Delta H_{vap}$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The logarithmic relationship means a **10-fold reduction in pressure** roughly reduces the boiling point by **80 – 120 °C** for heavy hydrocarbons. This is the single most important principle that justifies the existence of the vacuum column.

### 2.1.2 Effect of Open Steam (Steam Stripping)

When **open (live) steam** is injected at the bottom of the column, the partial pressure of each hydrocarbon is reduced according to **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**:

$$P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$$

$$P_{HC,\,partial} = P_{total} - P_{steam}$$

This means the hydrocarbon boils at the temperature corresponding to $P_{HC,partial}$ rather than $P_{total}$. The **steam-to-oil ratio** (SOR) is typically **0.3 – 0.5 kg steam per kg feed**.

### 2.1.3 Why the 380 °C Ceiling?

Below ~380 °C, hydrocarbon C–C bonds are stable. Above this:
- **Free-radical cracking** initiates.
- Olefins form and rapidly polymerize.
- Coke deposits form on tray decks, packing, and furnace tubes.
- Product streams become unstable and dark.

The vacuum column is therefore **"starved of temperature"** — it does not have enough thermal driving force to make sharp cuts on every product. Hence, vacuum columns use:
- **Pumparound (PA) and pumpback (PB) circuits** to remove heat and create internal reflux.
- **Draw-off trays with side-strippers** to make product cuts.
- Generally **less sharp separation** than the atmospheric column.

## 2.2 Process Flow Architecture

A typical vacuum distillation unit (VDU) consists of:

1. **Feed preheat train** — long residue is preheated by pumparound/product exchangers and finally in a **fired heater** to the flash zone temperature.
2. **Vacuum distillation column** — divided into three zones: wash zone (top), HVGO draw zone (middle), LVGO draw zone (lower), and flash zone (bottom).
3. **Vacuum producing system** — series of **steam ejectors** with **surface condensers** (or barometric condensers + ejectors).
4. **Product recovery** — LVGO and HVGO go to storage; vacuum residue is cooled in a heat exchanger and sent to storage or further processing.
5. **Overhead system** — non-condensables and steam are handled in the ejector train.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Clausius–Clapeyron (integrated) | $\ln(P_2/P_1) = (-\Delta H_{vap}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | $P$ in Pa or mmHg, $T$ in K |
| 2 | Antoine Vapor Pressure | $\log_{10}(P) = A - B / (C + T_{^\circ C})$ | $P$ in mmHg |
| 3 | Dalton's Partial Pressure | $P_i = y_i P_{total}$ | $y_i$ = mole fraction |
| 4 | Overall Material Balance | $F = D + W$ | $F$=feed, $D$=distillate, $W$=bottom |
| 5 | Component Material Balance | $F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$ | Mole fractions |
| 6 | Steam-to-Oil Ratio | $SOR = m_{steam}/m_{feed}$ | kg/kg, typical 0.3 – 0.5 |
| 7 | Equivalent Atmospheric Boiling Point (EABP) | $T_{EABP} \approx T_{vac} + f(P_{vac})$ | Empirical correction |
| 8 | Flashing Vapor Fraction | $y = (H - H_{liquid}) / (H_{vapor} - H_{liquid})$ | From enthalpy balance |
| 9 | Tray Pressure Drop | $\Delta P_{tray} = 3 - 6$ mmHg | For sieve/valve trays |
| 10 | Polytropic Compression (Ejector) | $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ | Steam ejector work |
| 11 | Ideal Gas (Partial Pressure Reduction) | $P_{HC} = P_{tot} - P_{H_2O}$ | Stripping steam partial |
| 12 | Ejector Steam Consumption | $W_{steam} = f(P_{boost},\,P_{suction})$ | Manufacturer curves |
| 13 | Convective Heat Transfer (Furnace) | $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Fired heater duty |
| 14 | Reflux Ratio | $R = L/D$ | Internal to distillate |
| 15 | NRTL / Wilson Activity Coeff. | $\ln \gamma_i$ | For non-ideal mixtures |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board examinations, **Formulas 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8** are the most frequently tested. The Clausius–Clapeyron equation in particular appears in nearly every vacuum-distillation numerical.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The vacuum distillation unit is the **"value-extraction engine"** of a modern refinery:
- A barrel of crude leaves ~**30 – 50 % long residue** after atmospheric distillation. Without vacuum distillation, this heavy fraction would only yield low-value **fuel oil**.
- Vacuum gas oils are the **primary feed for Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** and **hydrocracking units**, which convert them into gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel.
- Vacuum residue feeds **delayed cokers, visbreakers, solvent deasphalters, and bitumen units**.
- The VDU is therefore directly responsible for upgrading ~**50 – 70 %** of every barrel of crude to high-margin transportation fuels.

The instrumentation of this unit is mission-critical: a 5 °C error in the flash zone temperature can shift the LVGO/HVGO cut by several percentage points, costing the refinery millions of dollars per year in yield mis-distribution.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Boiling Point Reduction under Vacuum

### Problem Statement
A heavy vacuum gas oil component has a normal boiling point of **540 °C at 760 mmHg**. Its latent heat of vaporization is approximately **260 kJ/mol**. Estimate its boiling point at a column overhead pressure of **30 mmHg**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_1 = 540 + 273.15 = 813.15 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = ? \text{ K (to be found)}$$

**Step 2 — State the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{-\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known values:**

- $P_1 = 760$ mmHg (atmospheric)
- $P_2 = 30$ mmHg (vacuum)
- $\Delta H_{vap} = 260{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T_1 = 813.15$ K

$$\ln\left(\frac{30}{760}\right) = \frac{-260{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{813.15}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the left-hand side:**

$$\ln(0.03947) = -3.2318$$

**Step 5 — Solve the right-hand side algebraically:**

$$-3.2318 = -31{,}273.5 \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{813.15}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{813.15} = \frac{-3.2318}{-31{,}273.5} = 1.0334 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 1.0334 \times 10^{-4} + \frac{1}{813.15}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 1.0334 \times 10^{-4} + 1.2298 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 1.3331 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $T_2$:**

$$T_2 = \frac{1}{1.3331 \times 10^{-3}} = 750.1 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7 — Convert back to °C:**

$$T_2 = 750.1 - 273.15 = 476.9 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 8 — Apply the cracking constraint:**

This theoretical value (476.9 °C) is **still above** the 380 °C cracking limit. In practice, the **open steam** in the column further reduces the partial pressure. Using Dalton's Law:

$$P_{HC,partial} \approx P_{total} - P_{steam}$$

With ~70 % steam partial pressure at the flash zone, $P_{HC,partial} \approx 9$ mmHg, which lowers the boiling point by another ~80 °C. Therefore, the **actual operating temperature** is around **380 – 400 °C**, comfortably below the cracking threshold.

### Analytical Insight
This derivation quantitatively demonstrates that **a 25× reduction in absolute pressure** (760 → 30 mmHg) **alone** lowers the boiling point by ~336 °C, and the additional **steam stripping** further reduces it by ~80 °C, allowing safe fractionation of residues that would otherwise require destructive cracking.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Balance for Vacuum Column with Three Draw-Offs

### Problem Statement
A vacuum distillation column processes **100,000 kg/h** of long residue. The products are:
- LVGO: 25,000 kg/h
- HVGO: 35,000 kg/h
- Vacuum Residue: 40,000 kg/h

Verify the material balance and compute the **mass yield distribution** (in wt%).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply overall material balance:**

$$F = D_1 + D_2 + W$$

$$100{,}000 = 25{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 100{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute yield of each product (wt%):**

$$\text{LVGO Yield} = \frac{25{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 25.0 \text{ wt\%}$$

$$\text{HVGO Yield} = \frac{35{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 35.0 \text{ wt\%}$$

$$\text{Vacuum Residue Yield} = \frac{40{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 40.0 \text{ wt\%}$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance (simplified, ignoring losses):**

$$Q_{heater} + Q_{feed} = Q_{overhead} + Q_{pumparound} + Q_{residue} + Q_{loss}$$

**Step 4 — Steam-to-feed mass ratio (if steam rate is 30,000 kg/h):**

$$SOR = \frac{30{,}000}{100{,}000} = 0.30 \text{ kg steam/kg feed}$$

This falls within the design range of **0.3 – 0.5 kg/kg**, confirming the design is feasible.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Instrumentation Variables vs. Control Strategy

| Controlled Variable | Sensor Type | Manipulated Variable | Final Control Element | Control Strategy |
|--------------------|-------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| Flash zone temperature | Thermocouple (Type K) | Fuel gas flow to heater | Control valve on fuel line | PID with feed-forward on feed rate |
| Overhead absolute pressure | Capacitance manometer / Baratron | Motive steam to last-stage ejector | Steam control valve | Pressure cascade with temperature |
| LVGO draw temperature | RTD | LVGO product flow | LVGO draw valve | PID with composition trim |
| HVGO draw temperature | RTD | HVGO product flow | HVGO draw valve | PID with composition trim |
| Wash zone temperature | RTD | Wash oil reflux flow | Reflux valve | PID |
| Residue level | Differential pressure (DP) cell | Residue product flow | Residue bottom valve | PID with cascading on temperature |
| Steam-to-oil ratio | Orifice plate (steam) + Coriolis (oil) | Steam flow | Steam control valve | Ratio control |
| Overhead accumulator level | DP / displacer | Reflux flow | Reflux valve | PID |
| Pumparound flow | Orifice / vortex | PA return flow | PA valve | Flow PID |
| Ejector motive steam pressure | Pressure transmitter | Pressure letdown | Motive steam valve | Pressure regulator |

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Why the Flash Zone Temperature is the Master Variable

The flash zone temperature $T_{FZ}$ is controlled tightly because:

**Step 1 — Effect of $T_{FZ}$ on Vapor Fraction $y$:**

Using the flash vaporization equation:

$$y = \frac{H_{feed} - H_{liquid}(T_{FZ}, P_{FZ})}{H_{vapor}(T_{FZ}, P_{FZ}) - H_{liquid}(T_{FZ}, P_{FZ})}$$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity:**
- A +5 °C change in $T_{FZ}$ increases the vapor fraction by ~3 – 5 %.
- This excess vapor must leave through the LVGO/HVGO draw trays.
- The cut points (T5%, T50%, T95% of the products) shift accordingly.

**Step 3 — Economic Impact:**
- A 1 % drift in LVGO/HVGO cut point can shift ~$1 – 3$ million USD/year in margin (typical 100,000 bbl/day refinery).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects students to recognize that **flash zone temperature is the most critical controlled variable** in vacuum distillation, and that it is **tightly cross-coupled** to feed rate, heater outlet temperature, and steam rate.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define vacuum distillation. Why is it preferred over atmospheric distillation for separating long residue?**

**Model Answer:**
Vacuum distillation is a fractionation process carried out at sub-atmospheric pressures (typically **25 – 50 mmHg absolute**) to separate high-boiling hydrocarbons from long residue. It is preferred because:
1. The long residue contains hydrocarbons that would thermally **crack above 380 °C**.
2. By lowering the absolute pressure, the boiling points of these hydrocarbons are reduced (Clausius–Clapeyron effect) below the cracking threshold.
3. Additional **open steam stripping** further lowers hydrocarbon partial pressure, enabling safe fractionation.
4. Higher-value products (LVGO, HVGO) are recovered for FCC/hydrocracking feed.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the typical products of a vacuum distillation unit and state one end-use of each.**

**Model Answer:**
| Product | End-Use |
|---------|---------|
| LVGO (Light Vacuum Gas Oil) | Feed to **hydrocracker** or **FCC** for gasoline/diesel production |
| HVGO (Heavy Vacuum Gas Oil) | Feed to **FCC** as cracking stock |
| Vacuum Residue (VR) | Feed to **delayed coker**, **visbreaker**, **solvent deasphalter**, or **bitumen** unit |
| Overhead distillate (slop wax) | Recycle to atmospheric column or hydrotreater |

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain with a neat sketch the **process flow scheme of a vacuum distillation unit**. Identify the major equipment and state the function of each.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A heavy hydrocarbon has a normal boiling point of **580 °C at 760 mmHg**. Its latent heat of vaporization is **300 kJ/mol**. Estimate the boiling point of this hydrocarbon at **20 mmHg** using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation. Comment on whether open steam would still be required to avoid thermal cracking.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Discuss the **major instrumentation and control loops** of a vacuum distillation column. Draw a labeled block diagram and explain the role of cascade control on the flash zone temperature and ratio control on steam-to-oil.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A vacuum column processes **80,000 kg/h** of long residue and produces the following: LVGO = 20,000 kg/h, HVGO = 30,000 kg/h, VR = 30,000 kg/h. Open steam to the column is **32,000 kg/h**. Calculate:
(i) Overall material balance verification
(ii) Mass yield distribution (wt%)
(iii) Steam-to-oil ratio
(iv) Comment on whether the SOR is within the recommended range.

### Model Solutions
