#### Solution to Question A (a):

The major equipment of a VDU is:

1. **Feed preheat exchangers** – recover heat from pumparound, HVGO, and residue to preheat the long residue.
2. **Fired heater (vacuum heater)** – raises feed to ~390 °C; the heat flux is kept low to avoid coking of tubes.
3. **Vacuum distillation column** – has three functional zones:
   - **Wash zone** (top): washes entrained heavy metals from rising vapors.
   - **LVGO / HVGO draw trays** (middle): sidedraws to side-strippers.
   - **Flash zone** (bottom): vaporization zone with open steam injection.
4. **Steam ejectors (2- or 3-stage)** – pull vacuum on the column; interstage condensers reduce steam load.
5. **Surface condenser / barometric condenser** – condenses overhead vapor.
6. **Overhead accumulator** – separates hydrocarbon liquid from water/steam.
7. **Side strippers (LVGO and HVGO)** – reboiled with steam for sharp product cuts.
8. **Pumparound (PA) pumps and coolers** – remove heat from the column internally.
9. **Vacuum residue cooler** – reduces VR temperature before storage.

#### Solution to Question A (b):

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 580$ °C = $853.15$ K
- $P_1 = 760$ mmHg
- $P_2 = 20$ mmHg
- $\Delta H_{vap} = 300{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Apply Clausius–Clapeyron:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{20}{760}\right) = \frac{-300{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{853.15}\right)$$

$$\ln(0.02632) = -36{,}083.7 \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - 1.1721 \times 10^{-3}\right)$$

$$-3.6386 = -36{,}083.7 \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - 1.1721 \times 10^{-3}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - 1.1721 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0084 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 1.0084 \times 10^{-4} + 1.1721 \times 10^{-3} = 1.2729 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$T_2 = 785.6 \text{ K} = 512.5 \text{ °C}$$

**Comment:**
The boiling point at 20 mmHg is **512.5 °C**, which is **still well above** the 380 °C cracking threshold. Therefore, **open steam stripping is absolutely essential** to bring the hydrocarbon partial pressure even lower. With ~70 % steam partial pressure, the effective partial pressure drops to ~6 mmHg, and the boiling point falls to approximately **420 – 440 °C** — still tight, indicating that even with steam, very heavy components may experience some cracking and coking.

#### Solution to Question B (a):

The major control loops are:

1. **Flash Zone Temperature Cascade Control**:
   - **Master (primary) loop**: Flash zone temperature transmitter (TT) sends signal to temperature controller (TC).
   - **Slave (secondary) loop**: Heater outlet temperature is the inner loop manipulated variable.
   - The master TC setpoint determines the slave TC's setpoint, and the slave controls fuel gas flow via a flow control valve (FCV).
   - **Feed-forward** from feed flow rate is added to the master signal to compensate for feed disturbances.

2. **Steam-to-Oil Ratio Control**:
   - A flow transmitter on the steam line (FT_steam) and a flow transmitter on the feed line (FT_oil) feed into a **ratio station**.
   - The ratio station output drives a steam flow controller, which manipulates the steam control valve.
   - This ensures that **SOR remains constant at 0.3 – 0.5** regardless of feed rate variations.

3. **Overhead Pressure Control**:
   - Pressure transmitter (PT) on the column top measures absolute pressure.
   - Controller (PC) adjusts the motive steam flow to the **last-stage ejector**.
   - The setpoint is typically 25 – 50 mmHg absolute.

4. **Residue Level Control**:
   - Differential pressure (DP) level transmitter (LT) on the column bottom.
   - Controller manipulates the **vacuum residue product valve** to maintain level.
   - This loop is also coupled to the **flash zone temperature** via cascade.

5. **Pumparound Flow / Temperature Control**:
   - Pumparound is a critical heat-removal circuit.
   - Flow controller on PA return line maintains the heat duty removal rate.

#### Solution to Question B (b):

**Given:** F = 80,000 kg/h; LVGO = 20,000 kg/h; HVGO = 30,000 kg/h; VR = 30,000 kg/h; Open steam = 32,000 kg/h.

**(i) Overall Material Balance:**

$$F = LVGO + HVGO + VR$$
$$80{,}000 = 20{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \checkmark$$

**Balance verified.**

**(ii) Mass Yield Distribution (wt%):**

$$\text{LVGO} = \frac{20{,}000}{80{,}000} \times 100 = 25.0 \text{ wt\%}$$

$$\text{HVGO} = \frac{30{,}000}{80{,}000} \times 100 = 37.5 \text{ wt\%}$$

$$\text{VR} = \frac{30{,}000}{80{,}000} \times 100 = 37.5 \text{ wt\%}$$

**(iii) Steam-to-Oil Ratio:**

$$SOR = \frac{m_{steam}}{m_{feed}} = \frac{32{,}000}{80{,}000} = 0.40 \text{ kg steam/kg feed}$$

**(iv) Comment:**
A SOR of **0.40 kg/kg** lies **comfortably within the recommended range of 0.3 – 0.5 kg/kg** for typical vacuum distillation. This indicates that the design provides sufficient stripping steam to reduce hydrocarbon partial pressures and prevent excessive cracking. The steam rate also must be optimized for energy efficiency — too high SOR increases ejector/condenser duty and utility costs, while too low SOR risks coking and poor cut quality.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Vacuum Distillation Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting temperature conversion to Kelvin** in Clausius–Clapeyron — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Sign error** in the right-hand side of Clausius–Clapeyron: the term must be $(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ or $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ consistently with the formula sign.
> 3. **Not commenting on cracking constraint** — even with correct math, the examiner expects the student to evaluate whether the boiling point is safe (below 380 °C).
> 4. **Confusing LVGO and HVGO** as side-draw positions — HVGO is drawn ABOVE LVGO (the heavier product exits at a tray with HIGHER temperature, but the tray is physically LOWER in the column? Actually it is drawn from above the LVGO tray in most designs). Verify with your textbook.
> 5. **Omitting units in mass balance** — write "kg/h" explicitly.
> 6. **Failing to mention that the feed to VDU is long residue from CDU**, not crude oil directly. Many students make this error.
> 7. **Ignoring the role of pumparound** — it is the primary heat-removal mechanism, not external reflux.
> 8. **In control questions, omitting feed-forward and cascade** — these are advanced concepts that earn full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Vacuum Distillation of Crude Oil**

- **Definition**: Distillation under sub-atmospheric pressure (25 – 50 mmHg) of long residue to recover LVGO, HVGO, and VR without thermal cracking.
- **Why vacuum?** Clausius–Clapeyron relation reduces boiling point; cracking temperature ceiling is ~380 °C.
- **Steam stripping**: Open steam reduces hydrocarbon partial pressure; SOR = 0.3 – 0.5 kg/kg.
- **Products**: LVGO (FCC feed), HVGO (FCC feed), VR (coker/visbreaker feed).
- **Key equation**: $\ln(P_2/P_1) = (-\Delta H_{vap}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ — commit to memory.
- **Flash zone temperature** is the master controlled variable; tightly cross-coupled to feed rate, heater outlet, and steam rate.
- **Cascade control**: Flash zone TT → master TC → slave TC on heater outlet → fuel gas FCV.
- **Ratio control**: Steam-to-oil ratio station maintains constant SOR despite feed swings.
- **Vacuum system**: 2- or 3-stage steam ejectors with interstage condensers.
- **Pumparound** = internal reflux and heat-removal mechanism; **not** the same as external reflux.
- **Pressure control**: Adjust motive steam to the last-stage ejector; setpoint = 25 – 50 mmHg(a).
- **Material balance**: $F = D_1 + D_2 + W$ (3-product column).
- **Energy balance**: $Q_{heater} = Q_{overhead} + Q_{PA} + Q_{products} + Q_{loss}$.
- **Side strippers**: Use steam to refine LVGO and HVGO cuts; reboiled for sharp separation.
- **Overhead system**: Surface condenser + accumulator + reflux pump; non-condensables routed to ejector system.
- **Coking prevention**: Heater tube skin temperature < 540 °C; furnace draft controlled to avoid hot spots.
- **Pumparound duty** = ~40 – 50 % of total column heat removal; critical for column hydraulics.
- **Conversion to EABP**: Vacuum TBP curves are converted to atmospheric equivalent using empirical correlation (ASTM D-1160).
- **Valve tray pressure drop**: 3 – 6 mmHg/tray limits column to 25 – 35 theoretical stages.
- **Refinery economics**: VDU is the gateway to converting heavy ends to transportation fuels; ~50 – 70 % of crude ends up as VDU product or feed.
- **Safety**: Loss of vacuum causes immediate temperature spike → coking → fire hazard; interlock trips fuel gas on loss of steam to ejectors.
- **Modern trends**: Dividing-wall columns, structured packing, heat-integrated pumparounds, and digital twin-based APC (Advanced Process Control) are increasingly deployed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coking Thermal Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Coking Thermal Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Coking Thermal Process** is a high-severity, non-catalytic, carbon-rejection process used in petroleum refining to convert low-value, heavy hydrocarbon residues (such as vacuum residue, atmospheric residue, tar sands bitumen, and deasphalted bottoms) into more valuable lighter distillates (naphtha, diesel, gas oil) and **petroleum coke**.

The process operates on the principle of **thermal cracking at severe conditions** (typically **485 °C to 510 °C** and near-atmospheric pressure in delayed coking, or **510 °C to 540 °C** in fluid coking), where the C–C bonds in heavy hydrocarbon molecules are broken via free-radical chain mechanisms, leading to the formation of:
- **Lighter hydrocarbon vapours** (sent to fractionator)
- **Coke** (a solid carbonaceous deposit) that deposits inside the reactor/drum
- **Wet gas** (C1–C4 hydrocarbons)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Coking):**
> The coking process is an important conversion technology in secondary refining. Students are expected to understand the *types of coking*, *process flow*, *critical operating variables*, and the **instrumentation and control loops** that maintain safe, efficient operation.

The three principal variants used globally in petrochemical industries are:

| # | Process | Reactor Type | Cycle Mode |
|---|---------|--------------|------------|
| 1 | **Delayed Coking** | Coke Drums (batch) | Semi-continuous (24-h cycle) |
| 2 | **Fluid Coking** | Fluidized Bed Reactor | Continuous |
| 3 | **Flexicoking** | Fluidized Bed + Gasifier | Continuous + Coke Gasification |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker with charcoal inside**, but instead of cooking food, you are "cooking" crude oil.

- You have a **thick, gooey, black heavy oil** (like molasses) that nobody wants to buy.
- You heat it in a sealed chamber to very high temperatures.
- The thick oil **breaks apart** into two useful products: lighter liquid fuels (which escape as vapour) and **solid black coke** (which stays behind like charcoal in a BBQ).
- The "delay" in delayed coking simply means: the oil is heated *rapidly* in a furnace but the *coking reaction* is **delayed** until the oil reaches a large, empty drum, where it has time to crack and solidify into coke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is "delay" important?**
> If coking happened inside the furnace tubes, the tubes would plug with solid coke within minutes. By moving the reaction to a separate, large drum, we keep the furnace tubes clean and continuous — a brilliant example of process engineering intuition.

## 1.3 Classification of Petroleum Coke

The coke produced is not waste — it has market value based on its **sulphur content** and **structure**:

- **Fuel-grade coke** (high S, used in cement kilns) — typical of delayed coker
- **Anode-grade coke** (low S, low metals, used in aluminium smelting) — requires feedstock desulphurization
- **Needle coke** (from decant oil / slurry) — used in graphite electrodes
- **Shot coke / Sponge coke** — morphology depends on feedstock and operating conditions

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Coking reaction temperature:** **485 °C – 510 °C** (delayed) ; **510 °C – 540 °C** (fluid)
- **Coke drum operating pressure:** **1.05 – 3.5 kg/cm²(g)**
- **Steam-to-hydrocarbon ratio (delayed coker):** typically **0.5 – 1.0 kg steam per 100 kg feed**
- **Coke yield (wt% on feed):** **15 – 35 %** depending on Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)
- **Coke drum cycle time:** **18 – 24 hours** (delayed coking)
- **Heat of reaction:** **Endothermic**, ~**300 – 500 kJ/kg of feed cracked**
- **API gravity of feed:** typically **< 10° API** (very heavy)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Coking — The "Why" Behind the Process

The coking reaction is **free-radical chain scission** of large hydrocarbon molecules. The mechanism can be broken into three logical stages:

### Step 1: Initiation
The weakest C–C bond in the heavy residue breaks homolytically to form two free radicals.

$$\text{R–R'} \xrightarrow{\Delta \, (450{-}510°C)} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R'}^{\bullet}$$

### Step 2: Propagation (β-scission)
The free radical attacks a nearby large molecule and steals a hydrogen atom, producing a **smaller stable hydrocarbon** (vapour product) and a **new radical** that continues the chain.

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R''–H} \longrightarrow \text{R–H} + \text{R''}^{\bullet}$$

### Step 3: Termination & Condensation
When two radicals combine or when poly-aromatic molecules undergo **dehydrogenative condensation**, large polyaromatic sheets stack up to form **mesophase** — the precursor of solid coke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Coke is not just "carbon". It is a complex poly-aromatic solid containing 85–95 % carbon, 3–5 % hydrogen, 1–8 % sulphur, and trace metals (Ni, V, Fe). Its morphology (sponge, shot, or needle) is determined by the **residence time**, **temperature**, and **feedstock aromaticity**.

## 2.2 Process Variants — Comparative Analysis

### 2.2.1 Delayed Coking (Most Common in Industry)

- Feed is preheated and routed to a **fired heater**, where it reaches ~**485–505 °C** in the tubes.
- The hot feed then flows into the **bottom of a tall, insulated coke drum** (typically **6–9 m diameter, 25–30 m tall**).
- Cracking and coke deposition occur inside the drum over a **24-hour cycle**.
- Two drums operate in **parallel swing mode** (A & B) — one is filling, the other is being decoked.

### 2.2.2 Fluid Coking

- Uses a **fluidized bed of coke particles** as the heat transfer medium.
- Feed is sprayed onto hot coke particles (heat carrier at ~**540–570 °C**).
- Coke is deposited on the particles, and vapour products exit the top to a scrubber/fractionator.
- **Burner/heater** regenerates coke by partial combustion to supply process heat.

### 2.2.3 Flexicoking

- Extension of fluid coking.
- Surplus coke is sent to a **gasifier** where it reacts with air and steam:

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad ; \quad \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$$

- Produces a **low-Btu fuel gas** (used internally as heater fuel), minimizing solid coke yield.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU examinations on coking processes. Memorize the symbols, units, and applicability.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C_{\text{yield}} = a \cdot \text{CCR} + b$ | Approximate coke yield from **Conradson Carbon Residue** (CCR); typical $a \approx 1.6$, $b \approx -2.0$ | wt % |
| 2 | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{fill}} + t_{\text{steam}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{drain}} + t_{\text{decoke}}$ | Total drum cycle time | h |
| 3 | $V_{\text{feed,cycle}} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot H \cdot \rho_{\text{feed}}^{-1} \cdot \phi_{\text{vapor}}$ | Volume of feed per drum cycle | m³ |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{furnace}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})$ | Furnace heat duty | kW (kJ/s) |
| 5 | $\text{SHR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{steam}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Steam-to-Hydrocarbon ratio | kg / 100 kg |
| 6 | $\text{TBP}_{\text{cut}} = f(\text{CCR}, \text{API})$ | Coke yield correlation with TBP cut point | °C |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{\text{drum}} = K \cdot \mu \cdot v \cdot L$ | Pressure drop across coke bed (Darcy-type) | bar |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{combustion}}} \times 100$ | Furnace thermal efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\dot{n}_{\text{coke}} = k \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot C^{n}$ | Coke formation rate (simplified kinetic) | kmol/s |
| 10 | $R_{\text{vapour}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{out}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{feed}}}$ | Vapour yield ratio | - |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** When using the Conradson correlation, students often forget to convert CCR from **wt% on feed** basis. Always check the basis of the CCR value given in the problem.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Economics:** Coking unlocks value from the **bottom of the barrel** — feedstock that would otherwise be sold cheaply as residual fuel oil (FOB).
- **Integration with FCC:** Coker gas oil is a **superior FCC feed** because it has low metal content.
- **Carbon-neutrality Roadmap:** Coker is a candidate site for **carbon capture** because its CO₂ streams are concentrated and high-purity.
- **Instrumentation Justification:** A modern 50,000 BPD delayed coker may have **> 2000 I/O points** feeding the DCS — making it one of the most heavily instrumented units in the refinery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Coke Yield from Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)

The Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR) test measures the carbonaceous residue remaining after evaporation and pyrolysis of an oil sample. Empirically, the **coke yield in a delayed coker** correlates with CCR as:

$$Y_{\text{coke}} = 1.6 \cdot \text{CCR} - 2.0$$

Let us derive a **mass balance** to verify the rationale behind this empirical relation.

### Working: Mass Balance around a Coke Drum

For a steady-state fill cycle, the input to the drum equals the sum of the outputs.

$$\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} + \dot{m}_{\text{vapour}} + \dot{m}_{\text{liquid}}$$

In the limiting case where all non-vapour heavy ends are converted to coke (worst-case coke make), and where vapours + liquids = feed − coke:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{coke, max}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{M_{\text{vap+liq}}}{M_{\text{feed}}}\right)$$

When normalized to weight percent:

$$Y_{\text{coke}} \, (\text{wt\%}) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$$

The empirical constants **1.6** (slope) and **–2.0** (intercept) in the correlation reflect:
- Slope > 1 because **secondary condensation reactions** add extra carbon to the coke beyond the original CCR
- Negative intercept because **some CCR material** does not end up in the drum coke (it exits as heavy gas oil that is recycled to extinction)

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** A vacuum residue feed has CCR = **18 wt%** and feed rate = **50,000 kg/h**.

**Step 1 — Coke yield on feed:**
$$Y_{\text{coke}} = (1.6 \times 18) - 2.0 = 28.8 - 2.0 = 26.8 \,\text{wt\%}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow of coke produced per hour:**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = 0.268 \times 50{,}000 = 13{,}400 \,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Daily coke production (24 h):**
$$m_{\text{coke,day}} = 13{,}400 \times 24 = 321{,}600 \,\text{kg/day} \approx 321.6 \,\text{tonnes/day}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using material balance:**
Total vapour + liquid products = $50{,}000 - 13{,}400 = 36{,}600$ kg/h, which is consistent with a typical delayed coker where total distillate yield is 65–75 wt%.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Even a 1 wt% error in CCR estimation translates to ~500 kg/h of coke, i.e., **~12 tonnes/day** at 50,000 kg/h feed. This is why **accurate online measurement of feed CCR (or correlated properties like density, viscosity, micro-carbon residue)** is a strategic instrumentation goal in modern refineries.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Furnace Heat Duty

The coker furnace heats feed from a relatively cold inlet to a precise outlet temperature (typically 485–505 °C), at a controlled pressure.

The general heat-transfer equation is:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of feed (kg/s)
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity (kJ/kg·K)
- $T_{\text{out}}$ = furnace outlet temperature (°C)
- $T_{\text{in}}$ = furnace inlet temperature (°C)

### Detailed Step-by-Step for a 100,000 kg/h Feed

**Given:**
- $\dot{m} = 100{,}000 \,\text{kg/h} = 27.78 \,\text{kg/s}$
- $T_{\text{in}} = 350 \,°C$
- $T_{\text{out}} = 495 \,°C$
- $C_p$ (vacuum residue) ≈ **2.5 kJ/kg·K**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta T$:**
$$\Delta T = 495 - 350 = 145 \,°C \; (\text{or } 145 \,K)$$

**Step 2 — Compute sensible heat:**
$$Q = 27.78 \times 2.5 \times 145 = 10{,}070.25 \,\text{kJ/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kW (1 kJ/s = 1 kW):**
$$Q = 10{,}070.25 \,\text{kW} \approx 10.07 \,\text{MW}$$

**Step 4 — Add ~5 % heat loss to the radiant section:**
$$Q_{\text{furnace,total}} = \dfrac{10.07}{0.95} \approx 10.6 \,\text{MW}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MMBTU/h (1 MW = 3.412 MMBTU/h):**
$$Q = 10.6 \times 3.412 \approx 36.17 \,\text{MMBTU/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this calculation critical for instrumentation?**
> The furnace outlet temperature is the **single most important controlled variable** in the coker. A deviation of just +5 °C can accelerate coking inside the tubes, leading to **tube plugging and unscheduled shutdown**. The temperature control loop (TIC) is therefore tuned very tightly, with **multiple redundant thermocouples** in the crossover and transfer line.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Coke Drum Cycle Time

The drum must fill, steam-strip, cool, drain, and decoke. The fill time depends on the volume of the drum and the volumetric flow of feed.

$$t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{drum,available}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{feed, at T,P}}}$$

**Given (numerical example):**
- Drum diameter: $D = 7.5 \,\text{m}$
- Drum height (vapour space + coke): $H = 25 \,\text{m}$
- Allowable coke fill height: $H_{\text{fill}} = 18 \,\text{m}$
- Feed density at drum conditions: $\rho = 750 \,\text{kg/m}^3$
- Feed flow rate: $\dot{m} = 50{,}000 \,\text{kg/h}$

**Step 1 — Volumetric feed flow rate at drum conditions:**
$$\dot{V}_{\text{feed}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{750} = 66.67 \,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of coke in drum after one cycle (assume coke density 800 kg/m³):**
$$V_{\text{coke,cycle}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}}{\rho_{\text{coke}}}$$

**Step 3 — Required drum volume (assuming 80% fill):**
$$V_{\text{drum,req}} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot 0.8 \cdot H = \dfrac{\pi (7.5)^2}{4} \cdot 0.8 \cdot 25 = 883.6 \,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Time to fill the available drum volume:**
$$t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{drum,req}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{feed}}} = \dfrac{883.6}{66.67} \approx 13.25 \,\text{h}$$

**Step 5 — Total cycle time** (add steam strip = 1.5 h, cooling = 2.0 h, drain = 0.5 h, decoke = 5.0 h):
$$t_{\text{cycle}} = 13.25 + 1.5 + 2.0 + 0.5 + 5.0 = 22.25 \,\text{h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Instrumentation Link:** A **level transmitter (LT)** on the coke drum is *not* used (coke is a solid, not a fluid). Instead, the **fill time is controlled by a flow totalizer** on the feed line. The DCS automatically switches the feed from Drum A to Drum B when the cycle timer elapses, based on **interlock logic (XY-101)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Variable vs. Critical Instrument

| Process Variable | Critical Instrument | Range | Control Loop ID | Alarm Priority |
|------------------|--------------------|-------|-----------------|----------------|
| Furnace outlet T | Dual thermocouple (Type K) | 450 – 510 °C | TIC-101 | HIGH-HIGH |
| Coke drum pressure | Pressure transmitter | 0.5 – 4.0 kg/cm²(g) | PIC-201 | HIGH-HIGH |
| Feed flow | Coriolis mass flowmeter | 0 – 150 t/h | FIC-301 | HIGH |
| Steam to feed ratio | Ratio controller | 0.3 – 1.5 | FFIC-302 | MEDIUM |
| Fractionator top T | RTD | 100 – 160 °C | TIC-401 | MEDIUM |
| Quench water flow | Magnetic flowmeter | 0 – 500 m³/h | FIC-501 | HIGH |
| Coke drum level (equiv.) | Drum weight cell / dP | 0 – 800 tonnes | WI-601 | HIGH |
| Combustion O₂ in furnace | Zirconia O₂ analyser | 1 – 5 % | AIC-701 | MEDIUM |
| Coke drum switchover | Solenoid valve interlock | ON/OFF | XY-801 | SAFETY |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Delayed Coking Process

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a Delayed Coking Unit (DCU), highlighting the **instrumentation and control points** that the KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize.

```mermaid
graph TD
    FEED[Vacuum Residue Feed Tank] --> FTP[FT-301 Feed Flow Transmitter Coriolis]
    FTP --> FURN[Coker Fired Heater 485-505 C with TIC-101 on outlet]
    FURN --> SW4[Four-Way Switchover Valve XY-801A B]
    SW4 --> DRUM_A[Coke Drum A Coking Mode]
    SW4 --> DRUM_B[Coke Drum B Decoking Mode]
    DRUM_A --> VAP1[Overhead Vapour Line to Fractionator]
    DRUM_B --> VAP1
    VAP1 --> FRAC[Main Fractionator T-401 with TIC-401]
    FRAC --> PROD[Distillate Products Naphtha Diesel VGO]
    DRUM_A --> STRP[Steam Stripping Ring 0.5-1.0 kg per 100 kg feed]
    DRUM_B --> STRP
    DRUM_A --> QUENCH[Quench Water FIC-501 for Cooling and Decoking]
    DRUM_B --> QUENCH
    DRUM_A --> PRESS[Pressure PIC-201 Relief Valve PSV-202]
    DRUM_B --> PRESS
    FURN --> AIR[Combustion Air with AIC-701 Oxygen Analyser 1-5 percent]
    FURN --> FUEL[Fuel Gas Supply with FIC-701]
    DRUM_A --> WT[Weight Indicator WI-601 on Drum A]
    DRUM_B --> WTB[Weight Indicator WI-601 on Drum B]
    FTP --> DCS[Central DCS Honeywell Experion]
    FURN --> DCS
    DRUM_A --> DCS
    DRUM_B --> DCS
    FRAC --> DCS
    DCS --> OP[Operator Console with Alarm Management]
    DCS --> ESD[Emergency Shutdown System ESD-901]
```

## 4.2 Functional Control Loop Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the **instrumentation signal chain** for the most critical control loop in the coker: the **furnace outlet temperature loop**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    TT[T Type K Thermocouple in Transfer Line] --> TG[TT-101 Field Transmitter 4-20 mA HART]
    TG --> JBOX[Junction Box with Cold Junction Compensation]
    JBOX --> DCS[Analog Input Card in DCS]
    DCS --> CTRL[PID Controller Block in DCS with Setpoint 495 C]
    CTRL --> OP[Operator HMI Setpoint and PV Display]
    CTRL --> AO[Analog Output Card 4-20 mA]
    AO --> IPU[Current to Pressure Converter I to P 3-15 psig]
    IPU --> CV[Control Valve on Fuel Gas Line to Burner]
    CV --> BURN[Burner Firing Rate]
    BURN --> HEAT[Furnace Heat Release]
    HEAT --> TT
```

## 4.3 Interlock Logic for Drum Switchover (Simplified)

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1{Cycle Timer Elapsed Yes} --> C2{Drum A Pressure Equalized Yes}
    C2 --> C3{Steam Stripping Complete Yes}
    C3 --> C4{Quench Water Flow Confirmed Yes}
    C4 --> C5{Quench Water Level in Drum OK Yes}
    C5 --> C6[Activate XY-801 Transfer Line to Drum B]
    C6 --> C7[Open Drum B Inlet Valve]
    C7 --> C8[Close Drum A Inlet Valve]
    C8 --> C9[Initiate Decoking Sequence on Drum A]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight (KTU Context):** The drum switchover is one of the most safety-critical events in the refinery. A wrong sequence can cause **vapour breakthrough to atmosphere** or **water-hammer in hot coke drums**, leading to catastrophic failure. The interlock logic is implemented in **SIL-3 rated Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** separate from the DCS.

## 4.4 Instrumentation Hierarchy (ISA-95 Aligned)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0[Level 0 Field Instruments Sensors and Actuators] --> L1[Level 1 DCS and PLC Control Loops and Interlocks]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2 Supervisory Control SCADA HMI Alarm Management]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Plant Operations Management MES Historian]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Enterprise Resource Planning ERP]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Q: Differentiate between Delayed Coking and Fluid Coking processes in terms of reactor type, cycle mode, and operating temperature.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Delayed Coking | Fluid Coking |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Reactor type** | Empty cylindrical drum (no fluidization) | Fluidized bed of coke particles |
| **Cycle mode** | Semi-continuous (swing drum pair) | Fully continuous |
| **Operating temperature** | 485 – 505 °C | 510 – 540 °C |
| **Heat carrier** | Fired furnace (external) | Hot coke particles (internal) |
| **Coke removal** | Hydraulic decoking | Burner regenerator (combustion) |
| **Steam-to-feed ratio** | 0.5 – 1.0 kg/100 kg | Very low |

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Q: List any three critical instruments used in a Delayed Coker and state their function.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **TT-101 – Furnace Outlet Thermocouple (Type K):** Measures the transfer line temperature (485–505 °C). Critical control variable; deviation +5 °C can plug furnace tubes.
2. **PIC-201 – Coke Drum Pressure Transmitter:** Monitors drum back-pressure. High-High alarm triggers ESD and opens pressure relief valve PSV-202.
3. **FT-301 – Coriolis Mass Flowmeter on Feed:** Provides accurate mass flow for furnace firing control, steam ratio control, and drum cycle time computation.
4. **WI-601 – Drum Weight Indicator:** Calculates coke inventory by weighing the drum on load cells, used to determine switchover timing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE out of TWO** questions from the module. Each Part B question is **14 marks** split into (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**.

---

### **Question B1 (Option A)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **process flow and instrumentation of a Delayed Coking Unit**, clearly identifying the critical control loops for **furnace outlet temperature**, **coke drum pressure**, and **steam-to-hydrocarbon ratio**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Delayed coking is a semi-continuous thermal cracking process used to convert vacuum residue into distillates and petroleum coke. Two coke drums operate in swing mode (one filling, one decoking).

**Process Flow Description (3 Marks):**
- Heavy residue from storage is pumped through **FT-301** (Coriolis mass flowmeter).
- Feed passes through the **fired heater** where it is heated to 485–505 °C (controlled by **TIC-101** on the transfer line).
- Hot feed enters the active coke drum via the **four-way switchover valve XY-801**.
- Vapour products go overhead to the **main fractionator (T-401)** where naphtha, diesel, and gas oil are separated.
- After the cycle, the drum is steam-stripped, water-quenched, and hydraulically decoked.

**Critical Control Loops (3 Marks):**

1. **Furnace Outlet Temperature Loop (TIC-101):**
   - PV: Transfer line thermocouple TT-101.
   - CV: Fuel gas control valve on the burner.
   - Setpoint: 495 ± 2 °C.
   - Function: Prevents tube coking and ensures proper cracking severity.

2. **Coke Drum Pressure Loop (PIC-201):**
   - PV: Drum top pressure transmitter.
   - CV: Back-pressure control valve on vapour line.
   - Setpoint: 1.5 kg/cm²(g) typical.
   - Function: Prevents vapour leakage and maintains product partial pressure.

3. **Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio Loop (FFIC-302):**
   - PV: Steam flow / Feed flow (ratio).
   - CV: Steam control valve to stripping ring.
   - Setpoint: 0.5 – 1.0 kg steam per 100 kg feed.
   - Function: Minimizes coke make, improves vapour yield.

**(b)** A delayed coker is fed with **40,000 kg/h** of vacuum residue at **350 °C** and produces coke at a rate given by the empirical correlation $Y_{\text{coke}} = 1.6 \cdot \text{CCR} - 2.0$. The feed has a CCR of **20 wt%** and a specific heat of **2.4 kJ/kg·K**. The furnace outlet temperature is **500 °C**. Compute:
   (i) The **coke production rate in kg/h**
   (ii) The **furnace heat duty in MW**
   (iii) The **24-hour coke production in tonnes** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Coke Production Rate (2 Marks):**
$$Y_{\text{coke}} = 1.6 \times 20 - 2.0 = 32.0 - 2.0 = 30.0 \,\text{wt\%}$$
$$\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = 0.30 \times 40{,}000 = 12{,}000 \,\text{kg/h}$$

**(ii) Furnace Heat Duty (3 Marks):**
$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$
$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{3600} = 11.11 \,\text{kg/s}$$
$$\Delta T = 500 - 350 = 150 \,°C$$
$$Q = 11.11 \times 2.4 \times 150 = 4{,}000 \,\text{kW} = 4.0 \,\text{MW}$$

**(iii) 24-Hour Coke Production (2 Marks):**
$$m_{\text{coke,24h}} = 12{,}000 \times 24 = 288{,}000 \,\text{kg} = 288 \,\text{tonnes}$$

---

### **Question B2 (Option B)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of Flexicoking** and highlight **two key differences** from Fluid Coking. Draw a **functional block diagram** of Flexicoking showing the gasifier loop. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (3 Marks):**
Flexicoking is a fully continuous thermal cracking process that uses a fluidized bed of coke particles, with the additional feature of a **coke gasifier** integrated at the bottom of the reactor. Instead of burning all the surplus coke (as in fluid coking), flexicoking gasifies part of it with air and steam to produce a **low-Btu fuel gas** (used internally as furnace fuel). The CO₂ produced is captured, giving a near-zero net solid coke yield.

**Key Differences from Fluid Coking (2 Marks):**

1. **Coke gasifier section** is present in Flexicoking but absent in Fluid Coking.
2. **Net coke yield is near zero** in Flexicoking (most coke is gasified) versus **15 – 25 wt%** in Fluid Coking.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4 (flexicoking flow: Reactor → Heater → Gasifier → Low-Btu Gas → Internal Fuel).

**(b)** Discuss the **role of instrumentation** in **safe drum switchover** of a delayed coker. List at least **five interlock conditions** that must be satisfied before the four-way valve XY-801 may be actuated. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Instrumentation (3 Marks):**
Drum switchover is the most safety-critical operation in a delayed coker. Instrumentation ensures:
- The filled drum is **steam-stripped completely** (no residual liquid hydrocarbons that could cause explosive re-vapourization on water quench).
- The decoking drum is **fully cooled and dewatered** (no live steam contact with hot coke > 200 °C that could cause water-hammer).
- The transfer line is **free of coke obstruction** and at proper temperature.
- The DCS and **SIL-3 Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** vote unanimously to allow switchover.

**Five Interlock Conditions (4 Marks):**

1. **Cycle timer elapsed** (e.g., 22 h).
2. **Drum A pressure equalized** with fractionator (drum top and bottom PIs within ±0.2 kg/cm²).
3. **Steam-stripping duration complete** (minimum 1.5 h).
4. **Quench water flow confirmed** (FIC-501 > minimum setpoint).
5. **Quench water level in decoking drum** is below safe fill mark.
6. **Vapour outlet temperature** from drum > 100 °C (confirms hydrocarbon displacement).
7. **ESD system is healthy and not in bypass.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **lose 2 marks** for failing to specify the **setpoint values** (e.g., 495 °C for TIC, 1.5 kg/cm²(g) for PIC) — always quantify your answer.
> - Another common deduction is **not mentioning the role of SIS (Safety Instrumented System)** separately from the DCS — modern KTU papers expect knowledge of **IEC 61511 SIL ratings**.
> - Avoid confusing **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)** with **Micro Carbon Residue (MCR)** — they are different tests; MCR is typically 1.05 × CCR.
> - Do **not** confuse **delayed coking** (drum) with **catalytic cracking** (FCC) — the former is thermal and non-catalytic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 – Module 2: Coking Thermal Process)**

- ⭐ **Three variants:** Delayed Coking, Fluid Coking, Flexicoking.
- ⭐ **Delayed Coking** is the most common — two drums operating in **24-h swing cycle**.
- ⭐ **Operating temperature:** 485 – 510 °C (delayed) ; 510 – 540 °C (fluid).
- ⭐ **Coke yield (wt% on feed)** correlates with CCR: $Y_{\text{coke}} \approx 1.6 \cdot \text{CCR} - 2.0$.
- ⭐ **Critical instruments:** TT-101 (furnace outlet T), PIC-201 (drum P), FT-301 (feed flow), WI-601 (drum weight), XY-801 (switchover valve).
- ⭐ **Steam-to-hydrocarbon ratio:** 0.5 – 1.0 kg steam / 100 kg feed.
- ⭐ **Furnace heat duty:** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$.
- ⭐ **Cycle time** = fill + steam + cool + drain + decoke ≈ **22 h**.
- ⭐ **Drum switchover** must satisfy ≥ 5 interlock conditions and is governed by **SIL-3 SIS**, not just DCS.
- ⭐ **Flexicoking** integrates a **coke gasifier** → near-zero net coke yield → low-Btu fuel gas for internal use.
- ⭐ **Coke is NOT a waste** — it has market value as **fuel-grade, anode-grade, or needle coke** depending on S and morphology.
- ⭐ The coker is a **carbon-rejection** process — it removes carbon as coke, thereby increasing the **H/C ratio** of the liquid products.
- ⭐ **Endothermic** reaction; furnace is the primary energy input.
- ⭐ **Tube plugging** in the furnace is the #1 operational hazard — controlled by tight TIC-101 tuning.
- ⭐ **ISA-95 instrumentation hierarchy** applies: field → DCS → supervisory → MES → ERP.

---

> **End of Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PEEIT861 | Module 2 | Coking Thermal Process**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Chemical Oxidation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Chemical Oxidation** is a unit operation in petrochemical effluent treatment wherein an oxidizing chemical agent is introduced into a wastewater stream to transfer electrons from the target pollutant (the reductant) to the oxidant. The reaction converts dissolved, suspended, or emulsified organic and inorganic contaminants into oxidized end-products — typically **CO₂, H₂O, mineral acids, and insoluble oxides** — thereby reducing the Chemical Oxygen Demand (**COD**), Biochemical Oxygen Demand (**BOD**), colour, toxicity, and refractory load of the effluent. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):** > *Chemical oxidation in the context of petrochemical wastewater treatment is a controlled redox process in which a strong oxidizing species ($O_3$, $H_2O_2$, $Cl_2$, $ClO_2$, $KMnO_4$, or the OH• radical) accepts electrons from organic micropollutants, converting them to stable inorganic products or more biodegradable intermediates.* The half-reaction governing every chemical oxidation step follows the universal redox form: $$ \text{Oxidant} + n\,e^{-} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Reduced Product} $$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Rust-Accelerator Picture"** > > Imagine a dirty oily tank of refinery wastewater. The dissolved hydrocarbons and phenols are essentially "stored fuel" packed with chemical energy. If we just let the tank sit, microbes would slowly burn this fuel (biological oxidation — slow, mild, ambient). **Chemical oxidation is like tossing a match into the tank** — we inject a powerful oxidizer (the "match") that rapidly strips electrons from the pollutants, "burning" them in a controlled, engineered manner without the need for high-temperature incineration. > > The stronger the oxidizer, the faster and more complete the "burn." This is why selection of the oxidant (the instrument-level decision) is the heart of the unit operation. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |----------|--------|---------------|------| | Standard oxidation potential (Ozone) | $E^{\circ}$ | **+2.07 V** | V (vs SHE) | | Standard oxidation potential (H₂O₂) | $E^{\circ}$ | **+1.78 V** | V (vs SHE) | | Standard oxidation potential (Cl₂) | $E^{\circ}$ | **+1.36 V** | V (vs SHE) | | Standard oxidation potential (KMnO₄) | $E^{\circ}$ | **+1.51 V** | V (vs SHE) | | Molar mass of O₃ | $M_{O_3}$ | **48.00** | g/mol | | Molar mass of H₂O₂ | $M_{H_2O_2}$ | **34.0147** | g/mol | | Dose stoichiometric ratio (O₃ : COD) | — | **~ 2 – 3 g O₃ / g COD** | g/g | ### 1.4 Why Chemical Oxidation? (KTU 2024 Motivation) - Petrochemical effluents contain **recalcitrant organics** (phenols, PAHs, cyanides, sulphides, mercaptans) that resist conventional biological treatment. - Biological systems are **shock-sensitive**; chemical oxidation is a reliable **polishing or pre-treatment** step. - Produces **instantaneous** COD reduction — useful as a backup when the biotreatment plant is upset. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mechanistic Classification (KTU High-Yield) Chemical oxidation is classified by the reactive species responsible for electron transfer: **(a) Direct (Conventional) Oxidation** The molecular oxidant itself attacks the substrate via an electrophilic or nucleophilic pathway. Example: $O_3$ reacting with a phenolic –OH at the ortho/para position to form quinones → muconic acids → CO₂. **(b) Indirect Oxidation** The oxidant first decomposes to generate secondary reactive species — most importantly the **hydroxyl radical (OH•)** — which then non-selectively oxidizes organics. > [!NOTE] > **Hydroxyl Radical (OH•) — The "Ultimate Oxidant"** > - Second-order rate constant with organics: $k = 10^8$ to $10^{10}\ \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ > - Standard potential: $E^{\circ}(\text{OH}^{\bullet}/\text{H}_2\text{O}) = +2.80\ \text{V}$ > - Non-selective: attacks virtually every dissolved organic. > - The driving force behind all **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)**. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of an Oxidation Reaction 1. **Dosing & Mass Transfer** — The oxidant is dissolved/dispersed into the liquid phase. For gases (O₃, Cl₂), this is governed by two-film theory; transfer rate $\propto (C^{*}-C_L)$. 2. **Decomposition (for AOPs)** — $H_2O_2$, $O_3$, or persulphate breaks down homolytically to yield OH•. Often catalysed by **Fe²⁺ (Fenton's), UV, or TiO₂ photocatalysis**. 3. **Radical Attack on Pollutant** — OH• abstracts an H atom or adds across a C=C double bond, generating organic radicals. 4. **Propagation Chain** — Organic radicals react with O₂ dissolved in the water, producing peroxy radicals → hydroperoxides → carbonyls → carboxylic acids → CO₂. 5. **Termination** — Two radicals combine, or radicals are scavenged by carbonates / bicarbonates / humic acids. ### 2.3 Common Oxidizing Agents — KTU Comparison Table | Oxidant | Formula | $E^{\circ}$ (V) | Best pH Range | Selectivity | Typical Dose | |---------|---------|---------------|---------------|-------------|--------------| | Ozone | $O_3$ | **+2.07** | 7 – 10 | Moderate (selective) | 2 – 5 mg O₃ / mg COD | | Hydrogen Peroxide | $H_2O_2$ | **+1.78** | 6 – 8 (alone) | Low alone; high with catalyst | 0.5 – 3 g / L | | Chlorine | $Cl_2$ | **+1.36** | 6 – 7.5 | Selective | 5 – 15 mg/L (disinfection) | | Chlorine Dioxide | $ClO_2$ | **+0.95** | 6 – 10 | Selective | 1 – 5 mg/L | | Potassium Permanganate | $KMnO_4$ | **+1.51** | 7 – 9 | Highly selective | 1 – 10 mg/L | | Fenton's Reagent | $Fe^{2+}/H_2O_2$ | OH• (+2.80) | **2.5 – 4.0** | Non-selective (radical) | $H_2O_2/COD = 0.5$ – $2.5$ | | Persulphate | $S_2O_8^{2-}$ | **+2.01** | 7 – 12 (activated) | Radical or direct | 0.5 – 5 g/L | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Higher $E^{\circ}$ ⇒ stronger oxidizer, but also higher cost and lower selectivity.** KTU board questions frequently test the *trade-off* between $E^{\circ}$ and selectivity. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — **not the bare vertical pipe** — inside any LaTeX expression that may be embedded in a markdown table to prevent table-syntax breakage. | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|-------------------|------------|----------------| | 1 | Stoichiometric O₃ demand | $D_{O_3} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{COD} \times 2.67}{M_{O_3}}$ | g O₃ per g COD removed; factor 2.67 = (8/3) electrons per mol O₃ | | 2 | Oxidant dose | $m_{\text{ox}} = D_{O_3} \cdot V \cdot C_{\text{COD}}$ | g, where $V$ in L, $C_{\text{COD}}$ in g/L | | 3 | Pseudo-first-order rate law | $-\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k\,C$ | $C$ = pollutant concentration (mg/L), $k$ = $s^{-1}$ | | 4 | Half-life (1st order) | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | s | | 5 | Fenton's H₂O₂ demand | $[H_2O_2] = 0.5 \cdot [Fe^{2+}]$ to $5 \cdot [Fe^{2+}]$ | mol / mol | | 6 | OH• formation (Fenton) | $Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{3+} + OH^{-} + OH^{\bullet}$ | k ≈ 70 M⁻¹s⁻¹ | | 7 | COD removal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{COD}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{COD,in}} - C_{\text{COD,out}}}{C_{\text{COD,in}}} \times 100$ | % | | 8 | Mass-transfer rate (gas oxidant) | $N_A = k_L a\,(C^{*} - C_L)$ | mg/(L·s); $k_L a$ depends on reactor geometry | | 9 | Henry's constant (O₃, 25°C) | $H_{O_3} \approx 0.082$ | mol/(L·atm) | | 10 | Breakpoint chlorination (residual Cl₂) | $2\,Cl_2 + 2\,NH_3 \to N_2 + 4\,HCl + 2\,H_2O$ | — destroys free chlorine residual | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Petrochemical Industries - **Pre-treatment** of phenolic wastewater (e.g., from catalytic cracking units) to reduce shock load on biological reactors. - **Polishing step** for treated effluent to meet **discharge limits**: COD < 100 mg/L, phenol < 0.5 mg/L (MoEF/KSPCB norms). - **Sludge conditioning** — Fenton's reagent simultaneously oxidizes and dewaters waste-activated sludge. - **Sour-water treatment** — Oxidation of $H_2S$, $S^{2-}$, $HS^{-}$ to elemental sulphur or sulphate. - **Decolourization** of dye-bearing wash water in polymer / speciality chemical plants. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Ozone Dose from COD The chemical oxygen demand is the mass of O₂ required to fully oxidize 1 L of wastewater: $$ \text{COD (mg/L)} = \dfrac{\text{mg } O_2 \text{ consumed}}{\text{L}} $$ One mole of O₂ accepts **4 electrons**: $$ O_2 + 4\,e^{-} + 4\,H^{+} \to 2\,H_2O $$ One mole of O₃, however, accepts only **6 electrons** (ozone → oxygen): $$ 2\,O_3 + 4\,e^{-} + 4\,H^{+} \to O_2 + 2\,H_2O $$ Therefore, **2 moles of O₃ accept the same electrons as 1 mole of O₂**. **Step-by-step logic:** > *Step 1* — Let $\Delta$COD = $C_{\text{COD,in}} - C_{\text{COD,out}}$ (mg/L as O₂). > *Step 2* — Convert ΔCOD into moles of O₂ required per litre: > $$ > n_{O_2} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{COD}}{32{,}000} \quad \text{(mol/L, since }M_{O_2}=32\text{ g/mol)} > $$ > *Step 3* — From the electron balance, moles of O₃ required = $2 \cdot n_{O_2}$: > $$ > n_{O_3} = 2 \times \dfrac{\Delta \text{COD}}{32{,}000} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{COD}}{16{,}000} > $$ > *Step 4* — Convert moles of O₃ to mass (g) per litre: > $$ > m_{O_3} = n_{O_3} \times 48 = \dfrac{\Delta \text{COD} \times 48}{16{,}000} = 0.003 \cdot \Delta \text{COD} > $$ > Equivalently, $1.5\ \text{g } O_3$ oxidizes the same load as $1\ \text{g } O_2$. > *Step 5* — Re-derivation via the commonly cited factor **2.67 g O₃ / g COD**: > $$ > \dfrac{g\,O_3}{g\,O_2} = \dfrac{2 \times 48}{1 \times 32} = 3.0 \quad \text{(theoretical for O₂ equivalent)} > $$ > However, in practice ozone is **only ~89 % utilized** for COD, so the practical factor is $\approx 2.67$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Examiner Trap:** Many students write the factor as **3.0** in worked problems. Always clarify whether the question asks for the **theoretical** (3.0) or the **practical applied dose** (≈ 2.67) when transfer efficiency is below 100 %. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pseudo-First-Order Kinetic Model (CSTR/Plug-Flow) Assume the oxidation follows: $$ -\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k_{\text{obs}}\,C $$ **Integrating from $C_0$ to $C$ over contact time $\tau$** (for an ideal plug-flow contactor or batch reactor): > *Step 1* — Separate variables: > $$ > \dfrac{dC}{C} = -k_{\text{obs}}\,dt > $$ > *Step 2* — Integrate both sides: > $$ > \int_{C_0}^{C} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -k_{\text{obs}} \int_{0}^{\tau} dt > $$ > *Step 3* — Result: > $$ > \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k_{\text{obs}}\,\tau > $$ > *Step 4* — Solving for the fractional removal $\eta$: > $$ > \eta = 1 - \dfrac{C}{C_0} = 1 - e^{-k_{\text{obs}}\,\tau} > $$ > *Step 5* — For an **ideal CSTR** with steady-state mass balance on the pollutant: > $$ > Q\,C_0 = Q\,C + V\,k_{\text{obs}}\,C > $$ > $$ > \dfrac{C}{C_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k_{\text{obs}}\,\tau} > \quad ; \quad > \eta_{\text{CSTR}} = \dfrac{k_{\text{obs}}\,\tau}{1 + k_{\text{obs}}\,\tau} > $$ > where $\tau = V/Q$ = hydraulic residence time (HRT). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** For a given $\tau$, the plug-flow reactor always gives higher removal than a CSTR. KTU board questions frequently ask students to **compare the two** for an AOP design. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical) **Problem:** A refinery wastewater with $C_0 = 800\ \text{mg/L}$ COD is to be treated by ozonation. Target effluent COD = 100 mg/L. Calculate: (a) Stoichiometric O₃ dose (using practical factor 2.67 g O₃ / g COD). (b) Required O₃ dose for $1\ \text{m}^3$ of wastewater. (c) Rate constant $k_{\text{obs}}$ such that 95 % removal is achieved in a plug-flow reactor with HRT = 30 min. **Given:** $\Delta\text{COD} = 800 - 100 = 700\ \text{mg/L}$, $V = 1\ \text{m}^3 = 1000\ \text{L}$. **Solution:** > **Part (a)** — Stoichiometric O₃ dose: > $$ > D_{O_3} = 2.67 \times 700\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} = 1869\ \frac{\text{mg O}_3}{\text{L}} > $$ > **Part (b)** — For 1 m³: > $$ > m_{O_3} = 1869\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 1000\ \text{L} = 1.869 \times 10^{6}\ \text{mg} = 1.869\ \text{kg O}_3 > $$ > **Part (c)** — From the PFR expression: > $$ > 0.95 = 1 - e^{-k_{\text{obs}}\,(30\ \text{min})} > $$ > $$ > e^{-k_{\text{obs}}\,(30)} = 0.05 > $$ > $$ > -k_{\text{obs}}\,(30) = \ln(0.05) = -2.9957 > $$ > $$ > k_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{2.9957}{30} = 0.0999\ \text{min}^{-1} \approx 0.1\ \text{min}^{-1} > $$ > In SI units: $k_{\text{obs}} = 0.1 / 60 = 1.665 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1}$. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fenton's Reagent H₂O₂ Stoichiometry Fenton's master reaction: $$ Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{3+} + OH^{-} + OH^{\bullet} \quad k_1 = 70\ \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1} $$ For a wastewater with $C_{\text{COD,in}} = 1000\ \text{mg/L}$, assuming optimal $[H_2O_2] / \text{COD} = 1.5\ \text{g/g}$: > *Step 1* — Required $H_2O_2$: > $$ > m_{H_2O_2} = 1.5 \times 1000\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} = 1500\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} > $$ > *Step 2* — Optimum $[Fe^{2+}] \approx 0.10 \times [H_2O_2]$ (molar basis): > $$ > [Fe^{2+}] = 0.10 \times \dfrac{1500 / 34{,}000}{1} \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{M} = 244\ \text{mg/L as FeSO}_4{\cdot}7H_2O > $$ > *Step 3* — pH adjustment: dose $H_2SO_4$ to reach **pH 3.0 ± 0.2**, then re-neutralize with NaOH to **pH 7** after Fenton reaction (to precipitate $Fe(OH)_3$ sludge). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — AOP Treatment Train ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Influent
Petrochemical
Wastewater] --> B[Equalization
and Primary
Clarifier] B --> C[Chemical
Oxidation
Reactor] C --> D[Neutralization
and pH
Adjustment] D --> E[Secondary
Biological
Treatment] E --> F[Tertiary
Polishing
Filter] F --> G[Discharge
or Recycle
to Process] C --> H[Oxidant
Generation
Skid] H --> I[Ozone
Generator] H --> J[Hydrogen
Peroxide
Storage] H --> K[Iron Catalyst
Dosing
Pump] I --> C J --> C K --> C C --> L[Off-gas
Destruction
Unit] ``` > *Figure 4.1 — Functional block diagram of a typical petrochemical wastewater treatment train with chemical oxidation as the key COD-reduction step.* ### 4.2 Process-Level Schematic — Ozonation Contacting System ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Oxygen
Supply] --> B1[Ozone
Generator
Corona Discharge] B1 --> C1[Ozone
Injector
Venturi] C1 --> D1[Contact
Tower 1
CSTR] D1 --> E1[Contact
Tower 2
CSTR] E1 --> F1[Treated
Effluent] A2[Raw
Wastewater] --> C1 D1 --> G1[Off-gas
to
Destruct Unit] E1 --> G1 G1 --> H1[Thermal
or
Catalytic
Destruct] B1 --> I1[Cooling
Water Loop] I1 --> B1 ``` > *Figure 4.2 — Two-stage CSTR ozone contacting system with off-gas destruction, the most common configuration in refinery ETP.* ### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Oxidant Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Select
Oxidant] --> S2{Is effluent
high in
sulphide or
cyanide?} S2 -- Yes --> S3[Use Chlorine
or
Permanganate] S2 -- No --> S4{Is target
colour or
micropollutant
removal?} S4 -- Yes --> S5[Use Ozone
or AOP] S4 -- No --> S6{Is the stream
sludge or
high COD
> 5000 mg/L?} S6 -- Yes --> S7[Use Fentons
Reagent] S6 -- No --> S8[Use H2O2
alone at
neutral pH] S3 --> END[Validate with
Jar Test and
Cost Analysis] S5 --> END S7 --> END S8 --> END ``` > *Figure 4.3 — KTU-recommended oxidant selection logic for petrochemical wastewater applications.* ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2] **Define chemical oxidation. List any four commonly used oxidizing agents in petrochemical wastewater treatment.** **Model Answer:** Chemical oxidation is a unit operation in which an oxidizing agent is added to wastewater to accept electrons from dissolved pollutants, converting them to stable oxidized end-products such as CO₂, water, mineral acids, or insoluble oxides. It is employed to reduce COD, BOD, colour, and toxicity of refinery and petrochemical effluents. Four commonly used oxidizing agents are: 1. **Ozone (O₃)** — strong, generates OH• in AOPs. 2. **Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂)** — clean, no by-products. 3. **Chlorine / Hypochlorite (Cl₂ / OCl⁻)** — cheap, effective for sulphides and cyanides. 4. **Potassium permanganate (KMnO₄)** — selective, useful for odour control. 5. *(Optional 5th: Fenton's reagent — Fe²⁺/H₂O₂ — for sludge conditioning.)* *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the list with at least one short note each.)* --- > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What are Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)? Why is the hydroxyl radical (OH•) called the "ultimate oxidant"?** **Model Answer:** AOPs are oxidation processes that generate the hydroxyl radical (OH•) in situ as the primary oxidizing species, typically by combining an oxidant (O₃, H₂O₂, persulphate) with a catalyst (Fe²⁺), UV light, ultrasound, or semiconductor photocatalyst (TiO₂). The OH• is called the "ultimate oxidant" because: - It has the **highest standard reduction potential** among common aqueous oxidizers: $E^{\circ}(\text{OH}^{\bullet}/\text{H}_2\text{O}) = +2.80\ \text{V}$. - It reacts with organics at **near diffusion-controlled rates**: $k = 10^{8}$ – $10^{10}\ \text{M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$. - It is **non-selective** — attacks virtually any organic pollutant. - It mineralizes organics completely to **CO₂, H₂O, and mineral acids**. *(3 marks: 1 for AOP definition, 2 for the four-point OH• justification.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Ozone-Based COD Reduction **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]** **(a)** Derive the stoichiometric relationship between the required ozone dose (g O₃) and the COD removed (g O₂-equivalent) in a refinery wastewater. State clearly the role of electron transfer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refinery wastewater has an influent COD of $1200\ \text{mg/L}$. After ozonation, the COD is to be reduced to **150 mg/L**. The treatment plant handles $50\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$ of wastewater. Assuming a practical ozone-to-COD ratio of 2.67 g O₃ / g COD and an ozone generator efficiency of 6 % (kg O₃ produced per kWh of electrical input),     (i) Calculate the **hourly ozone requirement** in kg/h.     (ii) Calculate the **electrical power consumption** of the ozone generator in kW.     (iii) If the oxidation follows pseudo-first-order kinetics with $k_{\text{obs}} = 0.15\ \text{min}^{-1}$, what is the required **HRT in a plug-flow reactor** to achieve 99 % COD removal? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Stoichiometric derivation (7 marks)** Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation. Key points expected by the examiner: 1. **Electron balance:** $O_2 + 4e^{-} \to 2H_2O$ (4 e⁻ per O₂); $2O_3 + 4e^{-} \to O_2 + 2H_2O$ (4 e⁻ per 2 O₃ = 2 e⁻ per O₃). 2. **1 mol O₂ ≡ 2 mol O₃** in terms of electron-accepting capacity. 3. Mass ratio: $(2 \times 48) / 32 = 3.0$ g O₃ per g O₂. 4. Practical factor including **transfer efficiency** and **side reactions**: ≈ **2.67 g O₃ / g COD**. *Marking: 2 marks for the electron-balance step, 2 marks for the ratio derivation, 1 mark for the practical factor, 2 marks for the role of electron transfer discussion.* **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks)** > **(i) Hourly ozone requirement:** > $$ > \Delta \text{COD} = 1200 - 150 = 1050\ \text{mg/L} > $$ > $$ > D_{O_3} = 2.67 \times 1050 = 2803.5\ \text{mg/L} > $$ > $$ > \text{Hourly flow} = 50\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} = 50{,}000\ \text{L/h} > $$ > $$ > m_{O_3} = 2803.5\ \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 50{,}000\ \text{L/h} = 1.40175 \times 10^{8}\ \text{mg/h} = \mathbf{140.2\ \text{kg O}_3/\text{h}} > $$ > **(ii) Electrical power:** > $$ > P = \dfrac{140.2\ \text{kg/h}}{0.06\ \text{kg/kWh}} = \mathbf{2336.6\ \text{kW}} > $$ > **(iii) Plug-flow HRT for 99 % removal:** > $$ > \eta = 0.99 \Rightarrow \dfrac{C}{C_0} = 0.01 > $$ > $$ > \ln(0.01) = -k_{\text{obs}}\,\tau > $$ > $$ > -4.6052 = -0.15\,\tau > $$ > $$ > \tau = \dfrac{4.6052}{0.15} = 30.7\ \text{min} > $$ *Marking: 2 marks (i), 2 marks (ii), 3 marks (iii).* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Fenton's Oxidation of Phenolic Wastewater **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]** **(a)** Explain the **Fenton's oxidation** mechanism. With the help of a reaction sequence, show how OH• radicals are generated and how they attack phenolic compounds. Discuss the role of pH. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A phenolic wastewater has an initial phenol concentration of $500\ \text{mg/L}$ and COD = $1200\ \text{mg/L}$. It is treated by Fenton's oxidation in a CSTR.     (i) Calculate the **molar concentrations** of $H_2O_2$ and $Fe^{2+}$ required if the optimum molar ratio is $H_2O_2/Fe^{2+} = 10$ and the dose is $H_2O_2 = 1.5 \times \text{COD (mass basis)}$.     (ii) If the rate constant is $k_{\text{obs}} = 0.08\ \text{min}^{-1}$, find the **HRT** in the CSTR to achieve 90 % COD removal.     (iii) List **two limitations** of Fenton's process. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Fenton's mechanism (7 marks)** Step 1: $Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{3+} + OH^{-} + OH^{\bullet}$ (chain initiation, k₁ = 70 M⁻¹s⁻¹). Step 2: $Fe^{3+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{2+} + HOO^{\bullet} + H^{+}$ (Fenton-like, regenerates Fe²⁺, k₂ = 0.001 – 0.01 M⁻¹s⁻¹ — slow). Step 3: $OH^{\bullet} + \text{PhOH} \to \text{Hydroxycyclohexadienyl radical} \to \text{Catechol / Hydroquinone}$. Step 4: Ring cleavage → muconic acid → lower acids → CO₂ + H₂O. **Role of pH (3 of 7 marks):** - **Optimum pH 2.5 – 4.0.** Above pH 4, Fe³⁺ precipitates as $Fe(OH)_3$, halting the catalytic cycle. Below pH 2.5, $H_3O_2^{+}$ (protonated peroxide) forms, scavenging OH•. The narrow pH window is a major operational constraint. **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks)** > **(i) Concentrations:** > $H_2O_2$ dose = $1.5 \times 1200 = 1800\ \text{mg/L}$ > $$ > [H_2O_2] = \dfrac{1800}{34{,}000} = 0.0529\ \text{M} > $$ > With $H_2O_2/Fe^{2+} = 10$: > $$ > [Fe^{2+}] = \dfrac{0.0529}{10} = 0.00529\ \text{M} = \mathbf{5.29\ \text{mM}} > $$ > Equivalent $FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O$ = $0.00529 \times 278 = 1.47\ \text{g/L}$. > **(ii) CSTR HRT for 90 % removal:** > $$ > \eta = \dfrac{k_{\text{obs}}\,\tau}{1 + k_{\text{obs}}\,\tau} = 0.90 > $$ > $$ > 0.90(1 + 0.08\,\tau) = 0.08\,\tau > $$ > $$ > 0.90 + 0.072\,\tau = 0.08\,\tau > $$ > $$ > 0.90 = 0.008\,\tau > $$ > $$ > \tau = \dfrac{0.90}{0.008} = 112.5\ \text{min} > $$ > **(iii) Two limitations of Fenton's process:** > 1. **Narrow pH window (2.5 – 4.0)** — requires acid dosing upstream and neutralization downstream, generating **iron-rich sludge** ($Fe(OH)_3$). > 2. **High reagent cost and slow Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ regeneration** — the Fenton-like reaction (Step 2) is rate-limiting, requiring continuous addition of fresh Fe²⁺. *Marking: 3 marks (i), 2 marks (ii), 2 marks (iii).* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Chemical-Oxidation Questions:** > 1. **Mixing up the factor** — Writing 3.0 instead of 2.67 g O₃ / g COD (or vice versa) when the question explicitly says "practical" or "theoretical." Examiner docks **1 mark** for this. > 2. **PFR vs CSTR confusion** — Many students use the CSTR formula for a PFR problem, leading to ~30 % error in HRT. State the reactor type first. > 3. **Units mismatch** — Mixing minutes and seconds in $k_{\text{obs}}$; or mg/L and g/m³ without conversion. KTU examiners are strict on **dimensional consistency**. > 4. **Forgetting the electron balance** — In derivation questions, the **electron half-reactions** must be written; a missing half-reaction loses **2 marks** in Section (a) of a 14-mark question. > 5. **Fenton pH window** — A common error is to state "pH 7 is optimum." Examiners *specifically* test for the pH 2.5 – 4.0 condition. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Chemical Oxidation** - ✔ **Definition** — Electron transfer from pollutant (reductant) to an added oxidant; converts organics to CO₂, H₂O, mineral acids. - ✔ **Oxidant selection hierarchy (highest E° first):** OH• (+2.80) > O₃ (+2.07) > S₂O₈²⁻ (+2.01) > H₂O₂ (+1.78) > KMnO₄ (+1.51) > Cl₂ (+1.36) > ClO₂ (+0.95). - ✔ **Practical O₃ / COD factor = 2.67 g/g** (theoretical = 3.0 g/g). - ✔ **Pseudo-first-order kinetics:** $C/C_0 = e^{-k\tau}$ (PFR), $C/C_0 = 1/(1 + k\tau)$ (CSTR). - ✔ **Fenton's master reaction:** $Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \to Fe^{3+} + OH^{-} + OH^{\bullet}$ ; **optimum pH = 2.5 – 4.0**. - ✔ **AOPs** = oxidation processes that produce OH• as the principal reactive species. - ✔ **Breakpoint chlorination** removes ammonia as N₂; stoichiometric dose ≈ 7.6 mg Cl₂ per mg NH₃-N (initial) → 10 mg/L (breakpoint). - ✔ **Mass-transfer limited** for gas oxidants (O₃, Cl₂): improve by increasing $k_L a$ via venturi injectors, diffusers, or packed columns. - ✔ **Industrial uses** — pre-treatment for high-COD refinery streams, polishing for toxic organics, sludge conditioning, sour-water treatment, decolourization. - ✔ **CSTR vs PFR:** PFR is more efficient for the same $\tau$; CSTR preferred when pH adjustment and reagent mixing are continuous. - ✔ **Reactor design sequence:** (1) Jar test → (2) kinetic study → (3) PFR/CSTR sizing → (4) off-gas treatment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Board Tip:** Always present the **electron half-reaction** in any derivation question — KTU examiners award at least **1 – 2 marks** for explicitly showing the redox logic. Avoid vague statements like "ozone is a strong oxidant" without quantitative support.

topics.title: Chemical Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chemical Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Chemical Reduction in Petrochemical Industries?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Chemical Reduction** in petrochemical engineering refers to a class of chemical processes in which the oxidation state of one or more elements in a compound is **decreased** through the gain of electrons, typically by the action of a **reducing agent** such as hydrogen (H₂), carbon monoxide (CO), methane (CH₄), or a metal. In petrochemical plants, reduction reactions are central to **hydrogenation**, **hydrotreating (HDS/HDN)**, **catalytic cracking support reactions**, and **emission control (SCR)** systems.

Mathematically, a generic reduction half-reaction is expressed as:

$$
\text{Oxidized Species} + n\,e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Reduced Species}
$$

where $n$ is the number of electrons transferred per mole of species reduced.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electron Bank"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Imagine reduction as a person (the reducing agent) **depositing money (electrons)** into a bank account (the molecule being reduced). When you "reduce" iron ore (Fe₂O₃) using carbon monoxide, the CO is the donor — it hands over electrons to the iron, converting it from Fe³⁺ to Fe⁰ (metallic iron). The donor (CO) itself gets "oxidized" to CO₂. So in every reduction, there is always an **oxidation happening simultaneously** — this is the **Redox Coupling Principle**.
>
> In a refinery, hydrogen plays the role of a universal "electron donor" — it pays electrons to sulfur, nitrogen, and oxygen impurities embedded in crude oil fractions, transforming them into H₂S, NH₃, and H₂O — all of which are easily stripped out of the product stream.

### 1.3 Classification of Reduction Processes in Petrochemical Industry

| S.No. | Type of Reduction | Reducing Agent | Industrial Example | Operating T (°C) |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:-:|
| 1 | Catalytic Hydrogenation | H₂ | Vegetable oil hardening, benzene → cyclohexane | 150 – 400 |
| 2 | Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) | H₂ | Removal of S from diesel, naphtha | 300 – 450 |
| 3 | Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN) | H₂ | Removal of N from feedstocks | 340 – 420 |
| 4 | Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) | NH₃ or urea | NOx removal from flue gas | 250 – 450 |
| 5 | Smelting / Carbothermic Reduction | C, CO | Ore → metal (Fe, Ni catalyst prep) | 800 – 1600 |
| 6 | Metallothermic Reduction | Al, Mg, Na | TiCl₄ + Mg → Ti (Kroll process) | 700 – 1100 |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard reduction potential** $E^\circ$ of H⁺/H₂ couple: **$E^\circ = 0.000\ \text{V}$ (at 25 °C, 1 atm)**.
> - **Faraday's constant**: $F = \mathbf{96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}}$.
> - **Standard-state Gibbs free energy** reference: $G^\circ = 0$ for elements in their standard state.
> - **Universal gas constant**: $R = \mathbf{8.314\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation of Reduction

Every reduction reaction is governed by the **change in Gibbs free energy** ($\Delta G$). A reaction is **spontaneous** only if:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta H - T\,\Delta S < 0
$$

For a redox couple, the spontaneity is also related to the **standard cell potential** $E^\circ$:

$$
\Delta G^\circ = -n\,F\,E^\circ_{\text{cell}}
$$

A positive $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ implies spontaneous reduction (at the cathode) and oxidation (at the anode).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in petrochemical plants:** Operators use Ellingham diagrams (plots of $\Delta G^\circ$ vs $T$ for oxide formation) to determine the **minimum temperature** at which a given reducing agent (C, CO, H₂) can reduce a metal oxide. For example, **carbon can reduce FeO only above ~700 °C** because above this temperature the C → CO line lies *below* the Fe → FeO line on the Ellingham diagram.

## 2.2 Why and How of Hydrogenation

In catalytic hydrogenation, an unsaturated hydrocarbon (C=C, C=O, C≡C, aromatic ring) reacts with H₂ on a metal catalyst surface (Ni, Pd, Pt, or sulfided Ni-Mo / Co-Mo for S-containing feeds). The mechanistic steps are:

1. **Adsorption** of H₂ on the metal site → dissociation into H atoms.
2. **Chemisorption** of the unsaturated molecule on an adjacent site.
3. **Surface reaction** — stepwise addition of H atoms to the π-bond.
4. **Desorption** of the saturated product from the catalyst surface.

## 2.3 Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) — The Workhorse of Refining

HDS is the largest-scale reduction process in any refinery. The generic stoichiometry for a thiophene-type sulfur compound is:

$$
\text{R–S–R}' + 2\,\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Co-Mo / Ni-Mo, 340–400 °C, 30–80 bar}} \text{R–H} + \text{R}'\text{–H} + \text{H}_2\text{S}
$$

The H₂S produced is then separated in a **stripper column** and routed to a **Claus plant** for sulfur recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Modern ultra-low-sulfur diesel (ULSD, < 10 ppm S) requires **second-stage HDS** with higher H₂ partial pressure (~100 bar) and more active catalysts (Ni-Mo on alumina). This is a *direct consequence* of the kinetic limitation imposed by refractory dibenzothiophene (DBT) species.

## 2.4 Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) of NOx

In petrochemical furnaces, gas turbines, and FCC regenerators, NOx emissions are reduced by injecting ammonia:

$$
4\,\text{NH}_3 + 4\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{V}_2\text{O}_5/\text{TiO}_2,\ 300{-}400\ ^\circ\text{C}} 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|:-:|:--|:--|:-:|
| 1 | $\Delta G^\circ = -n\,F\,E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ | Gibbs energy ↔ cell potential | J mol⁻¹ |
| 2 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Nernst equation for reduction potential | V |
| 3 | $k = A\,e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $-r_A = k\,C_A^n\,C_B^m$ | Power-law rate of reduction | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $X_A = 1 - e^{-k\,\tau}$ | Conversion in a CSTR (first order) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{\dot{V}}$ | Space time | s |
| 7 | $K_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{products}}^{\nu_p}}{P_{\text{reactants}}^{\nu_r}}$ | Equilibrium constant (Kₚ) | atm$^{\Delta\nu}$ |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta T_{\text{ad}}}{\Delta T_{\text{max}}}$ | Catalyst effectiveness factor (internal diffusion) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{TOF} = \dfrac{\text{moles reacted}}{\text{moles active site}\cdot \text{time}}$ | Turnover frequency | s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $Q = n\,F$ | Charge passed per mole of electrons | C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the daily tools of a process engineer tuning a hydrotreater — $k$ (Arrhenius) sets the reactor temperature window, $X_A$ (conversion) defines the recycle ratio, and the Nernst equation governs electrochemical sensors (ORP probes) that monitor H₂S scrubbers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation for a Reduction Half-Cell

**Starting Point — Van't Hoff isotherm for any redox couple:**

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + R\,T\,\ln Q
$$

**Step 1 — Relate $\Delta G$ to cell potential.** By definition of electrical work:

$$
\Delta G = -n\,F\,E \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -n\,F\,E^\circ
$$

where $n$ = electrons transferred and $F$ = Faraday constant.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Van't Hoff expression:**

$$
-n\,F\,E = -n\,F\,E^\circ + R\,T\,\ln Q
$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $-nF$:**

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{R\,T}{n\,F}\,\ln Q
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to base-10 logarithm (multiply by 2.303):**

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\,R\,T}{n\,F}\,\log_{10} Q
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the constant at 25 °C (298.15 K):**

$$
\frac{2.303\,R\,T}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916\ \text{V}
$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$
\boxed{\,E = E^\circ - \frac{0.05916}{n}\,\log_{10} Q \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $E$ becomes *more positive* when product concentrations increase (system becomes more reducing in reverse sense). For HDS off-gas analyzers, an ORP probe reads millivolts proportional to $\log[H_2S]/[H_2]$, directly using this equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: First-Order Conversion in a Plug-Flow Reactor (PFR)

Consider a first-order reduction $A \to B$ in a tubular reactor. The design equation is:

$$
\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A = -k\,C_A
$$

**Step 1 — Express $C_A$ in terms of conversion $X_A$:**

For constant density, $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
F_{A0}\,\frac{dX_A}{dV} = k\,C_{A0}(1 - X_A)
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables:**

$$
\frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{k\,C_{A0}}{F_{A0}}\,dV = \frac{k}{\dot{V}}\,dV
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from 0 to $X_A$ and 0 to $V$:**

$$
\int_0^{X_A}\frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \int_0^V \frac{k}{\dot{V}}\,dV
$$

$$
-\ln(1 - X_A) = \frac{k\,V}{\dot{V}} = k\,\tau
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for conversion:**

$$
\boxed{\,X_A = 1 - e^{-k\,\tau}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** This expression is used in sizing an HDS reactor. If $k = 0.4\ \text{s}^{-1}$ at 380 °C and the required sulfur conversion is 99 % ($X_A = 0.99$), then $\tau = -\ln(0.01)/0.4 = 11.5\ \text{s}$. Multiply by volumetric flow to obtain the catalyst volume $V_{\text{cat}}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Adiabatic Temperature Rise for an Exothermic Reduction

For an exothermic hydrogenation, the temperature rise across an adiabatic reactor is:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{-\Delta H_{rxn}\,X_A}{\sum_i F_i\,C_{p,i}}
$$

**Step 1 — Energy balance (adiabatic, no work, no heat loss):**

$$
\sum_i F_{i,\text{out}}\,C_{p,i}\,T_{\text{out}} = \sum_i F_{i,\text{in}}\,C_{p,i}\,T_{\text{in}} + (-\Delta H_{rxn})\,F_{A0}\,X_A
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract $T_{\text{in}}$ from both sides and group:**

$$
\sum_i F_{i,\text{out}}\,C_{p,i}\,(T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) = (-\Delta H_{rxn})\,F_{A0}\,X_A
$$

**Step 3 — Assuming $C_{p,i}$ and flows are approximately constant:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}} = \frac{-\Delta H_{rxn}\,F_{A0}\,X_A}{\sum_i F_{i,\text{out}}\,C_{p,i}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define $\Delta T_{\text{ad}}$ for complete conversion $X_A \to 1$:**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = \frac{-\Delta H_{rxn}\,F_{A0}}{\sum_i F_i\,C_{p,i}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Importance:** A runaway reduction reaction (e.g.,失控 hydrogenation) can produce $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} > 100\ °C$ — this is why **multiple quench zones** with cold H₂ injection are built into large hydrocracker reactors. The control system uses online temperature sensors to modulate the H₂ quench flow.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Industrial Reduction Reactors

| Feature | Fixed-Bed HDS | Slurry-Phase Hydrogenation | Fluidized-Bed SCR | Smelting Reduction |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Catalyst** | Co-Mo / Ni-Mo on Al₂O₃ | Raney Ni, Pd/C | V₂O₅ / TiO₂ | None (molten metal) |
| **Phase** | Gas–solid | Gas–liquid–solid | Gas–solid | Liquid–solid |
| **T (°C)** | 300 – 420 | 50 – 250 | 250 – 450 | 1400 – 1700 |
| **P (bar)** | 30 – 100 | 1 – 50 | 1 (atm) | 1 (atm) |
| **Conversion/Selectivity** | High / High | Very High / High | High / Moderate | High / Low |
| **Main Control Variable** | Bed T profile | Agitator speed, T | NH₃/NOx ratio | O₂/CO ratio |
| **Safety Concern** | Hot spots, H₂ leak | Pyrophoric catalyst | NH₃ slip | Slag foaming |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) Reactor Control Loop

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The following Mermaid block represents the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a typical HDS reactor with a cascade temperature control strategy. It maps signal flow rather than physical piping — exactly what an instrumentation engineer would document in a P&ID review.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Oil Pump] --> B[Pre-Heater Fired Furnace]
    B --> C[Reactor Inlet Mixer]
    H[H2 Compressor and Recycle] --> C
    C --> D[Fixed Bed Reactor Catalyst Ni-Mo on Al2O3]
    D --> E[Hot High Pressure Separator]
    E --> F[Reactor Bed Temperature Sensor TC1]
    F --> G[Temperature Controller TIC100]
    G --> B
    G --> I[Quench H2 Valve Position]
    I --> D
    E --> J[Cold Separator]
    J --> K[H2S Stripper Column]
    K --> L[Online H2S Analyzer AT200]
    L --> M[Sulfur Recovery Feedback]
    M --> H
    J --> N[Hydrotreated Product to Storage]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `TC1` (Bed Temperature Sensor) feeds the master controller `TIC100`.
- `TIC100` cascades two outputs: (i) furnace firing rate, and (ii) inter-bed cold H₂ quench flow.
- `AT200` (H₂S analyzer) provides a slow outer-loop trim that adjusts the H₂/feed ratio for long-term sulfur spec compliance.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) System for NOx Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NOx Source FCC Regenerator or Furnace] --> B[Dust Collector Cyclone]
    B --> C[Heater Bypass Duct]
    C --> D[SCR Reactor V2O5 on TiO2]
    D --> E[ID Fan Induced Draft]
    E --> F[Stack]
    P[Ammonia Storage and Vaporizer] --> Q[Ammonia Injection Grid]
    Q --> D
    R[NOx Analyzer at Stack] --> S[NOx Controller AIC50]
    T[NH3 Slip Analyzer] --> S
    S --> Q
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **NOx controller** `AIC50` compares the stack NOx reading with the desired setpoint (typically **30 – 50 mg/Nm³**).
- The output modulates the **ammonia injection valve** while the **NH₃ slip analyzer** provides a hard safety interlock (trip at > 5 ppm NH₃ slip to prevent atmospheric release).

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Control Strategy for H₂/Feed Ratio in HDS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[H2 Flow Transmitter FT101] --> C[Ratio Controller FFC200]
    B[Feed Flow Transmitter FT102] --> C
    C --> D[Control Valve on H2 Line]
    D --> E[Mixer at Reactor Inlet]
    E --> F[Reactor]
    F --> G[Sampling Probe to Online Sulfur Analyzer]
    G --> H[Analyzer Controller AIC300]
    H --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **ratio controller** `FFC200` maintains a constant **H₂/oil ratio** (typically **100 – 300 Nm³ H₂ / m³ oil**).
- The **analyzer controller** `AIC300` slow-trims the ratio setpoint to keep the product sulfur content below 10 ppm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instrumentation Practice:** All analyzer sample lines must be heat-traced and lagged to prevent H₂S condensation (dew point ~ 60 °C at 30 bar). Failure to do so introduces a 5 – 15 minute measurement lag, which destabilizes the feedback loop — a common KTU exam pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> *Define chemical reduction. Give two examples of reduction reactions used in the petrochemical industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Chemical reduction is a process in which a species gains one or more electrons, resulting in a decrease in its oxidation state. In the petrochemical industry, two common examples are:
