1. **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS):** Organic sulfur compounds in crude oil fractions are reduced by H₂ to form H₂S, e.g., $\text{R–S–R}' + 2\,\text{H}_2 \to \text{R–H} + \text{R}'\text{–H} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$.
2. **Catalytic Hydrogenation:** Unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes, aromatics) are reduced to saturated products by H₂ over Ni or Pd catalysts, e.g., benzene + 3 H₂ → cyclohexane.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> *What is the role of a catalyst in hydrodesulfurization? Name any two commercial HDS catalysts.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A catalyst in HDS lowers the activation energy for the C–S bond cleavage and provides active sites for hydrogen dissociation and sulfur uptake. The catalyst is typically a **sulfided transition metal** (Ni, Co, Mo, W) supported on a high-surface-area alumina (γ-Al₂O₃). Two commercial HDS catalysts are:
1. **Co-Mo / γ-Al₂O₃** — used for naphtha and atmospheric gas oil (moderate pressure).
2. **Ni-Mo / γ-Al₂O₃** — preferred for VGO and heavy feeds requiring deeper desulfurization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the instrumentation and control scheme of a fixed-bed hydrodesulfurization (HDS) reactor used in a petroleum refinery. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the Nernst equation for a generic reduction half-cell starting from the Van't Hoff isotherm. Discuss how an **ORP (Oxidation–Reduction Potential) probe** uses this equation to monitor H₂S in a scrubber. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Instrumentation & Control of HDS Reactor (7 marks):**
A fixed-bed HDS reactor operates at 30 – 100 bar and 320 – 420 °C. The instrumentation is designed around three critical control objectives:
1. **Bed temperature profile control** — Multiple thermocouples (typically 4 – 6) are installed along the bed length. A cascade control loop uses the **mid-bed TC** as the primary variable, with outputs to the **furnace firing rate** (slow loop) and **inter-bed H₂ quench valves** (fast loop). This prevents hot spots that can coke the catalyst.
2. **H₂/Feed ratio control** — A **ratio controller (FFC)** maintains a stoichiometric excess of H₂ (typically 150 – 300 Nm³/m³). Flow transmitters FT-101 (H₂) and FT-102 (oil) feed the ratio station.
3. **Product quality control** — An **online sulfur analyzer** (X-ray fluorescence, ASTM D5453) on the product stream provides a slow outer-loop trim to the H₂/feed ratio setpoint.

Additional safety interlocks include: (i) high-pressure trip on the reactor shell, (ii) low H₂ flow trip to prevent coking, and (iii) high ΔT across the bed (runaway detection).

**(b) Nernst Equation Derivation (7 marks):**
Starting from the Van't Hoff isotherm:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + R\,T\,\ln Q
$$

Substituting $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$:

$$
-nFE = -nFE^\circ + R\,T\,\ln Q
$$

Dividing by $-nF$ and converting to log₁₀:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\,R\,T}{nF}\,\log_{10} Q
$$

At 25 °C, $2.303RT/F = 0.05916$ V, so:

$$
E = E^\circ - \frac{0.05916}{n}\,\log_{10} Q
$$

**ORP probe application:** An ORP probe immersed in a NaOH-based H₂S scrubber measures the millivolt potential of the redox couple $\text{SO}_4^{2-} / \text{S}^{2-}$. Using the Nernst equation with $E^\circ = -0.14$ V and $n = 8$, the probe output (in mV) is directly related to $\log[\text{H}_2\text{S}]$, allowing continuous monitoring of scrubber efficiency.

---

> **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** process for NOx abatement in a petrochemical plant. Draw a labeled block diagram showing the ammonia injection loop and analyzer control. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A first-order catalytic reduction has rate constant $k = 0.4\ \text{s}^{-1}$ at 380 °C. Calculate the reactor volume required to achieve 99 % conversion of species A when the volumetric flow rate is 12 000 Nm³/h. The reaction is $\text{A} + \text{H}_2 \to \text{B}$ in a PFR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) SCR Process for NOx Abatement (7 marks):**
Selective Catalytic Reduction is the most widely used post-combustion NOx control technology in petrochemical plants (FCC units, ethylene crackers, gas turbines). Ammonia (or aqueous urea) is injected into the flue gas stream upstream of a catalyst bed containing V₂O₅/TiO₂ (or V₂O₅-WO₃/TiO₂). The principal reactions are:

$$
4\,\text{NH}_3 + 4\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \to 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
2\,\text{NH}_3 + \text{NO} + \text{NO}_2 \to 2\,\text{N}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The "selective" refers to the fact that NH₃ preferentially reacts with NOx rather than being oxidized by excess O₂. Operating window: 300 – 400 °C. Below 250 °C, ammonium bisulfate deposits and fouls the catalyst; above 450 °C, NH₃ is directly oxidized to NO, defeating the purpose.

The control loop consists of: (1) a **NOx analyzer** at the stack providing the controlled variable, (2) a **NH₃/NOx ratio controller** that computes the required NH₃ flow, (3) a **control valve** on the ammonia injection line, and (4) a **NH₃ slip analyzer** downstream of the reactor as a safety interlock (trip at 5 ppm).

**(b) Reactor Volume Calculation (7 marks):**
For a first-order PFR:

$$
X_A = 1 - e^{-k\,\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = -\frac{\ln(1 - X_A)}{k}
$$

Given: $X_A = 0.99$, $k = 0.4\ \text{s}^{-1}$.

$$
\tau = -\frac{\ln(1 - 0.99)}{0.4} = -\frac{\ln(0.01)}{0.4} = \frac{4.6052}{0.4} = 11.513\ \text{s}
$$

Convert volumetric flow:

$$
\dot{V} = 12{,}000\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{h} = \frac{12{,}000}{3600} = 3.333\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Reactor volume:

$$
V = \tau \times \dot{V} = 11.513 \times 3.333 = 38.38\ \text{m}^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$ — costs 2 marks.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert Nm³/h to m³/s — costs 1 mark.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Confusing CSTR and PFR design equations — for a CSTR the formula would be $V = F_{A0}\,X_A / (k\,C_{A0})$, giving a *larger* volume. Always re-read the problem for reactor type.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Forgetting to mention that the rate constant is temperature-dependent (Arrhenius) — a 1-mark deduction if the answer requires justification of operating temperature selection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Reduction = gain of electrons**; always paired with oxidation (redox coupling).
- **H₂ is the universal reducing agent** in refineries — used in HDS, HDN, hydrocracking, and saturation of olefins/aromatics.
- **HDS stoichiometry:** $\text{R-S-R}' + 2\,\text{H}_2 \to \text{R-H} + \text{R}'\text{-H} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$; Co-Mo and Ni-Mo are the workhorse catalysts.
- **SCR stoichiometry:** $4\,\text{NH}_3 + 4\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \to 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$; V₂O₅/TiO₂ catalyst, operating window 300 – 400 °C.
- **Nernst equation:** $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.05916}{n}\log_{10} Q$ (at 25 °C) — fundamental for ORP probes.
- **PFR first-order conversion:** $X_A = 1 - e^{-k\,\tau}$; **CSTR first-order conversion:** $X_A = \dfrac{k\,\tau}{1 + k\,\tau}$.
- **Adiabatic temperature rise** is the key safety parameter for exothermic hydrogenations — must be managed with quench zones and cascade control.
- **Three control objectives** of an HDS reactor: (i) bed T profile, (ii) H₂/feed ratio, (iii) product sulfur.
- **Two analyzer types:** Online XRF for sulfur, chemiluminescence / NDIR for NOx, electrochemical for H₂S.
- **Safety interlocks:** high-pressure trip, low H₂ flow trip, high ΔT trip, NH₃ slip trip.
- **Ellingham diagram** tells you the *minimum* temperature at which a chosen reductant (C, CO, H₂) can reduce a given metal oxide.
- **Turnover frequency (TOF)** and **effectiveness factor (η)** are the standard metrics for comparing industrial reduction catalysts.
- **Refractory sulfur species** (4,6-dimethyldibenzothiophene) govern the design of modern ULSD units — they require higher H₂ pressure and more active Ni-Mo catalysts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymerisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Polymerisation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polymerisation** is a chemical reaction process in which small, low-molecular-weight molecules called **monomers** (typically with reactive functional groups such as vinyl, epoxy, amine, or carboxyl groups) undergo repetitive chemical bonding to form very large, high-molecular-weight macromolecules known as **polymers**. The reaction is the fundamental heart of the petrochemical industry's downstream plastic, synthetic rubber, elastomer, adhesive, and synthetic fibre manufacturing sectors. In petrochemical engineering nomenclature, the monomer source is derived primarily from **cracking operations** (steam cracking of naphtha, ethane, or LPG) producing key feedstocks such as **ethylene (C₂H₄), propylene (C₃H₆), butadiene (C₄H₆), styrene (C₈H₈), and vinyl chloride (CH₂=CHCl)**. Mathematically, the polymerisation reaction can be expressed in its simplest general form as: $$ n \, M \longrightarrow [-M-]_n $$ where **M** is the monomer molecule, **n** is the **degree of polymerisation (DP)**, and the bracket notation $[-M-]_n$ denotes the repeating structural unit forming the polymer chain. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Pop-It Bead Chain Analogy"** > Imagine you are holding a bucket of identical, single Lego-like plastic beads (the *monomers*) that can chemically "click" together only when a special activator (the *initiator*) provides them with reactive "sticky" ends. When you add the activator, individual beads begin snapping together end-to-end, one by one, eventually forming a long, durable chain (the *polymer*). If you wanted the chain to be tougher, you could mix two different bead colours and connect them alternately (a *copolymer*). Some beads may also form small clusters of just 3-5 beads that never get added to the main chain — these are **oligomers**. The rate at which beads click together, the temperature at which they click, and the precise timing of when you stop the process all together dictate how strong, how long, and how uniform the final chain will be. **Polymerisation is exactly this process happening at the molecular level inside huge industrial reactors**, with thermocouples, pressure transmitters, flow controllers, and online viscometers replacing the human "feel" of the bucket. ## 1.3 The Five Pillars of Polymerisation in Petrochemical Industries > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight — Must Memorize:** > 1. **Monomer** — the fundamental building block (e.g., ethylene → polyethylene). > 2. **Polymer** — the macromolecular product. > 3. **Degree of Polymerisation (DP)** — number of monomer units in a polymer chain. > 4. **Mechanism** — the pathway by which monomers join (chain-growth or step-growth). > 5. **Reactor Environment** — the controlled physical conditions (T, P, catalyst, residence time) inside the polymerisation reactor. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics in Industrial Polymerisation:** > - **Molecular weight (M_w)** of commercial polymers typically lies in the range of **10,000 – 10,000,000 g/mol**. > - **Average Degree of Polymerisation (DP)** for commodity plastics is typically between **500 and 50,000**. > - **Standard Operating Pressure** ranges from vacuum (polycondensation) up to **3000 bar** (LDPE/Ziegler-Natta polyethylene). > - **Standard Operating Temperature** ranges from **−100 °C (anionic) to +300 °C (high-pressure polyethylene)**. > - **Conversion** per pass typically: **10–30 %** for bulk/solution continuous reactors; **80–99 %** for batch reactors. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Polymerisation Reactions Polymerisation is broadly classified into **two principal mechanisms** based on the underlying kinetics and bond formation pathway: ### 2.1.1 Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerisation This proceeds via a **chain reaction mechanism** involving discrete initiation, propagation, and termination steps. It is the dominant process used for vinyl monomers from petrochemical feedstocks. **General Chain Steps:** - **Initiation** — an initiator (I) decomposes to form a free radical R•, which attacks the first monomer to form an active centre M₁•. - **Propagation** — the active chain radical M_n• sequentially adds monomer molecules at the growing chain end. - **Chain Transfer** — the active centre is transferred to another molecule (monomer, polymer, solvent, or chain transfer agent), creating a new active centre while stopping growth of the original. - **Termination** — two active chains combine (combination) or disproportionate to form a dead polymer chain. > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Examples:** Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Polybutadiene, Polymethyl Methacrylate (PMMA). ### 2.1.2 Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerisation In step-growth polymerisation, any two reactive species (monomer, dimer, trimer, oligomer) can react with each other at any time, with the elimination of a small molecule (typically water, HCl, methanol, or ammonia). The polymer builds up slowly, and high molecular weight is achieved only at very high conversion (>98 %). **Industrial Examples:** Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), Nylon 6,6, Polycarbonate (PC), Phenol-Formaldehyde (Bakelite), Polyurethane (PU). ## 2.2 Detailed Free-Radical Polymerisation Kinetics (Chain-Growth) The kinetic scheme governing free-radical polymerisation in the steady-state regime forms the foundation of reactor design and control. ### Step-by-Step Reaction Sequence 1. **Initiator Decomposition:** $$ I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2R^{\bullet} $$ 2. **Initiation (Radical attack on monomer):** $$ R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} M_1^{\bullet} $$ 3. **Propagation (Chain growth):** $$ M_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} M_{n+1}^{\bullet} $$ 4. **Termination by Combination:** $$ M_n^{\bullet} + M_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{tc}} M_{n+m} $$ 5. **Termination by Disproportionation:** $$ M_n^{\bullet} + M_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{td}} M_n + M_m $$ 6. **Chain Transfer to Monomer (CTA = monomer):** $$ M_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_{tr,M}} M_n + M_1^{\bullet} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for control:** Each rate constant $(k_d, k_i, k_p, k_t)$ is **strongly temperature-dependent** following Arrhenius behaviour. This is the reason temperature control accuracy in industrial polymerisation reactors directly determines the **molecular weight distribution (MWD)** of the final product. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following equations — these are high-yield for KTU 2024 board exams.** | # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Degree of Polymerisation (Number Average)** | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i \cdot X_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Dimensionless; $X_i$ = DP of chain $i$ | | 2 | **Degree of Polymerisation (Weight Average)** | $\bar{X}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i X_i^2}{\sum N_i X_i}$ | Dimensionless; always $\geq \bar{X}_n$ | | 3 | **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n}$ | PDI = 1 (monodisperse); PDI = 1.5–3 (typical free radical) | | 4 | **Carothers Equation (Step-Growth, equimolar)** | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $p$ = fractional conversion of functional groups | | 5 | **Weight-Average DP (Step-Growth)** | $\bar{X}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p}$ | Used for MWD analysis | | 6 | **Rate of Initiation** | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | mol/(L·s); $f$ = initiator efficiency (0.3–0.8) | | 7 | **Rate of Propagation** | $R_p = k_p [M][M^{\bullet}]$ | mol/(L·s); dominant rate expression | | 8 | **Rate of Termination** | $R_t = k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$ | mol/(L·s); $k_t = k_{tc} + k_{td}$ | | 9 | **Steady-State Radical Concentration** | $[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | mol/L | | 10 | **Rate of Polymerisation (overall)** | $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | mol/(L·s) | | 11 | **Kinetic Chain Length (ν)** | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]}}$ | Average monomers added per active centre | | 12 | **Number-Average DP with Chain Transfer** | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_M + C_S \dfrac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \dfrac{[I]}{[M]}$ | Mayo equation; $C$ = chain transfer constants | | 13 | **Arrhenius Temperature Dependence** | $k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $E_a$ typically 80–120 kJ/mol for $k_p$ | | 14 | **Reactor Energy Balance (Adiabatic Temp Rise)** | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)[M]_0}{\rho C_p}$ | Critical for runaway safety | ## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why Instrumentation Engineers Must Master These Equations:** > - **Molecular weight** is the **single most critical product quality parameter**. It is not measured directly online but is **inferred** from temperature, initiator feed rate, monomer conversion, and online viscosity. > - **PDI control** requires precise **temperature uniformity** (±0.5 °C for bulk polyethylene) — drives investment in advanced cascade and model-predictive control (MPC). > - **Chain transfer constants** of solvents and impurities (e.g., H₂ in polypropylene synthesis) are deliberately exploited by adding hydrogen as an MWD modifier. > - **Heat removal** during highly exothermic polymerisation (ΔH for ethylene = ~95 kJ/mol) is the **#1 reactor design challenge** and dictates the use of cooled CSTRs, loop reactors, and tubular reactors. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Carothers Equation for Step-Growth Polymerisation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Carothers Equation Derivation (a KTU board favourite — practice the full derivation):** **Starting Premises:** Let $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules (with two reactive A-groups per molecule, e.g., HOOC-R-COOH). Let $N$ = number of molecules still present at conversion $p$ (whether monomer, dimer, oligomer, or polymer). A **bond (linkage) is formed** every time two functional groups react. **Step 1: Definition of fractional conversion.** $$ p = \dfrac{\text{Number of functional groups reacted}}{\text{Total initial number of functional groups}} $$ Since each bifunctional monomer has 2 functional groups: $$ \text{Total initial functional groups} = 2N_0 $$ $$ \text{Groups reacted at conversion } p = (2N_0)p $$ **Step 2: Relate groups reacted to bonds formed.** Every bond formation consumes 2 functional groups: $$ \text{Bonds formed} = \dfrac{(2N_0)p}{2} = N_0 p $$ **Step 3: Apply conservation of molecules.** When bonds are formed, the number of molecules decreases. Initially we had $N_0$ molecules, and each bond reduces the count by 1: $$ N = N_0 - N_0 p = N_0(1-p) $$ **Step 4: Define the Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation.** By definition, $\bar{X}_n$ = total monomer units initially / number of molecules remaining at conversion $p$: $$ \bar{X}_n = \dfrac{N_0}{N} $$ **Step 5: Substitute the expression for $N$.** $$ \bar{X}_n = \dfrac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \dfrac{1}{1-p} $$ **Result — Carothers Equation:** $$ \boxed{\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}} $$ **Critical Insight — High-Yield Numerical Interpretation:** - At $p = 0.50$ (50 % conversion): $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-0.5) = 2$ — only dimers. - At $p = 0.90$ (90 % conversion): $\bar{X}_n = 1/0.10 = 10$. - At $p = 0.99$ (99 % conversion): $\bar{X}_n = 1/0.01 = 100$. - At $p = 0.999$ (99.9 % conversion): $\bar{X}_n = 1000$ — high polymer. > [!WARNING] > **Exam Trap:** A common KTU exam trick is to ask the conversion needed to achieve $\bar{X}_n = 200$. Students wrongly invert the formula. The correct rearrangement is $p = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{200} = 0.995 = 99.5\%$. This is precisely why industrial step-growth reactors (PET, Nylon) are designed for **near-quantitative conversion**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Radical Concentration $[M^{\bullet}]$ **Step 1: At steady state, the rate of radical generation equals the rate of radical loss.** $$ R_i = R_t $$ **Step 2: Write the kinetic expressions for each.** $$ 2 f k_d [I] = k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2 $$ **Step 3: Solve for the radical concentration.** $$ [M^{\bullet}]^2 = \dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t} $$ $$ [M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}} $$ **Step 4: Substitute back into the propagation rate expression $R_p = k_p [M][M^{\bullet}]$.** $$ R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}} $$ **Step 5: Derive the kinetic chain length $\nu$.** $$ \nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}}{2 f k_d [I]} $$ $$ \nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]}} $$ **Significance:** - Increasing **[M]** → longer chains (higher molecular weight). - Increasing **[I]** → more radical centres → shorter chains. - Increasing **Temperature** → all rate constants rise, but $k_d$ typically rises faster → net shorter chains (viscosity drops) — this is why precise temperature control is essential. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Free-Radical vs. Step-Growth Polymerisation | Parameter | Free-Radical (Chain-Growth) | Step-Growth (Condensation) | |---|---|---| | **Active species** | Free radical R• | Functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂) | | **Initiation** | Required (initiator, heat, UV) | Not required — any two species react | | **Monomer disappearance** | Slow; consumed throughout reaction | Slow; most monomer consumed only near end | | **Polymer molecular weight** | High from the start; rises slowly | Very low initially; rises sharply near end | | **High MW at conversion** | Achievable at 20–40 % conversion | Requires > 98 % conversion | | **Reactive intermediates** | Only growing radical chain | All oligomers (dimer, trimer, ..., n-mer) | | **Reaction by-product** | None | Small molecule (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH) | | **Chain stopper** | Termination, chain transfer | Monofunctional impurity | | **Industrial reactor** | CSTR, Tubular, Loop, Suspension | Batch stirred, wiped-film evaporator | | **Examples** | LDPE, HDPE, PP, PVC, PS | PET, Nylon 6,6, PU, Phenolic resins | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** In a free-radical polymerisation of styrene at 60 °C using AIBN as initiator, the following data are given: > $[I]_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L, $[M]_0 = 5.0$ mol/L, $f = 0.6$, $k_d = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ s⁻¹, $k_p = 340$ L/(mol·s), $k_t = 7.2 \times 10^{7}$ L/(mol·s). Calculate (a) the steady-state radical concentration, (b) the rate of polymerisation, and (c) the kinetic chain length. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Radical Concentration:** $$ [M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.6 \times (3.0 \times 10^{-5}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})}{7.2 \times 10^{7}}} $$ Numerator: $2 \times 0.6 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 3.6 \times 10^{-8}$ $$ [M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.6 \times 10^{-8}}{7.2 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-16}} $$ $$ [M^{\bullet}] = 2.24 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol/L} $$ **Step 2 — Rate of Polymerisation:** $$ R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}] = 340 \times 5.0 \times 2.24 \times 10^{-8} $$ $$ R_p = 3.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/(L·s)} $$ **Step 3 — Kinetic Chain Length:** $$ \nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]}} = \dfrac{340 \times 5.0}{2 \times (2.24 \times 10^{-8})^{-1} \cdot \text{equivalent}} $$ A simpler direct calculation: $$ \nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{3.81 \times 10^{-5}}{3.6 \times 10^{-8}} = 1058 $$ This means an average of **1058 monomer units** are added per active radical centre before termination — yielding a moderate molecular weight polymer. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Polymerisation Reactor Control Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feedstock Storage
Ethylene Propylene
Vinyl Chloride Monomer] --> B[Monomer Purification
Drying Column
Molecular Sieve] B --> C[Mass Flow Controller
MFC Primary Loop] C --> D[Reactor Feed Preheater
Heat Exchanger] D --> E[Initiator Catalyst Dosing
Peroxide or ZN Catalyst] E --> F[Polymerisation Reactor
CSTR Tubular or Loop Type] G[Coolant Circulation Loop
Jacket or Internal Coil] --> F F --> H[Reactor Effluent Stream
Polymer Slurry or Melt] H --> I[Pressure Letdown Valve
and Separator] I --> J[Unreacted Monomer
Recycle Compressor] J --> B H --> K[Online Viscometer
Molecular Weight Proxy] H --> L[Online Refractive Index
Conversion Monitor] H --> M[Product Pelletiser
and Storage Silo] N[Reactor Temperature Sensor
RTD or Thermocouple Array] --> O[Distributed Control System
DCS Central Controller] P[Reactor Pressure Transmitter
PT High Range] --> O Q[Reactor Level Detector
Radar or DPT] --> O R[Coolant Flow Indicator
FIC on Jacket] --> O S[Monomer Feed FT] --> O O --> T[Control Valve on Coolant
TC Cascade to FT] O --> U[Control Valve on Monomer Feed
FC Master Loop] O --> V[Control Valve on Initiator
Ratio Controller Output] K --> O L --> O ``` ## 4.2 Mechanism Flow: Free-Radical Polymerisation Chain Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Initiator Molecule I] --> B[Step 1 Decomposition
kd] B --> C[Two Free Radicals R dot] C --> D[Step 2 Initiation
ki attack on monomer M] D --> E[Active Centre M1 dot
Chain Radical Started] E --> F[Step 3 Propagation
kp sequential monomer addition] F --> G[Growing Chain Radical Mn dot] G --> H{Step 4 Termination Event} H --> I[Combination kt c
Two chains fuse into one dead polymer] H --> J[Disproportionation kt d
Two dead polymer chains formed] G --> K[Step 5 Chain Transfer
ktr to monomer solvent or polymer] K --> L[Dead Polymer plus
New Active Centre M1 dot] L --> F ``` ## 4.3 Polymerisation Reactor Type Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Polymerisation Reactor] --> B{Monomer Phase?} B -->|Gas Phase| C[Gas Phase Fluidised Bed
Used for HDPE and LLDPE] B -->|Liquid Bulk| D[Continuous Stirred Tank CSTR
or Loop Reactor] B -->|Solution| E[Slimy CSTR with Solvent Recovery] B -->|Slurry Suspension| F[Loop or Autoclave CSTR
Used for PVC and PP] B -->|Emulsion| G[Batch Reactor with
Surfactant Stabilisation] D --> H{Heat Removal Need?} H -->|Very High| I[Tubular Reactor
LDPE High Pressure] H -->|Moderate| J[Jacketed CSTR
or External Loop Cooler] F --> K{Pressure Range?} K -->|Atmospheric| L[Standard CSTR] K -->|100 to 500 bar| M[Autoclave or Loop with
Hypercompressor] C --> N[Instrumentation includes
Bed Temperature Probes
Online Gas Chromatograph
Catalyst Feed Ratio] I --> O[Instrumentation includes
Multiple Zone TCs
Melt Viscometer at Outlet
Pressure Profile PI Array] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to Read These Diagrams in KTU Exams:** The KTU examiner typically asks block-diagram questions worth **7 marks**, where you must (1) identify each block, (2) describe the signal flow, (3) explain the control strategy applied, and (4) list at least three instruments used. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEIT861] **Define the term "Degree of Polymerisation" and explain its significance in determining polymer properties.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **degree of polymerisation (DP or $\bar{X}_n$)** is defined as the average number of monomer units present in a single polymer chain. For a polymer of number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n$ and monomer molecular weight $M_0$: $$ \bar{X}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} $$ **Significance:** - DP directly governs **mechanical strength** — higher DP yields tougher, more impact-resistant polymers (e.g., HDPE with DP > 10,000 used for piping). - DP governs **melt viscosity** — higher DP increases viscosity exponentially, complicating extrusion processing. - DP governs **end-use applications** — low DP (1,000–10,000) used for coatings and adhesives; high DP (>100,000) used for structural plastics. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEEIT861] **Distinguish between addition (chain-growth) and condensation (step-growth) polymerisation. Give one petrochemical industry example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Addition (Chain-Growth) | Condensation (Step-Growth) | |---|---|---| | Bond formation | Monomer units add to a growing chain | Any two reactive species combine | | By-product | None | Small molecule (H₂O, HCl, CH₃OH) | | Initiation | Required (initiator, heat, UV) | Not required | | Monomer concentration | Decreases steadily throughout | Most monomer consumed only near full conversion | | Industrial Example | **Polyethylene from ethylene** (LDPE/HDPE/LLDPE) | **PET from terephthalic acid + ethylene glycol** | ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PEEIT861] **Q. (a)** Explain the free-radical polymerisation mechanism in detail with the kinetic scheme for initiation, propagation, and termination. Derive the steady-state expression for the rate of polymerisation. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** A commercial polyethylene reactor operates at 150 °C with $[M]_0 = 8.5$ mol/L and initiator concentration $[I]_0 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L. Given: $k_d = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ s⁻¹, $k_p = 240$ L/(mol·s), $k_t = 6.0 \times 10^{7}$ L/(mol·s), and $f = 0.65$. Calculate the rate of polymerisation and the kinetic chain length. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** The free-radical polymerisation of a vinyl monomer $CH_2=CHR$ proceeds through four distinct kinetic steps. **Step 1 — Initiator Decomposition:** A thermal initiator such as benzoyl peroxide or AIBN decomposes homolytically: $$ I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2R^{\bullet} $$ Rate: $R_d = 2 f k_d [I]$, where $f$ = initiator efficiency (0.3–0.8). **Step 2 — Initiation:** The free radical $R^{\bullet}$ attacks the $\pi$-bond of the first monomer molecule, forming the active chain radical: $$ R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} M_1^{\bullet} $$ Rate: $R_i = k_i [R^{\bullet}][M]$. **Step 3 — Propagation:** The chain radical adds monomer molecules sequentially. This is the **dominant step** that builds molecular weight: $$ M_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} M_{n+1}^{\bullet} $$ Rate: $R_p = k_p [M^{\bullet}][M]$. **Step 4 — Termination:** Two chain radicals combine (giving one dead polymer chain) or disproportionate (giving two dead chains): $$ M_n^{\bullet} + M_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{tc}} M_{n+m} \text{ (combination)} $$ $$ M_n^{\bullet} + M_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{td}} M_n + M_m \text{ (disproportionation)} $$ Rate: $R_t = k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$. **Derivation of Steady-State $R_p$:** Applying the steady-state assumption to radical concentration: $$ \dfrac{d[M^{\bullet}]}{dt} = R_i - R_t = 0 \implies R_i = R_t $$ $$ 2 f k_d [I] = k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2 \implies [M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}} $$ Substituting into the propagation rate expression: $$ \boxed{R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}} $$ This is the **fundamental industrial design equation** for free-radical polymerisation reactors. --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Given:** $[M]_0 = 8.5$ mol/L, $[I]_0 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L, $k_d = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ s⁻¹, $k_p = 240$ L/(mol·s), $k_t = 6.0 \times 10^{7}$ L/(mol·s), $f = 0.65$. **Step 1 — Radical Concentration:** $$ [M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.65 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-4}) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-4})}{6.0 \times 10^{7}}} $$ Numerator: $2 \times 0.65 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 7.8 \times 10^{-8}$ $$ [M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{7.8 \times 10^{-8}}{6.0 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{1.30 \times 10^{-15}} = 3.61 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol/L} $$ **Step 2 — Rate of Polymerisation:** $$ R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}] = 240 \times 8.5 \times 3.61 \times 10^{-8} $$ $$ \boxed{R_p = 7.36 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/(L·s)}} $$ **Step 3 — Kinetic Chain Length $\nu$:** $$ R_i = 2 f k_d [I] = 2 \times 0.65 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 7.80 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol/(L·s)} $$ $$ \nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{7.36 \times 10^{-5}}{7.80 \times 10^{-8}} = 944 $$ **Interpretation:** Each active radical centre adds ~944 monomer units before terminating, giving a polymer with number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n = 944 \times 28 = 26{,}432$ g/mol (assuming ethylene monomer $M_0 = 28$). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, PEEIT861] **Q. (a)** Describe the different types of polymerisation reactors used in the petrochemical industry (batch, CSTR, tubular, loop, fluidised bed). Discuss their instrumentation and control strategies in detail. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Explain the Carothers equation. A nylon 6,6 production line has reached 99.5 % conversion of functional groups. Calculate the number-average degree of polymerisation and the weight-average degree of polymerisation. Comment on whether this polymer is suitable for textile fibre applications. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Reactor Type 1 — Batch Reactor:** Used for low-volume, high-value polymers (PMMA, specialty PS, phenolic resins). All reagents are charged at once. Polymerisation proceeds with time. - **Instrumentation:** RTD/thermocouple array, pressure transmitter, sampling valve for offline GPC. - **Control Strategy:** Reactor temperature controlled via jacket cooling using a cascade loop (master T-controller → slave jacket-T or coolant-FT). Conversion tracked by adiabatic temperature rise or online density meter. **Reactor Type 2 — Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR):** Used for bulk polymerisation (PS, PMMA). Steady-state operation. Heat removal is the limiting factor. - **Instrumentation:** Multiple temperature sensors at different reactor zones (hot spots form at impeller), residence time distribution via tracer studies. - **Control Strategy:** Feed-forward control on monomer feed based on FT, with feedback TC cascade to jacket coolant flow. Conversion inferred from heat balance. **Reactor Type 3 — Tubular Reactor:** Used for LDPE (high-pressure polyethylene). Operates at 1000–3000 bar and 150–300 °C. Long pipe (often >1 km) with multiple initiator injection points. - **Instrumentation:** Temperature profile measured by thermocouples at 10–20 axial positions, pressure profile by pressure transmitters, melt viscosity by online capillary rheometer at the outlet. - **Control Strategy:** Multizone temperature control with initiator injection ratio control — molecular weight distribution (MWD) is managed by adjusting initiator feed rate at each zone. **Reactor Type 4 — Loop Reactor:** Used for HDPE and PP slurry processes. High surface-area-to-volume ratio gives excellent heat removal. - **Instrumentation:** Circulating slurry flow meter (magnetic or Coriolis), inline density meter, online particle size analyzer. - **Control Strategy:** Slurry circulation rate controls heat removal; product concentration controlled by continuous bleed. **Reactor Type 5 — Fluidised Bed Gas-Phase Reactor:** Used for HDPE/LLDPE production (e.g., UNIPOL process). Gas-phase monomer fluidises catalyst particles. - **Instrumentation:** Bed temperature probes at multiple heights (critical to avoid hot-spot agglomeration), bed pressure drop measurement (ΔP for fluidisation quality), online gas chromatograph for gas composition. - **Control Strategy:** Reactor pressure directly controls monomer partial pressure → controls reaction rate. Hydrogen/ethylene ratio (R21) controls molecular weight. --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Carothers Equation Theory:** For a step-growth polymerisation of bifunctional monomers at fractional conversion $p$ of functional groups: $$ \bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p} $$ The weight-average degree of polymerisation for a Flory-distributed step-growth polymer: $$ \bar{X}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p} $$ **Given:** $p = 0.995$ (i.e., 99.5 % conversion). **Step 1 — Number-Average DP:** $$ \bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-0.995} = \dfrac{1}{0.005} = 200 $$ **Step 2 — Weight-Average DP:** $$ \bar{X}_w = \dfrac{1+0.995}{1-0.995} = \dfrac{1.995}{0.005} = 399 $$ **Step 3 — Polydispersity Index:** $$ \text{PDI} = \dfrac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{399}{200} = 1.995 \approx 2.0 $$ **Step 4 — Molecular Weight (assuming nylon 6,6 repeat unit $M_0 = 226$ g/mol):** $$ \overline{M}_n = 200 \times 226 = 45{,}200 \text{ g/mol} $$ **Step 5 — Suitability for Textile Fibre:** A PDI of approximately 2.0 is characteristic of step-growth polymerisation and is **acceptable for fibre spinning** (industrial textile nylon 6,6 typically has $\overline{M}_n$ in the range of **12,000–20,000 g/mol** for apparel, and **30,000–45,000 g/mol** for industrial/tire cord applications). The calculated value of 45,200 g/mol corresponds to **high-tenacity industrial-grade nylon 6,6**, ideal for tire cord, conveyor belts, and reinforced composites, but **too viscous for fine-denier apparel textile spinning**. Hence, this material is **suitable for industrial fibre applications but not for fine apparel textiles**. ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Polymerisation Questions:** > 1. **Confusing $\bar{X}_n$ and $\bar{X}_w$** — always remember $\bar{X}_w \geq \bar{X}_n$ and PDI $\geq 1$. > 2. **Inverting the Carothers equation** — to find $p$ for a given $\bar{X}_n$, it is $p = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n}$, NOT $p = 1 - \bar{X}_n$. > 3. **Forgetting the factor 2 in $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$** — many textbooks write $R_i = f k_d [I]$; KTU typically expects the factor 2 unless specified. > 4. **Wrong chain-transfer equation** — the Mayo equation is $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_M + C_S \dfrac{[S]}{[M]}$; do not forget all chain transfer terms (monomer, solvent, initiator). > 5. **Ignoring PDI** — when asked for polymer "quality," mention PDI along with $\overline{M}_n$. > 6. **Units** — always state mol/(L·s) for rate, g/mol for molecular weight, dimensionless for DP. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Polymerisation (Module 2):** - **Polymerisation** is the joining of monomers into polymers; central to petrochemical plastic, fibre, and rubber production. - **Degree of polymerisation (DP, $\bar{X}_n$)** = average number of monomer units per chain; controls strength, viscosity, and application. - **Two principal mechanisms:** Addition (chain-growth, free-radical, ionic, coordination) and Condensation (step-growth, with by-product). - **Free-radical kinetics:** Initiation → Propagation → Termination (+ Chain Transfer). Steady-state radical concentration is $\sqrt{2fk_d[I]/k_t}$. - **Carothers equation:** $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$. High MW requires > 98 % conversion in step-growth. - **PDI = $\bar{X}_w/\bar{X}_n$**; Free-radical PDI ≈ 1.5–3; Step-growth PDI ≈ 2 (theoretical max at high conversion). - **Reactor types:** Batch, CSTR, Tubular, Loop, Fluidised Bed — each requires different instrumentation and control strategy. - **Critical control parameters:** Temperature (T), Pressure (P), Monomer feed rate (F), Initiator/catalyst feed (I), Residence time (τ), and Conversion (X). - **Online analytical instruments:** Viscometer (melt/solution), Refractometer (conversion), NIR probe (composition), GPC (molecular weight), Densitometer. - **Chain transfer agents** (H₂, CCl₄, thiols) are used industrially to control MW. - **Inhibitors** (MEHQ, hydroquinone, TEMPO) are added during monomer storage to prevent runaway polymerisation. - **Safety priority #1:** Heat removal — polymerisation is highly exothermic; runaway leads to "unzipping" or explosive decompression. - **KTU 2024 board exam must-know:** Carothers derivation, free-radical $R_p$ derivation with steady-state assumption, reactor-type matching with product, control loop diagrams.

topics.title: Alkylation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alkylation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alkylation** is a critical secondary petrochemical unit process in which low-molecular-weight **isoparaffins** (predominantly **isobutane**, *iso*-C₄H₁₀) are chemically combined with low-molecular-weight **olefins** (typically C₃⁻, C₄⁻, and C₅⁻ olefins) in the presence of a strong liquid acid catalyst to produce a high-octane, branched-chain paraffinic product known as **alkylate** (predominantly C₈ isomers, principally **2,2,4-trimethylpentane**, i.e., **isooctane**).

The alkylation reaction is a **liquid-phase, exothermic, catalytic addition reaction** that does **not** consume the catalyst (catalyst acts as a proton-transfer medium), although catalyst deactivation occurs through acid dilution and acid-soluble-oil (ASO) formation, requiring continuous regeneration or make-up.

## 1.2 Two Principal Commercial Alkylation Routes

| Process | Catalyst | Operating Temp. | Acid Strength | Commercial Share |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sulfuric Acid Alkylation** | H₂SO₄ (98–99 wt %) | **0 °C to 10 °C** | 88–94 wt % in reactor | Widely used |
| **Hydrofluoric Acid Alkylation** | HF (anhydrous, ≥ 90 %) | **25 °C to 40 °C** | 85–95 wt % in reactor | Decreasing due to safety |
| **Solid Acid (Emerging)** | Zeolites / Sulfated ZrO₂ | 50 °C to 100 °C | Heterogeneous | Pilot/R&D |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** alkylation for instrumentation study, owing to its prevalence in Indian refineries (BPCL, IOCL, HMEL).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Molecular Lego Brick Assembly"

Think of alkylation like a **precision Lego assembly workshop**:
- The **isobutane** acts like a pre-formed, stable Lego **base plate** (saturated, stable, no free hands to grab things).
- The **olefin** is a reactive, "hungry" Lego **brick** with double-bond "hands" reaching out.
- The **acid catalyst** (H₂SO₄ or HF) is the **factory foreman** who hands a proton (H⁺) to the olefin, making it super-reactive (a **carbocation**).
- This carbocation then "locks onto" the isobutane base plate, forming a much larger, highly branched **isooctane Lego structure** that has excellent octane rating.

The resulting alkylate is a clean-burning, high-octane gasoline blending stock that has **zero olefins, zero aromatics, and very low sulfur**.

## 1.4 Key Process Performance Metrics

- **Octane Number (RON) of alkylate**: **92–96** (Research Octane Number, RON)
- **Motor Octane Number (MON)**: **90–94**
- **Sensitivity (RON − MON)**: ~2–3 (very low — desirable for modern engines)
- **Reactor vapor pressure (Reid)**: **4–6 psi** (very low — excellent volatility)
- **Sulfur content**: **< 50 ppm** (essentially zero for HF units)
- **Acid consumption (H₂SO₄)**: **0.5–1.0 kg acid per ton alkylate produced**
- **HF acid consumption**: **0.3–0.6 kg acid per ton alkylate**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Reaction Chemistry (Sulfuric Acid Alkylation)

The overall stoichiometry for the **primary alkylation reaction** of *iso*-butylene with isobutane is:

$$
\text{C}_4\text{H}_8 \; (\text{olefin}) + \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} \; (\text{isobutane}) \;\xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4}\; \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} \; (\text{alkylate})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The principal desired product is **2,2,4-trimethylpentane (isooctane)**, which by convention has a **Research Octane Number (RON) of 100**. *n*-Heptane has RON = 0. Therefore, alkylate is the **gold-standard gasoline blending stock**.

## 2.2 Side / Competing Reactions

1. **Polymerization (unwanted)**: Olefin + Olefin → heavy polymers (C₁₂⁺); consumes acid.
2. **Cracking / Disproportionation**: Alkylate → smaller paraffins + olefins; favored at high T.
3. **Ester formation**: Olefin + H₂SO₄ → alkyl sulfates (acid loss).
4. **Acid-Soluble Oil (ASO) formation**: Polymer + acid → reddish oil; dilutes acid.

These side reactions lower alkylate quality (lower octane) and increase acid consumption, making **process control of T, I/O ratio, and contact time** vital.

## 2.3 Mechanistic Steps (Carbocation Chain Mechanism)

| Step | Description |
|---|---|
| **Step 1 — Olefin Protonation** | H⁺ from H₂SO₄ adds to olefin → **tert-butyl carbocation** (C₄⁺) |
| **Step 2 — Hydride Abstraction** | C₄⁺ abstracts H⁻ from isobutane → forms **isooctyl carbocation** (C₈⁺) + new tert-butyl C₄⁺ (chain propagation) |
| **Step 3 — Proton Elimination / Termination** | C₈⁺ loses H⁺ → final **isooctane** (alkylate) — H⁺ returns to acid phase |
| **Step 4 — Undesired termination** | C₄⁺ reacts with olefin → polymerization (chain termination) |

> [!NOTE]
> The hydride transfer in **Step 2** is the **rate-limiting step** and the **key to selectivity**. High isobutane concentration ensures C₄⁺ meets isobutane rather than another olefin → suppresses polymerization.

## 2.4 Critical Process Variables (KTU-High-Yield)

1. **Isobutane-to-Olefin (I/O) Molar Ratio** — the **single most important control parameter**.
   - Typical range: **8 : 1 to 15 : 1** (industrial), higher I/O → higher octane.
   - Ratio < 5:1 → polymerization dominates → poor alkylate, high acid consumption.

2. **Reactor Temperature**:
   - H₂SO₄ units: **0 °C to 10 °C** (cooling required; ΔH_reaction ≈ −220 kJ/mol of olefin).
   - HF units: **25 °C to 40 °C**.

3. **Acid Strength (H₂SO₄)**: Must remain **≥ 88 wt %** in reactor; below ~85 % the reaction essentially stops.

4. **Contact Time**: **5 to 25 minutes** in reactor (for Stratco or Kellogg contactors).

5. **Agitation Intensity**: Emulsion of acid/hydrocarbon must be finely dispersed — typically **200–500 RPM** in stirred reactors.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Equation | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat of Reaction (Primary alkylation)** | $\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -220$ | kJ / mol olefin | Exothermic; reactor cooling duty |
| 2 | **Theoretical alkylate yield** | $Y_{alkylate} = \dfrac{M_{C_8H_{18}}}{M_{C_4H_8}+M_{C_4H_{10}}} \times 100 \approx 102.3$ | wt % (mass basis basis incl. H⁺) | Based on olefin feed |
| 3 | **I/O Molar Ratio** | $R_{I/O} = \dfrac{n_{iC_4}}{n_{olefin}}$ | dimensionless | Industrial: 8–15 |
| 4 | **Acid Consumption Rate** | $C_{acid} = \dfrac{m_{acid\,make-up}}{\text{tons alkylate produced}}$ | kg/ton | H₂SO₄: 0.5–1.0; HF: 0.3–0.6 |
| 5 | **Octane Blending Index** | $BI = RON_{alkylate} \times (\text{vol \%})$ | — | Used in refinery blend optimization |
| 6 | **Reactor residence time** | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{reactor}}{\dot{V}_{total\,liquid}}$ | minutes | 5–25 min typical |
| 7 | **Heat removal duty** | $\dot{Q}_{rem} = \dot{n}_{olefin} \times \vert \Delta H_{rxn} \vert$ | kW or BTU/hr | Mandatory cooling design |
| 8 | **Acid strength depletion rate** | $\Delta w_{acid} = \dfrac{(w_{in} - w_{out}) \times 100}{w_{in}}$ | % depletion | Triggers acid make-up logic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Pitfall:** When calculating I/O ratio in a **stream mix**, always use **molar flow rates** (kmol/h), never volumetric flow. Use ideal gas / liquid compositions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Balance Around the Alkylation Reactor

**Given:** A sulfuric acid alkylation unit processes a combined hydrocarbon feed of **500 kmol/h olefin** (as C₄⁻) and **5500 kmol/h isobutane** at an **I/O molar ratio of 11:1**. The acid phase (H₂SO₄, 98 wt %) flows at **2500 kg/h** and exits at **94 wt %** due to dilution by side-reaction water.

**Required:** Calculate the **fresh acid make-up rate** if alkylate production is **440 kmol/h** and acid consumption is **0.75 kg acid per ton alkylate**.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the overall stoichiometric flow.**

The net reaction is:
$$
\text{C}_4\text{H}_8 + \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10} \longrightarrow \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}
$$

Moles of isobutane consumed (assuming complete reaction of olefin):
$$
n_{iC_4,\,consumed} = n_{olefin,\,fed} = 500 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

Therefore, moles of alkylate produced (assuming 100 % primary selectivity):
$$
n_{C_8H_{18}} = 500 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply a mass balance on acid phase to find make-up.**

The acid phase enters at 98 wt % H₂SO₄ and exits at 94 wt % H₂SO₄. The depletion of acid (from 98 → 94) means **4 wt %** of the acid stream is consumed in side reactions (sulfation, ester formation) and replaced by water/ASO.

Let $\dot{m}_{in}$ = fresh strong acid (98 %) make-up rate, and $\dot{m}_{recycle}$ = re-concentrated acid return (assumed equivalent in mass to fresh + spent, simplified).

For steady-state acid inventory, the mass of pure H₂SO₄ entering must equal pure H₂SO₄ leaving + acid consumed:
$$
0.98 \,\dot{m}_{makeup} = 0.94 \,\dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{consumed}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute acid consumed using empirical specific consumption.**

Mass of alkylate produced per hour:
$$
\dot{m}_{alkylate} = n_{alkylate} \times M_{C_8H_{18}} = 500 \times 114.23 = 57{,}115 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Acid consumed per hour (using 0.75 kg acid per ton alkylate):
$$
\dot{m}_{consumed} = 0.75 \times \dfrac{57{,}115}{1000} = 42.84 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the make-up stream (assume $\dot{m}_{out} = 2500 + \dot{m}_{makeup}$ for mass balance closure).**

$$
0.98\,\dot{m}_{makeup} = 0.94\,(2500 + \dot{m}_{makeup}) + 42.84
$$

$$
0.98\,\dot{m}_{makeup} = 2350 + 0.94\,\dot{m}_{makeup} + 42.84
$$

$$
(0.98 - 0.94)\,\dot{m}_{makeup} = 2392.84
$$

$$
0.04\,\dot{m}_{makeup} = 2392.84
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{makeup} = \dfrac{2392.84}{0.04} = 59{,}821 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check.**

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{makeup} \approx 5.98 \times 10^{4} \text{ kg/h of fresh 98 \% H}_2\text{SO}_4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the **spent acid (88–90 %)** is sent to a **regeneration unit (e.g., Monsanto Stratco acid recovery)** which concentrates it back to 98 %, and the loop is closed. The make-up value above is theoretical worst-case without regeneration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat-Removal (Cooling) Duty Calculation

**Given:** The same unit. The reaction enthalpy is $\Delta H = -220 \text{ kJ/mol of olefin reacted}$.

**Required:** Heat-removal duty in **kW** and **tons of refrigeration (TR)**.

**Step 1 — Molar flow rate of olefin reacted:**

$$
\dot{n}_{olefin} = 500 \text{ kmol/h} = \dfrac{500}{3600} \text{ kmol/s} = 0.1389 \text{ kmol/s} = 138.9 \text{ mol/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Total heat released per second:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{rxn} = \dot{n}_{olefin} \times \vert \Delta H \vert = 138.9 \times 220 = 30{,}558 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Tons of Refrigeration (1 TR = 3.517 kW):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{TR} = \dfrac{30{,}558}{3.517} = 8{,}689 \text{ TR}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{cooling} \approx 30.6 \text{ MW} \;\; (\approx 8{,}690 \text{ TR})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This enormous cooling load is why industrial alkylation reactors use **propane refrigerant evaporators** or **ammonia chillers** with very large heat-exchange surface area. **KTU examiners frequently ask this calculation.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion Efficiency and Alkylate Yield

**Given:** Olefin feed = 500 kmol/h; unreacted olefin in effluent = 12 kmol/h; isobutane feed = 5500 kmol/h; isobutane in effluent = 5040 kmol/h; alkylate = 488 kmol/h.

**Required:** Olefin conversion, isobutane conversion, alkylate selectivity.

**Step 1 — Olefin conversion ($X_{olefin}$):**

$$
X_{olefin} = \dfrac{n_{olefin,\,in} - n_{olefin,\,out}}{n_{olefin,\,in}} = \dfrac{500 - 12}{500} = 0.976
$$

$$
\boxed{X_{olefin} = 97.6 \,\%}
$$

**Step 2 — Isobutane conversion ($X_{iC_4}$):**

$$
X_{iC_4} = \dfrac{5500 - 5040}{5500} = \dfrac{460}{5500} = 0.0836
$$

$$
\boxed{X_{iC_4} = 8.36 \,\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Selectivity to alkylate ($S$):**

$$
S = \dfrac{n_{alkylate}}{n_{olefin,\,consumed}} = \dfrac{488}{500 - 12} = \dfrac{488}{488} = 1.0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Selectivity = 1.0 means **all reacted olefin became alkylate** (no polymerization losses). In real units, selectivity is **0.95–0.99**; losses are due to side reactions forming C₁₂⁺ polymers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sulfuric Acid Alkylation Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED_PREP [Feed Preparation Section]
        A1[Olefin Feed C3 minus to C5] --> M1[Mixer: Recycle + Fresh Olefin]
        A2[Isobutane Feed Fresh] --> M2[Mixer: Recycle iC4 plus Fresh iC4]
        M2 --> M1
    end

    subgraph REACTOR [Reactor and Contactor Section]
        M1 --> R1[Alkylation Reactor Stratco Contactor]
        A3[Sulfuric Acid 98 wt percent Fresh] --> R1
        R1 --> R2[Acid Settler Decanter]
    end

    subgraph SEP [Product Separation Section]
        R2 --> D1[Deisobutanizer Tower]
        D1 --> S1[Recycle Isobutane to M2]
        D1 --> D2[Alkylate Product Draw]
        D1 --> D3[Normal Butane Sidestream]
    end

    subgraph ACID_REGEN [Acid Regeneration Section]
        R2 --> AR1[Spent Acid Surge 88 wt percent]
        AR1 --> AR2[Acid Regenerator Concentrator]
        AR2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph COOLING [Refrigeration Loop]
        R1 --> CL1[Propane Refrigerant Chiller]
        CL1 --> R1
    end

    subgraph INSTR [Instrumentation and Control Loops]
        T1[TIC Reactor Temperature 0 to 10 deg C] -.-> R1
        L2[LIC Acid Level in Contactor] -.-> R1
        AI1[AI Acid Strength Analyzer Online NIR] -.-> AR1
        FI1[FIC Olefin Flow Rate Controller] -.-> M1
        QIC1[QIC I over O Ratio Indicator Controller] -.-> R1
    end
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Narrative

1. **Feed Preparation**: Fresh olefin stream (C₃⁻, C₄⁻, C₅⁻ from FCC units) is mixed with **recycle isobutane** to achieve the required I/O ratio.
2. **Reaction**: Combined feed enters the **Stratco contactor** (or Kellogg horizontal contactor) along with **strong sulfuric acid (98 wt %)**. Mechanical agitation creates a fine emulsion; reaction proceeds in seconds.
3. **Settling**: Emulsion flows to a **settler/separator**; the denser acid phase drops to the bottom and is recycled; the lighter hydrocarbon phase (raffinate) flows to fractionation.
4. **Fractionation**: The **deisobutanizer** separates the hydrocarbon phase into:
   - **Top:** Isobutane recycle → back to reactor.
   - **Middle:** *n*-butane sidestream (LPG product).
   - **Bottom:** Distilled alkylate (gasoline blending stock).
5. **Acid Regeneration**: Spent acid (88–90 wt %) is sent to the regenerator where vacuum evaporation restores it to ~98 wt % and recycles it back.
6. **Cooling**: Refrigerant loop removes the ~220 kJ/mol exotherm; failure of this loop is the most common cause of runaway reactions.

## 4.3 Instrumentation Emphasis (KTU Focus)

| Instrument | Loop Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **TIC (Reactor)** | Feedback, cascade to propane valve | Maintains 0–10 °C; prevents polymerization |
| **LIC (Acid Level)** | Feedback | Maintains acid/hydrocarbon interface |
| **AI (Acid Strength)** | Analyzer (online NIR/titration) | Triggers acid make-up at 88 wt % |
| **FI / FQI (Olefin Flow)** | Ratio control with isobutane | Maintains I/O ratio |
| **PIC (Reactor Pressure)** | Feedback | Slight back-pressure; vapor containment |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define alkylation in the context of petrochemical refining. State the catalysts commonly used and the major product.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Alkylation is a catalytic refining process that combines light olefins (C₃⁻ to C₅⁻) with isoparaffins (mainly isobutane) in the presence of a strong liquid acid catalyst to produce a high-octane branched paraffinic gasoline blending stock called **alkylate** (predominantly C₈ isomers, principally 2,2,4-trimethylpentane, i.e., isooctane, with RON = 92–96). The two principal catalysts used industrially are **concentrated sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄, ~98 wt %)** and **anhydrous hydrofluoric acid (HF)**. Solid acid catalysts (zeolites) are under development as environmentally safer alternatives.

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark catalysts, 1 mark product characteristics.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the Isobutane-to-Olefin (I/O) ratio? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The I/O molar ratio is defined as the moles of isobutane fed per mole of olefin fed to the alkylation reactor:

$$
R_{I/O} = \dfrac{n_{iC_4}}{n_{olefin}}
$$

It is **the single most important control variable** in alkylation. At I/O ratios of **8 : 1 to 15 : 1**, the desired primary alkylation dominates, yielding high-octane alkylate. At low I/O ratios (< 5 : 1), the olefin-olefin reaction (polymerization) dominates, producing low-octane heavy polymers and consuming acid rapidly. Maintaining a high I/O ratio ensures high alkylate quality and low acid consumption.

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark typical range, 1 mark importance.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a)** Explain the reaction mechanism of sulfuric acid alkylation with the help of the carbocation chain mechanism. Discuss the role of the acid catalyst as a proton-transfer medium. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The mechanism proceeds in four steps:

1. **Protonation of the Olefin**: A proton (H⁺) from H₂SO₄ adds to the C=C double bond of the olefin (e.g., *iso*-butylene) per Markovnikov's rule, forming a stable **tert-butyl carbocation** (C₄H₉⁺):

   $$
   \text{C}_4\text{H}_8 + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+
   $$

2. **Hydride Abstraction (Rate-Limiting Step)**: The tert-butyl carbocation abstracts a hydride ion (H⁻) from isobutane, yielding **isooctane** and a new tert-butyl cation. This step propagates the chain:

   $$
   (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+ + (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+
   $$

3. **Termination**: Eventually, the carbocation loses a proton to the acid phase, returning the H⁺ to the catalyst:

   $$
   (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+ \longrightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}^+
   $$

4. **Side Reaction**: If the carbocation meets another olefin instead of isobutane, polymerization occurs (undesired). This is suppressed by maintaining a **high I/O ratio**.

**Role of Acid Catalyst**: H₂SO₄ acts **not as a reactant** but as a **proton-transfer medium**. The H⁺ initiates the chain and is regenerated at termination, so theoretically the acid is not consumed. However, side reactions (esterification, sulfation, dilution by water of reaction) gradually reduce acid strength, requiring continuous make-up/regeneration. *(7 marks: 2 mechanism, 2 H⁺ transfer, 2 role, 1 summary.)*

---

#### **(b)** A sulfuric acid alkylation unit receives **750 kmol/h of olefin feed** and **9000 kmol/h of isobutane**. The acid stream enters the contactor at **98 wt %** and leaves at **90 wt %**. If alkylate production is **720 kmol/h** and acid consumption is **0.8 kg/ton of alkylate**, calculate:
   (i) The I/O molar ratio of the feed.
   (ii) The heat-removal duty (kW), given $\Delta H_{rxn} = -220$ kJ/mol of olefin reacted.
   (iii) The fresh acid make-up rate (kg/h), assuming spent acid mass flow = 6000 kg/h.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) I/O ratio:**
$$
R_{I/O} = \dfrac{9000}{750} = 12.0
$$

**(ii) Heat-removal duty:**

Moles of olefin reacted per second:
$$
\dot{n} = \dfrac{750}{3600} = 0.2083 \text{ kmol/s} = 208.3 \text{ mol/s}
$$

Heat released:
$$
\dot{Q} = 208.3 \times 220 = 45{,}826 \text{ kW} \approx 45.8 \text{ MW}
$$

**(iii) Acid make-up:**

Mass of alkylate produced per hour:
$$
\dot{m}_{alkylate} = 720 \times 114.23 = 82{,}245.6 \text{ kg/h} = 82.25 \text{ ton/h}
$$

Acid consumed per hour:
$$
\dot{m}_{acid,consumed} = 0.8 \times 82.25 = 65.8 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Acid mass balance (steady state):
$$
0.98\,\dot{m}_{makeup} = 0.90\,\dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{consumed}
$$

With $\dot{m}_{out} = 6000 + \dot{m}_{makeup}$:
$$
0.98\,\dot{m}_{makeup} = 0.90\,(6000 + \dot{m}_{makeup}) + 65.8
$$
$$
0.98\,\dot{m}_{makeup} = 5400 + 0.90\,\dot{m}_{makeup} + 65.8
$$
$$
0.08\,\dot{m}_{makeup} = 5465.8
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{makeup} = 68{,}322.5 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{makeup} \approx 6.83 \times 10^{4} \text{ kg/h of fresh 98 \% H}_2\text{SO}_4}
$$

*(Award: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the **Sulfuric Acid Alkylation Process**. List the major pieces of equipment and explain the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Major Equipment and Roles:**

1. **Feed Mixers / Saturators**: Combine fresh olefin (from FCC) with recycled isobutane to set the desired I/O ratio. May include a **feed dryer** (molecular sieve) to remove trace water, since water dilutes acid.

2. **Alkylation Contactor (Reactor)**:
   - **Stratco Contactor** (vertical, stirred): high-intensity mixing; residence time ~10–20 min.
   - **Kellogg Contactor** (horizontal, baffled): also widely used.
   - Operates at **0–10 °C** under vigorous agitation to form a fine acid/hydrocarbon emulsion.

3. **Acid Effluent Cooler / Refrigerant Chiller**: Removes exothermic heat (~220 kJ/mol olefin); uses propane refrigerant.

4. **Settler / Acid Decanter**: Allows acid (denser) to separate and recycle back to reactor; hydrocarbon (raffinate) phase flows to fractionation.

5. **Deisobutanizer (DIB) Column**: Fractionates hydrocarbon phase — top: isobutane recycle; middle: *n*-butane sidestream; bottom: alkylate product.

6. **Spent Acid Surge Drum**: Buffers spent acid (88–90 wt %).

7. **Acid Regenerator (Concentrator)**: Vacuum-evaporates water to re-concentrate spent acid back to ~98 wt %; the recovered acid is returned to the reactor. By-product is **Acid-Soluble Oil (ASO)**.

8. **Caustic Wash & Water Wash (not always present)**: Neutralizes trace acid carryover from alkylate product.

**Process Flow Narrative**: As described in **Section 4.2** above — feed → mixer → contactor → settler → DIB → alkylate; with acid loop contactor → spent acid → regenerator → fresh acid, and refrigeration loop contactor → chiller → contactor.

*(Award: 2 marks flow, 3 marks equipment list with roles, 2 marks explanation.)*

---

#### **(b)** Discuss the **key process control loops** in an alkylation unit and explain why temperature control is considered the most critical. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Key Control Loops:**

1. **TIC — Reactor Temperature Control (Most Critical)**:
   - Maintains reactor at **0–10 °C** (H₂SO₄) or **25–40 °C** (HF).
   - Implementation: temperature transmitter cascades to refrigerant (propane) flow control valve; uses **cascade control** with secondary refrigerant flow loop.
   - Alarm at ~15 °C (high-high trip — emergency shutdown).
   - **Why critical**: The reaction is **strongly exothermic** (−220 kJ/mol). A temperature rise accelerates side reactions (polymerization, ASO formation) exponentially; at ~30 °C, polymerization dominates, alkylate quality crashes, and runaway exotherm can rupture the reactor.

2. **QIC — I/O Ratio Control**:
   - Online flow meters (Coriolis or orifice) on isobutane and olefin feed.
   - Ratio controller computes $R_{I/O}$ and modulates olefin FCV to maintain **8–15 : 1**.
   - Trip alarm if ratio drops below 5:1.

3. **LIC — Acid-Hydrocarbon Interface Level**:
   - Differential pressure level transmitter on settler / contactor.
   - Maintains interface below contactor top to prevent acid carryover into hydrocarbon effluent.

4. **AI — Acid Strength Analyzer**:
   - Online NIR or densitometer on acid recycle stream.
   - Triggers fresh acid make-up when strength falls below **88 wt %**.
   - In HF units, online HF analyzer (highly toxic — requires isolation & scrubbers).

5. **PIC — Reactor Pressure Control**:
   - Slight nitrogen blanket (~3–5 bar g) to keep hydrocarbons liquid.
   - Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) is mandatory.

6. **FIC — Refrigerant Flow Control**:
   - Secondary loop in cascade with TIC.

**Why Temperature is Most Critical**:
- Exothermic runaway risk.
- Direct link to selectivity (octane quality).
- Affects acid consumption rate (every 10 °C rise can double ASO formation).
- Affects safety — thermal runaway can lead to acid release, environmental disaster.

*(Award: 3 marks loop descriptions, 2 marks why-T-critical, 2 marks explanation of exotherm + safety.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse **RON** with **MON** in octane-related answers; alkylate has high RON but only moderate MON, giving low sensitivity.
> 2. **Always** write the **balanced chemical equation** explicitly; partial credit is often lost for missing states of matter.
> 3. When asked for **make-up acid**, students commonly forget the **dilution factor** (98 wt % vs. spent wt %) — failing to subtract spent acid strength from inlet strength correctly.
> 4. **Refrigeration duty** must be calculated using **moles reacted, not moles fed** — penalised if olefin conversion < 100 % is ignored.
> 5. Mermaid diagrams must be readable — examiners reward **clearly labeled streams** and **control loop indications**.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Alkylation** = isobutane + light olefins → **high-octane alkylate (isooctane)**.
- 📌 Catalysts: **H₂SO₄ (98 %)** or **HF**; emerging solid acids.
- 📌 **I/O ratio (8–15 : 1)** is the **#1 control knob** for quality.
- 📌 Temperature window: **0–10 °C (H₂SO₄)**, **25–40 °C (HF)**.
- 📌 Exotherm: **~−220 kJ/mol olefin** — requires **large refrigeration duty (~30–45 MW typical)**.
- 📌 Acid strength **must stay > 88 wt %** — online NIR analyzer with make-up logic.
- 📌 RON of alkylate = **92–96**; ideal RON reference = **100 (isooctane)**.
- 📌 Acid consumption: **0.5–1.0 kg/ton** (H₂SO₄), **0.3–0.6 kg/ton** (HF).
- 📌 Key equipment: **Contactor + Settler + Deisobutanizer + Acid Regenerator + Refrigerant Chiller**.
- 📌 Critical control loops: **TIC (reactor T)**, **QIC (I/O ratio)**, **LIC (acid level)**, **AI (acid strength)**, **PIC (pressure)**, **FIC (refrigerant flow)**.
- 📌 Side reactions (polymerization, ASO, cracking) lower octane and waste acid — controlled by T and I/O ratio.
- 📌 HF alkylation is being phased out globally due to extreme toxicity and airborne HF plume risks; **solid acid alkylation** is the future.
- 📌 For **material balances**, always use **molar flow rates**; for **acid consumption**, use **mass of pure H₂SO₄ per ton alkylate**.
- 📌 Heat removal duty: $\dot{Q} = \dot{n}_{olefin,\,reacted} \times 220$ kJ/mol → divide by 3.517 to convert kW → TR.
- 📌 **Acid make-up formula (acid mass balance):** $0.98 \dot{m}_{makeup} = 0.90 \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{consumed}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ISOMERISATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition

**Isomerisation** is a catalytic chemical process in petroleum refining and petrochemical industries in which straight-chain (normal or "n-") hydrocarbons of low octane rating are structurally rearranged into their branched-chain (isomer or "iso-") counterparts of higher octane rating, without any change in molecular formula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Isomerisation is the process of converting low-octane normal paraffins (n-C₄, n-C₅, n-C₆) into their high-octane branched isomers using a fixed-bed catalytic reactor under controlled temperature and hydrogen pressure."

### Formal Reactions (KTU Board Standard)
$$
\text{n-butane} \ (\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}) \rightleftharpoons \text{iso-butane} \ (\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10})
$$

$$
\text{n-pentane} \ (\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}) \rightleftharpoons \text{iso-pentane} \ (\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12})
$$

$$
\text{n-hexane} \ (\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}) \rightleftharpoons \text{2-methylpentane + 3-methylpentane + 2,2-DMB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The molecular formula **stays exactly the same** (CₙH₂ₙ₊₂); only the **arrangement of atoms** changes. This is why these molecules are called **structural isomers**.



## 2. Conceptual Analogy — The "Furniture Rearrangement" Model

Imagine you have a room with **10 identical chairs** (these are the carbon and hydrogen atoms). 

- In one arrangement, all chairs are lined up neatly along the wall → this is the **n-paraffin (straight chain)**, which is *boring, low-energy, and a poor performer*.
- In another arrangement, the same 10 chairs are split into a small "lounge cluster" plus a separate "study area" → this is the **iso-paraffin (branched chain)**, which is *compact, high-energy, and a superior performer*.

**Same atoms, same count — but a completely different *octane rating***. The octane number measures a fuel's resistance to knocking in an engine. Branched structures burn smoothly (high octane), while straight chains detonate prematurely (low octane).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric for KTU:** Research Octane Number (**RON**) and Motor Octane Number (**MON**) are the two standard metrics used to grade isomerate quality. Typical n-C₅ has RON ≈ **62**, while iso-pentane has RON ≈ **92**.

### 3. Purpose in the Petrochemical Industry

| Function | Industrial Benefit |
|----------|-------------------|
| Octane Enhancement | Boosts gasoline pool RON by **8–12 points** without adding aromatics or lead |
| Feed Preparation | Produces **iso-butane** as feed for alkylation units |
| Benzene Reduction | Removes n-paraffins that can form benzene via reforming |
| Cleaner Fuels | Produces low-sulfur, low-aromatic, high-octane blendstock |
| LPG Quality | Improves LPG vapor pressure and performance |

### 4. Standard Feedstocks and Products

| Feed Stream | Carbon Number | Typical Source | Main Product |
|-------------|---------------|----------------|--------------|
| Light Naphtha | C₅–C₆ | Atmospheric distillation | High-RON isomerate |
| n-Butane stream | C₄ | Gas processing | Iso-butane (alkylation feed) |
| Mixed C₄/C₅/C₆ | C₄–C₆ | Hydrostabilized naphtha | Multi-isomer blend |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Process Conditions (KTU Board Reference):**
> - **Temperature:** 120 °C to 280 °C
> - **Pressure:** 15 to 35 bar (gauge)
> - **H₂/HC molar ratio:** 1:1 to 4:1
> - **Catalyst:** Platinum on chlorided alumina or platinum on zeolite (e.g., Pt/H-MOR)
> - **Space Velocity (LHSV):** 1.0 to 3.0 h⁻¹

# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 1. The Chemistry Behind Isomerisation

Isomerisation is a mildly **exothermic**, **reversible**, and **equilibrium-limited** reaction. The reaction proceeds through a **carbenium ion mechanism** on acidic catalyst sites.

### Reaction Mechanism (Simplified KTU Version)

1. **Adsorption:** The n-paraffin molecule adsorbs onto an acidic site (Brønsted acid site) of the catalyst.
2. **Hydride Abstraction:** A surface proton (H⁺) abstracts a hydride ion (H⁻), forming a **carbenium ion** (carbonium intermediate).
3. **Methyl Shift:** The methyl group (–CH₃) migrates along the carbon skeleton, rearranging the structure.
4. **Hydride Return:** The carbenium ion accepts a hydride back from the surface, releasing the **iso-paraffin** product.
5. **Desorption:** The branched molecule desorbs from the active site.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is hydrogen (H₂) required?**
> Hydrogen is co-fed to:
> - Suppress **coke formation** on the catalyst (coking deactivation).
> - Maintain the catalyst in a **reduced metallic state**.
> - Prevent **polymerization** of olefinic intermediates.

### 2. Thermodynamic & Kinetic Foundations

#### 2.1 Equilibrium Constant and Conversion

For the generic reaction $n\text{-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} \rightleftharpoons i\text{-C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$, the equilibrium constant is:

$$
K_{eq} = \frac{[i\text{-paraffin}]_{eq}}{[n\text{-paraffin}]_{eq}}
$$

Higher **K_eq** values favor higher iso-paraffin yield. K_eq **decreases with temperature** because the reaction is exothermic. Hence, **lower temperatures favor higher equilibrium conversion**.

#### 2.2 Conversion Formula

$$
X = \frac{F_{n,feed} - F_{n,out}}{F_{n,feed}} \times 100\%
$$

where $F_{n,feed}$ is the molar flow rate of n-paraffin in the feed and $F_{n,out}$ is the molar flow rate of unconverted n-paraffin in the product.

#### 2.3 Octane Number (Research Octane Number — RON)

The **Research Octane Number (RON)** is determined under mild conditions (600 rpm, intake air at 52 °C). The blended RON of a product stream is calculated as:

$$
\text{RON}_{blend} = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot \text{RON}_i
$$

where $x_i$ is the mole (or volume) fraction of component $i$.

#### 2.4 Space Velocity (LHSV)

$$
\text{LHSV} = \frac{\text{Volumetric feed flow rate (liquid, m}^3/\text{h)}}{\text{Catalyst volume (m}^3\text{)}} \quad [\text{h}^{-1}]
$$

A higher LHSV means **lower residence time** and **lower conversion**, but **higher throughput**.

#### 2.5 Heat of Reaction (Typical Values)

| Reaction | ΔH°ᵣ (kJ/mol) | Type |
|----------|---------------|------|
| n-butane → iso-butane | −6.8 | Exothermic |
| n-pentane → iso-pentane | −7.1 | Exothermic |
| n-hexane → 2-methylpentane | −5.9 | Exothermic |

The exothermic nature means that **temperature control is the most critical control loop** in the reactor.

#### 2.6 Hydrogen-to-Hydrocarbon Molar Ratio

$$
\text{H}_2/\text{HC} = \frac{\text{Molar flow of H}_2}{\text{Molar flow of hydrocarbons}}
$$

Typical operating range: **1.0 to 4.0** (molar).

## 3. KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Conversion | $X = \dfrac{F_{n,in} - F_{n,out}}{F_{n,in}} \times 100\%$ | 40–90 % |
| 2 | Space Velocity (LHSV) | $\text{LHSV} = \dfrac{Q_{liquid}}{V_{cat}}$ | 1.0–3.0 h⁻¹ |
| 3 | Equilibrium Constant | $K_{eq} = \dfrac{[i\text{-C}]_{eq}}{[n\text{-C}]_{eq}}$ | 1.2–4.5 (depending on T) |
| 4 | RON Blend | $\text{RON}_{blend} = \sum x_i \cdot \text{RON}_i$ | 82–93 (isomerate) |
| 5 | H₂/HC Ratio | $\text{H}_2/\text{HC} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2}}{\dot{n}_{HC}}$ | 1.0–4.0 |
| 6 | Reactor Temperature | $T_{rxn} = T_{in} + \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r) \cdot X}{\rho \cdot C_p}$ | 120–280 °C |
| 7 | Catalyst Life | Empirical, based on coke deposition | 3–10 years |
| 8 | Octane Gain | $\Delta \text{RON} = \text{RON}_{product} - \text{RON}_{feed}$ | 8–12 RON points |

## 4. Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Refineries:** Every modern refinery running on Euro-VI / Bharat Stage-VI norms has an isomerisation unit to produce **clean, high-octane, low-sulfur gasoline** without using lead or excessive aromatics.
- **Petrochemicals:** Iso-butane produced here is the **primary feedstock for alkylation**, producing alkylate (a premium gasoline blendstock).
- **Instrumentation & Control Engineers:** Isomerisation units are textbook examples of **multi-loop control** — temperature control on the reactor, level control on separators, flow control on recycle hydrogen, and composition control via online analyzers.

> [!TIP]
> **Why engineers care:** The reactor is a **fixed-bed, adiabatic or isothermal, exothermic reactor** — this is the perfect example KTU uses to test your understanding of cascade control, exothermic temperature management, and analyzer feedback loops.
