## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define an instrument bubble (bubbler) level measurement system. Mention any two industrial applications.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — Definition 2 marks + 2 applications ½ mark each):**
>
> An instrument bubble or bubbler level measurement system is a hydrostatic level measurement technique in which a regulated purge gas (instrument air or nitrogen) is bubbled continuously through a dip tube immersed in the process liquid. The back-pressure developed in the tube is directly proportional to the liquid level above the tube outlet and is measured by a pressure or DP transmitter.
>
> **Applications:**
> 1. Crude oil storage tank level measurement in refineries.
> 2. Level measurement of corrosive acids (e.g., H₂SO₄) in petrochemical plants where direct-contact sensors are unsuitable.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State the fundamental hydrostatic equation used in bubbler level measurement and explain its physical meaning.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — Equation 1 mark + Explanation 2 marks):**
>
> **Equation:** $P_{back} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$
>
> **Physical meaning:** The back-pressure developed inside the bubble tube is equal to the weight per unit area of the liquid column standing above the tube outlet. The variables are: $P_{back}$ = back-pressure (Pa), $\rho$ = density of process liquid (kg/m³), $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²), and $h$ = depth of liquid above tube outlet (m). Hence, by measuring $P_{back}$, the level $h$ can be inferred using $h = P_{back}/(\rho g)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** forget to include units in the final numerical answer — this costs 1 full mark routinely.
> - **Do NOT** confuse gauge pressure with absolute pressure when working closed-vessel problems.
> - **Do NOT** state that the dip tube is filled with liquid — it is filled with purge gas; the liquid is *outside* the tube. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conceptual error.
> - **Always** draw a labelled schematic even if not explicitly asked — 2 marks are reserved for the diagram.
> - **Always** mention purge gas flow rate (0.5–1.5 SCFH) — this is a KTU-2024 Scheme favourite marker.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choose either A or B)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an instrument bubble (bubbler) level measurement system used in a pressurised petrochemical storage tank. List all major components and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bubble tube is installed in a cylindrical storage tank containing gasoline. The tube outlet is **0.4 m** below the tank bottom datum. The level range to be measured is **1.2 m to 9.5 m** above the datum. Density of gasoline = **720 kg/m³**. Calculate:
  1. The Lower Range Value (LRV) of the DP transmitter.
  2. The Upper Range Value (URV) of the DP transmitter.
  3. The Span.
  4. The current output (mA) of the transmitter when the actual level is **6.0 m** above the datum. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the schematic — drawing it here is mandatory for 2 marks):**

The system consists of the following components connected in series:

1. **Regulated air/N₂ supply (1.4–2.0 bar g):** Provides a clean, dry, constant-pressure purge medium. For hazardous/petrochemical areas, nitrogen is preferred over air for safety.
2. **Filter-regulator with pressure gauge:** Removes oil, dust, and moisture from the supply and maintains a constant output pressure despite upstream fluctuations.
3. **Restriction orifice / constant-flow regulator:** Limits the purge flow rate to a low, fixed value (0.5–1.5 SCFH) so that the level reading is independent of small supply variations.
4. **Rotameter (flow indicator):** Visually confirms that purge gas is actually flowing — a stopped rotameter indicates a blocked dip tube.
5. **Bubble tube (dip tube):** A small-bore (typically ½″ or ¾″) corrosion-resistant tube (SS316, Hastelloy-C, or PVDF) extending from above the tank down into the liquid. The open end is at the lowest measurement point.
6. **DP transmitter (with reference leg):** For a closed/pressurised tank, the high-pressure side is connected to the bubble tube and the low-pressure side to the vapour space. For open tanks, the low side vents to atmosphere.
7. **Indicator / Recorder / DCS:** Displays the inferred 4–20 mA level signal.

**Working:** The purge gas is forced down the bubble tube and escapes as a stream of fine bubbles from the submerged outlet. The liquid column above exerts a hydrostatic pressure $\rho g h$ on these bubbles. At steady state, the gas pressure inside the tube at the outlet point equals this hydrostatic head, so the back-pressure measured at the top of the tube is exactly $\rho g h$. The DP transmitter converts this pressure into a 4–20 mA signal proportional to the liquid level. **(3 marks for the explanation)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $h_0 = 0.4 \text{ m}$ (tube outlet below datum)
- $h_{min} = 1.2 \text{ m}$, $h_{max} = 9.5 \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 720 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Actual level at unknown point: $h_{act} = 6.0 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — LRV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LRV &= \rho \cdot g \cdot (h_{min} - h_0) \\
    &= 720 \times 9.81 \times (1.2 - 0.4) \\
    &= 720 \times 9.81 \times 0.8 \\
    &= 5650.56 \text{ Pa} \\
    &\approx 5.65 \text{ kPa (gauge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — URV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
URV &= \rho \cdot g \cdot (h_{max} - h_0) \\
    &= 720 \times 9.81 \times (9.5 - 0.4) \\
    &= 720 \times 9.81 \times 9.1 \\
    &= 64264.32 \text{ Pa} \\
    &\approx 64.26 \text{ kPa (gauge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Span:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Span} &= URV - LRV \\
            &= 64.26 - 5.65 \\
            &= 58.61 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Current output at $h_{act} = 6.0 \text{ m}$:**

First, compute the measured pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{meas} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot (h_{act} - h_0) \\
         &= 720 \times 9.81 \times (6.0 - 0.4) \\
         &= 720 \times 9.81 \times 5.6 \\
         &= 39553.92 \text{ Pa} \\
         &\approx 39.55 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then convert to current using the linear 4–20 mA mapping:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{out} &= 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{P_{meas} - LRV}{URV - LRV} \\
        &= 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{39.55 - 5.65}{64.26 - 5.65} \\
        &= 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{33.90}{58.61} \\
        &= 4 + 16 \cdot 0.5784 \\
        &= 4 + 9.254 \\
        &= 13.25 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- LRV = **5.65 kPa (g)**
- URV = **64.26 kPa (g)**
- Span = **58.61 kPa**
- Current output at 6.0 m level = **≈ 13.25 mA** ✔

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare bubbler level measurement with a conventional differential-pressure (DP) cell level measurement. List at least **four advantages** and **two limitations** of the bubbler system in petrochemical service. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bubbler system is used to measure the level of diesel (density **840 kg/m³**) in an atmospheric storage tank. The bubble tube outlet is **0.25 m** above the tank bottom. The transmitter is calibrated for a level range of **0.5 m to 7.0 m** measured from the tank bottom.
  1. Determine the calibrated range of the pressure transmitter in kPa.
  2. What purge-gas supply pressure (in bar g) should be provided, assuming a **0.4 bar (g)** margin above the maximum required back-pressure?
  3. If the rotameter indicates a sudden stoppage of purge flow, list **two possible causes** and **two corrective actions**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks — 4 marks for table + 3 marks for explanation):**

| Parameter | DP Cell (Wetted) | Bubbler System |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| Process contact | Diaphragm directly contacts process fluid | Only dip tube contacts process fluid |
| Corrosion resistance | Poor — needs exotic diaphragm / seal | Excellent — cheap tube in alloy |
| High temperature | Limited by diaphragm material | Excellent — tube tolerates >300 °C |
| Maintenance | High (seal flushing) | Low (tube blow-down) |
| Slurry / viscous service | Poor | Fair to good (self-cleaning) |
| Accuracy | ±0.1% of span | ±0.5 to ±1% of span |
| Cost of installation | Lower initially | Higher (needs air/N₂ utility) |
| Foam tolerance | Moderate | Poor (foam reads as level) |

**Advantages of Bubbler:**
1. The primary instrument (transmitter) never touches the process — greatly extends its life.
2. Highly suitable for corrosive, viscous, or hot process fluids.
3. Self-cleaning action of bubbles prevents plugging in many services.
4. Simple to retrofit on existing tanks — only a tube and a transmitter are needed.

**Limitations of Bubbler:**
1. Requires a clean, dry, regulated purge gas utility (instrument air or N₂).
2. Accuracy degrades in foaming or highly agitated liquids.
3. Slower response time compared to electronic level sensors.

---

**(b) Numerical & Diagnostic Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\rho = 840 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $h_0 = 0.25 \text{ m}$ (tube outlet above tank bottom)
- Range: $h_{min} = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $h_{max} = 7.0 \text{ m}$ (from tank bottom)

**Step 1 — Calibrated transmitter range:**

The pressure corresponds to the liquid column *above the tube outlet*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{min} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot (h_{min} - h_0) \\
        &= 840 \times 9.81 \times (0.5 - 0.25) \\
        &= 840 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 \\
        &= 2060.1 \text{ Pa} \\
        &\approx 2.06 \text{ kPa (g)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot (h_{max} - h_0) \\
        &= 840 \times 9.81 \times (7.0 - 0.25) \\
        &= 840 \times 9.81 \times 6.75 \\
        &= 55622.67 \text{ Pa} \\
        &\approx 55.62 \text{ kPa (g)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calibrated range = 2.06 kPa to 55.62 kPa (gauge).**

**Step 2 — Required purge gas supply pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{supply} &= P_{max} + \text{Margin} \\
           &= 55.62 \text{ kPa (g)} + 0.4 \text{ bar (g)} \\
           &= 55.62 \text{ kPa} + 40 \text{ kPa} \\
           &= 95.62 \text{ kPa (g)} \\
           &\approx 0.956 \text{ bar (g)} \\
           &\approx 1.0 \text{ bar (g)} \text{ (after rounding to standard supply)}
\end{aligned}
$$

A standard instrument air supply of **1.4 bar (g)** is typically provided to maintain a safe margin.

**Step 3 — Stoppage of purge flow — possible causes and corrective actions:**

| # | Possible Causes | Corrective Actions |
|---|-----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Bubble tube blocked by sludge, wax, or polymer deposits | Isolate the system, disconnect the tube at the top, and blow it out with high-pressure air or steam. |
| 2 | Restriction orifice choked by moisture/oil in supply air | Replace the orifice plug and check the upstream filter; install a coalescing filter if not already present. |
| 3 | Supply pressure regulator failure (delivering 0 bar) | Switch to standby regulator, repair the failed one. |
| 4 | Tube physically broken / detached at flange | De-pressurise the vessel, drain local section, and re-install tube with proper gasket. |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall before the exam):**
> - Bubbler = **purge-gas bubbled** through a dip tube; back-pressure ∝ liquid level.
> - Core equation: **$P = \rho g h$** — never forget to write units.
> - Always use **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** unless otherwise specified.
> - For **closed/pressurised** tanks → use a **DP cell with reference leg**; for **open** tanks → vent the low side.
> - Purge flow rate must be **low (0.5–1.5 SCFH)** — high flow artificially raises the back-pressure.
> - Always include a **rotameter** to confirm flow — a non-flowing rotameter = blocked tube.
> - Use **N₂ purge** in flammable / hazardous (Zone 1 / Class I Div 1) areas.
> - Transmitter calibration formulas:
  - **LRV** = $\rho g (h_{min} - h_0)$
  - **URV** = $\rho g (h_{max} - h_0)$
  - **Span** = URV − LRV
  - **Current** = $4 + 16 \cdot (P_{meas} - LRV)/(URV - LRV)$
> - **Avoid** wetted DP cells for corrosive, slurry, or >200 °C service — use bubbler instead.
> - **Common examiner traps:** forgetting units, confusing gauge vs. absolute pressure, claiming the tube is "filled with liquid" (it is filled with **gas**), and omitting the diagram in Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Equipment
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Equipment

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Process Equipment** in a petrochemical industry refers to the engineered, specialized mechanical apparatus and vessels used to physically and chemically transform crude oil and natural gas feedstocks into refined products, intermediates, and finished petrochemicals. These units operate under precisely controlled conditions of **temperature (typically 30 °C to 850 °C)**, **pressure (from -1 bar vacuum to +200 bar)**, and **flow rate (0.1 m³/h to 10,000 m³/h)** to enable unit operations such as separation, reaction, heat exchange, and fluid transport.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, process equipment is broadly classified under two engineering domains:
- **Static Equipment** (non-moving internals): Columns, vessels, reactors, heat exchangers, tanks.
- **Dynamic Equipment** (rotating/moving internals): Pumps, compressors, blowers, mixers, conveyors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the ESE (End Semester Examination), students must be able to *identify, classify, and select appropriate instrumentation* (level, flow, pressure, temperature, and analytical sensors) for the standard process equipment covered in this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **giant industrial kitchen** where crude oil is the raw ingredient (like uncooked flour and sugar), and petrol, diesel, LPG, and polymers are the final dishes (like cakes and pastries). The **process equipment** are the kitchen appliances:
- The **distillation column** is like a pressure cooker that separates ingredients based on how quickly they evaporate.
- The **heat exchanger** is like a double boiler that transfers heat between two liquids without mixing them.
- The **pump** is like the heart that circulates blood (fluid) throughout the body (plant).
- The **reactor** is like the oven where chemical "cooking" (reaction) happens.

Each appliance requires its own **gauges, knobs, and sensors** (instruments) to ensure the cooking process stays safe, efficient, and produces a consistent product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used:** Petrochemical equipment is sized in **Tons of Oil per Day (TPD)** or **Barrels per Stream Day (BPSD)**. Globally, **1 barrel = 0.159 m³ ≈ 159 liters**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Process Equipment (Structured Logic)

### A. Static (Non-Rotating) Equipment
1. **Distillation Columns** — Used to separate hydrocarbons based on boiling point differences.
2. **Reactors** — Vessels where chemical reactions (cracking, reforming, alkylation) occur.
3. **Heat Exchangers** — Devices that transfer thermal energy between two or more fluids.
4. **Pressure Vessels & Storage Tanks** — Hold fluids under pressure or atmospheric conditions.
5. **Separators** — Two/three-phase units that split gas, oil, and water streams.
6. **Filters & Dryers** — Remove solids, moisture, or contaminants.

### B. Dynamic (Rotating) Equipment
1. **Pumps** (Centrifugal, Reciprocating) — Increase fluid pressure for transport.
2. **Compressors** (Reciprocating, Centrifugal, Screw) — Boost gas pressure.
3. **Blowers & Fans** — Move large volumes of low-pressure gas.
4. **Agitators / Mixers** — Ensure homogeneous mixing in reactors and tanks.

## 2.2 Why Each Equipment Exists (The "Why")

- **Separation**: Crude oil is a mixture of >10,000 hydrocarbons. We need to isolate them into pure products (LPG, Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel, Lube oil, Residue).
- **Conversion**: Heavy hydrocarbons are not marketable directly. Reactors (e.g., FCC, Hydrocracker) break them into lighter, more valuable products.
- **Heat Management**: Most reactions and separations are temperature-sensitive. Heat exchangers recycle energy, reducing fuel costs by up to **40%**.
- **Transport**: Fluids must overcome friction, elevation, and equipment resistance — pumps and compressors provide the necessary head/pressure.

## 2.3 Operating Principles (The "How")

### 2.3.1 Distillation Column
- Operates on the principle of **vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE)**.
- Multiple **trays** or **structured packing** provide repeated vapor-liquid contact.
- Lighter components rise; heavier components descend.
- **Reflux** returned from the condenser improves separation efficiency.

### 2.3.2 Heat Exchanger
- Operates on **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** combined with **Newton's Law of Cooling**.
- Modes: **Counter-flow** (most efficient), **Co-current**, **Cross-flow**.

### 2.3.3 Pumps and Compressors
- **Pumps** transfer incompressible liquids using **centrifugal force** or **positive displacement**.
- **Compressors** compress gases using similar mechanisms but require multi-stage designs due to high compression ratios.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equipment / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Distillation - Reflux Ratio | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | L = Liquid reflux (kmol/h), D = Distillate (kmol/h) | $R \geq R_{min}$ (minimum reflux) |
| 2 | Heat Exchanger (LMTD) | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1$, $\Delta T_2$ = temperature differences at two ends (°C or K) | $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$ (counter-flow) |
| 3 | Heat Transfer Rate | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F$ | U = Overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), A = Area (m²), F = Correction factor | F = 1 (1-2 shell & tube) |
| 4 | Centrifugal Pump Head | $H = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g} + \Delta z + \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure rise (Pa), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), g = 9.81 m/s² | Incompressible flow |
| 5 | Pump Power | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$ | Q = flow rate (m³/s), $\eta$ = efficiency (typically 0.6 to 0.85) | $\eta < 1$ |
| 6 | Compressor Work (Polytropic) | $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$ | P = pressure (Pa), V = volume (m³), n = polytropic index | $P_2 / P_1$ = pressure ratio |
| 7 | Reactor Residence Time | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | V = reactor volume (m³), Q = volumetric flow (m³/s) | Plug flow assumption |
| 8 | Pressure Vessel (Thick Wall) | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot r_i}{t}$ | P = internal pressure (Pa), $r_i$ = inner radius (m), t = wall thickness (m) | Thin wall: $t / r_i < 0.1$ |
| 9 | Bernoulli's Equation | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$ | $h_f$ = friction head loss (m) | Steady, incompressible, inviscid (modified) |
| 10 | Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | Standard T = 273.15 K, P = 101,325 Pa |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Recovery**: Modern refineries use **Heat Integrated Distillation Columns (HIDiC)** to recover up to **60% of reboiler duty**, directly lowering CO₂ emissions.
- **Process Safety**: Process equipment is governed by **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC)** — a mandatory international standard.
- **Digital Twin**: Modern petrochemical plants use **digital twins** of process equipment for predictive maintenance, reducing unplanned downtime by **30–50%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: LMTD for Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

The **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is derived from the differential heat transfer along a counter-flow heat exchanger.

### Step 1: Define the System
Consider a differential element of heat exchanger area $dA$, where the hot fluid loses heat $dQ$ and the cold fluid gains the same $dQ$.

$$
dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T
$$

Where $\Delta T$ is the local temperature difference between hot and cold fluids.

### Step 2: Express dQ in Terms of Fluid Enthalpies

For the hot fluid (cooling from $T_h$ to $T_h - dT_h$):

$$
dQ = -m_h c_{ph} \, dT_h
$$

For the cold fluid (heating from $T_c$ to $T_c + dT_c$):

$$
dQ = m_c c_{pc} \, dT_c
$$

### Step 3: Establish the Temperature Profile
The change in temperature difference is:

$$
d(\Delta T) = dT_h - (-dT_c) = dT_h + dT_c
$$

(negative because cold fluid gains heat, hot fluid loses heat).

### Step 4: Express in Terms of dQ and Constants

$$
dT_h = -\dfrac{dQ}{m_h c_{ph}}, \quad dT_c = \dfrac{dQ}{m_c c_{pc}}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad d(\Delta T) = -dQ \left( \dfrac{1}{m_h c_{ph}} + \dfrac{1}{m_c c_{pc}} \right)
$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Integrate

$$
d(\Delta T) = -dQ \cdot K
$$

Where $K$ is a constant for a given exchanger. Substituting $dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T$:

$$
d(\Delta T) = -U \, dA \cdot \Delta T \cdot K
$$

$$
\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U K \, dA
$$

Integrating from inlet (1) to outlet (2):

$$
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U K \int_0^A dA
$$

$$
\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -U K A
$$

### Step 6: Relate Total Heat Transfer Q to Temperatures

Total heat transfer is also:

$$
Q = m_h c_{ph} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = m_c c_{pc} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

Combining all relations, the LMTD is:

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

This derivation is **exam-critical** for any heat exchanger numerical problem in the KTU board exam.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Vessel Hoop Stress (Thin-Wall Cylinder)

### Step 1: Force Balance on Half Cylinder
Consider a thin cylindrical pressure vessel of inner radius $r_i$, wall thickness $t$, and length $L$, subjected to internal pressure $P$.

The bursting force on the projected area:

$$
F = P \cdot (2 r_i L)
$$

### Step 2: Resisting Force from Wall
The resisting force is provided by the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting on the cross-sectional wall area:

$$
F_{resist} = \sigma_h \cdot (2 t L)
$$

### Step 3: Equate Forces at Equilibrium

$$
\sigma_h \cdot (2 t L) = P \cdot (2 r_i L)
$$

### Step 4: Solve for Hoop Stress

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot r_i}{t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The longitudinal (axial) stress is exactly **half** the hoop stress: $\sigma_{axial} = \dfrac{P \cdot r_i}{2t}$. This is why pressure vessels usually rupture **axially** (split lengthwise), not in girth.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Equipment Selection Guide

| Process Goal | Recommended Equipment | Key Control Parameter | Typical Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Separate liquids by boiling point | Distillation Column | Tray Temperature, Reflux Ratio | RTD / Thermocouple |
| Transfer liquids | Centrifugal Pump | Flow Rate, Discharge Pressure | Differential Pressure Transmitter |
| Compress natural gas | Centrifugal Compressor | Suction/Discharge Pressure, Speed | Pressure Gauge + Vibration Sensor |
| Cool reactor effluent | Shell & Tube Heat Exchanger | Outlet Temperature, Flow | Thermocouple + Flowmeter |
| Store crude oil | Floating Roof Tank | Level, Temperature | Servo Level Gauge + RTD |
| Crack heavy hydrocarbons | Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) Reactor | Riser Temperature, Catalyst Flow | Multi-point Thermocouple |
| Remove acid gases | Amine Absorber | Tray Temperature, Lean Amine Flow | Conductivity + pH sensor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Equipment in Petrochemical Industry] --> B[Static Equipment]
    A --> C[Dynamic Equipment]
    
    B --> B1[Distillation Columns]
    B --> B2[Chemical Reactors]
    B --> B3[Heat Exchangers]
    B --> B4[Pressure Vessels]
    B --> B5[Storage Tanks]
    B --> B6[Separators]
    
    C --> C1[Pumps]
    C --> C2[Compressors]
    C --> C3[Blowers]
    C --> C4[Agitators]
    
    B1 --> B1a[Crude Distillation Unit]
    B1 --> B1b[Vacuum Distillation]
    B1 --> B1c[Atmospheric Distillation]
    
    B2 --> B2a[FCC Reactor]
    B2 --> B2b[Hydrocracker]
    B2 --> B2c[Reformer]
    B2 --> B2d[Alkylation Unit]
    
    B3 --> B3a[Shell and Tube]
    B3 --> B3b[Plate Type]
    B3 --> B3c[Air Cooled Fin Fan]
    
    B4 --> B4a[Horizontal]
    B4 --> B4b[Vertical]
    B4 --> B4c[Spherical]
    
    B5 --> B5a[Fixed Roof]
    B5 --> B5b[Floating Roof]
    B5 --> B5c[Spherical LPG Bullets]
    
    C1 --> C1a[Centrifugal Pump]
    C1 --> C1b[Reciprocating Pump]
    C1 --> C1c[Positive Displacement]
    
    C2 --> C2a[Centrifugal Compressor]
    C2 --> C2b[Reciprocating Compressor]
    C2 --> C2c[Screw Compressor]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Crude Distillation Unit (CDU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED[Crude Oil Feed] --> DESALTER[Desalter Unit]
    DESALTER --> FURNACE[Heated Furnace up to 350 C]
    FURNACE --> DISTCOL[Atmospheric Distillation Column]
    DISTCOL --> SIDE1[Naphtha Draw]
    DISTCOL --> SIDE2[Kerosene Draw]
    DISTCOL --> SIDE3[Diesel Draw]
    DISTCOL --> BOTTOM[Reduced Crude Oil]
    BOTTOM --> VACUUM[Vacuum Distillation Column]
    DISTCOL --> OVHD[Overhead Vapor]
    OVHD --> COND[Condenser]
    COND --> REFLUX[Reflux Drum]
    REFLUX --> PUMP[Reflux Pump]
    PUMP --> DISTCOL
    REFLUX --> NAPHTHA[Naphtha Product]
    VACUUM --> VBOT[Vacuum Residue]
    VACUUM --> VGASO[Vacuum Gas Oil]
    BOTTOM --> HX[Heat Exchanger Network]
    HX --> FEED
```

## 4.3 Process Control Loop on a Heat Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HX[Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger] --> TT[TT Temperature Transmitter]
    TT --> PIC[PID Controller]
    PIC --> CV[Control Valve on Cold Stream]
    CV --> HX
    HX --> FT[FT Flow Transmitter for Monitoring]
    HX --> PT[PT Pressure Transmitter for Safety]
```

## 4.4 Pump and Compressor Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUCT[Suction Source] --> SVR[Suction Strainer and Valve]
    SVR --> PUMP[Centrifugal Pump]
    PUMP --> DISC[Discharge Check Valve]
    DISC --> RELIEF[Pressure Relief Valve]
    RELIEF --> OUTLET[Process Outlet]
    PUMP --> VBIB[Vibration Sensor]
    PUMP --> TTEMP[Bearing Temperature Sensor]
    PUMP --> TSEAL[Mechanical Seal Monitor]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between centrifugal pumps and reciprocating pumps. Mention any two petrochemical applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Centrifugal Pump:**
- Uses **rotating impeller** to impart kinetic energy to the fluid.
- Operates at **high flow rate, low to medium head**.
- Continuous discharge; flow is **proportional to speed**.
- **Applications:** Cooling water circulation, boiler feed water, crude oil transfer in refineries.

**Reciprocating Pump:**
- Uses **back-and-forth piston/ plunger motion**.
- Operates at **low flow rate, very high head**.
- Pulsating discharge; flow is **independent of pressure** within design limits.
- **Applications:** Injection of chemicals (corrosion inhibitors), high-pressure hydrocracker feed, lube oil transfer.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain the function of a reflux drum in a distillation column. Why is reflux necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

A **reflux drum** is a horizontal vessel installed between the condenser and the distillation column. Its functions are:
1. **Phase Separation:** It separates the condensed overhead vapor into liquid reflux and distillate product.
2. **Surge Volume:** Provides buffer storage for smooth operation during flow upsets.
3. **Pump Suction Source:** Acts as a suction reservoir for the reflux pump.

**Why Reflux is Necessary:**
- Reflux enriches the **top tray** with liquid, maintaining **vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE)**.
- Higher reflux → **better separation efficiency** but **higher reboiler energy**.
- A minimum reflux ratio ($R_{min}$) is required to achieve the desired product purity; operating below $R_{min}$ gives **no separation** (pinch point).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger**. List any **four instrumentation points** used to monitor its performance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A counter-flow shell and tube heat exchanger is used to cool **5 kg/s of hot oil (cp = 2.1 kJ/kg·K)** from **150 °C to 80 °C** using cooling water entering at **25 °C** with a flow rate of **3 kg/s (cp = 4.18 kJ/kg·K)**. Calculate the **LMTD** and the **heat transfer area** if **U = 600 W/m²·K** and correction factor **F = 0.95**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for (a):

A **Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger** consists of a cylindrical shell containing a bundle of tubes.

**Construction:**
- **Shell:** Outer pressure vessel.
- **Tube Bundle:** U-tubes or straight tubes with baffles for turbulence.
- **Tubesheets:** Hold the tubes at both ends.
- **Baffles:** Direct shell-side fluid across the tubes, increasing velocity and heat transfer.
- **Channels and Bonnets:** Inlet/outlet headers for tube-side fluid.

**Working Principle:**
- Hot fluid flows through the shell side (or tube side); cold fluid flows through the other side in **counter-flow** for maximum efficiency.
- Heat transfers through the tube wall by **conduction** and is exchanged by **convection** on either side.

**Four Instrumentation Points:**
1. **Inlet/Outlet Temperature** (RTD/ Thermocouple) for both streams.
2. **Flow Transmitter** (Orifice + DPT) on both streams.
3. **Pressure Transmitter** on shell side (for fouling detection) and tube side.
4. **Vibration Sensor** if mounted as kettle-type reboiler to detect tube bundle issues.

### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Heat Duty of Oil**

$$
Q = m_h \cdot c_{ph} \cdot \Delta T_h = 5 \times 2.1 \times (150 - 80) = 5 \times 2.1 \times 70
$$

$$
Q = 735 \text{ kW} = 735{,}000 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Outlet Temperature of Water**

$$
\Delta T_c = \dfrac{Q}{m_c c_{pc}} = \dfrac{735{,}000}{3 \times 4180} = \dfrac{735{,}000}{12{,}540} \approx 58.6 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
T_{c,out} = 25 + 58.6 = 83.6 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3: Compute End Temperature Differences (Counter-Flow)**

- **Hot inlet (150 °C) vs Cold outlet (83.6 °C):** $\Delta T_1 = 150 - 83.6 = 66.4 \text{ °C}$
- **Hot outlet (80 °C) vs Cold inlet (25 °C):** $\Delta T_2 = 80 - 25 = 55.0 \text{ °C}$

**Step 4: Compute LMTD**

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{66.4 - 55.0}{\ln(66.4 / 55.0)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{11.4}{\ln(1.2073)} = \dfrac{11.4}{0.1884} \approx 60.5 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Heat Transfer Area**

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F} = \dfrac{735{,}000}{600 \times 60.5 \times 0.95}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{735{,}000}{34{,}485} \approx 21.3 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Final Answer:** $A \approx 21.3 \text{ m}^2$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a **Centrifugal Pump**. Derive the equation for the **power required** by the pump. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers **0.05 m³/s** of water against a **head of 30 m**. If the **efficiency is 70%**, calculate (i) the power input to the pump, and (ii) the **electricity cost per day** if the pump runs 24 hours and electricity costs ₹8 per kWh. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction of Centrifugal Pump:**
- **Casing:** Volute or diffuser-type.
- **Impeller:** Backward-curved, forward-curved, or radial vanes.
- **Shaft:** Connects impeller to motor.
- **Mechanical Seal:** Prevents leakage along the shaft.
- **Suction and Discharge Nozzles.**

**Working Principle:**
- The motor rotates the impeller at high speed.
- Fluid enters the eye (center) of the impeller axially.
- The impeller's vanes impart **kinetic energy** to the fluid, accelerating it radially outward.
- The volute casing converts this kinetic energy into **pressure energy**, discharging the fluid at high pressure.

**Derivation of Power Equation:**

The energy added to the fluid per unit weight is the head **H** (in meters).

Mass flow rate of fluid:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q
$$

Energy transferred per second (Water Power or Hydraulic Power):

$$
P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H
$$

The shaft power input must account for pump efficiency $\eta$:

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta}
$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of fluid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $H$ = head developed (m)
- $\eta$ = pump efficiency (dimensionless, $0 < \eta < 1$)

### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H = 30 \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.70 = 70\%$
- Density of water, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Hydraulic Power (Water Power)**

$$
P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 30
$$

$$
P_{water} = 14{,}715 \text{ W} = 14.715 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: Shaft Power Input**

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{\eta} = \dfrac{14.715}{0.70} \approx 21.02 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Daily Energy Consumption**

$$
E_{daily} = 21.02 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 504.5 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4: Daily Electricity Cost**

$$
\text{Cost} = 504.5 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 4036
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Power input ≈ **21.02 kW**
- Daily cost ≈ **₹ 4036**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **LMTD Numerical Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply the **correction factor F** for multi-pass heat exchangers. The KTU board examiner deducts **2 marks** if $F$ is omitted in a 7-mark numerical.
> 2. **Pump Power Pitfall:** Many students calculate *water power* and submit it as the answer. Always state **shaft (input) power** separately by dividing by efficiency.
> 3. **Unit Trap:** In LMTD, both temperature differences must be in the **same units (°C or K)**. Mixing °F with °C is a common error that costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Distillation Diagram:** In descriptive questions, drawing a column without labeling **trays/packing, reboiler, condenser, reflux, feed, top/bottom product** loses up to **3 marks** in KTU evaluation.
> 5. **Reflux Ratio:** The answer must clearly distinguish between **reflux ratio R = L/D** and **minimum reflux ratio $R_{min}$** — examiners mark them as separate concepts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process Equipment** is broadly classified as **Static** (columns, vessels, tanks, heat exchangers) and **Dynamic** (pumps, compressors, blowers).
- **Distillation columns** operate on **Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)** and use **reflux** for separation efficiency.
- **Heat Exchangers** follow the **LMTD equation** $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ and require **correction factor F** for multi-pass designs.
- **Centrifugal pumps** develop head by converting **kinetic energy to pressure energy**; power equation is $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$.
- **Pressure vessels** fail axially because hoop stress $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P r_i}{t}$ is **twice** the longitudinal stress.
- **Polytropic compression** work: $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$.
- **Reactor residence time**: $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ — critical for determining reaction conversion.
- Always remember the **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC)** is the governing standard for pressure vessel design.
- **Instrumentation** for process equipment includes **temperature, pressure, level, flow, and analytical sensors** — each equipment has its own unique sensor map.
- For KTU exams, always **draw a labeled diagram** for descriptive questions (worth at least **3 marks** out of 7).
- Numerical answers must show **step-by-step substitution** with **units at every step** to gain full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Valve Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Valve in a Petrochemical Plant?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **valve** is a mechanical device installed in a piping system to control, isolate, regulate, or direct the flow of process fluids (liquids, gases, slurries, or vapors) by opening, closing, or partially obstructing the passage. In petrochemical industries, valves are the **final control elements** that physically execute the commands issued by the control system (DCS/PLC).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Police of the Pipeline"

Imagine a busy four-lane highway (your process pipe) carrying crude oil at high pressure. Now imagine **traffic police officers** standing at strategic points:

- One officer is a **Gate Valve** — he drops a heavy metal barrier across the road. When down, NOTHING moves. When up, EVERYTHING moves. He is the "all or nothing" guy.
- One officer is a **Globe Valve** — he controls traffic by squeezing the lane narrower or wider, allowing him to fine-tune the flow rate precisely.
- One officer is a **Ball Valve** — he rotates a giant ball with a hole through it. Hole aligned with road = full flow. Hole perpendicular = zero flow. Quarter turn, that's it.
- One officer is a **Butterfly Valve** — he swings a disc like a turnstile gate, controlling how many cars squeeze through.
- One officer is a **Check Valve** — a one-way swing gate. Cars can only go North, never South.
- The **Control Valve** is the most sophisticated officer — he has a **brain connected to a sensor** (say, a flow meter at the end of the highway). If traffic gets too fast, he automatically squeezes the lane to slow it down. If too slow, he opens up.

This is exactly how valves function in a refinery or petrochemical plant — they are the **muscles** of the process, while sensors are the **eyes** and the DCS is the **brain**.

### 1.2 Classification of Valves — The KTU Master Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Classification (must be memorized):**

```
VALVES
├── 1. ON/OFF (Isolation) Valves
│       ├── Gate Valve
│       ├── Ball Valve
│       ├── Plug Valve
│       └── Butterfly Valve
│
├── 2. Throttling (Flow Control) Valves
│       ├── Globe Valve
│       ├── Needle Valve
│       └── Control Valve (Pneumatic, Hydraulic, Motorized)
│
├── 3. Check (Non-Return) Valves
│       ├── Swing Check Valve
│       ├── Lift Check Valve
│       └── Dual Plate Check Valve
│
├── 4. Safety & Relief Valves
│       ├── Safety Relief Valve
│       ├── Rupture Disc
│       └── Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)
│
└── 5. Special Purpose Valves
        ├── Diaphragm Valve
        ├── Pinch Valve
        └── Cryogenic Valve
```

### 1.3 Why Valve Selection is Critical in Petrochemical Industries

- **Process Safety:** A wrong valve can cause a leak, fire, or explosion. Petrochemicals are **flammable**, **toxic**, and operate at **high pressure (up to 600 bar)** and **high temperature (up to 850 °C)**.
- **Flow Accuracy:** Refineries need precise flow control. A deviation of even 1% can ruin product quality.
- **Corrosion Resistance:** Crude oil contains H₂S, CO₂, chlorides — highly corrosive. Valves must be made of **Stainless Steel (SS 316)**, **Hastelloy**, or **Inconel**.
- **Standards:** All valves follow **API (American Petroleum Institute)** and **ASME B16.34** standards.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards You Must Know for KTU Exam:**
> - **API 600** — Gate, Globe, and Check Valves (Bolted Bonnet)
> - **API 602** — Compact Steel Gate & Globe Valves
> - **API 6D** — Pipeline Valves (Ball, Check, Gate)
> - **API 607** — Fire Test for Soft-Seated Quarter-Turn Valves
> - **ANSI/FCI 70-2** — Control Valve Seat Leakage Classification (Classes I to VI)
> - **ISA 75.01** — Control Valve Sizing Equations

| Standard Metric | Value/Unit |
|:---|:---|
| Standard Line Sizes | **15 mm (½") to 600 mm (24")** |
| Pressure Class | **ANSI 150, 300, 600, 900, 1500, 2500** |
| Operating Temp Range | **-196 °C to 850 °C** |
| Leakage Class VI Limit | **0.5 mL/min** (Bubble-tight) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Major Valve Type

### A. Gate Valve (Most Common On/Off Valve)

**Operating Principle:** A wedge or gate moves **linearly (up/down)** to block or open the flow path. The flow is straight-through when fully open.

**Key Engineering Points:**
- Used **only in fully open or fully closed** position — **NOT for throttling**.
- When used for throttling, the gate vibrates against the fluid, causing **seat erosion** and **wire drawing** (a small groove eroded on the seat surface).
- Offers **minimum pressure drop** when fully open because the flow path is unobstructed.
- **Slow operation** due to multi-turn handwheel.
- **Bi-directional** flow possible.

**Parts of a Gate Valve:**
1. **Body** — Main pressure-containing shell.
2. **Bonnet** — Top cover, houses the stem packing.
3. **Stem** — Connects handwheel/actuator to the gate.
4. **Gate/Wedge** — The blocking element (solid wedge, split wedge, or flexible wedge).
5. **Seat Rings** — Provide the sealing surface.
6. **Packing** — Prevents leakage around the stem.
7. **Handwheel / Actuator** — Operator interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Petrochemical Application:** Used in **crude oil pipeline isolation**, **refinery shut-down sections**, and **storage tank outlet lines**.

---

### B. Globe Valve (The Throttling King)

**Operating Principle:** Fluid flow changes direction (S-shape or Z-shape) inside the body. A disc/plug moves **perpendicular to the seat** to throttle flow.

**Key Engineering Points:**
- The **best valve for throttling** because of linear, predictable flow characteristics.
- Higher **pressure drop** than gate valve due to tortuous flow path.
- **Shorter stroke** than gate, faster operation.
- Can be equipped with **actuator (pneumatic diaphragm or electric motor)** for automatic control.
- The disc and seat are subject to **wire drawing erosion** if used with high-pressure drop.

**Internal Geometry — The Z-Shape:**
- Fluid enters horizontally → turns 90° upward → passes through the seat orifice → turns 90° → exits horizontally.
- The plug (tapered) sits on the seat ring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Petrochemical Application:** Used as **control valve** in **distillation column reflux lines**, **steam headers**, and **fuel oil flow regulation**.

---

### C. Ball VALVE (The Quarter-Turn Champion)

**Operating Principle:** A **spherical ball with a bore** rotates 90° (quarter-turn) to open or close. When the bore aligns with the pipe → open. When bore is perpendicular → closed.

**Key Engineering Points:**
- **Quarter-turn** = very fast operation (1–2 seconds).
- **Tight shutoff** with soft seats (PTFE, RPTFE) → **Bubble-tight (Class VI)**.
- **Low torque** to operate.
- Used for **on/off service** and **emergency isolation** (ESD — Emergency Shutdown).
- **Fire-safe design** available per **API 607**.
- Three-way ball valves can **divert or mix** flows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Petrochemical Application:** **LNG loading lines**, **gas pipelines**, **hydrocarbon storage terminals** — anywhere fast isolation is critical.

---

### D. Butterfly Valve (Lightweight, Large Diameter)

**Operating Principle:** A **circular disc** rotates on a central shaft. When parallel to flow → open. When perpendicular → closed.

**Key Engineering Points:**
- **Compact and lightweight** — ideal for large diameter (up to 2000 mm).
- Lower cost than ball/gate for large sizes.
- Suitable for **low to medium pressure** (up to ANSI 600).
- Can be used for **throttling** with proper disc design (e.g., high-performance double-offset disc).
- **Resilient seated** (rubber/PTFE) for bubble-tight shutoff.
- Can have **double-offset** (high-performance) or **triple-offset** (metal-seated, fire-safe) design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Petrochemical Application:** **Water cooling systems**, **firewater networks**, **crude oil transfer lines**.

---

### E. Check Valve (The Silent Guardian)

**Operating Principle:** Allows **one-way flow only**. Automatically closes when flow reverses, preventing **backflow** that could damage pumps, reverse process flow, or cause water hammer.

**Types:**
- **Swing Check** — Hinged disc swings open with forward flow, closes by gravity on reverse.
- **Lift Check** — Disc lifts vertically; suitable for high-pressure steam.
- **Dual Plate (Wafer) Check** — Two spring-loaded plates; compact and fast-closing.
- **Tilting Disc Check** — Disc tilts; used in high-velocity services.

> [!NOTE]
> **Petrochemical Application:** Pump discharge lines, compressor discharge, boiler feedwater.

---

### F. Control Valve (The Brain-Connected Final Control Element)

This is the **most important valve for instrumentation engineers** in petrochemical plants.

**Definition (KTU Standard):**
A control valve is a **power-operated valve** used to **automatically modulate** fluid flow rate, pressure, or temperature in response to a control signal (typically **3–15 psi pneumatic** or **4–20 mA electronic**) from a controller (DCS/PLC).

**Components:**
1. **Actuator** — Converts the control signal into mechanical motion.
   - **Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator** (most common) — uses air pressure on a flexible diaphragm.
   - **Piston Actuator** (cylinder type) — higher thrust.
   - **Electric Actuator** — uses a motor; for remote locations without air supply.
   - **Hydraulic Actuator** — for very high thrust.
2. **Positioner** — Senses valve stem position and compares with control signal. Corrects stem position for **accuracy and speed**. Uses I/P (current-to-pressure) converter for electronic input.
3. **Valve Body (Trim)** — The actual throttling element (globe-type cage, V-port ball, eccentric plug butterfly).
4. **Stem and Packing** — Connects actuator to plug.
5. **Handwheel (Manual Override)** — For operation during air failure.

**Action Types:**
- **Air-to-Open (ATO) / Air-to-Close (ATC)**: Determined by spring direction and diaphragm orientation.
- **Fail-Open (FO)**: Spring pushes plug open when air fails — used for **cooling water** (need flow on failure).
- **Fail-Close (FC)**: Spring pushes plug closed when air fails — used for **fuel gas** (need to stop fire on failure).

**Valve Characteristics (Inherent Flow Characteristics):**
This is the relationship between **valve travel (h)** and **flow coefficient (Cv)** at **constant pressure drop**.

1. **Quick-Opening:** Large Cv change for small travel. Used for **on/off** service.
2. **Linear:** Cv changes proportionally to travel. $C_v = C_{v,max} \cdot h$
3. **Equal Percentage:** Small Cv at small travel, exponential growth. Equal increments of travel produce equal percentage changes in Cv.

$$C_v = C_{v,max} \cdot R^{(h-1)}$$

Where **R = rangeability** (typically **30:1 to 50:1**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equal Percentage is the most widely used** in petrochemical processes because it provides **fine control at small openings** (precise at low flow) and **high capacity at large openings** (handles high flow).

---

### G. Safety Relief Valve (The Last Line of Defense)

**Operating Principle:** Opens **automatically** at a set pressure to relieve excess fluid, preventing catastrophic vessel rupture.

**Types:**
- **Spring-Loaded Safety Valve (PSV)** — Most common. Set pressure determined by spring compression.
- **Rupture Disc** — Single-use; bursts at set pressure.
- **Pilot-Operated Safety Valve (POSV)** — Uses a smaller pilot valve to operate the main valve; for high-pressure / large-capacity service.

> [!NOTE]
> **Petrochemical Application:** Every pressure vessel, distillation column, heat exchanger, and storage tank has a PSV/PRD per **API 520/521** sizing standards.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU University Exam**

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Valve Flow Coefficient (Cv)** | $$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{G/\rho}{\Delta P}}$$ | $Q$ = flow (gpm), $G$ = specific gravity, $\rho$ = density, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (psi) |
| **Cv for Liquid** | $$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}}$$ | $SG$ = specific gravity (water = 1.0) |
| **Cv for Gas (SI Metric form)** | $$C_v = \dfrac{Q}{N_1 \cdot P_1 \cdot Y \cdot \sqrt{x/\left(GTZ\right)}}$$ | $N_1$ = numerical constant, $P_1$ = inlet pressure (psia), $T$ = temperature (°R), $Z$ = compressibility, $Y$ = expansion factor |
| **Equal Percentage Characteristic** | $$C_v(h) = C_{v,\min} \cdot R^{h-1}$$ | $h$ = fractional travel (0 to 1), $R$ = rangeability |
| **Inherent Rangeability** | $$R = \dfrac{C_{v,\max}}{C_{v,\min}}$$ | Ratio of max to min Cv |
| **Valve Authority (Control Valve Sizing)** | $$\beta = \dfrac{\Delta P_v}{\Delta P_v + \Delta P_{system}}$$ | $\Delta P_v$ = pressure drop across valve, $\Delta P_{system}$ = pipe+fittings drop |
| **Actuator Thrust (Pneumatic)** | $$F = A_{eff} \cdot P_{air} - k \cdot x$$ | $A_{eff}$ = diaphragm area, $P_{air}$ = signal pressure, $k$ = spring constant, $x$ = stem travel |
| **Relief Valve Orifice Area (API 520)** | $$A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot K_w \cdot K_c \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M \cdot T \cdot Z}{Z_1}}}$$ | $W$ = required flow, $C$ = gas constant, $K_d$ = effective discharge coefficient, $K_w$ = backpressure correction |
| **Leakage Rate (Class IV to VI)** | $$L = \text{constant} \cdot \Delta P^N$$ | $L$ = mL/min bubbles, $N$ = 1.0 to 1.5 depending on class |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery:** Globe valves control crude pre-heat train temperature; ball valves isolate atmospheric and vacuum columns during maintenance.
- **Petrochemical Plant:** Control valves modulate steam-to-feed ratio in ethylene cracking furnaces.
- **LNG Plant:** Cryogenic ball valves with extended bonnets handle -162 °C LNG.
- **Polymer Plant:** Control valves with anti-cavitation trim handle high-pressure monomer flow.
- **Safety:** PSVs on hydrocracker reactors prevent runaway reaction overpressure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Control Valve Sizing Equation for Liquid Service

We will derive the standard **ISA 75.01.01** equation for valve sizing for an **incompressible liquid** (e.g., water, hydrocarbon liquids).

**Starting Point — Bernoulli's Energy Equation:**

For an incompressible fluid flowing through a restriction (the valve):

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}$$

**Step 1:** Solve for velocity $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 2:** Volumetric flow rate $Q$ equals velocity times cross-sectional area $A$:

$$Q = A \cdot v = A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 3:** The **valve flow coefficient $C_v$** is **defined** as the flow rate of water (specific gravity = 1.0) in gallons per minute (gpm) through the valve when the pressure drop is **1 psi**. So when $Q$ is in gpm, $\rho$ is in lbm/ft³, $\Delta P$ is in psi, and we use the engineering conversion constants:

The empirical equation becomes:

$$Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{SG}}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging for $C_v$:

$$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}}$$

**Step 5 (Choked Flow Check):** For liquids, when the outlet pressure drops below the vapor pressure of the liquid, **cavitation** or **flashing** occurs. The maximum $\Delta P$ before choking is:

$$\Delta P_{choked} = (P_1 - P_v) \cdot F_L^2$$

Where $F_L$ is the **Liquid Pressure Recovery Factor** (from valve manufacturer, typically 0.6 to 0.95).

**Step 6 (Example Numerical Application):**

> **Example:** A globe valve controls a hydrocarbon liquid (SG = 0.85) flow of **180 gpm** with an inlet pressure of **150 psia**, outlet pressure of **120 psia**, and vapor pressure of **10 psia**. $F_L = 0.9$. Size the valve.

**Given:**
$Q = 180$ gpm, $SG = 0.85$, $P_1 = 150$ psia, $P_2 = 120$ psia, $P_v = 10$ psia, $F_L = 0.9$.

**Step 6.1:** Calculate actual pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = 150 - 120 = 30 \text{ psi}$$

**Step 6.2:** Calculate maximum allowable pressure drop before choking:

$$\Delta P_{choked} = (P_1 - P_v) \cdot F_L^2 = (150 - 10) \cdot (0.9)^2 = 140 \cdot 0.81 = 113.4 \text{ psi}$$

**Step 6.3:** Since actual $\Delta P = 30 < \Delta P_{choked} = 113.4$, **no choking** → use standard equation.

**Step 6.4:** Calculate required $C_v$:

$$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}} = 180 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.85}{30}}$$

$$C_v = 180 \cdot \sqrt{0.02833} = 180 \cdot 0.1684 = 30.3$$

**Step 6.5 (Selection):** Choose the next standard valve size with $C_v \geq 30.3$ → **2-inch globe valve with $C_v = 35$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equal Percentage Inherent Flow Characteristic

**Starting Point — Definition:**
In an **equal percentage** characteristic, an equal increment of valve travel produces an **equal percentage** change in the existing flow coefficient $C_v$.

**Step 1:** Let $h$ = fractional travel (0 = closed, 1 = fully open). The relative change in $C_v$ over a small travel $dh$ is:

$$\dfrac{dC_v}{C_v} = k \cdot dh$$

Where $k$ = constant of proportionality.

**Step 2:** Integrate from $h = 0$ (where $C_v = C_{v,\min}$) to $h$ (where $C_v = C_v(h)$):

$$\int_{C_{v,\min}}^{C_v(h)} \dfrac{dC_v}{C_v} = k \cdot \int_0^h dh$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{C_v(h)}{C_{v,\min}}\right) = k \cdot h$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\dfrac{C_v(h)}{C_{v,\min}} = e^{k \cdot h}$$

**Step 5:** Define **rangeability** $R$ as $C_{v,\max}/C_{v,\min}$ at $h = 1$:

$$R = e^{k \cdot 1} \implies k = \ln(R)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$C_v(h) = C_{v,\min} \cdot e^{h \cdot \ln(R)} = C_{v,\min} \cdot R^h$$

**Step 7 (Final Standard Form):** Most references use the form:

$$\boxed{C_v(h) = C_{v,\max} \cdot R^{(h-1)}}$$

**Step 8 (Verification):** At $h = 0$: $C_v = C_{v,\max} \cdot R^{-1} = C_{v,\max}/R = C_{v,\min}$ ✓
At $h = 1$: $C_v = C_{v,\max} \cdot R^0 = C_{v,\max}$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Comparison Table — Memorize for KTU Exam**

| Parameter | Gate Valve | Globe Valve | Ball Valve | Butterfly Valve | Control Valve |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Motion** | Linear (up/down) | Linear (perpendicular) | Quarter-turn (90°) | Quarter-turn (90°) | Linear (varies) |
| **Best Use** | On/Off isolation | Throttling | On/Off, ESD | On/Off, large dia. | Modulating control |
| **ΔP when Open** | Very low | High | Very low | Low | Variable |
| **Throttling** | ❌ Poor | ✅ Excellent | ⚠ Limited | ⚠ Limited | ✅ Excellent |
| **Leakage Class** | Class IV–V | Class IV–V | Class VI (soft seat) | Class VI | Class IV–V |
| **Speed** | Slow (multi-turn) | Medium | Fast (¼ turn) | Fast (¼ turn) | Medium |
| **Cost (relative)** | Medium | Medium | Low–Medium | Lowest | Highest |
| **Size Range** | ½"–36" | ½"–16" | ½"–60" | 2"–120" | ½"–24" |
| **Direction** | Bi-directional | Uni-directional (preferred) | Bi-directional | Bi-directional | Uni-directional |
| **Cavitation Resistance** | Low | High (cage trim) | Low | Low | Highest (multi-stage) |
| **Typical Service** | Block valve, line isolation | Manual throttling | ESD, swing valve | Cooling water | Process variable control |
| **Standard** | API 600/602 | API 600/602 | API 6D/607 | API 609 | ISA 75 / IEC 60534 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Control Valve Assembly

```mermaid
graph TD
    DCS[DCS Controller Output 4-20mA] --> IPC[I to P Converter 3-15 psi]
    IPC --> POS[Positioner]
    POS --> ACT[Actuator Pneumatic Diaphragm]
    ACT --> STEM[Valve Stem Linear Motion]
    STEM --> PLUG[Valve Plug and Cage Trim]
    PLUG --> FLOW[Process Flow Modulation]
    FLOW --> FB[Process Variable Flow, Pressure, Temperature]
    FB --> SENSOR[Field Sensor Transmitter]
    SENSOR --> DCS
    POS -. Feedback .-> STEM
    HW[Manual Handwheel Override] -. Parallel .-> STEM
    SUP[Air Supply 20 psi] --> ACT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram left-to-right, top-to-bottom.** The DCS sends a current signal → I/P converter → pneumatic signal to positioner → positioner compares with stem feedback and adjusts air pressure to actuator diaphragm → actuator moves stem → plug throttles flow → process variable changes → sensor sends new measurement → DCS recomputes → loop continues.

---

## 4.2 Valve Type Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Need a Valve] --> Q1{Full Open or Full Close?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, On/Off| Q2{Pipe Size?}
    Q1 -->|No, Throttle Modulate| Q3{Pressure Class?}
    Q2 -->|Under 2 inch| Q4{Fast Operation Needed?}
    Q2 -->|Over 2 inch| Q5{High Pressure ANSI 600+?}
    Q3 -->|Low Pressure| Q6[Globe Valve or Control Valve]
    Q3 -->|High Pressure| Q7[Control Valve with Anti-Cavitation Trim]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q8[Ball Valve Quarter Turn]
    Q4 -->|No| Q9[Gate Valve Multi Turn]
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q10[Gate Valve API 600 High Class]
    Q5 -->|No| Q11[Butterfly Valve Lightweight]
```

---

## 4.3 P&ID Symbol Reference (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S1[Valve Symbols on P and ID]
        A[Gate Valve] --> A1[Two Triangles Point to Point]
        B[Globe Valve] --> B1[Disc Sits on Horizontal Line]
        C[Ball Valve] --> C1[Two Filled Triangles]
        D[Butterfly Valve] --> D1[Disc on Shaft Line]
        E[Check Valve] --> E1[Disc with Arrow Indicator]
        F[Control Valve] --> F1[Globe Body with Diaphragm Actuator on Top]
        G[Safety Valve] --> G1[Spring Loaded on Vessel Top]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Control Valve Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Control Signal 4-20mA or 3-15 psi] --> B[Positioner]
    B --> C[Relay and I to P]
    C --> D[Actuator Air Chamber]
    D --> E[Diaphragm Flexes]
    E --> F[Stem Moves Up or Down]
    F --> G[Plug Changes Position in Cage]
    G --> H[Flow Area Changes]
    H --> I[Process Variable Modulated]
    SP[Spring Opposes Air Force] --> E
    M[Manual Handwheel] -. Override .-> F
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Explain the working principle of a globe valve with a neat sketch. Why is it preferred for throttling service?**

**Model Answer:**

A globe valve consists of a **Z-shaped body** with an internal seat ring and a **movable disc/plug** attached to a stem that moves perpendicular to the seat. The handwheel/actuator rotates the stem, raising or lowering the plug.

When the plug **lifts off the seat**, fluid flows through the annular opening. The **flow area is proportional to the stem travel**, which is why globe valves provide **linear, predictable throttling**. Fluid undergoes a **tortuous S-shaped path**, which causes significant pressure drop — exactly what is needed for modulation.

**Why preferred for throttling:**
- **Linear relationship** between stem travel and flow rate at constant $\Delta P$.
- The disc and seat can be machined precisely for fine control.
- The plug can be easily replaced without disassembling the valve body.
- Available with **cage trim** for anti-cavitation and reduced noise.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a ball valve and a butterfly valve. List two petrochemical applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Ball Valve | Butterfly Valve |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Closing element** | Spherical ball with bore | Circular disc on shaft |
| **Operation** | Quarter-turn (90°) | Quarter-turn (90°) |
| **Shutoff quality** | Bubble-tight (Class VI) | Bubble-tight with soft seat |
| **Pressure rating** | Up to ANSI 2500 | Up to ANSI 600 |
| **Size range** | ½" to 60" | 2" to 120" (larger) |
| **Weight & cost** | Heavier, costlier for large sizes | Lightweight, economical |

**Ball valve applications:** (1) LNG loading line isolation, (2) Gas pipeline ESD.
**Butterfly valve applications:** (1) Cooling water network, (2) Firewater main isolation.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) (7 marks)** Describe in detail the **classification of control valves** based on (i) actuator type, (ii) body type, and (iii) flow characteristic. Explain the **equal percentage characteristic** with its mathematical expression and state **two situations** where it is preferred.

**b) (7 marks)** A control valve handles liquid hydrocarbon (SG = 0.78) at flow rate **250 gpm**, with $P_1$ = **200 psia**, $P_2$ = **160 psia**, $P_v$ = **15 psia**, and $F_L$ = **0.85**. Calculate the required $C_v$ and select the standard valve size. Check for cavitation.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Control Valves:**

**(i) Based on Actuator Type:**
- **Pneumatic Diaphragm** — Most common; uses 3–15 psi air on a flexible rubber diaphragm. Simple, reliable, spring-opposed for fail-safe action.
- **Pneumatic Piston** — Uses a piston in a cylinder; provides higher thrust for large valves.
- **Electric Motor** — Uses a reversible electric motor with gear train; suitable for remote locations without instrument air.
- **Hydraulic** — Uses oil pressure; highest thrust, for very large valves or high-pressure drops.
- **Electro-Hydraulic** — Combines electric signal with hydraulic actuator.

**(ii) Based on Body Type:**
- **Globe body (single-port, double-port)** — Most common, best for throttling.
- **Angle body** — Handles high-pressure drops, slurry service.
- **V-port ball** — Quarter-turn with equal percentage characteristic.
- **Eccentric plug butterfly** — High capacity, moderate throttling.
- **Sleeve (cage) trim** — Anti-cavitation, low noise.

**(iii) Based on Flow Characteristic:**
- **Quick-Opening** — Large flow change for small travel; for on/off service.
- **Linear** — $C_v \propto h$; for systems with constant $\Delta P$.
- **Equal Percentage** — Logarithmic; for systems with high pressure drop variation.

**Equal Percentage Mathematical Expression:**

$$C_v(h) = C_{v,\max} \cdot R^{(h-1)}$$

Where $h$ = fractional stem travel (0 to 1), $R$ = rangeability (typically 30 to 50), $C_{v,\max}$ = maximum Cv at full open.

**Two situations where Equal Percentage is preferred:**
1. **High pressure drop variation across the valve** — equal percentage provides fine control at low flow (small openings) and high capacity at large openings.
2. **Process with wide rangeability** — e.g., reactor coolant flow, where flow must be precisely controlled from 5% to 100% of maximum.

**(b) Valve Sizing Calculation:**

**Given:**
$Q = 250$ gpm, $SG = 0.78$, $P_1 = 200$ psia, $P_2 = 160$ psia, $P_v = 15$ psia, $F_L = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Calculate Pressure Drop:**

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 200 - 160 = 40 \text{ psi}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Choked Pressure Drop:**

$$\Delta P_{choked} = (P_1 - P_v) \cdot F_L^2 = (200 - 15) \cdot (0.85)^2$$

$$\Delta P_{choked} = 185 \cdot 0.7225 = 133.7 \text{ psi}$$

**Step 3 — Cavitation Check:**

Since $\Delta P = 40 \text{ psi} < \Delta P_{choked} = 133.7 \text{ psi}$ → **No cavitation**, use standard liquid sizing equation.

**Step 4 — Calculate $C_v$:**

$$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}} = 250 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.78}{40}}$$

$$C_v = 250 \cdot \sqrt{0.0195} = 250 \cdot 0.1396 = 34.9$$

**Step 5 — Select Standard Valve:**
Choose the next standard $C_v$ greater than 34.9 → **3-inch globe valve with $C_v = 46$** (per manufacturer catalog).

**Conclusion:** A 3-inch globe valve with $C_v = 46$ is suitable; operating at $34.9/46 = 76\%$ of max capacity, leaving **24% turndown** for throttling range.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**a) (7 marks)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a pneumatic diaphragm-actuated control valve with a valve positioner**. State its **fail-safe actions** and explain how they are achieved.

**b) (7 marks)** Compare **gate, globe, and ball valves** in terms of (i) flow direction, (ii) pressure drop, (iii) throttling suitability, (iv) typical application. State the relevant **API standard** for each.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pneumatic Diaphragm-Actuated Control Valve with Positioner:**

**Components:**
1. **Diaphragm Actuator** — A flexible rubber diaphragm clamped between two casing halves. Instrument air (3–15 psi) is applied above the diaphragm; below it, a **calibrated spring** pushes back.
2. **Stem** — Connected to the diaphragm center; moves up/down.
3. **Valve Plug and Seat** — The throttling element in the body.
4. **Positioner** — A separate device mounted on the actuator yoke. Receives the 4–20 mA signal, converts to 3–15 psi, and continuously adjusts stem position to match the signal precisely. Uses stem position feedback (lever + cam + flapper-nozzle mechanism).
5. **Handwheel** — For manual override during air failure.

**Working:**
The 4–20 mA signal from DCS enters the positioner. The positioner outputs a 3–15 psi signal to the actuator diaphragm. The diaphragm deflects against the spring, moving the stem and plug. The positioner senses the resulting stem travel and compares it to the signal; any error causes a correction in the air pressure, ensuring **precise stem positioning**.

**Fail-Safe Actions:**

1. **Fail-Open (FO) — Air to Close (ATC):**
   - The spring is mounted **above** the diaphragm.
   - When air fails, spring pushes diaphragm **down**, stem moves **up**, plug moves **away from seat** → valve **opens fully**.
   - Used for **cooling water, instrument air, firewater** — flow must continue on failure.

2. **Fail-Close (FC) — Air to Open (ATO):**
   - The spring is mounted **below** the diaphragm.
   - When air fails, spring pushes diaphragm **up**, stem moves **down**, plug moves **into seat** → valve **closes fully**.
   - Used for **fuel gas, furnace oil, reactor feed** — flow must stop on failure to prevent hazard.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Gate Valve | Globe Valve | Ball Valve |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **(i) Flow Direction** | Bi-directional | Uni-directional (preferred) | Bi-directional |
| **(ii) Pressure Drop** | Very low (straight-through) | High (tortuous path) | Very low (full-bore) |
| **(iii) Throttling** | Not suitable | Excellent | Limited |
| **(iv) Typical Application** | Block valve on pipeline, tank isolation | Manual throttling, bypass line | ESD, frequent operation |
| **API Standard** | **API 600** (bolted bonnet) / API 602 (compact) | **API 600** / API 602 | **API 6D** (pipeline) / API 607 (fire-safe) |

---

## KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exam:**
>
> 1. **Using a gate valve for throttling** — they will mark zero. Always say **"NOT for throttling — causes wire drawing and seat erosion."**
> 2. **Confusing Air-to-Open with Fail-Open** — ATO means air OPENS the valve; FO means the valve OPENS on air failure. They are **opposite** terms. ATO + FC; ATC + FO.
> 3. **Forgetting the vapor pressure $P_v$ in liquid sizing** — cavitation check is **mandatory** in any liquid sizing question.
> 4. **Wrong unit for $C_v$** — remember $C_v$ is defined for **water in gpm at 1 psi ΔP** in US units; in metric, use $K_v$ (m³/h at 1 bar ΔP). Don't mix.
> 5. **Drawing gate valve with horizontal stem** — stem is **vertical**; it moves up/down.
> 6. **Forgetting the positioner** — modern control valves **always have a positioner** in KTU answers.
> 7. **Writing only "Gate Valve" without citing API 600** — KTU 2024 expects **standards** in every answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam**

- ☐ **Gate valve** = on/off only, multi-turn, low ΔP, API 600.
- ☐ **Globe valve** = throttling king, Z-shape, cage trim for anti-cavitation.
- ☐ **Ball valve** = quarter-turn, bubble-tight, fire-safe (API 607), ESD service.
- ☐ **Butterfly valve** = lightweight, large diameter, ≤ ANSI 600, water/air service.
- ☐ **Check valve** = prevents backflow, swing/lift/dual-plate, on pump discharge.
- ☐ **Safety valve** = last line of defense, set pressure per API 520.
- ☐ **Control valve** = pneumatic diaphragm + positioner + cage trim.
- ☐ **Equal percentage** characteristic: $C_v = C_{v,\max} \cdot R^{(h-1)}$.
- ☐ **Liquid Cv equation**: $C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{SG/\Delta P}$.
- ☐ **Cavitation check**: $\Delta P_{choked} = (P_1 - P_v) \cdot F_L^2$.
- ☐ **Fail-Open (FO)** = cooling water service (flow must continue).
- ☐ **Fail-Close (FC)** = fuel/reactor feed (flow must stop on failure).
- ☐ **API 600** = Gate/Globe/Check (bolted bonnet).
- ☐ **API 6D** = Ball/Check/Gate (pipeline service).
- ☐ **API 607** = Fire-safe soft-seated quarter-turn.
- ☐ **ISA 75.01** = Control valve sizing equations.
- ☐ **ANSI/FCI 70-2 Class VI** = bubble-tight (0.5 mL/min).
- ☐ **Valve Authority** $\beta = \Delta P_v / \Delta P_{total}$ — should be **0.3 to 0.7** for good control.
- ☐ **Inherent rangeability** $R = C_{v,\max} / C_{v,\min}$ (typically **30:1 to 50:1** for equal percentage).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P&I Diagram of Petroleum Refinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** P&I Diagram of Petroleum Refinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: P&I DIAGRAM OF PETROLEUM REFINERY
## Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is a detailed, schematic engineering drawing that graphically represents the complete process flow of a plant—including all piping, equipment, instrumentation, control loops, and safety systems—using standardized symbols defined primarily by the **International Society of Automation (ISA) Standard ISA-5.1** ("Instrumentation Symbols and Identification") and supplemented by **ISA-5.3, ANSI/ISA-S5.4, ISO 14617**, and **IEC 62424**.

For a petroleum refinery, the P&ID is the master engineering document that integrates:
- **Process piping** (size, material, line class, insulation, tracing)
- **Process equipment** (distillation columns, reactors, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, storage tanks, separators)
- **Instrumentation** (sensors, transmitters, controllers, valves)
- **Control strategies** (feedback, feedforward, cascade, ratio, split-range)
- **Safety and Shutdown Systems** (Emergency Shutdown System – ESD, Safety Instrumented System – SIS, interlocks, relief valves, flare headers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The P&ID is NOT a Process Flow Diagram (PFD). The PFD shows only major process flow streams, mass/energy balances, and primary equipment. The P&ID is the *quantitative, instrumented, and constructible* version—essential for control system design, plant commissioning, and HAZOP studies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint of a Living Plant"

Imagine a **hospital patient monitoring system**: just as doctors see heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation on a screen, a refinery P&ID shows every "vital sign" of the process. Each **circle with a tag (e.g., TIC-101)** is like a sensor/sticker on the body. **Lines connecting instruments** are like nerves carrying signals to and from the brain (the DCS/PLC). **Control valves** are like reflexes that automatically adjust to keep the body healthy. The P&ID is the *complete nervous system + skeletal map* of the refinery.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Simple Words:** A P&ID is the language that allows process engineers, instrument engineers, and operators to "speak" the same operational story of a refinery. It is the document used to wire up the entire plant's brain (control system) and skeleton (piping).

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics Used in P&ID Design

- **Pipe sizes:** NPS ½" to NPS 60", designated by **Nominal Pipe Size (NPS)** in inches.
- **Line class:** Defined by **ASME B16.5 / B16.47** flange ratings (150#, 300#, 600#, 900#, 1500#, 2500#).
- **Process pressure ranges:** Crude units: **10–30 bar**; Hydrocrackers: **100–200 bar**; Atmospheric/Vacuum Distillation: **0.1–3 bar (vacuum)**.
- **Standard signal types:** **4–20 mA DC** (electronic), **3–15 psig** (pneumatic), **0.2–1.0 bar** (pneumatic EU), **HART**, **FOUNDATION Fieldbus**, **Profibus PA**.
- **ISA Tag Numbering:** Typically follows `XYY-NNN` format, where `X` is a functional letter, `YY` is a loop number series, and `NNN` is the unique loop number.

### 1.4 Why P&ID is Indispensable in a Petroleum Refinery

| Purpose | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| Design review | Confirms process & control intent |
| HAZOP study | Identifies hazards node-by-node |
| DCS/PLC programming | Source of I/O list and interlock logic |
| Construction | Fabricates piping, installs instruments |
| Commissioning | Loop checks & functional validation |
| Operations | Operator training & emergency response |
| Maintenance | Troubleshooting and turnarounds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Note (KTU 2024):** A P&ID must be developed *after* the PFD and before the **Control Philosophy**, **Cause-&-Effect Matrix**, and **I/O List**. It is the *single source of truth* for all downstream control engineering documents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ISA-5.1 Instrument Identification — The Tag Numbering Logic

Every instrument in a P&ID is represented by a **circle (or bubble)** with a tag inside, broken into functional sections.

**Tag Format:**
$$ \text{ISA Tag} = \underbrace{X}_{\text{First Letter}} \; \underbrace{YZ}_{\text{Successive Letters}} \; - \; \underbrace{NNN}_{\text{Loop Number}} $$

Where:
- **First letter (X)** = Measured variable (e.g., `T` = Temperature, `P` = Pressure, `F` = Flow, `L` = Level, `A` = Analyzer)
- **Succeeding letters (YZ)** = Function modifier or output (e.g., `I` = Indicate, `C` = Control, `R` = Record, `T` = Transmit, `V` = Valve)
- **NNN** = Unique loop number (e.g., `101`, `102A`)

### 2.2 Functional Letter Code Chart (ISA-5.1)

| First Letter | Measured Variable | Successive Letter | Function/Modifier |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Analysis | **A** | Alarm |
| **B** | Burner/Combustion | **C** | Control |
| **C** | Conductivity | **E** | Element (Primary sensor) |
| **D** | Density | **G** | Glass/Gauge |
| **E** | Voltage/EMF | **I** | Indicate |
| **F** | Flowrate | **K** | Control Station |
| **H** | Hand/Manual | **L** | Light/Pilot |
| **I** | Current | **P** | Point/Test |
| **J** | Power | **R** | Record |
| **K** | Time/Schedule | **S** | Switch/Safety |
| **L** | Level | **T** | Transmit |
| **M** | Moisture | **V** | Valve/Damper |
| **N** | (User defined) | **W** | Well |
| **O** | (User defined) | **X** | Unclassified |
| **P** | Pressure/ Vacuum | **Y** | Relay/Compute |
| **Q** | Quantity | **Z** | Driver/Final Element |
| **R** | Radioactivity | — | — |
| **S** | Speed/Frequency | — | — |
| **T** | Temperature | — | — |
| **U** | Multivariable | — | — |
| **V** | Vibration | — | — |
| **W** | Weight | — | — |
| **X** | Unclassified | — | — |
| **Y** | Event/State | — | — |
| **Z** | Position/Dimension | — | — |

### 2.3 Signal Line Conventions (Critical for P&ID)

| Line Type | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid thick line** | ━━━ | Process piping (main line) |
| **Solid thin line** | ─── | Process connection to instrument (capillary/tubing) |
| **Dashed line** | - - - | Pneumatic signal (3–15 psig) |
| **Solid line with crosshatch** | ━━╪━━ | Electrical signal (4–20 mA) |
| **Double crosshatch** | ╬╬╬ | Hydraulic or capillary tubing |
| **Dotted line** | · · · | Software/electronic data link (DCS internal) |
| **Heavy dashed line** | ╍╍╍ | Mechanical linkage |
| **Circle with bar across** | ⊘ | Instrument shared between two control loops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** ISA symbol recognition questions are extremely common in KTU examinations. Memorize the **circle-with-line-vs-dashed** distinction between *pneumatic* and *electronic* signals.

### 2.4 Control Valve Body Symbols in P&ID

| Symbol | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Bowtie (filled)** | ▷◁ | Gate valve (isolation) |
| **Bowtie (open)** | ◁▷ | Ball valve |
| **Filled circle + bowtie** | ⚫◁▷ | Globe valve (control) |
| **Triangle** | ▲ | Check valve (NRV) |
| **Bowtie + spring** | ▷◁/\\| | Safety/Relief valve |
| **Bowtie + solenoid** | ▷◁┤ | Solenoid-operated valve |
| **3-way bowtie** | ⧖ | Three-way valve |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for P&ID Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> Although P&IDs are largely qualitative, several quantitative relationships govern control loop selection and valve sizing, which often appear in KTU numerical problems.

**1) Control Valve Sizing (ISA-75.01 / IEC 60534):**

$$ Q = C_v \, N_1 \, F_P \, Y \, \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{G_g \, T_1 \, Z}} $$

Where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (m³/h)
- $C_v$ = Valve flow coefficient
- $N_1$ = Numerical constant (depends on units)
- $F_P$ = Piping geometry factor
- $Y$ = Expansion factor
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across valve
- $G_g$ = Specific gravity of fluid
- $T_1$ = Inlet temperature (K)
- $Z$ = Compressibility factor

**2) Mass Balance across a Control Loop (Refinery Naphtha Splitter):**

$$ F_{\text{in}} = F_{\text{top}} + F_{\text{bottom}} $$

$$ F_{\text{in}} \, x_{i,\text{in}} = F_{\text{top}} \, x_{i,\text{top}} + F_{\text{bottom}} \, x_{i,\text{bottom}} $$

Where $F$ = molar flow, $x_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$.

**3) Standard Signal Conversion (4–20 mA to Engineering Units):**

$$ E = E_{\min} + (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) \times \frac{I - 4}{20 - 4} $$

Where:
- $E$ = Engineering value (e.g., 0–100 °C, 0–50 bar)
- $I$ = Current signal in mA
- $E_{\min}$, $E_{\max}$ = Range lower/upper limits

**4) Control Loop Time Constant (Process Dynamics):**

$$ \tau_{\text{loop}} = \tau_P + \tau_I + \tau_V + \tau_T $$

Where:
- $\tau_P$ = Process time constant
- $\tau_I$ = Transmitter lag
- $\tau_V$ = Valve response time
- $\tau_T$ = Transmission line delay

**5) Pressure Drop in Piping (Darcy-Weisbach):**

$$ \Delta P_f = f \, \frac{L}{D} \, \frac{\rho v^2}{2} $$
