Used when two streams must be combined in a fixed proportion (e.g., air-to-fuel in fired heaters, steam-to-feed in cracking). The *wild flow* is measured uncontrolled; the *controlled flow* is set as a fixed ratio $k$ of the wild stream.

$$F_{controlled} = k \cdot F_{wild}$$

### 2.3.3 Cascade Control
The *primary* (master) loop controls a downstream variable (e.g., feed drum level), and its output becomes the *set point* of the *secondary* (slave) flow loop. This dramatically attenuates disturbances.

### 2.3.4 Feedforward Control
Disturbance variable(s) are measured *before* they reach the process, and the controller pre-positions the valve. Eliminates the dead time inherent in feedback control.

### 2.3.5 Override (Constraint) Control
Multiple controllers share a single valve; a *selector* (low/high signal selector) chooses the controller that wants the valve *most-closed* or *most-open* to enforce a safety/process constraint.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM GOLD:** These formulas appear repeatedly in KTU ESE papers. Memorize the form, units, and typical numerical values.

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Volumetric flow through orifice / control valve | $C_d$ dimensionless, $A$ m², $\Delta P$ Pa, $\rho$ kg/m³ |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \, Q$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s |
| 3 | $h = w \, C_d \sqrt{2 g \Delta P}$ | Head-type flow (open channel) | m |
| 4 | $F_{B} = k \cdot F_{A}$ | Ratio control: B follows A | $k$ dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | $u(t) = K_p \!\left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\!\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$ | PID controller output (ideal form) | $K_p$, $T_i$ (s), $T_d$ (s) |
| 6 | $G_c(s) = K_p\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right)$ | PID transfer function | Laplace-domain |
| 7 | $Q_{feed} = \dot{m} C_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | Feed preheat duty | kW |
| 8 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas feed equation | $P$ Pa, $V$ m³, $T$ K |
| 9 | $z_c = \dfrac{0.5 \, \Delta P_v}{\Delta P_v + P_{avg}}$ | Compressibility factor correction (orifice) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Reynolds = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Re for flow meter sizing | dimensionless, turbulent if $> 4000$ |
| 11 | $K_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho / \rho_0}{\Delta P}}$ | Valve flow coefficient (metric) | $Q$ m³/h, $\Delta P$ bar |
| 12 | $T_t = T_s + 273.15$ | Temperature K from °C | K |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ in tables, use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mathrm{abs}(x)$ — never use the bare vertical pipe inside a markdown table cell; it breaks the column delimiter.

## 2.5 Why Feed Control Matters in Real Plants

In a refinery, the **crude unit feed** is the most critical controlled variable. A 1% drop in feed rate reduces gasoline yield by ~0.5% and increases per-barrel energy cost by ~2%. A **fluid catalytic cracker (FCC)** is even more sensitive — feed composition changes propagate through the riser, regenerator, and main fractionator within minutes. Hence KTU's strong emphasis on feed control in petrochemical instrumentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Flow Through an Orifice Plate (Feed Flow Measurement)

The orifice plate is the single most common primary element used to measure feed flow. We derive the working equation from Bernoulli's equation plus a discharge coefficient.

### Step 1 — Apply Steady-State Energy Balance Between Upstream (1) and Throat (2)

For an incompressible fluid in a horizontal pipe (no elevation change), Bernoulli's equation reads:

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^{2} = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^{2}$$

This is the **conservation of mechanical energy** along a streamline, neglecting friction.

### Step 2 — Solve for the Pressure Differential

Rearranging and isolating the pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\!\left(v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Apply Continuity (Conservation of Mass)

By mass conservation in steady flow:

$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_1 = v_2 \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$$

where $A_1$ is the pipe cross-section and $A_2$ is the throat cross-section. This is the **no-mass-loss assumption** of the orifice model.

### Step 4 — Express the Velocity in Terms of $\Delta P$

Substituting the continuity expression back into the Bernoulli result and defining the diameter ratio $\beta = d_2/d_1$:

$$v_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{4}}} \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

The factor $1/\sqrt{1-\beta^{4}}$ is the **velocity-of-approach factor**, denoted $E_v$.

### Step 5 — Introduce the Discharge Coefficient

In a real orifice, the vena contracta is smaller than the geometric throat and there is friction. We lump these effects into a single empirical **discharge coefficient** $C_d$ (typically **0.6 – 0.65** for $\beta \approx 0.7$). The working volumetric flow equation is:

$$Q = C_d \, A_2 \, E_v \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

This is the **standard ISO 5167 orifice equation**, the workhorse of refinery feed-flow metering.

### Step 6 — Convert to Mass Flow

Multiplying by density gives the mass flow rate, the variable most often used in petrochemical control:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, Q = C_d \, A_2 \, E_v \, \sqrt{2 \rho \, \Delta P}$$

For gas feeds, an **expansion factor** $Y$ and **compressibility** $z_c$ are added:

$$\dot{m}_{gas} = C_d \, A_2 \, E_v \, Y \, \sqrt{2 \rho_1 \, \Delta P}$$

This derivation is **directly tested** in KTU module exams and lab viva.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ratio Control Steady-State Bias

A ratio station multiplies a *wild* measurement by a gain $k$. We want to show that, at steady state, the controlled stream exactly tracks the wild stream scaled by $k$.

### Step 1 — Wild Stream Measurement

$$F_A^{measured} = \alpha_A F_A^{true}$$

where $\alpha_A$ is the transmitter scaling constant (e.g., 4–20 mA → 0–100%).

### Step 2 — Ratio Multiplier Output

The ratio relay output (set point to the slave controller) is:

$$F_{B}^{SP} = k \cdot F_A^{measured} = k \, \alpha_A F_A^{true}$$

### Step 3 — Slave Controller Acts to Eliminate Error

The slave flow controller drives the measured $F_B$ to its set point, so at steady state $F_B^{measured} = F_{B}^{SP}$. Therefore:

$$\alpha_B F_B^{true} = k \, \alpha_A F_A^{true}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the True Ratio

$$k_{true} = \dfrac{F_B^{true}}{F_A^{true}} = \dfrac{k \, \alpha_A}{\alpha_B}$$

Because $\alpha_A = \alpha_B$ in most modern DCS implementations (unified signal scaling), we obtain the **clean result**:

$$\boxed{\,F_B^{true} = k \cdot F_A^{true}\,}$$

This proves that the *indicated* ratio equals the *actual* ratio when the transmitters are properly ranged — a frequent viva question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Feed Control Strategy

The matrix below is a **decision tool** — KTU examiners often give a scenario and ask "which control strategy is most appropriate?" Use this matrix.

| Process Goal | Recommended Strategy | Primary Variable | Secondary Variable | Key Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hold feed flow constant | Single-loop FC | $\dot{m}$ | — | Orifice + dPT |
| Hold air-to-fuel ratio | Ratio control | $F_{air}$ | $F_{fuel}$ | Two flow meters |
| Hold column overhead composition | Feedforward + feedback | $T_{feed}$, $F_{feed}$ | $x_{D}$ | RTD, GC |
| Hold feed drum level | Cascade | $L_{drum}$ | $F_{out}$ | DP level, FT |
| Avoid pump cavitation | Override (low-selector) | $P_{suction}$ | $F_{out}$ | PT on suction |
| Avoid feed cooler flooding | Override (high-selector) | $L_{cooler}$ | $F_{in}$ | LT on cooler |
| Preheat optimization | Feedforward | $T_{in}$ | $Q_{furnace}$ | TT, FT |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Heuristic:** "Use cascade when you see dead time; use feedforward when you can measure the disturbance; use ratio when two streams must move together; use override when a hard limit must be enforced."

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style)

**Problem:** A refinery feed line uses an orifice plate with throat diameter $d_2 = 80$ mm in a pipe of $D = 200$ mm. The differential pressure measured is $\Delta P = 25$ kPa, the fluid density is $\rho = 850$ kg/m³, and the discharge coefficient is $C_d = 0.62$. Compute the mass flow rate.

### Step 1 — Diameter Ratio

$$\beta = \dfrac{d_2}{D} = \dfrac{80}{200} = 0.40$$

### Step 2 — Velocity-of-Approach Factor

$$E_v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{4}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 0.40^{4}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0256}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{0.9744}}$$

$$E_v = 1.01306$$

### Step 3 — Throat Area

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_2^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^{2} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Volumetric Flow

$$Q = C_d \, A_2 \, E_v \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.01306 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 25000}{850}}$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.01306 \times \sqrt{58.824}$$

$$Q = 3.157 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.01306 \times 7.6702$$

$$Q = 2.452 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

### Step 5 — Mass Flow

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, Q = 850 \times 2.452 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 20.84 \, \text{kg/s} \;\;(\approx 75.0 \, \text{tonnes/hour})}$$

This answer is **physically reasonable** for a refinery side-stream — a perfect KTU-style numerical result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Loop Feed Flow Control (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIPE[Feed Pipe] --> ORIF[Orifice Plate Primary]
    ORIF --> DPT[Differential Pressure Transmitter]
    DPT --> SIG[4-20 mA Signal]
    SIG --> FC[Flow Controller FC-101]
    FC --> I_P[PID Algorithm in DCS]
    I_P --> VALV[Feed Control Valve FCV-101]
    VALV --> PIPE
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> FC
    ALARM[Hi/Lo Alarm] --> SIG
```

**Interpretation:** The flow transmitter (DPT) senses $\Delta P$, the DCS-resident PID controller computes the valve position, and the control valve modulates flow. The signal `SP` is the operator-entered target. `ALARM` is the annunciation path.

## 4.2 Cascade Control — Feed Drum Level → Outlet Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DRUM[Feed Drum] --> LEV[Level Transmitter LT-201]
    LEV --> LIC[Level Controller LIC-201 - Master]
    LIC -->|Remote Setpoint| FIC[Flow Controller FIC-202 - Slave]
    FT[Outlet Flow Transmitter FT-202] --> FIC
    FIC --> FCV[Outlet Control Valve FCV-202]
    FCV --> NEXT[Downstream Unit]
    SP_L[LIC Setpoint] --> LIC
```

**Why cascade here?** Drum level changes are slow; flow disturbances are fast. The slave flow loop attenuates flow disturbances in seconds, so the master level loop only "sees" the slow drift and corrects gradually.

## 4.3 Ratio Control — Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Feed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HC[Hydrocarbon Feed - Wild] --> FT_A[FT-301]
    FT_A --> RATIO[Ratio Relay Multiplier k]
    RATIO -->|Setpoint| FIC_B[FIC-302]
    FT_B[Steam Flow FT-302] --> FIC_B
    FIC_B --> FCV_B[Steam Valve FCV-302]
    SP_K[Operator-Entered Ratio k] --> RATIO
```

**Reading:** Hydrocarbon flow is *wild* (uncontrolled). The ratio station multiplies it by $k$ and feeds the resulting set point to the steam flow controller, which manipulates the steam valve. The system enforces $F_{steam} = k \cdot F_{HC}$ at all times.

## 4.4 Feedforward Control — Compensating Feed Temperature Disturbance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TT[Temperature Transmitter TT-401 on Inlet]
    TT --> FF[Feedforward Block FF-401]
    FF -->|Bias/Additive Output| FIC[FIC-403 Master]
    LIC[LIC-402 Master] --> FIC
    FT[FT-404] --> FIC
    FIC --> FCV[FCV-404 on Preheat Steam]
    DELAY[Lead-Lag Dynamic Compensator] --> FF
    DELAY --> TT
```

**Insight:** The feedforward branch acts *instantly* on a measured disturbance, while the feedback branch trims out modeling error. The **lead-lag block** dynamically compensates for the difference in process lags of the two paths.

## 4.5 Override (Constraint) Control — Pump Cavitation Protection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[FIC-501 - Flow Loop Output] --> LSEL[Low Signal Selector]
    P1[PIC-502 - Suction Pressure Loop Output] --> LSEL
    LSEL --> FCV[Common Control Valve FCV-501]
    FT2[Flow Transmitter] --> F1
    PT2[Suction Pressure Transmitter] --> P1
    SP1[FIC Setpoint] --> F1
    SP2[PIC Setpoint with High Alarm] --> P1
```

**Operating logic:** The low-signal selector passes through whichever controller wants the valve *more closed*. Under normal conditions, the flow controller dominates. If suction pressure drops toward NPSH limit, the pressure controller takes over and throttles the valve to protect the pump.

## 4.6 Master Control Hierarchy (Process to Management)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4[L4 - Planning and Scheduling] --> L3[L3 - Real-Time Optimization RTO]
    L3 --> L2[L2 - Advanced Regulatory Control MPC]
    L2 --> L1[L1 - Basic Regulatory Loops]
    L1 --> L0[L0 - Field Sensors and Actuators]
    L0 --> PROC[Petrochemical Process]
    PROC -.-> L0
```

**Take-away:** The single feed-flow loop sits at **Layer 1**, but its *set point* is driven from **Layer 3** (RTO) and ultimately **Layer 4** (planning). A KTU examiner often asks you to *map* a control strategy onto this hierarchy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define feed control. List any four objectives of feed control in a petrochemical process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):* Feed control is the coordinated measurement and manipulation of the quantity, composition, and state (T, P) of raw material streams entering a processing unit, to maintain stable operation and product quality.

*Four objectives (½ mark each):*

1. Maintain throughput stability against upstream disturbances.
2. Hold the correct ratio of feed components (e.g., steam-to-hydrocarbon).
3. Protect downstream equipment from surge, cavitation, and over-pressure.
4. Optimize energy usage through controlled preheat temperature.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between ratio control and cascade control. Give one petrochemical example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Ratio Control | Cascade Control |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Hold *two streams* in a fixed proportion | Hold a *secondary variable* tightly to improve the *primary loop* response |
| Structure | Wild measurement → ratio station → slave SP | Master output → slave SP |
| Petrochemical example | Steam-to-hydrocarbon in a reformer furnace (½ mark) | Feed-drum level (master) → outlet flow (slave) (½ mark) |
| Distinguishing feature | Open-loop on the wild stream (½ mark) | Closed-loop on the secondary variable (½ mark) |

*(Total: 3 marks — Definition 1 mark + Comparison 1 mark + Example 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, every Part B question carries an **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below.

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU Model Paper — 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **ratio control system** for maintaining a constant air-to-fuel ratio in a process furnace. Derive the steady-state relationship between the two flow rates. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A horizontal feed line (D = 250 mm) carries oil ($\rho$ = 880 kg/m³) through an orifice plate ($d$ = 100 mm, $C_d$ = 0.61). The measured differential pressure is 18 kPa. Calculate (i) the volumetric flow rate, and (ii) the mass flow rate in kg/h. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a)

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Fuel Wild Flow] --FT_F--> RATIO STATION (k) --SP--> [FIC_AIR]
                                                            |
[Air Flow] ----FT_A--------------------------------> FIC_AIR
                                                            |
                                                          [FCV_AIR]
```

**Working (2 marks):**
The fuel (wild) flow is measured by `FT_F`. Its signal is multiplied by an operator-set ratio $k$ to generate the set point for the *air* flow controller. The controller compares the measured air flow (`FT_A`) with this set point and positions the air control valve. Any drift in fuel flow causes an immediate correction in air flow.

**Steady-state derivation (2 marks):**

Let $F_F$ = fuel flow, $F_A$ = air flow, $\alpha_F$, $\alpha_A$ = transmitter scaling constants. The controller drives $F_A$ to satisfy:

$$\alpha_A F_A = k \, \alpha_F F_F$$

With unified scaling ($\alpha_F = \alpha_A$):

$$\boxed{\,F_A = k \, F_F\,}$$

This is the **ratio control equation**, valid at steady state.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:** $D = 0.250$ m, $d = 0.100$ m, $\rho = 880$ kg/m³, $C_d = 0.61$, $\Delta P = 18{,}000$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Diameter ratio (1 mark):**

$$\beta = \dfrac{0.100}{0.250} = 0.40, \quad \beta^{4} = 0.0256$$

**Step 2 — Velocity-of-approach factor (1 mark):**

$$E_v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0256}} = \dfrac{1}{0.98722} = 1.01297$$

**Step 3 — Throat area (1 mark):**

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric flow (2 marks):**

$$Q = C_d A_2 E_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} = 0.61 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.01297 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 18000}{880}}$$

$$Q = 4.853 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.01297 \times \sqrt{40.909}$$

$$Q = 4.853 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.01297 \times 6.3958$$

$$\boxed{Q = 3.146 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \;(\approx 113.3 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{h})}$$

**Step 5 — Mass flow (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 880 \times 3.146 \times 10^{-2} = 27.69 \, \text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 99{,}700 \, \text{kg/h} \;(\approx 99.7 \, \text{tonnes/hour})}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice) **[KTU Model Paper — 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working of a **cascade control system** used for feed-drum level control. Draw the block diagram and state two advantages of cascade control over single-loop control. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** What is **feedforward control**? How does it differ from feedback control? With a block diagram, explain how feedforward is applied to compensate for feed temperature disturbances in a distillation column. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a)

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Drum Level] --LT--> [LIC Master] --RS--> [FIC Slave] --MV--> [Outlet Valve]
                              ^                       |
                              |                       v
                          Setpoint              [FT Outlet Flow]
```

**Working (2 marks):**
The level transmitter (LT) measures the drum level and sends it to the level controller (LIC). The LIC's output is *not* a valve command — it is the **remote set point** of the slave flow controller (FIC). The FIC compares this set point with the measured outlet flow (FT) and positions the outlet control valve. The level loop runs slowly (large $T_i$); the flow loop runs fast (small $T_i$).

**Two advantages (2 marks):**

1. **Disturbance rejection:** Disturbances in the outlet line (e.g., downstream pressure changes) are corrected by the *slave* before they affect the *master* level loop.
2. **Reduced loop dead time:** The slave loop has minimal dead time, so the master can be tuned more aggressively without instability.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Feedforward control measures a *disturbance* variable before it enters the process and applies a corrective action **proactively** — without waiting for the controlled variable to deviate.

**Difference table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Feedback | Feedforward |
|---|---|---|
| Action | Reactive (after deviation) | Predictive (before deviation) |
| Requires disturbance model? | No | Yes |
| Steady-state error? | Possible (zero with integral action) | Possible (model error) |
| Dead-time sensitivity | High | Very high |

**Block diagram and explanation (4 marks):**

```
[Feed T Disturbance] --TT--> [Lead-Lag FF Block] --+--> [FIC Master]
                                                       |
[Column Temp] --TT---> [TIC Master] -----------------+--> [Feed Valve]
```

*Explanation:* When feed temperature rises (cooler → harder to vaporize), the FF block instantaneously trims the steam (or reboiler duty) set point *upward*, partially counteracting the disturbance. The feedback TIC trims out the residual modeling error. The lead-lag block aligns the FF path dynamics with the FB path dynamics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing tag names** — write `FT`, `FC`, `FCV` consistently; don't mix `F` and `F1`.
> 2. **Forgetting the velocity-of-approach factor** in orifice problems; examiners deduct **1 full mark**.
> 3. **Writing the PID equation as $u(t) = K_p + K_i + K_d$** — the correct form is the integral of error, not a constant.
> 4. **Confusing ratio control with cascade control** — the former has *one* controlled variable, the latter has *two*.
> 5. **Skipping the ratio derivation** in ratio-control questions; even if the diagram is correct, the *steady-state proof* carries 2 marks.
> 6. **Units mismatch** in numerical answers; always end with the **explicit SI unit** in a box.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Feed Control (Module 3):**

- **Feed control = controlling $\dot{m}$, $x_i$, $T_f$, $P_f$** of the inlet stream to a process unit.
- **Single-loop FC** is the building block — uses orifice + dPT + PID + control valve.
- **Cascade control** uses a master output as the *remote set point* of a slave loop → excellent for drum level, reactor temperature.
- **Ratio control** multiplies a *wild* flow by $k$ to drive a *controlled* flow → used in fired heaters, chlorination, alkylation.
- **Feedforward control** measures the disturbance, applies a *dynamic compensation* (lead-lag), and pre-positions the valve.
- **Override / constraint control** uses a *low or high signal selector* to enforce process limits (e.g., suction pressure, level Hi).
- **Orifice equation** (memorize): $\dot{m} = C_d A_2 E_v \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$.
- **Ratio equation** (memorize): $F_B = k F_A$ (steady state, equal scaling).
- **Heat duty** of a feed preheater: $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$.
- **NPSH, surge, hydrate, cavitation** are *physical limits* that override control — design override loops for each.
- **Hierarchy**: Field → Regulatory → Supervisory → Optimization → Planning.
- **For KTU 2024 numericals**: always include $E_v$, the expansion factor $Y$ for gases, and report the result with a **boxed final answer and unit**.
- **For diagrams**: use ISA-standard tag conventions (FT, FC, FCV, LT, LIC, TT, TIC) — examiners award marks for correct symbology.
- **Real-world anchor**: a 1% feed drift in an FCC can cost **₹1–2 crore/day**; this makes feed control one of the highest-leverage loops in the plant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflux Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reflux Control # 1. Reflux Control — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **distillation column instrumentation**, **reflux** is defined as the portion of the condensed overhead vapor that is returned (recycled) back to the top tray (or top packing section) of a distillation column. The hardware subsystem that regulates the rate at which this condensed liquid is returned is called the **Reflux Control System**. Mathematically, the **External Reflux Ratio** is the most fundamental control parameter and is expressed as: $$R = \frac{L}{D}$$ where: - $R$ = External reflux ratio (dimensionless) - $L$ = External reflux flow rate returned to the column, expressed in **kmol/hr** or **kg/hr** - $D$ = Distillate product flow rate withdrawn as top product, expressed in **kmol/hr** or **kg/hr** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Reflux control is the *primary* manipulated variable used to control the **top product composition (XD)** in a distillation column. It is the single most important regulatory loop on a fractionator in any petrochemical refinery or gas-processing plant. ## 1.2 Physical Role of Reflux in a Distillation Column The reflux stream performs three essential engineering functions in a separation process: 1. **Mass Transfer Agent:** It creates the descending liquid phase that contacts the rising vapor phase on the trays, enabling the equilibrium-stage separation of volatile (light) and less-volatile (heavy) components. 2. **Thermal Load Provider:** It supplies a portion of the liquid traffic needed for the energy balance of the column, reducing the reboiler duty requirement. 3. **Composition Manipulator:** By varying its flow rate, the operator/engineer directly alters the internal liquid-to-vapor ratio (L/V), which shifts the operating line on the McCabe–Thiele diagram, thereby changing the top product purity (XD) and the separation sharpness. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coffee Siphon" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine brewing coffee in a siphon. The vapor rises and condenses in the top chamber. If you pour back *some* of the condensed coffee (reflux) to percolate through the grounds again, you get a stronger, richer extract. If you pour *all* of it back, you maximize strength but get almost no coffee in your cup (zero distillate). If you pour back *very little*, you get a weak brew. **Reflux control is exactly this balancing act** — pouring back just the right amount to hit a target purity while still producing enough product in the cup. ## 1.4 Classification of Reflux | Type | Definition | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | **Total Reflux** | All overhead condensate is returned; distillate withdrawal $D = 0$ | Used at startup; theoretical maximum separation, but no production | | **External Reflux** | Condensed liquid returned after leaving the condenser; sub-cooled | Most common in industry; temperature is below bubble point | | **Internal Reflux** | The liquid actually flowing down inside the top tray at column pressure | Used in advanced control strategies (e.g., internal reflux control via temperature) | | **Cold Reflux** | Reflux cooled significantly below its bubble point | Provides sub-cooling load; absorbs more heat per kg on the top tray | | **Hot Reflux** | Reflux returned at or near its bubble point | Used when condenser capacity is limited; reduces reboiler duty | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition for KTU:** *Internal Reflux ($L_i$)* is the liquid flow rate on the top tray inside the column at column operating pressure, as distinct from the *External Reflux ($L$)* measured after the reflux drum at a lower temperature. The relationship between them is governed by the **energy balance around the reflux drum and top of column**, derived in Section 3. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Reflux Is the Primary Control Handle To control top product composition $X_D$, the control engineer has two main manipulated variables: **reflux flow (L)** and **distillate flow (D)**. Reflux flow is preferred because: - It has a **fast dynamic response** (short residence time in the reflux line and accumulator). - It has **minimal interaction with the bottom composition** $X_B$ (servo control with low coupling). - A change in $L$ directly tilts the rectifying section operating line slope, immediately altering $X_D$. The **rectifying section operating line** in McCabe–Thiele analysis, which the reflux ratio directly sets, is: $$y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{X_D}{R+1}$$ > [!NOTE] > This equation is the geometric signature of reflux action. As $R$ increases, the operating line slope approaches unity (45°), pinch with the equilibrium curve vanishes, and the number of theoretical stages required for a given separation decreases. Conversely, lowering $R$ increases the required stages and can cause the column to "run dry" on separation. ## 2.2 Energy Balance Around the Reflux Drum and Top of the Column Let: - $V_T$ = Vapor flow rate leaving the top tray (kmol/hr) - $L$ = External reflux flow rate (kmol/hr) at temperature $T_R$ - $D$ = Distillate flow rate (kmol/hr) at temperature $T_D$ - $V_C$ = Vapor flow rate entering the condenser - $T_T$ = Temperature of the vapor at top tray (≈ top tray temperature) - $T_R$ = Temperature of the external reflux liquid - $T_D$ = Temperature of the distillate leaving the reflux drum - $H_v$ = Molar enthalpy of saturated vapor at $T_T$ - $H_L$ = Molar enthalpy of liquid at $T_R$ - $h_D$ = Molar enthalpy of distillate at $T_D$ The **total condenser energy balance** (steady state, negligible heat loss): $$V_C \cdot H_v = (L + D) \cdot h_D + Q_C$$ where $Q_C$ is the condenser duty. For a *total* condenser, $V_C = L + D$, and the vapor leaving the top tray equals the vapor entering the condenser (no sub-cooling in column itself): $$V_T = V_C = L + D$$ ## 2.3 Internal Reflux — The Smith Correlation The **internal reflux** $L_i$ is the liquid that actually contacts the vapor on the top tray. It is greater than $L$ when the external reflux is sub-cooled, because as the cold reflux enters the column, it partially vaporizes and mixes with the rising vapor, but the net liquid descending the tray increases. The classical **Boyd/Smith energy balance** for internal reflux: $$L_i = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda} \right]$$ where: - $C_p$ = Average molar heat capacity of reflux liquid (**kJ/kmol·K**) - $\lambda$ = Latent heat of vaporization at top tray conditions (**kJ/kmol**) - $T_T - T_R$ = Temperature difference between top tray and reflux drum (**K** or **°C**) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the cornerstone equation for **Internal Reflux Control (IRC)**, an advanced strategy. The term $C_p(T_T - T_R)/\lambda$ is called the **sub-cooling factor** or **reflux cooling factor**. It can be as large as 0.15–0.25 in highly sub-cooled systems, meaning internal reflux is 15–25% higher than external reflux. ## 2.4 Material Balance Constraints For total condenser and constant molar overflow, the **top section material balance** gives: $$V_T = L + D = L(1 + 1/R)$$ The **operating line slope** is: $$\frac{L}{V} = \frac{R}{R+1}$$ > [!NOTE] > The condition for column operation is $0 \leq L < V$, i.e., $0 \leq R < \infty$. Total reflux corresponds to $R = \infty$ (theoretically), and total takeoff (no reflux) corresponds to $R = 0$. Industrial columns typically operate at $R = 1.2$ to $R = 5.0$ times the **minimum reflux ratio $R_{min}$**. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R = L/D$ | $R$ = dimensionless; $L, D$ = kmol/hr | External reflux ratio definition | | 2 | $L_i = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda} \right]$ | $L_i, L$ = kmol/hr; $C_p$ = kJ/kmol·K; $T$ in K; $\lambda$ = kJ/kmol | Internal reflux calculation | | 3 | $V_T = L + D$ | kmol/hr | Total condenser vapor rate | | 4 | $y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{X_D}{R+1}$ | mole fractions | Rectifying operating line | | 5 | $L/V = R/(R+1)$ | dimensionless | Operating line slope | | 6 | $R_{min} = \frac{X_D - y^*_{int}}{y^*_{int} - x^*_{int}}$ | dimensionless | Underwood/Gilliland minimum reflux | | 7 | $N_{min}$ from staircase at $R_{min}$ | number of stages | Gilliland correlation input | | 8 | $\Delta Q_R = D \cdot \lambda \cdot (R_2 - R_1)/(R_2 + 1)$ | kW | Reboiler duty change with $R$ | | 9 | $\tau_{reflux} = V_{accum}/(L+D)$ | hours | Reflux loop time constant | | 10 | $K_c^{reflux} = \frac{\partial X_D}{\partial L} \bigg\vert_{D, F, Q_R}$ | (mol frac)/(kmol/hr) | Process gain of reflux loop | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** When the question asks "what happens if reflux increases?", the answer is always: *Top product becomes more pure ($X_D \uparrow$), bottom product becomes less pure ($X_B \uparrow$), reboiler duty must increase to maintain the boil-up, and the column approaches total-reflux behavior.* ## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Reflux Control In a real petrochemical complex, reflux control loops are deployed in: - **Crude Distillation Units (CDU)** — controlling naphtha/kerosene cut points. - **Vacuum Distillation Columns** — controlling lube-oil base stock quality. - **Ethylene Cracker Primary Fractionators** — controlling C2/C3 separation. - **Aromatics Extraction Plants** — controlling benzene/toluene/xylene (BTX) purity. - **Natural Gas Liquids (NGL) Recovery Trains** — controlling deethanizer/depropanizer overheads. - **Air Separation Units (ASU)** — controlling oxygen/nitrogen product purity. The economic value of tight reflux control is enormous: a 1% improvement in separation sharpness can save millions of dollars per year in energy and product giveaway costs in a large refinery. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Internal Reflux Equation (Smith/Boyd Energy Balance) ### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume Consider the top of the column including: - The top tray (Tray 1 from the top). - The vapor line from top tray to condenser. - The total condenser. - The reflux drum. - The reflux line from drum to top tray. ### Step 2 — Enumerate Streams Crossing the Control Volume - **Inlet:** Vapor $V_T$ at temperature $T_T$, enthalpy $H_v$ (per kmol). - **Outlet 1:** External reflux $L$ at temperature $T_R$, enthalpy $H_L$ (per kmol). - **Outlet 2:** Distillate $D$ at temperature $T_D$, enthalpy $h_D$ (per kmol). - **Outlet 3:** Internal reflux $L_i$ descending from top tray inside the column. - **Energy In:** $V_T \cdot H_v$ - **Energy Out:** $L \cdot H_L + D \cdot h_D + L_i \cdot h_{L_i}$ where $h_{L_i}$ is the enthalpy of the internal reflux liquid at top tray temperature $T_T$. ### Step 3 — State the Steady-State Energy Balance At steady state, the total enthalpy entering equals the total enthalpy leaving (assuming adiabatic top section, no heat loss to surroundings): $$V_T \cdot H_v = L \cdot H_L + D \cdot h_D + L_i \cdot h_{L_i}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Material Balance for Total Condenser For a total condenser, the overhead vapor rate equals the total condensed liquid rate: $$V_T = L + D$$ ### Step 5 — Apply Material Balance at the Top Tray The internal reflux descending from the top tray equals the external reflux plus any vapor condensed by the cold incoming reflux. At the top tray, the cold reflux enters and is heated from $T_R$ to $T_T$. The heat required to warm the external reflux from $T_R$ to $T_T$ comes from condensing a portion of the rising vapor. This adds to the descending liquid: $$L_i = L + \Delta L_{condensed}$$ The condensed portion $\Delta L_{condensed}$ is calculated by equating the heat needed to warm the cold reflux to the heat released by partial condensation of vapor: $$L \cdot C_p (T_T - T_R) = \Delta L_{condensed} \cdot \lambda$$ Solving for $\Delta L_{condensed}$: $$\Delta L_{condensed} = \frac{L \cdot C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda}$$ ### Step 6 — Substitute Back Combining the two contributions: $$L_i = L + \frac{L \cdot C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda}$$ Factoring out $L$: $$L_i = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda} \right]$$ This is the **Smith/Boyd Internal Reflux Equation** — the cornerstone of internal reflux control. ### Step 7 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Given:** - $L = 100$ kmol/hr - $T_T = 80^\circ C$ - $T_R = 40^\circ C$ - $C_p = 150$ kJ/kmol·K - $\lambda = 30{,}000$ kJ/kmol **Calculation of sub-cooling factor:** $$\frac{C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda} = \frac{150 \times (80 - 40)}{30{,}000} = \frac{150 \times 40}{30{,}000} = \frac{6000}{30{,}000} = 0.20$$ **Internal reflux:** $$L_i = 100 \times [1 + 0.20] = 100 \times 1.20 = 120 \text{ kmol/hr}$$ **Interpretation:** The cold external reflux at 40°C enters the column, absorbs heat from rising vapor, and the resulting internal liquid traffic on the top tray is 20% higher than the measured external reflux. The control system must compensate for this or risk composition drift. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Reflux Control Loop Steady-State Process Gain ### Step 1 — Define the Manipulated and Controlled Variables - **MV (Manipulated Variable):** External reflux flow $L$ - **CV (Controlled Variable):** Top product composition $X_D$ - **Disturbances:** Feed composition $Z_F$, Feed flow $F$, Reboiler steam $Q_R$ ### Step 2 — Linearize the Rectifying Operating Line The rectifying operating line intercept on the y-axis is $X_D/(R+1)$. For small changes around an operating point: $$d\left[\frac{X_D}{R+1}\right] = \frac{1}{R+1} dX_D - \frac{X_D}{(R+1)^2} dR$$ ### Step 3 — Express $dR$ in Terms of $dL$ Since $R = L/D$ and $D$ is held constant (in single-ended reflux control, $D$ is flow-controlled separately): $$dR = \frac{dL}{D} \quad \text{(for constant D)}$$ ### Step 4 — Combine and Solve for the Process Gain At a fixed feed and fixed boil-up, the equilibrium-stage calculation (or shortcut Underwood equation) relates $X_D$ to $R$. To first order: $$K_p = \frac{\partial X_D}{\partial L} \approx \frac{X_D}{L(R+1)} \cdot f(\alpha, N)$$ where $\alpha$ is the relative volatility and $N$ is the number of stages. The function $f(\alpha, N)$ is a positive number increasing with both $\alpha$ and $N$. For typical columns, $K_p$ is on the order of $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ (mol fraction)/(kmol/hr). ### Step 5 — Sign and Direction The process gain is **positive**: an increase in $L$ causes an increase in $X_D$. The PID controller must therefore be configured for **reverse action** (increase MV when PV is below SP, decrease MV when PV is above SP). ## 3.3 Derivation of Reflux Loop Time Constant The dominant lag in the reflux control loop is the **reflux accumulator (drum) holdup**. Let: - $V_{accum}$ = Reflux drum liquid volume (m³) - $\rho_L$ = Liquid density (kmol/m³) - $L$ = Reflux outflow (kmol/hr) - $D$ = Distillate outflow (kmol/hr) - $L_{in}$ = Condensed liquid inflow (kmol/hr) Mass balance on the drum: $$V_{accum} \cdot \rho_L \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} = L_{in} - L - D$$ Linearizing around the operating holdup $h_{op}$: $$\tau_{accum} = \frac{V_{accum} \cdot \rho_L}{L + D} = \frac{V_{accum} \cdot \rho_L}{V_T}$$ For typical column with $V_{accum} = 5$ m³, $\rho_L = 10$ kmol/m³, and $V_T = 200$ kmol/hr: $$\tau_{accum} = \frac{5 \times 10}{200} = 0.25 \text{ hr} = 15 \text{ minutes}$$ This is the open-loop time constant of the dominant first-order lag. The PI controller integral time is typically set to $T_i = 0.5 \times \tau_{accum}$ to $T_i = 2 \times \tau_{accum}$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reflux Control Loop Components | Loop Element | Symbol | Typical Value/Range | Engineering Function | |---|---|---|---| | Controlled Variable | $X_D$ | 0.90–0.99 (mole fraction) | Top product purity | | Manipulated Variable | $L$ | 50–500 kmol/hr | External reflux flow | | Sensor | QT analyzer / T probe | 0.5–5 min response | Composition/temperature measurement | | Transmitter | 4–20 mA HART | 1 sec update | Signal conditioning | | Controller | DCS PID block | $K_c$, $T_i$ tunable | Control law execution | | Final Control Element | Reflux control valve | Equal % or linear | Throttle reflux flow | | Disturbance Variables | $F, Z_F, T_F, Q_R$ | stochastic | Feed and energy inputs | | Process Gain | $K_p$ | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Open-loop sensitivity | | Process Time Constant | $\tau$ | 5–30 min | Dynamic response time | | Dead Time | $\theta$ | 1–5 min | Composition analyzer lag | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Distillation Column with Reflux — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **physical flow architecture** of a distillation column equipped with a reflux control loop. The control signal flow is shown in dashed teal lines, and the process flow in solid black lines. ```mermaid flowchart TD feed1[Feed F
Hot Liquid] --> col1[Distillation Column
Trays or Packing] reb1[Reboiler Q_R
Steam Heated] --> col1 col1 --> vap1[Overhead Vapor V_T
Top Tray] vap1 --> cond1[Total Condenser
Heat Exchanger] cond1 --> liq1[Subcooled Liquid
L + D] liq1 --> drum1[Reflux Accumulator
Drum] drum1 --> ref1[Refux Line L] drum1 --> prod1[Distillate Product D] ref1 --> col1 prod1 --> co1[Composition Analyzer
On Distillate] co1 --> tc1[Analyzer Transmitter
4-20 mA] tc1 --> pic1[DCS PID Controller
Refux Loop] pic1 --> vc1[Refux Control Valve
FCV on Refux Line] vc1 --> ref1 reb1 -. Steam Duty .-> pic1 ``` ## 4.2 Reflux Control Loop — Instrumentation Signal Flow This Mermaid block shows the **classical feedback control loop** for reflux control, with the composition analyzer as the primary sensor and the reflux control valve as the final control element. ```mermaid flowchart LR sp1[Set Point X_D_SP
From Operator] --> err1[Summing Junction] pv1[Process Variable X_D
From Analyzer] --> err1 err1 --> pid1[PID Controller
Reverse Action] pid1 --> op1[Controller Output
4-20 mA] op1 --> fcv1[Refux Control Valve
FCV] fcv1 --> proc1[Column Process
Refux Flow L] proc1 --> pv1 dist1[Disturbances
Feed F, Z_F, Q_R] -.-> proc1 ``` ## 4.3 Internal Reflux Control (Advanced) — Compensation Block This block diagram illustrates how the **internal reflux temperature controller** compensates for changes in the top tray temperature to maintain a constant *internal* reflux flow, which directly correlates with composition control quality. ```mermaid flowchart TD ttray[Top Tray Temp T_T
Sensor] --> comp1[Smith/Boyd
Compensator] tref[Refux Drum Temp T_R
Sensor] --> comp1 cpc[Properties Block
C_p and Lambda] --> comp1 comp1 --> calc1[Calculated Internal
Refux L_i] calc1 --> pic2[PIC Internal Refux
Controller] pic2 --> fcv2[Refux Control Valve
FCV] fcv2 --> proc2[Column Refux Line] proc2 --> ttray proc2 --> tref ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note:** The Smith/Boyd compensator block computes $L_i$ in real time from $T_T$, $T_R$, $C_p$, and $\lambda$. When the top tray gets hotter (e.g., due to a feed composition upset making the column run hot), the compensator automatically increases the external reflux set point to keep $L_i$ constant, preventing composition drift. ## 4.4 Composition vs. Temperature vs. Flow — Cascade Reflux Control In high-purity columns, single-loop composition control is too slow. Industry standard is **cascaded temperature-composition control** where the master loop is composition and the slave loop is reflux flow. ```mermaid flowchart TD comp2[Top Product X_D
Analyzer] --> master1[Master PID
Composition] master1 --> sp2[Slave SP
Refux Flow L] sp2 --> slave1[Slave PID
Flow Controller] slave1 --> fcv3[Refux Valve FCV] fcv3 --> col3[Column Top Section] col3 --> flow1[Refux Flow
Measurement FT] flow1 --> slave1 col3 --> comp2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define external reflux and internal reflux. Why is the internal reflux different from the external reflux in a distillation column?** **Model Answer:** **External Reflux (L):** It is the liquid stream condensed in the overhead condenser, collected in the reflux drum, and pumped back to the top tray of the distillation column. It is measured after the condenser at a temperature typically below the bubble point of the distillate (sub-cooled). Its flow rate is the primary manipulated variable for top-product composition control. **Internal Reflux ($L_i$):** It is the liquid flow rate actually descending from the top tray *inside* the column at column operating pressure and the top tray temperature. Because the external reflux is sub-cooled, it absorbs sensible heat from the rising vapor as it enters the column, causing additional vapor to condense. The internal reflux is therefore greater than the external reflux by the sub-cooling factor. **Quantitative relationship:** $$L_i = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda} \right]$$ > [!TIP] > **Valuation Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if the student mentions (i) the definition of both, (ii) the temperature difference, and (iii) the Smith/Boyd formula. Many students write only the definitions and lose 1 mark. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Explain the role of reflux ratio on the separation efficiency of a distillation column. State the effect of increasing reflux on top product purity.** **Model Answer:** The reflux ratio $R = L/D$ is the principal operating variable controlling the separation sharpness in a distillation column. The rectifying section operating line equation $y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{X_D}{R+1}$ shows that the slope of the operating line is $R/(R+1)$, which increases monotonically with $R$. **Effect of increasing reflux ratio:** 1. The operating line rotates upward, decreasing the number of theoretical stages required to achieve a specified separation (Gilliland correlation). 2. At a fixed number of stages, the **top product purity $X_D$ increases** and approaches the limit of equilibrium purity. 3. The **bottom product purity $X_B$ decreases** (heavies leak into the bottoms) unless reboiler duty is simultaneously increased. 4. The **reboiler heat duty $Q_R$ must increase** because more vapor must be boiled up to support the higher liquid traffic. 5. There is an economic optimum at $R \approx 1.2$ to $2.0 \times R_{min}$. **Operating limits:** Total reflux ($R = \infty$, $D = 0$) gives the best separation theoretically but produces no product. Zero reflux ($R = 0$) gives no separation. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **composition control loop using reflux flow as the manipulated variable** in a distillation column. Identify the controlled variable, manipulated variable, disturbances, sensor, controller, and final control element. **(b) [7 Marks]** A distillation column is operating with the following data: - External reflux flow $L = 150$ kmol/hr at $T_R = 45^\circ C$ - Top tray temperature $T_T = 95^\circ C$ - Average heat capacity of liquid $C_p = 160$ kJ/kmol·K - Latent heat of vaporization $\lambda = 28{,}000$ kJ/kmol - Distillate flow $D = 75$ kmol/hr **Calculate:** 1. The external reflux ratio $R$. 2. The internal reflux flow $L_i$. 3. The internal reflux ratio $R_i = L_i/D$. 4. The percent increase in reflux traffic inside the column compared to the external measurement. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram and Explanation [7 Marks]:** The composition control loop using reflux flow consists of the following elements: - **Controlled Variable (PV):** Top product composition $X_D$ (measured by an online gas chromatograph or NIR analyzer in the distillate line). - **Set Point (SP):** Desired $X_D$ value, set by the operator or computed by an optimizer. - **Sensor & Transmitter:** Composition analyzer with sampling system, housed in an analyzer shed; output 4–20 mA HART signal. - **Controller:** DCS PID block configured for **reverse action** (since process gain $K_p = \partial X_D/\partial L$ is positive). - **Final Control Element:** Reflux control valve (typically an equal-percentage globe valve with high turn-down ratio) installed on the reflux line leaving the reflux drum. - **Manipulated Variable (MV):** External reflux flow $L$. - **Disturbance Variables:** Feed flow $F$, feed composition $Z_F$, feed temperature $T_F$, reboiler steam pressure, condenser cooling water temperature, ambient temperature (affects reflux sub-cooling). **Loop Action:** If $X_D$ falls below SP (off-spec product), the controller increases the controller output, which opens the reflux valve further, increasing $L$. The increased reflux tilts the operating line, raising $X_D$ back to SP. The reverse occurs for an $X_D$ above SP. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part a):** Many students draw the loop but forget to specify **reverse action** of the controller. Since the process gain is *positive* (more reflux → more pure distillate), the controller *must* be in reverse action. Forgetting this loses 1 mark. Also, do not write "feedback control" without specifying "single-loop feedback with reverse-acting PID." --- **(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:** **Given:** - $L = 150$ kmol/hr - $D = 75$ kmol/hr - $T_R = 45^\circ C$ - $T_T = 95^\circ C$ - $C_p = 160$ kJ/kmol·K - $\lambda = 28{,}000$ kJ/kmol **Step 1 — External Reflux Ratio:** $$R = \frac{L}{D} = \frac{150}{75} = 2.0$$ **Step 2 — Sub-cooling Factor:** $$\frac{C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda} = \frac{160 \times (95 - 45)}{28{,}000} = \frac{160 \times 50}{28{,}000} = \frac{8000}{28{,}000} = 0.2857$$ **Step 3 — Internal Reflux Flow:** $$L_i = L \left[ 1 + 0.2857 \right] = 150 \times 1.2857 = 192.86 \text{ kmol/hr}$$ **Step 4 — Internal Reflux Ratio:** $$R_i = \frac{L_i}{D} = \frac{192.86}{75} = 2.571$$ **Step 5 — Percent Increase:** $$\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{L_i - L}{L} \times 100 = \frac{192.86 - 150}{150} \times 100 = \frac{42.86}{150} \times 100 = 28.57\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** The most common error is forgetting to apply the temperature difference as $(T_T - T_R)$ in the correct order. Since $T_T > T_R$ always (the top tray is hotter than the sub-cooled reflux), the sub-cooling factor is **positive**, and $L_i > L$. Reversing the sign gives a physically impossible negative internal reflux — full 7 marks will be deducted. Also, students often forget to convert the temperature difference correctly; the answer is the same in K or °C since it is a *difference*, but be careful. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Internal Reflux Control (IRC)** with the help of a block diagram. How does it differ from conventional external reflux control, and under what conditions is IRC preferred? **(b) [7 Marks]** A crude distillation column has the following data: - Distillate (naphtha) flow $D = 100$ kmol/hr - Reflux flow $L = 180$ kmol/hr - Condenser duty $Q_C = 4500$ kW - Latent heat of vaporization $\lambda = 35{,}000$ kJ/kmol - Molar overhead vapor rate $V_T = 280$ kmol/hr **Calculate:** 1. The reflux ratio $R$ and the operating line slope $L/V$. 2. The condenser duty per kmol of overhead vapor. 3. The percent change in $R$ if the operator increases $L$ to 220 kmol/hr while holding $D$ constant. 4. Comment on the impact on reboiler duty $Q_R$. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Internal Reflux Control [7 Marks]:** Internal Reflux Control (IRC) is an advanced control strategy in which the **calculated internal reflux flow $L_i$** is used as the controlled variable, not the external reflux $L$. The calculation is done in real time using the **Smith/Boyd compensator equation**: $$L_i = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda} \right]$$ **Block Diagram Explanation:** 1. Temperature sensor TT-1 measures the top tray temperature $T_T$. 2. Temperature sensor TT-2 measures the reflux drum temperature $T_R$. 3. A DCS computation block reads both temperatures, the configured $C_p$ and $\lambda$ values, and the measured external reflux $L$ (from FT on reflux line). 4. The block computes $L_i$ using the formula. 5. The computed $L_i$ is the PV of a PID controller; its set point is operator-entered. 6. The controller output drives the reflux control valve. **Differences from External Reflux Control:** | Feature | External Reflux Control | Internal Reflux Control | |---|---|---| | Controlled variable | $L$ (measured directly) | $L_i$ (calculated) | | Compensation for sub-cooling | None | Automatic via Smith/Boyd | | Sensitivity to ambient $T_R$ changes | High (drift in $L_i$) | Low (compensated) | | Implementation complexity | Simple | Moderate (requires 2 temperature sensors) | | Performance during feed upsets | Slower response | Faster, more stable | **Preferred Conditions for IRC:** - High-purity columns where composition control is critical. - Columns exposed to large ambient temperature swings (e.g., winter-summer variation changes $T_R$). - Columns with significant sub-cooling in the reflux drum. - Energy-integrated columns where reflux temperature varies with preheat train operation. --- **(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:** **Given:** - $D = 100$ kmol/hr - $L = 180$ kmol/hr - $Q_C = 4500$ kW - $\lambda = 35{,}000$ kJ/kmol - $V_T = 280$ kmol/hr **Step 1 — Reflux Ratio and Operating Line Slope:** $$R = \frac{L}{D} = \frac{180}{100} = 1.8$$ $$\frac{L}{V} = \frac{R}{R+1} = \frac{1.8}{2.8} = 0.6429$$ **Step 2 — Condenser Duty per kmol of Overhead Vapor:** $$q_C = \frac{Q_C}{V_T} = \frac{4500}{280} = 16.07 \text{ kW·hr/kmol} = 16.07 \text{ kJ/s per kmol/hr}$$ Or equivalently, in kJ/kmol (multiplying by 3600): $$q_C = 16.07 \times 3600 = 57{,}857 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$ > **Check:** Since $V_T \cdot \lambda = 280 \times 35{,}000 = 9{,}800{,}000$ kJ/hr = 9,800,000/3600 = 2722 kW. But the given $Q_C$ is 4500 kW. The difference is because the condenser removes **both** latent heat and the sensible heat of sub-cooling. The latent duty alone is 2722 kW; the remaining 1778 kW is sensible-heat removal. This is consistent with a sub-cooled reflux stream. **Step 3 — New Reflux Ratio when L = 220 kmol/hr:** $$R_{new} = \frac{L_{new}}{D} = \frac{220}{100} = 2.2$$ $$\Delta R = R_{new} - R_{old} = 2.2 - 1.8 = 0.4$$ $$\% \text{ Change in } R = \frac{0.4}{1.8} \times 100 = 22.22\%$$ **Step 4 — Impact on Reboiler Duty:** The reboiler duty must increase to support the higher internal liquid traffic. Approximating the change using the energy balance (constant distillate rate): $$V_{T,new} = L_{new} + D = 220 + 100 = 320 \text{ kmol/hr}$$ $$\Delta V_T = 320 - 280 = 40 \text{ kmol/hr}$$ $$Q_{R,new} \approx Q_{R,old} + \Delta V_T \cdot \lambda = Q_{R,old} + 40 \times 35{,}000 = Q_{R,old} + 1{,}400{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$ Converting to kW: $$\Delta Q_R = \frac{1{,}400{,}000}{3600} = 388.9 \text{ kW} \approx 389 \text{ kW increase}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** For Part (b), Step 2, students often confuse $Q_C$ (condenser duty in kW) with the molar latent heat term $V_T \cdot \lambda$ (in kJ/hr). Remember: $1$ kW $= 1$ kJ/s $= 3600$ kJ/hr. Always state the units explicitly to earn full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Reflux Control** - ✅ **Reflux ratio** $R = L/D$ is dimensionless and is the **most important operating variable** for top-product composition control. - ✅ **Total reflux** ($R = \infty$) is a startup condition; **zero reflux** ($R = 0$) is a flooded or no-separation condition. - ✅ **External reflux** $L$ is measured after the condenser; **internal reflux** $L_i$ is the liquid on the top tray inside the column. - ✅ **Smith/Boyd Equation:** $L_i = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p (T_T - T_R)}{\lambda} \right]$ — memorize this verbatim for KTU exams. - ✅ **Sub-cooling factor** $C_p(T_T - T_R)/\lambda$ is typically 0.10 to 0.30 in industrial columns. - ✅ **Increasing $R$** → higher $X_D$, lower $X_B$ (if $Q_R$ fixed), higher $Q_R$ demand, fewer stages needed, more operating cost. - ✅ **PID controller for reflux must be REVERSE ACTING** because process gain $K_p = \partial X_D/\partial L$ is positive. - ✅ **Cascade control** (composition master + flow slave) is the industry standard for high-purity columns. - ✅ **Internal Reflux Control (IRC)** is preferred for columns with large sub-cooling or ambient temperature swings. - ✅ **Dominant time constant** of the reflux loop is the reflux accumulator holdup time $\tau = V_{accum} \cdot \rho_L / V_T$, typically 5–30 minutes. - ✅ **Valve type:** Reflux control valves are almost always **equal-percentage** trim with characterized positioners for high turn-down. - ✅ **Analyzer lag:** Online composition analyzers (GC/NIR) have 1–5 minute dead time; cascade or inferential temperature control is often used to overcome this. - ✅ **Material balance check:** $V_T = L + D$ for a total condenser; this is the single most-tested equation on reflux control. - ✅ **Energy balance check:** $Q_C = V_T \cdot \lambda + \text{sensible sub-cooling load}$ — the condenser duty has two components. - ✅ **Operating range:** Industrial columns operate at $R = (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0) \times R_{min}$ for economic optimum. - ✅ **Feedforward augmentation:** Distillation reflux loops benefit greatly from feedforward action on $F$ and $Z_F$ to handle disturbances before they propagate.

topics.title: Reboiler Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reboiler Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **reboiler** is a heat exchanger used in distillation columns to supply the necessary thermal energy (vapor boil-up) to the liquid descending from the bottom trays, thereby generating the rising vapor stream required to maintain the vapor-liquid equilibrium essential for mass transfer and component separation.

**Reboiler Control**, as defined under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, refers to the systematic regulation of reboiler duty (heat input rate) and the corresponding vapor boil-up rate to maintain the desired bottoms product composition, column temperature profile, and overall material/energy balance. The manipulated variable is typically the heating medium flow rate (steam, hot oil, or fired-fuel flow), while the controlled variables are most commonly the bottoms composition, column bottom temperature, or differential temperature across the column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Reboiler control is studied under the broader umbrella of **Distillation Column Control** and is treated as a vital sub-system because it directly influences the bottom product purity, the column's internal reflux ratio, and the hydraulic stability of the downcomers and sump. KTU examiners frequently (≈ 35% probability) test reboiler control in conjunction with overhead condenser control and overall column inventory control.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a pressure cooker on your kitchen stove. The flame underneath the cooker (analogous to the reboiler's heat source) must be carefully adjusted to maintain a steady boil. If the flame is too high, the contents evaporate excessively, and the liquid level drops too low (analogous to a dry sump and loss of bottom product). If the flame is too low, the boil stops, and the separation of flavors (analogous to separation of components) is incomplete. A chef constantly watches the steam release, the pressure gauge, and the liquid level to decide how to adjust the flame — this is precisely what a reboiler control loop does in a petrochemical distillation column.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following key engineering metrics are universally tracked in reboiler control:

- **Heat Duty (Q):** Measured in **kcal/hr, kW, or MMBtu/hr**.
- **Vapor Boil-up Rate (V):** Measured in **kg/hr or kmol/hr**.
- **Steam Flow Rate:** Measured in **kg/hr** for steam-heated reboilers.
- **Reboiler Steam Pressure:** Measured in **bar(g) or psig**.
- **Heat Transfer Coefficient (U):** Measured in **W/m²·K** or **BTU/hr·ft²·°F**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Constant Reminder:** The latent heat of vaporization of water at atmospheric pressure is **2,260 kJ/kg (≈ 540 kcal/kg)**, and a typical refinery steam-heated reboiler operates at **3 bar(g) to 15 bar(g)** saturated steam pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reboiler Operational Principles

A reboiler performs three critical functions within a distillation column:

1. **Generates Vapor Boil-up:** Converts a portion of the descending liquid bottoms into vapor that returns to the column.
2. **Removes Heavy Key Component:** Drives the heavy (less volatile) component out of the bottom product.
3. **Maintains Hydraulic Seal:** Provides the liquid head required for proper downcomer operation.

The control strategy is built around adjusting the **reboiler duty (Qᵣ)** to control one of three primary controlled variables:

- **Bottoms Composition (xB):** The most direct indicator of separation quality.
- **Column Bottom Temperature (T_bottom):** An indirect but reliable proxy for composition.
- **Differential Pressure (ΔP) or Differential Temperature (ΔT) Across Column:** A more sensitive indicator of composition than absolute temperature.

### 2.2 Types of Reboilers (and Their Control Implications)

| Reboiler Type | Heat Source | Residence Time | Control Response | Typical Application |
|---------------|-------------|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Kettle Reboiler** | Steam / Hot Oil | High | Slow (large thermal lag) | Vacuum columns, fouling services |
| **Thermosiphon Reboiler** | Steam | Low | Fast (natural circulation) | Most refinery columns |
| **Forced Circulation Reboiler** | Steam | Variable | Fast (pump-driven) | High-viscosity bottoms, fouling |

### 2.3 Standard Reboiler Control Schemes

#### Scheme 1: Simple Steam-Flow Control (Open-Loop Duty Control)
- **MV:** Steam flow to reboiler.
- **CV:** Bottoms temperature (or composition).
- **Drawback:** Open-loop; disturbances in steam pressure or feed composition are not rejected.

#### Scheme 2: Bottoms Temperature → Steam Flow Cascade
- **Primary (Master):** T_bottom controller → setpoint to steam flow.
- **Secondary (Slave):** Steam flow controller.
- **Advantage:** Compensates for steam pressure disturbances.

#### Scheme 3: Differential Temperature Control (ΔT Control)
- **MV:** Steam flow.
- **CV:** ΔT = T_top – T_bottom (or similar).
- **Advantage:** Cancels pressure disturbances on absolute temperature measurement.

#### Scheme 4: Valve Position Control (VPC) on Reboiler Steam
- **MV:** Steam flow (reboiler) at intermediate level.
- **CV:** Reflux valve position held constant by reboiler action.
- **Application:** Energy optimization in dual-product columns.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Overall heat transfer equation | Q in W, U in W/m²·K, A in m², ΔT in K |
| 2 | $Q = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | Heat duty from steam mass flow | mₛ in kg/s, λₛ in kJ/kg |
| 3 | $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K or °C |
| 4 | $\dfrac{Q}{F} = \lambda \cdot \dfrac{D}{F} = \lambda \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{B}{F}\right)$ | Fractional heat duty vs distillate-to-feed ratio | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_{min} \le R \le R_{max}$ | Reflux ratio operating range | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $V = L + D$ | Vapor boil-up balance | kmol/hr |
| 7 | $V = L_0 + qF$ | Vapor boil-up from feed condition | kmol/hr |
| 8 | $K_c = T_u / (L_u \cdot K_p)$ | Ziegler-Nichols ultimate-cycle tuning (PI) | Kc in %/%, Tu, Lu in minutes |
| 9 | $\tau_p = \dfrac{M c_p}{U A}$ | Process time constant (thermal) | Seconds |
| 10 | $C_v = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{\sqrt{\Delta P_{steam}}}$ | Steam valve sizing relation | Cv in US GPM units |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries:** Crude atmospheric and vacuum distillation units use thermosiphon reboilers controlled by bottom temperature cascades.
- **Petrochemical Plants:** Ethylene fractionators, propylene/propane splitters, and butadiene extractors rely on reboiler control for tight product specifications.
- **LNG Plants:** Cryogenic demethanizers use kettle reboilers with very tight ΔT control to minimize methane losses in the bottoms.
- **Energy Optimization:** Valve Position Control (VPC) on reboilers can save **3–8% of column energy consumption** by shifting the energy load to whichever utility (reboiler or condenser) is more cost-effective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Practice:** In modern APC (Advanced Process Control) implementations, reboiler duty is one of the most common **manipulated variables (MVs)** in Model Predictive Control (MPC) of distillation columns. The reboiler steam valve is typically a **fast MV** (response time 5–15 seconds) compared to reflux (response time 30–60 seconds), making it the preferred handle for high-frequency composition disturbances.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reboiler Heat Duty from Steam Mass Flow

The fundamental heat balance for a steam-heated reboiler is derived by equating the heat released by condensing steam to the heat absorbed by the boiling process fluid.

**Starting Point — Steam-Side Energy Balance:**

$$Q_{released} = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$$

where:
- $Q_{released}$ = heat released by condensing steam, in kW
- $m_s$ = mass flow rate of steam, in kg/s
- $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of vaporization of steam at operating pressure, in kJ/kg

**Process-Side Energy Balance (Sensible + Latent Heat):**

$$Q_{absorbed} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient, in kW/m²·K
- $A$ = heat transfer area, in m²
- $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ = log-mean temperature difference, in K

**Equating Both Sides (Steady-State Energy Conservation):**

$$m_s \cdot \lambda_s = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

**Solving for Steam Mass Flow Rate (Manipulated Variable):**

$$m_s = \dfrac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}{\lambda_s}$$

**Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation — Worked Example:**

Given:
- $U = 850 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·K} = 0.850 \;\text{kW/m}^2\text{·K}$
- $A = 50 \;\text{m}^2$
- Steam temperature $T_s = 180 \;\text{°C}$ (saturated, ~10 bar)
- Process inlet temperature $T_{1} = 130 \;\text{°C}$
- Process outlet temperature $T_{2} = 145 \;\text{°C}$
- $\lambda_s = 2,015 \;\text{kJ/kg}$ (latent heat at 10 bar saturated steam)

**Step 1 — Compute Temperature Differences:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_s - T_1 = 180 - 130 = 50 \;\text{°C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_s - T_2 = 180 - 145 = 35 \;\text{°C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute LMTD:**

$$LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{50 - 35}{\ln(50 / 35)}$$

$$LMTD = \dfrac{15}{\ln(1.4286)} = \dfrac{15}{0.3567} = 42.06 \;\text{°C}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Heat Duty:**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD = 0.850 \times 50 \times 42.06$$

$$Q = 1,787.6 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Steam Mass Flow Rate:**

$$m_s = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \dfrac{1,787.6}{2,015} = 0.887 \;\text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{m_s = 0.887 \;\text{kg/s} = 3,193 \;\text{kg/hr}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Vapor Boil-Up from Overall Mass and Energy Balance

For a distillation column operating at total condenser with feed condition $q$:

**Vapor-Liquid Balance at the Top:**

$$V = L + D$$

**Vapor-Liquid Balance at the Feed Plate:**

$$V = L_0 + qF$$

**Subtracting the Two Equations:**

$$L_0 + qF = L + D$$

**Using the Reflux Ratio Definition $R = L/D$:**

$$L = R \cdot D$$

$$L_0 = R \cdot D - (1 - q)F$$

Substituting back:

$$V = R \cdot D - (1 - q)F + qF$$

$$V = R \cdot D + F(q - 1 + 1)$$

$$\boxed{V = R \cdot D + qF}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The vapor boil-up $V$ leaving the reboiler is **the sum of the reflux flow and the feed vapor fraction contribution**. Since reboiler duty $Q \propto V$ (because $Q = V \cdot \lambda_{mix}$), controlling reboiler duty is functionally equivalent to controlling vapor boil-up, and indirectly, the reflux ratio $R$ when $D$ is held constant.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Reboiler Process Time Constant (Dynamic Model)

The first-order dynamic response of a kettle reboiler is given by:

$$\tau_p = \dfrac{M \cdot c_p}{U \cdot A}$$

where:
- $M$ = mass of liquid holdup in the kettle, in kg
- $c_p$ = specific heat of process fluid, in kJ/kg·K
- $U \cdot A$ = overall heat transfer conductance, in kW/K
- $\tau_p$ = process time constant, in seconds

**Numerical Example:**

Given:
- $M = 5,000 \;\text{kg}$
- $c_p = 2.5 \;\text{kJ/kg·K}$
- $U \cdot A = 350 \;\text{kW/K} = 350 \;\text{kJ/s·K}$

**Substitution:**

$$\tau_p = \dfrac{5,000 \times 2.5}{350} = \dfrac{12,500}{350} = 35.7 \;\text{seconds}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_p \approx 36 \;\text{seconds}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Implication:** A reboiler with $\tau_p$ in the 30–90 second range is considered **moderately fast**. This justifies cascade control — the inner steam flow loop should be tuned at least **3× faster** (i.e., $\tau_{steam} \approx 8$–12 seconds) for proper cascade decoupling.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Reboiler Control Schemes

| Criterion | Simple T → Steam | Cascade T/F | ΔT Control | Valve Position Control |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------|-------------|------------------------|
| Speed of response | Slow | Fast | Fast | Medium |
| Disturbance rejection | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| Steam pressure compensation | None | Yes (slave) | Implicit | Yes |
| Tuning complexity | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| Energy efficiency | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| KTU exam frequency | High | Very High | Medium | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Bottoms Temperature → Steam Flow Cascade Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSP[Setpoint: T_bottom desired] --> nodeA[Controller TC: Master PI]
    nodeA --> nodeSet[Remote Setpoint: F_steam desired]
    nodeSet --> nodeB[Controller FC: Slave PI]
    nodeB --> nodeValve[Steam Control Valve MV]
    nodeValve --> nodeReboil[Reboiler Process Unit]
    nodeReboil --> nodeFmeas[Steam Flow Transmitter FT]
    nodeFmeas --> nodeB
    nodeReboil --> nodeTmeas[Bottom Temperature Transmitter TT]
    nodeTmeas --> nodeA
    nodeReboil -.-> nodeFeed[Distillate D and Bottoms B exits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Loop:** The **outer (master) loop** compares the measured bottom temperature (TT) with the desired setpoint and computes a steam flow setpoint. The **inner (slave) loop** adjusts the steam valve to maintain that flow setpoint, rejecting steam pressure disturbances on a **time scale ~10× faster** than the master loop.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — ΔT Control Across Column

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeTop[Top Tray Temperature T_top] --> nodeDiff[Differentiator]
    nodeBot[Bottom Tray Temperature T_bot] --> nodeDiff
    nodeDiff --> nodeDeltaT[Delta T = T_top - T_bot]
    nodeDeltaT --> nodeCtrl[Delta T Controller]
    nodeCtrl --> nodeSteam[Steam to Reboiler]
    nodeSteam --> nodeReboil[Reboiler Duty Q]
    nodeReboil --> nodeCol[Column Internal Profile]
    nodeCol --> nodeTop
    nodeCol --> nodeBot
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Valve Position Control (VPC) on Reboiler

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeGoal[Objective: Minimize Operating Cost] --> nodeSelect{Select Cheaper Energy Source}
    nodeSelect --> nodeReflux[Reflux as Energy Sink]
    nodeSelect --> nodeReboil[Reboiler as Energy Sink]
    nodeReflux --> nodeValveR[Reflux Valve Position PV_R]
    nodeReboil --> nodeValveS[Steam Valve Position PV_S]
    nodeValveR --> nodeVPC[Valve Position Controller]
    nodeValveS --> nodeVPC
    nodeVPC --> nodeSteam2[Reboiler Steam Manipulated]
    nodeVPC --> nodeReflux2[Reflux Manipulated]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In VPC, the reboiler steam valve is operated at an **intermediate position (typically 40–70% open)** by a supervisory controller. The remaining 20–30% valve authority is reserved to handle dynamic disturbances, ensuring the loop never saturates. This is a common KTU-level question for understanding **multi-variable control interaction** in distillation.

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Reboiler Control Sub-system

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEASURE[Measurement Layer]
        m1[TT - Bottom Temperature]
        m2[FT - Steam Flow]
        m3[PT - Steam Pressure]
        m4[LT - Kettle Level]
        m5[AT - Bottoms Composition]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Control Layer]
        c1[TC - Master PI]
        c2[FC - Slave PI]
        c3[LIC - Level Controller]
        c4[AIC - Analyzer Controller]
    end

    subgraph FINAL[Final Control Element]
        f1[Steam Control Valve PCV-101]
        f2[Bottoms Pump Speed]
    end

    subgraph EQUIP[Process Equipment]
        e1[Kettle / Thermosiphon Reboiler]
        e2[Distillation Column]
    end

    m1 --> c1
    c1 --> c2
    m2 --> c2
    c2 --> f1
    f1 --> e1
    e1 --> e2
    m4 --> c3
    c3 --> f2
    m5 --> c4
    c4 --> c1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualizing APC Integration:** Note how the **Analyzer Controller (AIC)** is shown with a dotted (or independent) path to the master TC — this represents the slow but accurate composition feedback that overrides or trims the temperature-based cascade. This is the architecture typically implemented in **refinery-grade Distillation APC systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
Define a reboiler. List any two types of reboilers commonly used in petrochemical industries.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **reboiler** is a heat exchanger that supplies the necessary heat to the bottom of a distillation column to vaporize a portion of the liquid descending from the bottom tray, thereby providing the vapor boil-up required for mass transfer and component separation. **(1.5 marks)**

Two common types of reboilers used in petrochemical industries are:
1. **Kettle Reboiler:** A once-through shell-and-tube exchanger with a large vapor space; suitable for fouling and high-vacuum services. **(0.75 marks)**
2. **Thermosiphon Reboiler:** A natural-circulation reboiler where the density difference between the boiling two-phase mixture in the tubes and the single-phase liquid in the downcomer drives circulation; offers faster thermal response. **(0.75 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
State any three objectives of reboiler control in a distillation column.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Maintain Bottoms Product Purity:** By regulating heat input, the reboiler controls the heavy-key component concentration in the bottoms stream. **(1 mark)**
2. **Stabilize the Internal Reflux Ratio:** Vapor boil-up from the reboiler directly determines the liquid traffic on the trays; stable boil-up ensures stable tray hydraulics. **(1 mark)**
3. **Reject Steam Pressure Disturbances:** Through cascade and feedforward schemes, the reboiler control loop compensates for upstream steam header pressure variations, ensuring column stability. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note to Students:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 3 questions carry an **internal choice** — students answer **ONE** of the two sub-questions (a) or (b) at 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat P&ID sketch, explain the **bottoms temperature → steam flow cascade control** scheme for a kettle reboiler in a distillation column. Why is cascade preferred over a single-loop control? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steam-heated kettle reboiler has $U = 850\;\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$, heat transfer area $A = 60\;\text{m}^2$, saturated steam at $T_s = 185 \;\text{°C}$, process inlet $T_1 = 125\;\text{°C}$, and process outlet $T_2 = 142\;\text{°C}$. The latent heat of steam is $\lambda_s = 2,013\;\text{kJ/kg}$. Calculate:
1. The LMTD of the reboiler.
2. The total heat duty in kW.
3. The required steam mass flow rate in kg/hr. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**P&ID Sketch (Narrative Description):**

The cascade control scheme consists of two control loops:
- **Master (Outer) Loop:** A temperature transmitter (TT) senses the bottom tray temperature and sends a 4–20 mA signal to the **Temperature Controller (TIC)**. The TIC compares this PV with the desired setpoint (SP) and outputs a remote setpoint to the slave flow controller.
- **Slave (Inner) Loop:** A flow transmitter (FT) on the steam line measures the reboiler steam flow and sends a signal to the **Flow Controller (FIC)**. The FIC manipulates the **steam control valve (PCV / TCV)** on the steam header to the reboiler.

**Why Cascade is Preferred:**

1. **Steam Pressure Disturbance Rejection:** If the upstream steam header pressure drops, the FIC immediately compensates by opening the steam valve further, restoring the setpoint steam flow within seconds. A single-loop T → Valve scheme would only react when the temperature actually drifts. **(2 marks)**
2. **Non-linearity Compensation:** The steam valve exhibits non-linear characteristics ($Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P}$). The inner flow loop linearizes this, making the outer temperature loop easier to tune. **(1 mark)**
3. **Faster Inner Loop:** The flow loop has a typical time constant of 5–10 seconds, much faster than the temperature loop (30–90 seconds), enabling the cascade to track setpoint changes before the temperature deviates significantly. **(1 mark)**

**Schematic (P&ID):**

```
       SP        +-----+
        \        |     |       Remote SP
         \       | TIC |-----------►+
          +------|     |            |
                 +-----+            |
                    |               |
                    | PV (4-20mA)   | CV (4-20mA)
                    ▼               |
              +-----------+    +----------+   Air   +--------+
              |  TT (PT-  |    |   FIC    |--------►| Steam  |
              |  100)     |    |          |         | Valve  |
              +-----------+    +----------+         +--------+
                    ▲               ▲                    |
                    |               | PV                 |
                    |               |                    ▼
              Bottom Tray      +---------+         +-----------+
              Temperature      |  FT     |         | REBOILER  |
                              +---------+         +-----------+
```

**(Model sketch using any P&ID convention — 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for explanation as above)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $U = 850 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·K} = 0.850 \;\text{kW/m}^2\text{·K}$
- $A = 60 \;\text{m}^2$
- $T_s = 185 \;\text{°C}$
- $T_1 = 125 \;\text{°C}$
- $T_2 = 142 \;\text{°C}$
- $\lambda_s = 2{,}013 \;\text{kJ/kg}$

**Step 1 — Compute Temperature Differences:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_s - T_1 = 185 - 125 = 60 \;\text{°C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_s - T_2 = 185 - 142 = 43 \;\text{°C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute LMTD:**

$$LMTD = \dfrac{60 - 43}{\ln(60/43)} = \dfrac{17}{\ln(1.3953)} = \dfrac{17}{0.3329}$$

$$\boxed{LMTD = 51.07 \;\text{°C}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Heat Duty:**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD = 0.850 \times 60 \times 51.07$$

$$Q = 2,604.6 \;\text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 2{,}605 \;\text{kW}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Steam Mass Flow Rate:**

$$m_s = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \dfrac{2{,}604.6}{2{,}013} = 1.294 \;\text{kg/s}$$

$$m_s = 1.294 \times 3{,}600 = 4{,}658 \;\text{kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{m_s \approx 4{,}658 \;\text{kg/hr}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Valve Position Control (VPC) scheme** for a distillation column reboiler. How does VPC contribute to energy optimization in dual-product columns? **(7 marks)**
