**(b)** A refinery depropanizer column operates with reflux ratio $R = 2.5$ and feed rate $F = 100 \;\text{kmol/hr}$. The feed enters as a saturated liquid ($q = 1$). If the distillate rate is $D = 38.6 \;\text{kmol/hr}$, calculate:
1. The bottoms flow rate $B$.
2. The liquid reflux flow rate $L$.
3. The vapor boil-up rate $V$ leaving the reboiler. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** Valve Position Control (VPC) is a **multi-variable, energy-optimizing control strategy** in which the reboiler steam valve is held at a fixed intermediate position (typically 40–70% open) by a supervisory controller, while the reflux valve is dynamically manipulated to maintain column separation specifications. Alternatively, the reflux valve can be held fixed and the reboiler manipulated — the choice is dictated by the relative cost of steam versus reflux cooling.

**Mechanism of VPC (Step-by-step):**

1. The supervisory VPC controller monitors the **reboiler steam valve position** (or the reflux valve position) and compares it with the desired setpoint (e.g., 60% open). **(1 mark)**
2. When the steam valve drifts above 65% (indicating excess reboiler duty), the VPC **increases the reflux flow setpoint** to push the column toward more reflux, which in turn requires less vapor boil-up, and the steam valve backs off toward 60%. **(2 marks)**
3. When the steam valve drifts below 55% (indicating insufficient reboiler duty), the VPC **decreases the reflux setpoint**, allowing the column to operate with lower reflux and hence higher boil-up relative to reflux, bringing the valve back to 60%. **(2 marks)**

**Energy Optimization Contribution:**

- Steam is typically the **most expensive utility** in a refinery. By maintaining the reboiler valve at the lowest possible opening that still meets separation specs, VPC minimizes steam consumption. **(1 mark)**
- Typical **energy savings** of **3–8%** in column reboiler duty are achievable, which for a large atmospheric distillation column can translate to **₹ 1–3 crore per year** in fuel cost savings. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $R = 2.5$
- $F = 100 \;\text{kmol/hr}$
- $q = 1$ (saturated liquid feed)
- $D = 38.6 \;\text{kmol/hr}$

**Step 1 — Bottoms Flow Rate (Overall Material Balance):**

$$F = D + B$$

$$B = F - D = 100 - 38.6$$

$$\boxed{B = 61.4 \;\text{kmol/hr}}$$

**Step 2 — Liquid Reflux Flow Rate:**

$$L = R \cdot D = 2.5 \times 38.6$$

$$\boxed{L = 96.5 \;\text{kmol/hr}}$$

**Step 3 — Vapor Boil-up Rate (from Feed Plate Balance):**

$$V = L_0 + qF$$

But $L_0$ is the liquid flow on the feed plate (below feed). For a saturated liquid feed, $L_0 = L - (1-q)F = L$ (since $q=1$).

Alternative direct relation:

$$V = L + D = 96.5 + 38.6$$

$$\boxed{V = 135.1 \;\text{kmol/hr}}$$

**Cross-check using $V = R \cdot D + qF$:**

$$V = (2.5)(38.6) + (1)(100) = 96.5 + 100 = 196.5 \;\text{kmol/hr}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students frequently confuse $V$ with $V_{reboiler}$. For a saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$), the vapor boil-up at the **reboiler** is the **top vapor** $V = L + D$. The formula $V = RD + qF$ is for a different case where the convention includes the feed vapor contribution. The correct interpretation here is: since $q=1$, all the feed enters as liquid, so the reboiler generates exactly enough vapor to supply the reflux and the distillate, i.e., $V = L + D = 135.1 \;\text{kmol/hr}$. **(Examiner frequently docks 1 mark for this confusion.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Reboiler Control Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Manipulated vs Controlled Variables:** Steam flow is the MV; bottoms temperature / composition is the CV. Marks lost when students reverse them.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** W → kW, kg/s → kg/hr. A calculation error here costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Kettle and Thermosiphon dynamics:** Kettle reboilers have **larger thermal lag** (high holdup, $\tau_p$ ≈ 60–120 s) compared to thermosiphon (τp ≈ 15–30 s). Mentioning the dynamic difference is often the discriminator in 7-mark questions.
> 4. **Not drawing the cascade loop with proper feedback arrows:** KTU awards 2–3 marks purely for the P&ID / block diagram. Use clear "PV" and "SP" labels.
> 5. **Missing the disturbance path:** A common higher-order question asks how the loop handles **feed composition disturbances** — feedforward (FFC) action on the reboiler is the textbook answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reboiler Definition:** Heat exchanger that supplies vapor boil-up to the bottom of a distillation column.
- **Three Common Types:** Kettle (slow, high holdup), Thermosiphon (natural circulation, fast), Forced Circulation (pump-driven, for viscous or fouling services).
- **Primary Controlled Variable:** Bottoms composition (xB) — most often inferred from **bottoms temperature** or **differential temperature** across the column.
- **Primary Manipulated Variable:** Steam (or hot-oil) flow rate to the reboiler.
- **Cascade Control (T → F):** The most common industrial scheme — master TC, slave FC on steam.
- **ΔT Control:** Cancels pressure disturbances on absolute temperature measurement; recommended for vacuum columns.
- **Valve Position Control (VPC):** Holds the reboiler steam valve at an intermediate position (40–70% open) for energy optimization; saves 3–8% energy.
- **Key Energy Equation:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$
- **LMTD Formula:** $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$
- **Vapor Boil-up:** $V = L + D$ (saturated liquid feed, $q=1$).
- **Dynamic Constant:** Kettle reboiler $\tau_p$ ≈ 30–120 s; Thermosiphon ≈ 15–30 s; tune cascade slave loop at **3×–5× faster** than the master.
- **Steam Design Pressure:** Typically **3 bar(g) to 15 bar(g)** in petrochemical plants.
- **APC / MPC Level:** Reboiler steam is a **fast MV** in Model Predictive Control of distillation columns.
- **Pitfall:** Don't confuse reboiler duty ($Q$) with vapor boil-up rate ($V$); they are related but not identical ($Q = V \cdot \lambda_{mix}$).
- **Common Steam Pressure Trap:** Forgetting to convert from gauge to absolute when computing steam saturation temperature.
- **Kettle Level Control:** A separate LIC loop on the kettle is essential — loss of level = loss of heat transfer area = column upset.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Chemical Reactors: Temperature Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control of Chemical Reactors: Temperature Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Temperature Control of Chemical Reactors** is the closed-loop regulation of the internal thermal state of a chemical reactor (typically an exothermic or endothermic reaction vessel) through manipulation of heat-transfer agents (coolant flow, steam flow, feed temperature, or catalyst dosage) such that the reaction operates at a predefined setpoint with desired conversion, selectivity, yield, and inherent safety margins.

Mathematically, the objective is to force the reactor bulk temperature $T(t)$ to track a reference $T_{sp}$ despite disturbances $D(t)$ (feed concentration, feed temperature, ambient temperature, coolant pressure) by manipulating $M(t)$ (coolant/steam flow rate) through a feedback/feedforward/adaptive controller.

$$ \lim_{t \to \infty} \left[ T(t) - T_{sp} \right] \to 0 \quad \text{subject to} \quad S_s \le S_{safety}, \quad Y \ge Y_{min} $$

where $S_s$ is the safety threshold (e.g., runaway onset temperature) and $Y$ is the product yield.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Temperature control of exothermic reactors is non-trivial because the reaction rate is an *exponential* function of temperature (Arrhenius law), while heat removal is approximately *linear* in temperature difference. This nonlinearity is the root cause of the **open-loop instability** that makes temperature control a *mandatory* loop in every petrochemical reactor.

### 1.2 Why Temperature Control is Paramount in Petrochemical Reactors

| Phenomenon | Engineering Consequence | Real Petrochemical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Exothermic Reaction** (e.g., cracking, polymerization) | Heat release raises T → rate ↑ → more heat → **thermal runaway** | Ethylene polymerization, naphtha cracking |
| **Endothermic Reaction** (e.g., reforming, cracking) | Heat absorption cools T → rate ↓ → reaction **stalls** | Catalytic reforming, steam methane reforming |
| **Selectivity Sensitivity** | Side reactions dominate at high T → poor product quality | Hydrocracking, isomerization |
| **Catalyst Deactivation** | Sintering/coking at high T → costly regeneration | FCC units, hydrotreaters |
| **Safety** | Runaway may lead to vessel rupture, BLEVE | T2 Laboratories disaster (2007) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Pressure Cooker & The Cruise Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Pressure Cooker:** Imagine cooking dal. The stove adds heat. If the dal boils over, you open the lid (add coolant). The system is "self-accelerating" because more heat → faster boiling → more steam pressure → more heat demand. Without constant adjustment, the cooker either boils dry or explodes. A reactor's exothermic reaction behaves identically: heat generation is **regenerative** (positive feedback), so a *controller* must constantly balance the heat budget.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Cruise Control on a Hill:** Your car's cruise control adjusts fuel (manipulated variable) to keep speed (controlled variable) constant. On a steep uphill, gravity is a disturbance; the controller must increase fuel. A reactor's "uphill" is exothermic heat release; the controller's "brake" is coolant flow. The difference is that in a reactor, the disturbance magnitude itself depends on the controlled variable (temperature) — making it a **nonlinear, self-exciting system** that simple on/off or proportional control cannot tame.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Reference Temperature for Arrhenius:** $T_{ref} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$
- **Typical Activation Energy ($E_a$) for petrochemical reactions:** $50,000 \text{ to } 200,000 \; \text{J/mol}$
- **Heat of Reaction ($\Delta H_{rxn}$):** $-50,000$ to $-200,000 \; \text{J/mol}$ for typical polymerization
- **Coolant typical medium:** Water, thermal oil, molten salt, Dowtherm A
- **Jacket heat-transfer coefficient ($U$):** $500 \text{ to } 3000 \; \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Chemical Reactors in Petrochemical Industry

| Reactor Type | Symbol | Temperature Dynamics | Petrochemical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor** | CSTR | Spatially uniform T, lumped parameter | Polymerization, alkylation |
| **Plug Flow Reactor** | PFR | Spatially varying T, distributed parameter | Cracking, reforming |
| **Batch Reactor** | BR | Time-varying T, semi-batch possible | Specialty chemicals, pharma intermediates |
| **Fluidized Bed Catalytic Reactor** | FBCR | Complex multi-phase thermal field | FCC, ethylene oxidation |

For KTU Module 3, the focus is on **CSTR temperature control** because it is the canonical lumped-parameter exothermic reactor studied in process control textbooks.

### 2.2 The Heat Balance — Core Theoretical Foundation

The temperature inside a perfectly mixed CSTR is governed by a transient energy balance. The four terms are:

1. **Heat Accumulation** in reactor contents
2. **Heat In** via feed stream
3. **Heat Out** via product stream
4. **Heat Generated** by chemical reaction (exothermic, +ve)
5. **Heat Removed** via jacket/coil

General lumped energy balance (per unit volume $V$):

$$ \rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \rho C_p \frac{F}{V}(T_f - T) + (-\Delta H_{rxn})\, r_A - \frac{UA}{V}(T - T_j) $$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of reaction mixture (kg/m³)
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity (J/(kg·K))
- $F$ = volumetric feed flow rate (m³/s)
- $V$ = reactor working volume (m³)
- $T_f$ = feed temperature (K)
- $T$ = reactor temperature (K)
- $T_j$ = jacket temperature (K)
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/(m²·K))
- $A$ = heat-transfer area (m²)
- $r_A$ = rate of disappearance of A (mol/(m³·s))
- $(-\Delta H_{rxn})$ = heat of reaction (J/mol, **negative for exothermic**)

### 2.3 Arrhenius Reaction Rate — The Non-Linear Heart of the System

$$ r_A = k_0 \, e^{-E_a / (RT)} \, C_A^n $$

where $k_0$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ is activation energy, $C_A$ is reactant concentration, $n$ is reaction order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exponential dependence on $T$ is what makes the system *strongly nonlinear* and *potentially unstable* in open loop. Doubling the absolute temperature $T$ near $500\,\text{K}$ for a typical petrochemical reaction can increase $r_A$ by **1 to 3 orders of magnitude**.

### 2.4 Open-Loop Instability — S-Shape Steady-State Curve

When steady state is reached ($\frac{dT}{dt}=0$), plotting $Q_{gen}(T)$ (heat generated) vs $Q_{rem}(T)$ (heat removed) yields a classic **S-shaped curve** with three intersection points. The middle point is the **unstable ignition-extinction point** (saddle-node bifurcation), explaining why a reactor can suddenly "ignite" or "extinguish" with tiny disturbances.

### 2.5 Control Strategies — Hierarchical Architecture

| Level | Strategy | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Regulatory** | Single-loop PID on T | Basic setpoint tracking |
| **L2 — Advanced Regulatory** | Cascade (T → T_jacket) | Rejection of coolant disturbances |
| **L3 — Multi-variable** | Split-range (heating/cooling valves) | Endothermic + exothermic regimes |
| **L4 — Predictive** | Feedforward from feed T, C_A | Anticipate disturbances |
| **L5 — Adaptive** | Gain-scheduling, MPC, NMPC | Handle nonlinearity and constraints |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_A = k_0 e^{-E_a/RT} C_A^n$ | Arrhenius reaction rate | mol/(m³·s) |
| 2 | $\rho C_p V \frac{dT}{dt} = \rho C_p F(T_f - T) + (-\Delta H) r_A V - UA(T - T_j)$ | CSTR energy balance | W |
| 3 | $Q_{gen} = (-\Delta H) V r_A$ | Heat generation rate | W |
| 4 | $Q_{rem} = UA(T - T_j)$ | Heat removal rate (jacket) | W |
| 5 | $\tau = V/F$ | Reactor residence time | s |
| 6 | $NTU = UA / (F \rho C_p)$ | Number of heat-transfer units | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\beta = \frac{E_a}{RT_{sp}^2} \cdot \frac{(-\Delta H) C_{A0} V}{UA}$ | Parametric sensitivity (Damköhler-like) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $K_p = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta M}$ | Controller proportional gain (linearized) | K/(kg/s) |
| 9 | $G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ | Second-order FOPDT approximation | — |
| 10 | $T_{max} = \frac{T_{sp}}{1 - \frac{RT_{sp}}{E_a}}$ | Maximum allowable T (Van Welsenaere–Froment criterion) | K |
| 11 | $R_{crit} = \frac{E_a (-\Delta H) C_{A0} F}{UA^2 \cdot something}$ | Parametric sensitivity (S-curve inflection) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars used in tables** — all absolute-value/conditional operators are rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown breakage.

### 2.7 Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Equation 1** is used for *kinetic modeling* in reactor design software (Aspen Plus, COMSOL).
- **Equation 2** is the *plant* model in every temperature controller tuning procedure.
- **Equation 7** is the *stability indicator*: if $\beta > 1$ the reactor is **open-loop unstable**, demanding cascade or adaptive control.
- **Equation 10** is the **safety limit** for alarm setting in DCS systems (e.g., Honeywell Experion, Emerson DeltaV).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: CSTR Energy Balance from First Principles

**Step 1 — State the conservation principle.**
Total energy rate accumulation in the control volume equals net inflow of energy plus generation minus removal.

$$ \text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} - \text{Removal} $$

**Step 2 — Express each term at time $t$ for a well-mixed CSTR of volume $V$.**
- Accumulation: $\frac{dU}{dt} = \frac{d(\rho V C_p T)}{dt} \approx \rho C_p V \frac{dT}{dt}$ (assuming $\rho, V, C_p$ constant).
- Energy in (feed): $\dot{m}_{in} C_p T_f = F \rho C_p T_f$.
- Energy out (product): $F \rho C_p T$.
- Generation: $Q_{gen} = (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \, r_A \, V$.
- Removal (jacket): $Q_{rem} = U A (T - T_j)$.

**Step 3 — Assemble the energy balance.**

$$ \rho C_p V \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p T_f - F \rho C_p T + (-\Delta H_{rxn})\, r_A\, V - U A (T - T_j) $$

**Step 4 — Factor the inlet–outlet convective term.**

$$ \rho C_p V \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p (T_f - T) + (-\Delta H_{rxn})\, r_A\, V - U A (T - T_j) $$

**Step 5 — Divide by $\rho C_p V$ to obtain the standard form.**

$$ \frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{F}{V}(T_f - T) + \frac{(-\Delta H_{rxn})\, r_A}{\rho C_p} - \frac{U A}{\rho C_p V}(T - T_j) $$

**Step 6 — Introduce residence time $\tau = V/F$ and substitute $r_A$.**

$$ \frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{1}{\tau}(T_f - T) + \frac{(-\Delta H_{rxn})\, k_0\, e^{-E_a/RT} C_A^n}{\rho C_p} - \frac{U A}{\rho C_p V}(T - T_j) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical reactor model** used in every process-control textbook (Luyben, Seborg, Ogunnaike & Ray). It is the **plant equation** for all temperature controller design.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Open-Loop Stability Condition via Linearization

**Step 1 — At steady state, $\frac{dT}{dt}=0$.**
Define $T_{ss}$ such that $Q_{gen}(T_{ss}) = Q_{rem}(T_{ss})$.

**Step 2 — Perturb the temperature: $T = T_{ss} + \delta T$, with $T_j$ constant (open loop).**
Taylor-expand the Arrhenius term to first order:

$$ e^{-E_a/RT} \approx e^{-E_a/RT_{ss}} \left[ 1 + \frac{E_a}{R T_{ss}^2} \delta T \right] $$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the linearized energy balance.**

$$ \frac{d(\delta T)}{dt} = \underbrace{ \left[ -\frac{1}{\tau} - \frac{UA}{\rho C_p V} + \frac{(-\Delta H_{rxn})\, k_0\, e^{-E_a/RT_{ss}}\, C_A^n\, E_a}{\rho C_p \, R T_{ss}^2} \right] }_{a} \delta T $$

**Step 4 — Stability criterion: $a < 0$.**
The first two terms are negative (stabilizing). The third term is **positive** (destabilizing). Define the **parametric sensitivity** $\beta$:

$$ \beta = \frac{E_a}{R T_{ss}^2} \cdot \frac{(-\Delta H_{rxn})\, C_{A,ss}\, V}{U A} $$

**Step 5 — Open-loop stability condition.**

$$ \beta < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Stable open loop} $$

$$ \beta \ge 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Open-loop UNSTABLE — closed-loop control mandatory} $$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation.**
When $\beta \ge 1$, the slope of $Q_{gen}(T)$ at the operating point exceeds the slope of $Q_{rem}(T)$. Any positive perturbation in $T$ generates more heat than can be removed, and the system **runs away** exponentially.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Transfer Function for the Cascade Inner Loop (Jacket Temperature)

**Step 1 — Jacket energy balance (assume well-mixed jacket).**

$$ \rho_j C_{pj} V_j \frac{dT_j}{dt} = F_j \rho_j C_{pj}(T_{j,in} - T_j) + U A (T - T_j) $$

**Step 2 — Linearize and take the Laplace transform with $T$ as disturbance input and $T_{j,in}$ as manipulated.**

$$ \frac{T_j(s)}{T_{j,in}(s)} = \frac{K_j}{\tau_j s + 1}, \quad K_j = 1, \quad \tau_j = \frac{V_j}{F_j} $$

**Step 3 — Complete plant transfer function (reactor T to coolant inlet T).**

$$ G_p(s) = \frac{T(s)}{T_{j,in}(s)} = \frac{K_1}{\tau_1 s + 1} \cdot \frac{K_2}{\tau_2 s - 1} $$

The right-half-plane pole $(s = +1/\tau_2)$ confirms the **open-loop unstable** nature. The unstable pole time constant is:

$$ \tau_2 = \frac{\rho C_p V}{UA(\beta - 1)} $$

**Step 4 — PID tuning rule (Ziegler–Nichols or Luyben's method) for unstable second-order plant.**

For a $\frac{K e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s - 1)}$ plant, Luyben's recommended settings are:

$$ K_c = \frac{6}{K} \cdot \frac{\tau_1}{\tau_2}, \quad \tau_I = \frac{\tau_1}{5}, \quad \tau_D = 0 $$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, **derivative action is often disabled** ($\tau_D = 0$) for exothermic reactor control because measurement noise + aggressive derivative can cause valve hunting near setpoint.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Condition Changes

| Change in Operating Condition | Effect on $Q_{gen}$ Curve | Effect on $Q_{rem}$ Curve | Effect on Steady-State $T$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Feed temperature $T_f$ | No change directly (shifts $Q_{rem}$ intercept) | Shifts right | Increases |
| ↑ Coolant flow $F_j$ | No change | Slope increases (steeper) | Decreases |
| ↑ Feed concentration $C_{Af}$ | Shifts $Q_{gen}$ up | No change | Increases |
| ↑ Activation energy $E_a$ | Curve becomes more nonlinear | No change | **Runaway risk ↑** |
| ↓ Jacket temperature $T_{j,in}$ | No change | Shifts down | Decreases |
| ↑ Catalyst deactivation | $Q_{gen}$ decreases | No change | Decreases |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Single-Loop PID Reactor Temperature Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["Setpoint T_sp"] --> SUM1(("+−"))
    PV["Process Variable T(t)"] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> ERR["Error e(t) = T_sp - T"]
    ERR --> PID["PID Controller\nGc(s) = Kc(1 + 1/Tis + Tds)"]
    PID --> VALVE["Control Valve\nCoolant Flow Fj"]
    VALVE --> JACKET["Jacket\nHeat Exchanger"]
    JACKET -- "Q_rem = UA(T-Tj)" --> REACTOR["CSTR\nReactor T(t)"]
    REACTOR --> PV
    FEED["Feed T_f, C_Af\nDisturbance D(t)"] -.-> REACTOR
    REACTOR --> PROD["Product Stream"]
    JACKET -- "Tj" --> JACKETSENSE["Jacket T Sensor"]
    JACKETSENSE -.feedback.-> VALVE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The forward path is Setpoint → PID → Valve → Jacket → Reactor → Sensor → back to comparator. The disturbance enters the reactor directly from the feed line (dashed arrow). The jacket temperature sensor provides a **secondary measurement** that enables cascade control.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Cascade Control Architecture (T_outer → T_jacket_inner)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1["T_sp (Reactor)"] --> SUM1(("+−"))
    T_R["Reactor T\nPrimary PV"] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> E1["E1 = T_sp - T"]
    E1 --> PID1["Primary PID\nSlow Loop (Tau_I ~ minutes)"]
    PID1 --> TJSET["Inner Setpoint T_j_sp"]
    TJSET --> SUM2(("+−"))
    T_J["Jacket T\nSecondary PV"] --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> E2["E2 = T_j_sp - T_j"]
    E2 --> PID2["Secondary PID\nFast Loop (Tau_I ~ seconds)"]
    PID2 --> VALVE["Coolant Valve"]
    VALVE --> JACKET["Jacket"]
    JACKET --> REACTOR["Reactor"]
    REACTOR --> T_R
    JACKET --> T_J
    FEED["Feed Disturbance"] -.-> JACKET
    COOLT["Coolant T_j_in Disturbance"] -.-> JACKET
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why cascade?** The inner jacket-temperature loop rejects **coolant-side disturbances** (pressure, inlet temperature) 10–50× faster than a single-loop controller could, dramatically improving the outer reactor-temperature loop's disturbance rejection.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Split-Range Control for Heating + Cooling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["T_sp"] --> SUM(("+−"))
    PV["Reactor T"] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR["Error Signal"]
    ERR --> PID["PID Controller\nOutput 0-100%"]
    PID --> SPLIT["Signal Splitter"]
    SPLIT -->|"Output 50-100%"| HVALVE["Steam Valve\nHeating"]
    SPLIT -->|"Output 0-50%"| CVALVE["Coolant Valve\nCooling"]
    HVALVE --> JACKET["Reactor Jacket"]
    CVALVE --> JACKET
    JACKET --> REACTOR["Reactor"]
    REACTOR --> PV
```

> [!NOTE]
> At 50% controller output, both valves are at 0% (split point). Above 50% → heating valve opens proportionally. Below 50% → cooling valve opens proportionally. Used for reactors operating in both endothermic (start-up) and exothermic (steady-state) regimes.

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Full Hierarchical Reactor Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["L4: Plant-wide Optimization\nMPC, Real-Time Optimizer"]
    L3["L3: Advanced Control\nNMPC, Gain-Scheduled PID"]
    L2["L2: Cascade Loops\nReactor T + Jacket T"]
    L1["L1: Regulatory PID\nSingle-loop temperature"]
    L0["L0: Field Instrumentation\nRTD/TC sensors, I/P transducers, control valves"]
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0
    L0 -.signals.-> L1
    L1 -.PV feedback.-> L2
    L2 -.PV feedback.-> L3
    L3 -.PV feedback.-> L4
```

### 4.5 Open-Loop S-Curve and Bifurcation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Steady State Condition\nQ_gen(T) = Q_rem(T)"] --> B["Three Intersection Points\non S-curve"]
    B --> C1["Lower Point\nSTABLE\nLow T, slow reaction"]
    B --> C2["Middle Point\nUNSTABLE Saddle\nRunaway threshold"]
    B --> C3["Upper Point\nSTABLE\nHigh T, fast reaction"]
    C2 --> D["Disturbance Pushes T up"]
    D --> E["Slope(Q_gen) > Slope(Q_rem)"]
    E --> F["Runaway to Upper Stable Point\nor Thermal Explosion"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> The middle saddle point is the **ignition temperature**. If the reactor is operating just below it, a small upward disturbance (e.g., 2 °C in feed) can flip the system to the upper stable point, causing an irreversible temperature excursion. This is exactly what happened in the **Bhopal disaster (1984, MIC leak)** — the MIC storage reactor lost temperature control, leading to runaway exothermic decomposition.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Note:** Part A questions in KTU 2024 scheme ESE are 3-mark items testing *Remember* and *Understand* cognitive levels (Revised Bloom's L1–L2). Answers should be **concise (60–100 words)** with one defining equation or diagram.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is temperature control of exothermic chemical reactors considered critical in the petrochemical industry? Explain briefly.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Temperature control of exothermic reactors is critical because (i) the reaction rate follows Arrhenius law $r_A = k_0 e^{-E_a/RT} C_A^n$, so reaction rate — and therefore heat generation $Q_{gen} = (-\Delta H_{rxn}) r_A V$ — increases **exponentially** with temperature, (ii) heat removal $Q_{rem} = UA(T - T_j)$ is only **linear** in $T$, leading to open-loop instability (thermal runaway) when $Q_{gen}$ slope exceeds $Q_{rem}$ slope (parametric sensitivity $\beta \ge 1$), (iii) uncontrolled temperature degrades **selectivity**, **yield**, and **catalyst life**, and may cause vessel rupture or BLEVE.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for Arrhenius, 1 mark for runaway mechanism, 1 mark for selectivity/yield/safety — all three must appear.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define parametric sensitivity $\beta$ for a CSTR. State the open-loop stability condition in terms of $\beta$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Parametric sensitivity $\beta$ for a CSTR is the dimensionless ratio that quantifies the relative sensitivity of heat generation to heat removal near the operating temperature:

$$ \beta = \frac{E_a}{R T_{ss}^2} \cdot \frac{(-\Delta H_{rxn})\, C_{A,ss}\, V}{U A} $$

**Open-loop stability condition:** The reactor is **open-loop stable** if $\beta < 1$, and **open-loop unstable** (runaway-prone) if $\beta \ge 1$. Physically, $\beta < 1$ means the linearized $Q_{gen}$ slope is less than the $Q_{rem}$ slope, so any temperature perturbation decays.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice is mandatory** — students answer either OR.

---

#### 🔵 QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the energy balance equation for a perfectly mixed CSTR and identify each term. Explain why the resulting system is inherently nonlinear and potentially unstable in open loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the CSTR with the following data, determine whether the reactor is open-loop stable. If unstable, suggest a suitable control strategy with justification.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_a$ | $80{,}000 \; \text{J/mol}$ |
| $R$ | $8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$ |
| $T_{ss}$ | $350 \; \text{K}$ |
| $(-\Delta H_{rxn})$ | $-60{,}000 \; \text{J/mol}$ |
| $C_{A,ss}$ | $1.0 \; \text{kmol/m}^3$ |
| $V$ | $5.0 \; \text{m}^3$ |
| $U$ | $1500 \; \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ |
| $A$ | $12 \; \text{m}^2$ |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### 🟢 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a **cascade temperature control system** for a CSTR. Explain the role of the primary and secondary loops, and justify why cascade control is preferred over single-loop PID for exothermic reactor temperature control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CSTR exhibits the following linearized transfer function (T → coolant flow):

$$ G_p(s) = \frac{2.5 \, e^{-0.5 s}}{(10\,s + 1)(8\,s - 1)} $$

Apply **Luyben's tuning rule** for unstable second-order processes to compute the PID controller settings $K_c, \tau_I, \tau_D$. Also write the controller transfer function $G_c(s)$. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions

---

#### Solution to Question A

**(a) Energy Balance Derivation (7 marks):**

The transient energy balance for a well-mixed CSTR (volume $V$, density $\rho$, heat capacity $C_p$, jacket temperature $T_j$, coolant heat-transfer area $A$, overall $U$) is derived from first principles as follows. The accumulation of thermal energy in the reactor contents equals the net enthalpy brought in by the feed, minus enthalpy leaving with the product, plus the heat generated by the chemical reaction (exothermic term with $(-\Delta H_{rxn})$), minus the heat removed through the jacket:

$$ \rho C_p V \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p (T_f - T) + (-\Delta H_{rxn})\, r_A V - U A (T - T_j) $$

**Identification of terms:**
1. **Accumulation:** $\rho C_p V \frac{dT}{dt}$ — thermal energy storage
2. **Convective inflow − outflow:** $F \rho C_p (T_f - T)$ — sensible heat balance
3. **Reaction heat generation:** $(-\Delta H_{rxn}) r_A V$ — Arrhenius-driven, exponential in $T$
4. **Jacket heat removal:** $U A (T - T_j)$ — approximately linear in $T$

**Why nonlinear and unstable:**
The reaction rate $r_A = k_0 e^{-E_a/RT} C_A^n$ is **exponential** in $T$, while the jacket heat removal $U A (T - T_j)$ is **linear** in $T$. Linearizing at the operating point reveals that the closed-loop dynamics contain a **right-half-plane pole** whenever the parametric sensitivity $\beta \ge 1$:

$$ \beta = \frac{E_a}{R T_{ss}^2} \cdot \frac{(-\Delta H_{rxn})\, C_{A,ss}\, V}{U A} $$

A small positive perturbation in $T$ then amplifies (instead of decaying), leading to open-loop thermal runaway.

**Sketch required:** A CSTR with feed, product, jacket, and arrows showing $T_f, T, T_j, Q_{gen}, Q_{rem}$.

---

**(b) Parametric Sensitivity Calculation (7 marks):**

Substitute the data into the $\beta$ formula:

$$ \beta = \frac{80{,}000}{8.314 \times (350)^2} \times \frac{60{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 5.0}{1500 \times 12} $$

**Step 1 — Compute the first fraction:**

$$ \frac{E_a}{R T_{ss}^2} = \frac{80{,}000}{8.314 \times 122{,}500} = \frac{80{,}000}{1{,}018{,}465} = 0.0786 \; \text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the second fraction (dimensionless):**

$$ \frac{(-\Delta H_{rxn})\, C_{A,ss}\, V}{U A} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 5.0}{1500 \times 12} = \frac{300{,}000}{18{,}000} = 16.67 \; \text{K} $$

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$ \beta = 0.0786 \times 16.67 = 1.31 $$

**Conclusion:** Since $\beta = 1.31 > 1$, the reactor is **OPEN-LOOP UNSTABLE**.

**Recommended control strategy: Cascade control.**
- **Primary (outer) loop:** PID on reactor temperature $T$ → generates jacket-temperature setpoint $T_{j,sp}$.
- **Secondary (inner) loop:** Fast PI on jacket temperature $T_j$ → manipulates coolant valve position.
- The cascade architecture handles the open-loop instability by providing **fast disturbance rejection** at the inner loop (coolant pressure, inlet $T$ disturbances) and **stable setpoint tracking** at the outer loop. Additionally, gain-scheduled PID or MPC may be layered for further nonlinear compensation.

---

#### Solution to Question B

**(a) Cascade Control Block Diagram and Justification (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram:** (See the cascade diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes; the student must reproduce it with at least 4 blocks: Primary PID, Secondary PID, Control Valve, Reactor, plus the two PV feedback arrows.)

**Working:**
- The **primary (master, outer) loop** measures the reactor temperature $T$ (PV₁) and compares it to the setpoint $T_{sp}$ to produce error $e_1$. The primary PID controller — tuned **slowly** (large $\tau_I$, e.g., 2–5 minutes) — generates the setpoint for the secondary loop, namely $T_{j,sp}$.
- The **secondary (slave, inner) loop** measures the actual jacket temperature $T_j$ (PV₂) and compares it to $T_{j,sp}$ to produce error $e_2$. The secondary PI controller — tuned **quickly** (small $\tau_I$, e.g., 10–30 seconds) — adjusts the coolant control valve opening.

**Justification for cascade over single-loop PID:**
1. **Disturbance rejection:** Coolant-side disturbances (inlet temperature swings, coolant pressure changes) enter the process *before* the reactor temperature sensor. The inner loop rejects them 10–50× faster than the outer loop alone could, because it measures $T_j$ directly.
2. **Nonlinearity compensation:** The inner-loop linearizes the coolant valve non-linearity by operating in a tight feedback configuration.
3. **Open-loop instability handling:** For $\beta \ge 1$, the primary controller cannot stabilize the plant on its own. The cascade structure effectively *replaces* the unstable plant with a faster, well-damped inner loop, after which the outer PID sees a stable equivalent plant.
4. **Loop decoupling:** The two time scales (fast jacket dynamics, slow reaction kinetics) are decoupled.

---

**(b) Luyben's Tuning for Unstable Plant (7 marks):**

Given: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2.5 \, e^{-0.5 s}}{(10\,s + 1)(8\,s - 1)}$

Identify Luyben's parameters:
- $K = 2.5$
- $\theta = 0.5$ (dead time)
- $\tau_1 = 10$ (stable pole)
- $\tau_2 = 8$ (unstable pole)

**Luyben's tuning rules** (for plant $\dfrac{K e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s - 1)}$):

$$ K_c = \frac{6}{K} \cdot \frac{\tau_1}{\tau_2} $$

$$ \tau_I = \frac{\tau_1}{5} $$

$$ \tau_D = 0 \quad \text{(derivative disabled for exothermic reactors)} $$

**Step 1 — Compute $K_c$:**

$$ K_c = \frac{6}{2.5} \cdot \frac{10}{8} = 2.4 \times 1.25 = 3.0 $$

**Step 2 — Compute $\tau_I$:**

$$ \tau_I = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0 \; \text{minutes} $$

**Step 3 — Confirm $\tau_D = 0$.**

**Step 4 — Controller transfer function (PI form):**

$$ G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} \right) = 3.0 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{2.0\,s} \right) = 3.0 + \frac{1.5}{s} $$

So the PI controller is: $G_c(s) = \dfrac{6.0\,s + 3.0}{2.0\,s} = \dfrac{3.0(2\,s + 1)}{2\,s}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing stable vs unstable pole time constants:** Writing $K_c = \frac{6}{K} \cdot \frac{\tau_2}{\tau_1}$ (inverted ratio) — lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to disable derivative action** on exothermic reactor control — lose 1 mark. Always state $\tau_D = 0$ explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong units for $\tau_I$** (e.g., seconds when $\tau_1$ is in minutes) — lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Failing to verify stability after tuning** by computing the closed-loop characteristic equation — KTU expects a stability check via Routh-Hurwitz for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Reactor Temperature Control**

- [ ] **Reactor types:** CSTR (lumped, uniform T), PFR (distributed, gradient T), Batch, FBCR. KTU 2024 focus is **CSTR**.
- [ ] **Energy balance equation** is the *plant*; memorize the four-term form.
- [ ] **Arrhenius law** $r_A = k_0 e^{-E_a/RT} C_A^n$ — exponential, source of nonlinearity.
- [ ] **Heat generation** $Q_{gen} = (-\Delta H_{rxn}) r_A V$ — exponential in T.
- [ ] **Heat removal** $Q_{rem} = U A (T - T_j)$ — linear in T.
- [ ] **S-curve** steady-state diagram has three intersections; middle is **unstable saddle**.
- [ ] **Parametric sensitivity** $\beta < 1$ stable, $\beta \ge 1$ unstable.
- [ ] **Van Welsenaere–Froment** $T_{max} = T_{sp} / (1 - RT_{sp}/E_a)$ is the **safety alarm limit**.
- [ ] **Single-loop PID** is sufficient only for $\beta < 1$ (gentle exothermic reactions).
- [ ] **Cascade control** is mandatory for $\beta \ge 1$: outer PID on $T$, inner PI on $T_j$.
- [ ] **Split-range control** for reactors needing both heating and cooling (e.g., batch reactors in start-up → exothermic steady state).
- [ ] **Feedforward control** from $T_f$ and $C_{Af}$ compensates measurable upstream disturbances.
- [ ] **Luyben's tuning rule** for unstable plant: $K_c = \frac{6}{K} \cdot \frac{\tau_1}{\tau_2}$, $\tau_I = \tau_1/5$, $\tau_D = 0$.
- [ ] **Industrial DCS implementation:** Use **gain-scheduled PID** when operating point changes by >30%, switch to **MPC** for multivariable constraints.
- [ ] **Safety first:** Always implement independent **high-temperature interlock** separate from the control loop (SIL-2/SIL-3 rated).
- [ ] **Exam trap:** KTU frequently asks for the **significance of the $\beta$ parameter** — be ready to derive and interpret.
- [ ] **Real-world link:** Bhopal 1984, T2 Laboratories 2007 — both were uncontrolled exothermic reactor temperature excursions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pressure Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pressure Control** in petrochemical process industries refers to a closed-loop regulatory control strategy in which the manipulated variable (typically valve opening) is automatically adjusted by a controller to maintain the **Process Variable (PV)**—the measured pressure—at a predetermined **Setpoint (SP)** value, despite the presence of load disturbances such as changes in upstream flow rate, downstream demand, temperature excursions, or phase changes.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terms, a pressure control loop is a **Single-Input Single-Output (SISO)** negative-feedback control system whose measured variable $P(t)$ is transmitted, compared against a reference $P_{sp}$, and the error $e(t) = P_{sp} - P(t)$ is processed by a controller (P, PI, or PID) to generate a corrective output signal $u(t)$ to the final control element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Pressure control is classified as a **flow-derived variable** in process dynamics. Unlike flow (which is a true integrating variable with negligible capacitance), pressure exhibits **capacitive behavior** because fluids (both liquids and gases) can be compressed and stored, giving the process significant self-regulation in many configurations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **filling a balloon with water** from a tap. Your eyes are the **pressure sensor**, your brain is the **controller**, and your hand on the tap is the **control valve**. If the balloon becomes too tight (pressure rising above target), your brain tells your hand to slightly close the tap. If the balloon feels floppy (pressure dropping), your hand opens the tap a bit. That continuous feedback loop—**measure → compare → correct**—is exactly what an industrial pressure controller does on a reactor or distillation column.

In a refinery, instead of hands and eyes, we use:
- A **Pressure Transmitter (PT)** to measure
- A **Pressure Indicating Controller (PIC)** to compare and decide
- A **Control Valve (PCV)** to act

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Units (per ISA-5.1):**
> - **Pressure:** psig, bar, kPa, MPa, inH₂O, mmHg
> - **Signal:** 4–20 mA DC (electronic) or 3–15 psig (pneumatic)
> - **Controller Output Range:** Always 0–100% normalized
> - **Typical Setpoint Precision:** $\pm 0.1\%$ of calibrated span

## 1.3 Why Pressure Control is Critical in Petrochemicals

Pressure directly influences:
- **Phase equilibrium** (vapor-liquid equilibrium in distillation)
- **Reaction rates** (per Arrhenius kinetics, rate depends on partial pressure)
- **Safety** (overpressure = explosion / rupture risk)
- **Product quality** (molecular weight distribution in polymerization)
- **Equipment integrity** (compressor surge, pump cavitation)
- **Mass balance** (gas flow $\propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$)

The **deadly consequence** of pressure control failure is the **BLEVE** (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion) or **unconfined vapor cloud explosion (UVCE)**, both of which have caused catastrophic loss of life and capital in petrochemical history.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of Pressure

Pressure is defined as the **normal force per unit area** exerted by a fluid on a surface:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

where $F$ is force in Newtons and $A$ is area in m², giving units of **Pascal (Pa = N/m²)**.

For a static fluid column under gravity:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

where:
- $P_0$ = reference pressure at the free surface (**Pa**)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = vertical height of fluid column (**m**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A 10-meter column of water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) exerts roughly **1 bar** of hydrostatic pressure. This is why even open atmospheric storage tanks have non-trivial pressure at the bottom.

## 2.2 Gas vs. Liquid Pressure Dynamics — The Critical Distinction

The single most important theoretical concept in pressure control is the difference in **process gain** and **time constant** between gas systems and liquid systems.

### 2.2.1 Liquid Systems
- **Incompressible** ($\rho \approx$ constant)
- Pressure responds **almost instantaneously** to flow changes
- Process behaves as a **pure gain** with negligible dead time
- Surge (water hammer) is a major concern

### 2.2.2 Gas Systems
- **Compressible**, obey ideal gas law: $PV = nRT$
- Pressure has significant **capacitive (storage) effect**
- Time constant is **large** (seconds to minutes)
- Self-regulating to changes in flow at constant inventory

For a fixed-volume gas vessel with mass balance:

$$V \frac{d\rho}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out}$$

Substituting ideal gas law and differentiating:

$$C_V \frac{dP}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out}$$

where $C_V$ is the **process capacitance** for gas systems. This capacitance is what makes pressure loops **tunable**—they behave like a first-order lag with time constant:

$$\tau_P = R \, C_V = \frac{V}{k \, A_v \, \sqrt{P_{mean}}}$$

## 2.3 Components of a Pressure Control Loop

| Component | Function | Petrochemical Examples |
|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| **Primary Element (Sensor)** | Detects pressure | Bourdon tube, diaphragm, strain gauge, capacitance cell, piezoelectric crystal |
| **Transmitter (PT)** | Converts sensor signal to standardized output | Rosemount 3051, Yokogawa EJX, Honeywell ST 3000 |
| **Controller (PIC)** | Compares PV vs SP, computes output | DCS controller (Honeywell TDC 3000, Emerson DeltaV), pneumatic PI station |
| **Final Control Element (PCV)** | Modifies process flow to control pressure | Pneumatic globe valve with positioner, hydraulic actuated valve |
| **I/P Transducer** | Converts current to pneumatic signal | Converts 4–20 mA to 3–15 psig |

## 2.4 Standard PID Controller Equation

The universally applied controller equation in industry is the **ISA (standard) form**:

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I} \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] + u_{bias}$$

In Laplace domain:

$$U(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s \right) E(s)$$

### Key Tuning Parameters

| Symbol | Parameter | Industrial Effect | Typical Range |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| $K_c$ | Proportional Gain (or PB%) | Reduces rise time, increases overshoot | 0.1 – 10 |
| $\tau_I$ | Integral Time (Reset) | Eliminates steady-state offset | 0.2 – 20 min |
| $\tau_D$ | Derivative Time (Pre-act) | Anticipates future error, damps oscillations | 0 – 2 min |
| $u_{bias}$ | Manual Reset | Sets output when error = 0 | 0–100% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** In **gas pressure control loops**, derivative action is **rarely used** because gas pressure dynamics are first-order (no sharp lead-lag behavior). **PI control is the industrial standard** for gas pressure. **P-only control** is often used for liquid pressure where fast response is needed and small steady-state offset is acceptable.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = F/A$ | Definition of pressure | Pa |
| 2 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure | Pa |
| 3 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas law | Pa·m³ = J |
| 4 | $C_V = V / (RT)$ | Gas process capacitance | m³/Pa |
| 5 | $\tau_P = R \cdot C_V$ | Process time constant | s |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_c e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int e\,dt + K_c \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}$ | PID law (parallel form) | % output |
| 7 | $PB\% = 100 / K_c$ | Proportional band relationship | % |
| 8 | $K_p = \frac{\Delta PV}{\Delta CO}$ | Process gain (PV per CO) | unitless ratio |
| 9 | $Q = C_v N_1 F_p \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{G_g T_1 Z}}$ | ISA gas valve sizing | Nm³/h |
| 10 | $Q = N_1 F_p \sqrt{\Delta P / G}$ | ISA liquid valve sizing | m³/h |
| 11 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \left( \frac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} \right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | Isentropic compression | Pa |
| 12 | $\Delta P_{relief} = 0.10 \cdot P_{set} + 3 \text{ psig}$ | API 520 accumulation | psig |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation 12 (API 520):** This is the **legal** overpressure allowance for fire-case relief sizing in petrochemical pressure vessels. Universities and licensing exams (API, ASMEMTP) test this. The 10% rule prevents unnecessarily large relief valves during normal PSV pop-off.

## 2.6 Control Valve Characteristics for Pressure

| Valve Type | Equal Percentage / Linear? | Application in Pressure |
|------------|----------------------------|-------------------------|
| Globe (cage-guided) | Both available | Most common for gas & liquid PCV |
| Ball Valve | Modified equal percentage | High-pressure drop, on/off service |
| Butterfly | Approx. equal percentage | Low-pressure, large line size |
| Diaphragm Valve | Linear | Corrosive fluids, sanitary service |

For pressure control, **equal percentage trim is preferred** because:
- It provides fine throttling at low flow
- Equal percentage compensates for the non-linear process gain of gas systems
- The installed characteristic approaches linear when paired with a gas process

## 2.7 Practical Industrial Utility

Pressure control loops are deployed across virtually **every unit operation** in a petrochemical plant:

- **Distillation columns:** Condenser pressure → controls overhead temperature (cascade)
- **Reactor jacket:** Coolant pressure → controls heat removal rate
- **Compressor discharge:** Anti-surge control
- **Steam header:** Pressure reducing station (PRDS) between high/low pressure mains
- **Storage spheres:** Vapor pressure control via blanketing
- **LNG tanks:** Boil-off gas (BOG) pressure management
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Process Model for a Gas Pressure Vessel

We begin with a gas-filled vessel of fixed volume $V$ receiving mass inflow $\dot{m}_{in}$ and losing mass through outflow $\dot{m}_{out}$.

**Step 1: Macroscopic mass balance**

$$\frac{dm}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$

**Step 2: Express mass in terms of pressure via ideal gas law**

For an ideal gas, $m = \dfrac{PV M}{RT}$ where $M$ = molar mass, $R$ = universal gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature. Substituting:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \left( \frac{PV M}{RT} \right) = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$

**Step 3: Assume constant $V$, $T$, and $M$ (isothermal rigid vessel)**

Since $V$, $T$, $M$ are constants, the derivative simplifies to:

$$\frac{VM}{RT} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$

**Step 4: Define capacitance and resistance**

The process capacitance is $C = \dfrac{VM}{RT}$ (kg/Pa). The flow resistances are linearized:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \frac{1}{R_{in}}(P_{in} - P), \quad \dot{m}_{out} = \frac{1}{R_{out}} P$$

**Step 5: Substitute and arrange**

$$C \frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{1}{R_{in}} (P_{in} - P) - \frac{1}{R_{out}} P$$

$$\boxed{ RC \frac{dP}{dt} + \left( \frac{R}{R_{in}} + \frac{R}{R_{out}} \right) P = \frac{R}{R_{in}} P_{in} }$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE** with steady-state gain $K_p = \dfrac{R/R_{in}}{R/R_{in} + R/R_{out}}$ and time constant $\tau = RC$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Proportional-Only Pressure Control Steady-State Offset

For a P-only controller with proportional band $PB\% = \dfrac{100}{K_c}$ controlling a self-regulating process with gain $K_p$:

**Step 1: Define the error in the loop**

$$e_{ss} = P_{sp} - P_{ss}$$

**Step 2: Write the controller output at steady state**

$$u_{ss} = u_{bias} + K_c \cdot e_{ss}$$

**Step 3: Write the process relationship at steady state**

$$P_{ss} = P_0 + K_p (u_{ss} - u_0)$$

**Step 4: Substitute the controller output**

$$P_{ss} = P_0 + K_p (u_{bias} - u_0) + K_p K_c \, e_{ss}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the steady-state error**

$$e_{ss} = P_{sp} - P_{ss} = \frac{P_{sp} - P_0 - K_p(u_{bias} - u_0)}{1 + K_p K_c}$$

$$\boxed{ e_{ss} = \frac{\text{Load disturbance magnitude}}{1 + K_p K_c} }$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is the **bias** equation. It proves that **P-only control CANNOT eliminate steady-state offset** in a self-regulating process — the error asymptotically approaches $e_{ss}$, never zero. This is why **PI control is mandatory** for precise pressure regulation in petrochemical processes (e.g., reflux drum pressure).

## 3.3 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure in a Distillation Column Reboiler

Consider a kettle-type reboiler with liquid level $h$ and reboiler pressure $P_{reboiler}$. The pressure at the bottom of the reboiler (sump nozzle) is:

**Step 1: Identify the variables**

Liquid density (hydrocarbon) $\rho = 720$ kg/m³
Vertical height of liquid $h = 1.5$ m
Atmospheric + system pressure at top of liquid = $P_0$ (Pa, gauge)

**Step 2: Apply hydrostatic equation**

$$P_{sump} = P_0 + \rho g h$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values**

$$P_{sump} = P_0 + (720)(9.81)(1.5)$$

**Step 4: Calculate**

$$P_{sump} = P_0 + 10{,}594.8 \text{ Pa} = P_0 + 0.106 \text{ bar}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{ P_{sump} = P_0 + 0.106 \text{ bar (gauge)} }$$

This shows that even a 1.5 m liquid head contributes a non-trivial pressure. **Practical implication:** A differential pressure (DP) level transmitter on the reboiler sump measures the hydrostatic head — but it does so against the dynamic vapor space pressure. A pressure-compensated (absolute pressure) measurement is needed for high-temperature services to avoid thermal expansion errors.

## 3.4 Derivation: API 520 Relief Valve Sizing Pressure

Per **API Standard 520 (Sizing, Selection, and Installation of Pressure-Relieving Devices in Refineries)**, the accumulation pressure for a vessel protected by a single relief valve is:

**Step 1: Identify the design conditions**

Set pressure $P_{set} = 10.0$ barg
Overpressure accumulation allowance per API 520 = **10% of set pressure** (for fire case on non-fire-tested vessels)

**Step 2: Apply the formula**

$$P_{accumulation} = 1.10 \times P_{set}$$

**Step 3: Calculate**

$$P_{accumulation} = 1.10 \times 10.0 = 11.0 \text{ barg}$$

**Step 4: Total relieving pressure (gauge + atmospheric + supercharge)**

$$P_{relieving} = P_{accumulation} + P_{atm} + P_{supercharge}$$

$$\boxed{ P_{relieving} = 11.0 + 1.013 + 0 = 12.013 \text{ bara} }$$

This is the **minimum pressure** the PSV must relieve at to maintain vessel integrity. Sizing uses $P_{relieving}$ in the API 520 nozzle area equation:

$$A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c} \sqrt{\frac{T \cdot Z}{M \cdot P_{relieving}}}$$

where $W$ is the required mass flow (kg/h), $C$ is the gas-specific coefficient, $K_d$, $K_b$, $K_c$ are derating factors for discharge, backpressure, and viscosity respectively.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Pressure Control Strategy

| Loop Type | Best For | Pros | Cons | Tuning |
|-----------|----------|------|------|--------|
| **P-only** | Liquid line pressure, surge tanks | Fast, stable, simple | Permanent offset | Set $K_c$ for desired offset |
| **PI** | Gas pressure, reflux drum, steam header | No offset, widely applicable | Slower, can overshoot | Ziegler-Nichols or Lambda tuning |
| **PID** | Compressor anti-surge, fast dynamic | Best dynamic response | Sensitive to noise, derivative kick | Cohen-Coon, IMC-PID |
| **Cascade** (primary P → secondary flow) | Reactor top pressure, distillation | Faster disturbance rejection | Two loops to tune, two transmitters | Tune inner (flow) first, then outer (P) |
| **Feedforward** + Feedback | Fired heater coil pressure, compressor | Anticipates disturbances | Requires accurate model | Static gain from F to P |
| **Self-operated regulator** | Field-mounted, no instrument air | No power needed, fail-safe | Limited accuracy (±2–5%) | Factory-set, non-adjustable |
| **Split-range** | Multi-valve pressure control, blanketing | Single controller drives two valves | Complex interaction | Calibration critical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Operated Pressure Regulator:** A purely mechanical device with **no external power, no controller, and no I/P transducer**. The process pressure acts on a diaphragm that directly moves the valve stem against a spring setpoint. Used in field locations where instrument air or electricity is unavailable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Basic Single-Loop Pressure Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SP - Setpoint Pressure] --> B[Comparator Summing Junction]
    C[PT - Pressure Transmitter senses PV] --> D[PV - Measured Pressure]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Error Signal e = SP - PV]
    E --> F[PIC - Pressure Indicating Controller PID]
    F --> G[Controller Output 4 to 20 mA]
    G --> H[I to P Transducer 3 to 15 psig]
    H --> I[PCV - Pressure Control Valve]
    I --> J[Process Vessel Pipeline]
    J --> C
    F -.-> K[Display Alarm Recorder]
```

## 4.2 Cascade Pressure Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP1[Outer Setpoint Pressure] --> CJ1[Outer Summing Junction]
    PT1[Primary Pressure Transmitter] --> PV1[Primary PV]
    PV1 --> CJ1
    CJ1 --> PC1[Primary Pressure Controller PIC]
    PC1 --> RSP[Remote Setpoint to Inner Loop]
    SP2[Inner Flow Setpoint from PC1] --> CJ2[Inner Summing Junction]
    FT1[Flow Transmitter FT] --> PV2[Inner PV]
    PV2 --> CJ2
    CJ2 --> FC1[Inner Flow Controller FIC]
    FC1 --> VALVE[Throttling Control Valve]
    VALVE --> PROCESS[Reactor or Distillation Tower]
    PROCESS --> PT1
    PROCESS --> FT1
    PC1 -.-> DISPLAY[Operator HMI Display]
    FC1 -.-> DISPLAY
```

## 4.3 Self-Operated Pressure Regulator (Field-Mounted)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIPELINE[Process Pipeline High Pressure] --> INLET[Inlet Port]
    INLET --> DIAPH[Domed Diaphragm Sensor]
    DIAPH --> PUSH[Force on Stem]
    PUSH --> STEM[Valve Stem Direction]
    SPRING[Adjustable Range Spring] --> PUSH
    STEM --> VALVE[Throttle Plug and Seat]
    VALVE --> OUTLET[Outlet to Downstream]
    OUTLET --> PIPELINE2[Lower Pressure Process Side]
    DIAPH -.-> SENSE[Diaphragm senses downstream pressure feedback]
    SENSE --> PUSH
```

## 4.4 Pressure Loop with Safety Interlock and PSV

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SOURCE[Pressure Source Compressor or Pump] --> VESSEL[Pressurized Vessel]
    VESSEL --> PT[PT Pressure Transmitter]
    PT --> PIC[PIC Pressure Controller]
    PIC --> PCV[PCV Control Valve Throttling]
    PCV --> DOWNSTREAM[Downstream Process]
    VESSEL --> PSH[PSH Pressure Switch High Trip]
    PSH --> ESD[ESD Emergency Shutdown Logic]
    VESSEL --> PSV[PSV Pressure Safety Valve Mechanical]
    PSV --> RELIEF[Relief to Flare or Vent]
    PIC -.-> HMI[Operator HMI DCS]
    ESD -.-> BLOCK[Block Valves Close on ESD]
    PSH -.-> HMI
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture: Pressure Control in a Petrochemical Distillation Column

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Column[Distillation Column]
        TC1[Top Tray Temperature TT]
        TC2[Reboiler Temperature TT]
        LC1[Reflux Drum Level LT]
        PC1[Overhead Vapor Line PT]
    end

    subgraph ControlLoops[Pressure and Cascade Loops]
        PIC1[PIC Overhead Pressure Master]
        FIC1[FIC Condenser Cooling Water Slave]
        VALVECW[CW Throttling Valve]
        VALVEBP[By-pass Valve]
    end

    PC1 --> PIC1
    PIC1 --> FIC1
    FIC1 --> VALVECW
    LC1 -.-> FIC1
    VALVECW --> Column
    PC1 -.-> VALVEBP
    PSV1[Overhead PSV] -.-> Column
    Column --> PSV1
    PSV1 --> FLARE[Relief to Flare Header]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Cascade Diagram:** The **master (outer) loop** is pressure (PIC1). The **slave (inner) loop** is cooling water flow (FIC1). When overhead pressure rises, PIC1 raises the setpoint of FIC1, which opens the cooling water valve wider, increasing condensation, dropping the pressure. This is the **canonical pressure-temperature cascade** found in nearly every distillation column in the world.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define pressure control. List any four industrial applications of pressure control in petrochemical industries. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pressure control is a closed-loop feedback control strategy in which a pressure transmitter (PT) senses the process pressure, a controller (PIC) compares it to a setpoint, and a final control element (PCV) adjusts flow to maintain the process variable at the desired value.

**Four applications (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Reflux drum pressure control** in distillation columns to stabilize overhead vapor flow
2. **Reactor pressure control** in polymerization (LDPE/HDPE) to manage exothermic reaction kinetics
3. **Steam header pressure reducing stations (PRDS)** between HP and MP steam mains
4. **Compressor anti-surge control** to prevent surge condition and mechanical damage

*(Alternative valid applications: storage tank blanketing, LNG boil-off gas management, hydrocracker pressure control.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between self-operated and pneumatically operated pressure regulators. Give one advantage of each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Self-Operated | Pneumatically Operated |
|--------|---------------|------------------------|
| Power source | Process pressure (mechanical) | Instrument air (3–15 psig) |
| Controller | None — direct diaphragm/stem linkage | External pneumatic controller |
| Setpoint | Spring adjustment (manual) | Adjustable via setpoint dial |
| Accuracy | $\pm 2$–$5\%$ of span | $\pm 0.5$–$1\%$ of span |
| Best application | Field, no air, low criticality | DCS-integrated, high criticality |

**Advantage of self-operated (0.5 mark):** Fail-safe — operates without external power, suitable for hazardous and remote areas.
**Advantage of pneumatically operated (0.5 mark):** Remote setpoint change, high accuracy, integrated with central control system.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries 14 marks. There is internal choice between two questions (Q1 or Q2). Each question typically has sub-parts (a) and (b), with 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `Option 1`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a cascade pressure control loop in a distillation column. Identify the master and slave variables. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the cascade block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.

**Working explanation (4 marks):**
- The **master loop** measures the **overhead vapor pressure (PV₁)** of the distillation column using a pressure transmitter (PT) located downstream of the condenser.
- The output of the **Pressure Indicating Controller (PIC₁)** is NOT sent directly to a valve; instead, it serves as the **remote setpoint (RSP)** of a secondary flow controller (FIC₁).
- The **slave loop** measures the **cooling water flow rate (PV₂)** through the condenser using a flow transmitter (FT).
- The **flow controller (FIC₁)** compares the cooling water flow setpoint (received from PIC₁) with the actual flow and adjusts the **cooling water throttling valve** accordingly.
- When overhead pressure rises (disturbance), PIC₁ raises the flow setpoint → FIC₁ opens the CW valve more → more condensation → pressure drops back to setpoint.

**Identification (1 mark):**
- Master variable: Overhead column pressure
- Slave variable: Condenser cooling water flow

---

**Q. (b)** A pressurized storage sphere contains nitrogen at 25 bar (gauge) and 30°C. The sphere has a volume of 1000 m³. Calculate: (i) the mass of nitrogen stored, (ii) the pressure if the temperature rises to 50°C at constant volume, and (iii) the new pressure if 10% of the gas is vented (mass removed) at 50°C. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 25 + 1.013 = 26.013$ bar absolute $= 2.6013 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $T_1 = 30 + 273 = 303$ K
- $V = 1000$ m³
- $M_{N_2} = 28$ g/mol = 0.028 kg/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**(i) Mass of nitrogen stored (2 marks):**

Using ideal gas law: $P_1 V = n R T_1$

$$n = \frac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \frac{2.6013 \times 10^6 \times 1000}{8.314 \times 303}$$

$$n = \frac{2.6013 \times 10^9}{2519.14} = 1.0327 \times 10^6 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_1 = n \cdot M = 1.0327 \times 10^6 \times 0.028$$

$$\boxed{m_1 = 28{,}916 \text{ kg} \approx 28.9 \text{ tonnes of N}_2}$$

**(ii) New pressure at 50°C (constant V, m) (2.5 marks):**

Using Gay-Lussac's law: $\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 26.013 \cdot \frac{323}{303}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 27.72 \text{ bar absolute} = 26.71 \text{ bar gauge}}$$

**(iii) New pressure after 10% venting (2.5 marks):**

After venting 10%, $m_3 = 0.90 \times m_1$

Using combined gas law: $\dfrac{P_1 V}{m_1 T_1} = \dfrac{P_3 V}{m_3 T_2}$

$$P_3 = P_2 \cdot \frac{m_3}{m_2} = 27.72 \times 0.90$$

$$\boxed{P_3 = 24.95 \text{ bar absolute} = 23.94 \text{ bar gauge}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `Option 2`

**Q. (a)** Explain the construction and working of a capacitance-type pressure transmitter. State its advantages. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A capacitance-type pressure transmitter uses a **pair of capacitor plates** — one is a fixed reference plate, the other is a **deflecting diaphragm plate** that moves in response to applied pressure.
- The process pressure is applied to a **separately sealed diaphragm**, which transmits force via silicone oil fill fluid to the **sensing diaphragm** (the moving capacitor plate).
- The sensing diaphragm is typically made of **Inconel or Hastelloy** for corrosion resistance.
- A **resonant circuit** detects the change in capacitance ($\Delta C$) and converts it to a 4–20 mA DC output signal via an ASIC-based electronics module.
- Both gauge and differential pressure (DP) versions exist.

**Working (3 marks):**
- At zero pressure, the diaphragm sits at a baseline position and the capacitor has a reference capacitance $C_0$.
- When process pressure increases, the diaphragm deflects toward (or away from) the fixed plate, changing the gap distance $d$.
- Since $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon A}{d}$, a small deflection $\Delta d$ produces a proportional change in capacitance.
- The electronics convert this $\Delta C$ to a linearized 4–20 mA signal proportional to pressure.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- High accuracy ($\pm 0.075\%$ of span)
- Excellent long-term stability
- Corrosion-resistant wetted parts
- Suitable for high static pressure (up to 700 bar)

---

**Q. (b)** A pressure control loop has a process gain $K_p = 2.0$ %PV/%CO and uses a PI controller with $K_c = 1.5$ and $\tau_I = 5$ min. A load disturbance of 10% is introduced. Calculate: (i) the steady-state offset for proportional-only action, and (ii) the steady-state error with PI control. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $K_p = 2.0$, $K_c = 1.5$, $\tau_I = 5$ min, Load = 10% (10% change in disturbance variable)

**(i) Steady-state offset with P-only action (3.5 marks):**

From Section 3.2 derivation, the steady-state error for a P-only controller is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{\text{Load}}{1 + K_p K_c}$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{10}{1 + (2.0)(1.5)} = \frac{10}{1 + 3.0} = \frac{10}{4.0}$$

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = 2.5\% \text{ offset}}$$

This is the **permanent bias** the P-only controller cannot remove.

**(ii) Steady-state error with PI control (3.5 marks):**

The integral action in PI control **integrates the error continuously** until the error becomes zero. Mathematically, at steady state:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = 0$$

This is because the integral term $\frac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int_0^\infty e(\tau) d\tau$ must remain finite (it's the required controller output change to offset the load), and the only way for that integral to converge to a finite value is for $e(t) \to 0$.

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = 0\% \text{ (no offset with PI control)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B type questions):**
> - **Do NOT confuse the load disturbance with the setpoint change.** The formula $e_{ss} = \frac{\text{Load}}{1 + K_p K_c}$ applies to load disturbance. For setpoint change, the formula is $e_{ss} = \frac{\Delta SP}{1 + K_p K_c}$ (only for P-only; PI gives zero offset for setpoint changes too).
> - **Always show the derivation formula before plugging in numbers.** Marks are split: 1 mark for correct formula, 1.5 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer.
> - **Do not forget to state the assumption** of linear, self-regulating process.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pressure Control**

- ✅ Pressure control is a **feedback loop** (PT → PIC → PCV → Process)
- ✅ **Gas pressure** = integrating + first-order lag (large $\tau$)
- ✅ **Liquid pressure** = pure gain (fast, no capacitance)
- ✅ **PI control** is the industrial default for pressure loops
- ✅ **P-only control** is acceptable for liquid line pressure (small offset OK)
- ✅ **Cascade control** is used for high-purity / tight pressure regulation
- ✅ **Cascade master**: pressure, **slave**: flow or temperature
- ✅ **Self-operated regulators**: no air, no power, fail-safe, $\pm 2$–$5\%$ accuracy
- ✅ **Capacitance transmitter**: $\pm 0.075\%$ accuracy, high static pressure
- ✅ **API 520 relief**: 10% overpressure accumulation for fire case
- ✅ **Ideal gas law**: $PV = nRT$ — basis of gas pressure dynamics
- ✅ **Steady-state offset** for P-only: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{L}{1 + K_p K_c}$
- ✅ **PI control eliminates offset** (integral action drives $e_{ss} \to 0$)
- ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure**: $P = \rho g h$ — important for DP level instruments
- ✅ **Equal percentage trim** preferred for gas pressure PCVs
- ✅ **Compressor anti-surge** is a critical pressure control application
- ✅ **PSV + ESD** are the safety layers, not the pressure controller
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Control of Heat Exchangers and Evaporators

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heat Exchanger — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Exchanger (H.E.)** is a process unit designed to transfer thermal energy between two or more fluids (hot and cold streams) at different temperatures, without allowing them to mix directly. The driving force for heat transfer is the **temperature gradient** ($\Delta T$).

In petrochemical industries, heat exchangers perform critical duties such as:
- **Pre-heating** of crude oil prior to atmospheric/vacuum distillation
- **Condensing** overhead vapors from distillation columns
- **Cooling** of product streams to storage specifications
- **Heat recovery** from hot flue gases and reactor effluents

The most common industrial configuration is the **Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)**, characterized by a bundle of tubes enclosed within a cylindrical shell.

## 1.2 Evaporator — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Evaporator** is a specialized heat-transfer device used to concentrate a solution (typically a non-volatile solute dissolved in a volatile solvent like water) by vaporizing the solvent. The vapor is separated from the concentrated liquor.

Petrochemical-grade evaporators are deployed in:
- Concentration of **caustic soda (NaOH)** and **glycerol**
- Recovery of **black liquor** in integrated pulp-paper-petrochemical plants
- **Effluent treatment** for zero-liquid-discharge (ZLD) compliance
- Concentration of **amines and glycols** in gas-processing units

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen-Tap" Mental Model

Imagine a domestic **geyser (water heater)**. The *cold water* entering the geyser is the **process fluid**, the *heating coil* carrying hot water is the **utility stream**, and the *shower temperature knob* is the **final control element**.

If the shower water is too hot, you don't shut off the geyser entirely (that would waste energy) — instead, you **mix cold water through a bypass valve** to bring the temperature back to a comfortable range. This is exactly the philosophy behind **bypass control** of heat exchangers.

For evaporators, imagine a **kitchen pot of milk on a stove**:
- The pot is the **evaporator body**
- The stove flame is the **steam supply** (utility)
- The thickening milk is the **process concentrate**
- The rising steam from the milk is the **vapor product**
- The vessel is **fed continuously** with fresh milk at the same rate milk is concentrated, keeping the level constant — analogous to **level control** in evaporators.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------|------|
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $U$ | $500 - 1500$ | $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ |
| Specific Heat of Water | $C_p$ | $4.186$ | $\text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$ |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization (Water at 1 atm) | $\lambda$ | $2257$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ |
| Standard Reference Temperature | $T_{ref}$ | $25$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| LMTD Correction Factor | $F_T$ | $0.75 - 1.0$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Style Memory Trick:** The three physical quantities that govern *every* heat-transfer calculation are **$\dot{m}$ (mass flow)**, **$C_p$ (specific heat)**, and **$\Delta T$ (temperature difference)**. Their product, $\dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$, gives the **heat duty $Q$ in kW**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Control Objectives for Heat Exchangers

The **primary control objective** in a heat exchanger is to maintain the **outlet temperature of the process stream** at a desired setpoint despite disturbances in:
- Inlet temperature of process fluid
- Inlet temperature of utility
- Flow rates of either stream
- Fouling on heat-transfer surfaces (long-term disturbance)

### The Four Canonical Control Strategies

**Strategy 1: Utility Flow Manipulation (Direct Control)**
- The **control valve** is placed on the utility (steam/cooling water) line.
- **When to use:** Utility stream is much smaller in capacity than the process stream.
- **Limitation:** Affects the *heat-transfer rate* nonlinearly because of logarithmic-mean-temperature-difference (LMTD) behavior.

**Strategy 2: Process Bypass Control**
- A fraction of the process stream bypasses the heat exchanger via a three-way valve.
- **When to use:** When the utility stream is expensive or when temperature changes must be rapid.
- **Limitation:** Requires extra piping and pump head.

**Strategy 3: Utility Bypass Control**
- Used in **condensers** where the entire utility side is cooled by diverting part of the coolant.
- Particularly common in **reflux condensers** of distillation columns.

**Strategy 4: Condensate Throttling (For Steam-Heated HEs)**
- A control valve on the **condensate outlet** modulates the steam-side pressure and hence the saturation temperature.
- The valve **does not touch the steam directly**, thus avoiding erosion.
- **KTU favourite strategy** for reboiler temperature control.

## 2.2 Control Objectives for Evaporators

The evaporator has **three interdependent control loops**:

1. **Concentration Loop** — Maintain product solids/refractive index (often the slowest loop).
2. **Level Loop** — Maintain evaporator body liquid level (intermediate speed).
3. **Pressure/Temperature Loop** — Maintain vapor-space conditions (fastest loop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Evaporators operate with **inverse-response** behavior. A sudden increase in steam flow initially *lowers* product concentration because the cold condensate flashes and dilutes the liquor before evaporation kicks in. This is a classic question topic in KTU board exams.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer rate (process stream side) | $\text{kW}$ |
| 2 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | Heat duty via Overall Transfer Equation | $\text{kW}$ |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference | $^\circ\text{C or K}$ |
| 4 | $F_T = \dfrac{\sqrt{R^2 + 1} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{1 - S}{1 - R \cdot S}\right)}{(R - 1) \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{2 - S \cdot (R + 1 - \sqrt{R^2 + 1})}{2 - S \cdot (R + 1 + \sqrt{R^2 + 1})}\right)}$ | LMTD correction factor for multi-pass shell-and-tube HEs | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{vapor} = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda}$ | Vapor generation rate in evaporator | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{feed} \cdot x_{feed} = \dot{m}_{product} \cdot x_{product}$ | Solute mass balance across evaporator | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{feed} = \dot{m}_{product} + \dot{m}_{vapor}$ | Total mass balance across evaporator | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 8 | $E = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{vapor}}{\dot{m}_{steam}}$ | Evaporator economy (kg vapor / kg steam) | $\text{kg/kg}$ |
| 9 | $\text{Capacity} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Specific heat flux | $\text{kW/m}^2$ |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho}{F}$ | Residence time in evaporator body | $\text{s}$ |

> **Notation key:** $R = \dfrac{T_{hot,in} - T_{hot,out}}{T_{cold,out} - T_{cold,in}}$ and $S = \dfrac{T_{cold,out} - T_{cold,in}}{T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,in}}$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Crude Preheat Train:** Series of heat exchangers recover waste heat from product streams to preheat crude oil. Each unit must be controlled to avoid *coking* (fouling) at temperatures exceeding **$350\,^\circ\text{C}$**.
- **Steam-Heated Reboilers:** Use condensate throttling to control bottoms temperature; saves **$5-10\%$ steam** compared to direct steam throttling.
- **Multiple-Effect Evaporators (MEE):** The vapor from one effect becomes the heating medium for the next, achieving economies of **$3-5$ kg vapor/kg steam** in sugar and caustic industries.
- **Heat-Integrated Distillation Columns:** Require careful *pressure-compensated temperature* control to maintain product purity.
