> 5. **Fouling factor application:** Apply it before computing the area, not after — the design U must be used consistently.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Rapid Checklist for Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchangers:**

- ☐ **Resistance-in-series** governs U; include convection, wall conduction, and fouling.
- ☐ **LMTD** is the correct mean driving force for linear (no phase change) LLHX problems.
- ☐ **Counter-current** arrangement yields the highest possible LMTD and is the design ideal.
- ☐ **Co-current** is used only when **outlet temperature cross-over** must be avoided (e.g., temperature-sensitive fluids).
- ☐ **ε–NTU** is preferred when outlet temperatures are unknown or fluid properties vary strongly.
- ☐ **Fouling factor** must be added to the clean resistance before design U is computed; use design U to find area.
- ☐ **F correction factor** is mandatory for **multi-pass** shell-and-tube exchangers; verify $F \geq 0.75$ (KTU design rule).
- ☐ **Maximum possible heat transfer** $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$; effectiveness always lies in $[0, 1]$.
- ☐ In petrochemical service, expect **fouling resistances of 0.0001 – 0.0008 m²·K/W** depending on fluid.
- ☐ **Plate heat exchangers** offer higher U (up to 3000 W/m²·K) but lower pressure ratings.
- ☐ **Spiral heat exchangers** handle fouling-prone and viscous liquids; use when S&T would plug.
- ☐ **P&ID essentials** for LLHX control: TT on hot outlet, FT on cold inlet, FCV on cold inlet for cascade temperature control.
- ☐ **Energy balance** is the foundation: $\dot m_h c_{p,h} \Delta T_h = \dot m_c c_{p,c} \Delta T_c$ — always verify.
- ☐ **Units audit:** $Q$ (W), $U$ (W/m²·K), $A$ (m²), $\Delta T$ (K), $c_p$ (J/kg·K), $\dot m$ (kg/s).
- ☐ Common **petrochemical applications**: crude preheat train, hydrocracker feed-effluent, amine cooler, lube-oil cooler, naphtha reboiler.
- ☐ KTU-favourite formulas: $Q = U A \Delta T_{lmtd} F$, $\varepsilon = (1 - e^{-NTU(1-C_r)})/(1 - C_r e^{-NTU(1-C_r)})$, $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k_w} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}$.
- ☐ Remember to **double-check the second law**: $T_{h,o} > T_{c,o}$ always; otherwise the result is unphysical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Heaters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steam Heaters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Steam Heater** is a specific class of **process heat exchanger** employed extensively in petrochemical, refinery, and chemical process plants, in which **saturated or superheated steam** is used as the **primary heating utility (hot utility)** to transfer thermal energy to a process fluid (the cold stream). The fundamental operating principle relies on the **isothermal phase change (condensation)** of steam, which releases a very large quantity of **latent heat of vaporization ($\lambda$)** at a constant saturation temperature ($T_{sat}$), which is dictated by the operating pressure of the steam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (for 3-mark and 14-mark answers):**
> A steam heater is a heat transfer device that uses the latent heat released during the condensation of steam to raise the temperature of a process fluid. Steam heaters are predominantly of the **shell-and-tube** type, although **plate-type** and **direct steam injection (DSI)** configurations are also used depending on process requirements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are boiling water in a pressure cooker to cook food. The steam trapped inside the cooker stays at a **constant, fixed temperature** (around 120 °C for a typical pressure cooker), no matter how vigorously it boils. You, the cook, simply rely on this *predictable, constant-temperature steam* to cook your food evenly.

A steam heater works on the **exact same principle**:
- The **pressure cooker** is the **steam chest / shell side** of the heater.
- The **food** is the **process fluid** flowing through the tubes.
- The **constant-temperature steam** is the heating utility that releases its **latent heat** when it condenses back to water, thereby heating the process fluid to a controlled target temperature.
- The **condensate** (water formed after steam gives up its heat) is drained out, just like you would release steam periodically from the cooker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why steam is uniquely suited as a heating medium in petrochemical industries:**
> 1. **High latent heat of vaporization** ($\lambda \approx$ 2200 kJ/kg at atmospheric pressure) — a small mass of steam carries a huge amount of energy.
> 2. **Isothermal heat delivery** — steam gives up its heat at a *constant* temperature, making temperature control simple and accurate.
> 3. **Wide range of operating temperatures** — by varying steam pressure, you can select any saturation temperature from 100 °C (1 bar) up to 374 °C (220.6 bar, critical point).
> 4. **Ease of transportation** — steam is piped easily and is non-toxic, non-flammable, and inexpensive.
> 5. **Self-pumping action** — condensation creates a slight vacuum that helps in condensate drainage.
> 6. **Excellent heat transfer coefficient** — condensing steam has one of the highest film coefficients (typically **h $\approx$ 5000–15000 W/m²·K**).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following physical constants and standard design metrics are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 numerical problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of steam (1 atm) | $\lambda$ | **2257** | kJ/kg |
| Latent heat of steam (10 bar) | $\lambda$ | **2019** | kJ/kg |
| Specific heat of water | $C_p$ | **4.186** | kJ/kg·K |
| Specific heat of crude oil (typical) | $C_p$ | **1.9 – 2.5** | kJ/kg·K |
| Steam condensing film coefficient | $h_{steam}$ | **5000 – 15000** | W/m²·K |
| Process-side film coefficient (oil) | $h_{oil}$ | **200 – 1000** | W/m²·K |
| Overall heat transfer coefficient (clean) | $U$ | **300 – 1500** | W/m²·K |
| Steam saturation temp at 4.76 bar | $T_{sat}$ | **151.8** | °C |
| Critical point of water | $T_c, P_c$ | **374 °C, 220.6 bar** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"7 times rule"** of steam design (a frequently tested KTU fact): Condensing steam typically delivers **7 to 10 times** more heat than the same mass of water cooling from 100 °C to 0 °C. This is why steam is preferred over hot water for process heating.

### 1.4 Classification of Steam Heaters (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

Petrochemical steam heaters are classified by **physical configuration** and **fluid contact mode**:

1. **Shell-and-Tube Steam Heaters** — The most common type in refineries. Process fluid flows inside tubes; steam condenses on the shell side. Used as **feedwater heaters, crude preheaters, reboilers**.
2. **Plate-Type Steam Heaters** — Compact, high-efficiency, used where footprint is critical (e.g., offshore platforms).
3. **Direct Steam Injection (DSI) Heaters** — Steam is injected directly into the process fluid via a sparger. Used when contamination of the process by condensate is acceptable.
4. **Kettle Reboilers** — A variant of shell-and-tube where a vapor space is provided on the shell side for steam/vapor disengagement.
5. **Steam Coil Heaters** — Simple helical or bayonet coils immersed in a tank of process liquid (e.g., bitumen storage tanks).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and the "How"

The operational sequence of a steam heater follows a **deterministic, four-step thermodynamic cycle**:

1. **Step 1 — Steam Admission (Sensible + Latent Heat Input):** Saturated or superheated steam at pressure $P_s$ enters the shell side through a **steam control valve (FCV-101)**. The steam is at its saturation temperature $T_{sat}(P_s)$, which is uniquely fixed by the pressure (Clausius-Clapeyron relation).
2. **Step 2 — Film Condensation (Latent Heat Transfer):** When steam contacts the cooler outer tube wall, it forms a thin liquid condensate film. Heat transfers through this film by conduction. The film coefficient $h_{steam}$ is extremely high (5000–15000 W/m²·K) because the film is thin and the temperature gradient across it is small.
3. **Step 3 — Conduction Through Tube Wall and Inner Film:** Heat passes through the **tube wall** (carbon steel, stainless steel, or alloy) and through the **process-side boundary layer** to the bulk process fluid.
4. **Step 4 — Condensate Discharge (Critical for Sustained Operation):** The liquid condensate must be **continuously and rapidly removed** by a **steam trap**; otherwise it will flood the heat transfer surface, reducing the effective area $A$ and the overall $U$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most critical design insight:** Steam heaters operate at **effectively constant hot-side temperature** ($T_{hot} = T_{sat}$ throughout the shell, since condensation is isothermal). This is the **defining mathematical advantage** that simplifies all subsequent heat-transfer equations.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates **all** equations required for solving Module 4 steam-heater problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. **No vertical bars are used inside any table cell to preserve Markdown compatibility.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat duty (energy balance) | $Q \;=\; \dot{m}_c \, C_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | $Q$ in W; $\dot{m}$ in kg/s |
| 2 | Heat duty (steam side) | $Q \;=\; \dot{m}_s \, \lambda_s$ | $\lambda_s$ = latent heat, J/kg |
| 3 | Steam mass flow rate | $\dot{m}_s \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_s}$ | kg/s |
| 4 | Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\text{LMTD} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$ | $T_{hot}$ = $T_{sat}$ (constant) |
| 5 | Condenser-type LMTD (special) | $\text{LMTD} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_{sat} - T_{c,in}}{T_{sat} - T_{c,out}}\right)}$ | For one shell, two tube passes with steam on shell side |
| 6 | Heat transfer rate (sizing) | $Q \;=\; U \, A \, \text{LMTD}$ | $U$ in W/m²·K, $A$ in m² |
| 7 | Heat transfer area | $A \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}}$ | m² |
| 8 | Overall $U$ from resistances | $\dfrac{1}{U} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{h_{steam}} + \dfrac{x}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_{process}} + R_f$ | $R_f$ = fouling factor, m²·K/W |
| 9 | Condensate film coefficient (Nusselt) | $h_{film} \;=\; 0.729 \left[\dfrac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda \, k_l^3}{\mu_l \, \Delta T_{film} \, L}\right]^{0.25}$ | Vertical/horizontal tube |
| 10 | Steam quality / dryness fraction | $x \;=\; \dfrac{m_{vapor}}{m_{vapor} + m_{liquid}}$ | 1.0 = dry saturated |
| 11 | Energy of superheated steam | $Q_{sup} \;=\; \dot{m} \, C_{p,v} \, (T_{sup} - T_{sat})$ | Sensible heat above $T_{sat}$ |
| 12 | Effectiveness (NTU method) | $\varepsilon \;=\; \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{sat} - T_{c,in}}$ | For a condenser $C_{max} = \infty$, $\varepsilon$ approach |
| 13 | Steam trap capacity | $\dot{m}_{trap} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{trap}}{\lambda_s + C_p (T_{sat} - T_{disch})}$ | kg/h |
| 14 | Control valve $\text{C}_\text{v}$ | $C_v \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{N_1 \cdot P_1 \cdot Y \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{x}{T_1 Z}}}$ | ISA sizing for steam |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note on Sign Convention:** In KTU 2024 valuation, $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ in the LMTD equation are **always taken as the larger and smaller temperature difference, respectively**, at the two ends of the exchanger. Reversing this convention is a common error that costs 1–2 marks.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Steam heaters are the **backbone of process heating** in:

- **Crude Distillation Units (CDU):** Preheating desalted crude oil from ambient (~40 °C) to 200–250 °C before the desalter and furnace.
- **Catalytic Reforming Units:** Re-boiling feed to reactors and stabilizing columns.
- **Hydrocracker / Hydrotreater Feed Preheating:** Bringing feed to reaction temperature using medium-pressure (MP) steam.
- **Storage Tank Heating:** Steam coils maintain bitumen, heavy fuel oil, and wax above pour point.
- **Air Preheaters for Boilers:** Heating combustion air using flue-gas/lp-steam economizers.
- **Distillation Column Reboilers:** Providing the vapor boil-up needed for separation in crude, vacuum, and fractionator columns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sustainability context (NEP 2020 alignment):** Modern petrochemical plants use **Heat Recovery Steam Generators (HRSGs)** to capture waste heat and generate steam, then route this steam to feedwater steam heaters — a direct energy-cascade integration that is increasingly examined in KTU 2024 design problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Balance for Steam Consumption

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Typical):**
A refinery uses a steam heater to preheat crude oil from $T_{c,in} = 80$ °C to $T_{c,out} = 160$ °C. The crude flow rate is $\dot{m}_c = 50{,}000$ kg/hr, and the specific heat is $C_{p,c} = 2.2$ kJ/kg·K. Saturated steam is available at $P_s = 10$ bar gauge ($\approx 11$ bar absolute), where $T_{sat} = 184.1$ °C and $\lambda_s = 2014$ kJ/kg. The overall heat transfer coefficient (dirty) is $U = 500$ W/m²·K.

**Tasks:** (a) Calculate the heat duty $Q$. (b) Calculate the steam mass flow rate $\dot{m}_s$. (c) Calculate the LMTD. (d) Calculate the required heat transfer area $A$.

---

**Part (a) — Heat Duty Calculation**

The energy absorbed by the process (cold) stream must equal the energy released by the condensing (hot) steam. We start with the cold-side energy balance.

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}}{3600 \text{ s/hr}} = 13.889 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q = \dot{m}_c \, C_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

$$
Q = 13.889 \text{ kg/s} \times 2.2 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times (160 - 80) \text{ K}
$$

$$
Q = 13.889 \times 2.2 \times 80 = 2444.45 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 2.444 \text{ MW} = 8.80 \text{ GJ/hr}}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the total thermal power that must cross the tube wall every second. This single value drives the rest of the design.

---

**Part (b) — Steam Consumption**

From the steam-side energy balance, the heat released by the condensing steam equals $Q$.

$$
\dot{m}_s \, \lambda_s = Q
$$

$$
\dot{m}_s = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_s = \frac{2444.45 \text{ kW}}{2014 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_s = 1.2137 \text{ kg/s} = 4369.2 \text{ kg/hr}}
$$

**Explanation:** Every second, about 1.21 kg of steam condenses to deliver the required heat. Note that the steam-side mass flow is much smaller than the crude flow, illustrating the **high energy density of latent heat**.

---

**Part (c) — LMTD Calculation**

Since steam condenses isothermally on the shell side, $T_{hot} = T_{sat} = 184.1$ °C (constant). The temperature profile is therefore of the **condenser type**.

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{sat} - T_{c,in} = 184.1 - 80 = 104.1 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{sat} - T_{c,out} = 184.1 - 160 = 24.1 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{104.1 - 24.1}{\ln(104.1 / 24.1)}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{80.0}{\ln(4.320)}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{80.0}{1.4635}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LMTD} = 54.66 \text{ °C}}
$$

**Explanation:** The arithmetic mean is (104.1 + 24.1)/2 = 64.1 °C, but because the temperature differences are unequal, the **logarithmic mean is smaller** and is the correct effective driving force.

---

**Part (d) — Heat Transfer Area**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}}
$$

$$
A = \frac{2.444 \times 10^6 \text{ W}}{500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K} \times 54.66 \text{ K}}
$$

$$
A = \frac{2.444 \times 10^6}{27{,}330}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 89.4 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**Explanation:** This 89.4 m² of heat transfer surface is the **bundle area** required. In practice, this is split among many tubes of, say, 25 mm OD and 5 m length, giving the actual tube count:

$$
N_{tubes} = \frac{A}{\pi \, d_o \, L} = \frac{89.4}{\pi \times 0.025 \times 5} \approx 228 \text{ tubes}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Correction Factor $F$ for Multi-Pass Shell-and-Tube Exchangers

In real plants, steam heaters are rarely single-pass. The standard TEMA configuration is **one shell pass, two tube passes (1-2)**, which breaks the pure counter-current flow assumed in the LMTD derivation. The actual mean temperature difference is:

$$
\Delta T_{actual} = F \cdot \text{LMTD}_{countercurrent}
$$

where $F$ is the **LMTD correction factor**, read from standard charts as a function of two dimensionless parameters:

$$
P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{sat} - T_{c,in}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{T_{sat} - T_{sat}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = 0 \quad \text{(since } T_{sat} \text{ is constant, R = 0)}
$$

For $R = 0$, the correction factor $F$ is **always 1.0** for any number of tube passes. This is a **special property of condenser-type heat exchangers**, and it is the most-tested "trick question" in KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight (High-Yield):** Because steam is condensing at a constant temperature, the LMTD correction factor $F = 1.0$ always, regardless of TEMA configuration. This means you **never** have to use $F$-charts for steam heaters, which simplifies design problems considerably. Examiners often award 2 marks for stating this explicitly.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steam Quality Adjustment

If the inlet steam is **wet** (quality $x < 1$), the available latent heat per kg is reduced:

$$
Q = \dot{m} \left[ x \cdot \lambda_s + (1-x) C_{p,l} (T_{sat} - T_{feed}) \right]
$$

If the steam is **superheated** (temperature $T_{sup} > T_{sat}$), the available energy is:

$$
Q = \dot{m} \left[ \lambda_s + C_{p,v} (T_{sup} - T_{sat}) \right]
$$

**Numerical Example:** If 10 bar steam is supplied at 250 °C (superheated), with $C_{p,v} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K, $T_{sat} = 184.1$ °C:

$$
Q_{per\,kg} = 2014 + 2.1 \times (250 - 184.1) = 2014 + 138.4 = 2152.4 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

The **gain from superheat** is 138.4/2014 = **6.9 %**, often neglected in rough sizing but counted in precise KTU problems.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Numerical Ranges in KTU Problems

| Variable | Symbol | Typical KTU Range | Effect of Increase on Heater |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steam pressure | $P_s$ | **3 – 40 bar** | Raises $T_{sat}$, increases $\Delta T$, reduces $A$ |
| Steam mass flow | $\dot{m}_s$ | **0.1 – 100 kg/s** | Directly proportional to $Q$ |
| Crude inlet temp | $T_{c,in}$ | **30 – 150 °C** | Higher $T_{c,in}$ lowers $Q$ and steam demand |
| Crude outlet temp | $T_{c,out}$ | **100 – 350 °C** | Higher $T_{c,out}$ raises $Q$ |
| Overall $U$ | $U$ | **200 – 1500 W/m²K** | Higher $U$ (clean tubes) reduces $A$ |
| LMTD | LMTD | **20 – 100 °C** | Higher LMTD reduces $A$ |
| Steam quality | $x$ | **0.85 – 1.0** | Lower $x$ → more steam needed for same $Q$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of a Crude Oil Steam Heater with Instrumentation

The following Mermaid block presents the **P&ID-style block-level architecture** of a typical steam-heater skid, including all major instruments as per ISA-5.1 tag conventions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Crude Oil Inlet\nTci 80 C\nFCR 101] --> P1[Strainer\nS 101]
    P1 --> P2[Flow Transmitter\nFT 101]
    P2 --> P3[Flow Indicator Controller\nFIC 101]
    P3 --> P4[Shutoff Valve\nXV 101]
    P4 --> P5[Steam Heater Shell and Tube\nE 101]
    TTin[RTD Temperature Sensor\nTE 101] --> TICin[Temperature Indicator\nTI 101]
    P5 --> TTin
    P5 --> TTout[RTD Temperature Sensor\nTE 102]
    TTout --> TICout[Temperature Indicator\nTI 102]
    TTout --> TIC[Temperature Indicator Controller\nTIC 102]
    TIC -. cascade signal .-> P3
    P5 --> P6[Heated Crude Outlet\nTco 160 C]
    P5 --> PT1[Pressure Transmitter Steam Side\nPT 101]
    PT1 --> PIC[Pressure Indicator Controller\nPIC 101]
    PIC --> SCV1[Steam Control Valve\nFCV 101]
    SCV1 --> P5
    P5 --> STR[Steam Trap\nSTR 101]
    STR --> LT1[Condensate Level Transmitter\nLT 101]
    LT1 --> LIC[Level Controller\nLIC 101]
    LIC --> LCV1[Condensate Drain Valve\nLCV 101]
    LCV1 --> COND[Condensate to Flash Drum\nV 101]
```

**Description of flow logic:**

1. **Crude oil** enters at the left, passes through a **strainer (S-101)** to remove particulates.
2. The flow is measured by **FT-101** and controlled by **FIC-101**, which cascades to the steam flow.
3. The crude enters the **shell-and-tube exchanger E-101**, where it is heated by condensing steam.
4. **Inlet temperature TE-101** and **outlet temperature TE-102** are measured by **RTDs (Pt100)**.
5. **TE-102** is the primary controlled variable; its controller **TIC-102** cascades a setpoint to **FIC-101** (master-slave cascade).
6. **Steam supply pressure** is monitored by **PT-101**; **PIC-101** modulates the **steam control valve FCV-101**.
7. **Condensate** exits through a **steam trap STR-101**; level in the trap is controlled by **LIC-101** via **LCV-101** to prevent condensate flooding.

### 4.2 Cascade Control Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Primary[Primary Loop - Master]
        SP1[Outlet Temperature Setpoint Tco] --> SUM1
        PV1[Outlet Temperature Tco measured] --> SUM1
        SUM1 --> TC[Temperature Controller TIC 102]
        TC -->|Remote Setpoint| SUM2
    end
    subgraph Secondary[Secondary Loop - Slave]
        SP2[Internal Flow Setpoint] --> SUM2
        PV2[Steam Flow Fs measured] --> SUM2
        SUM2 --> FC[Flow Controller FIC 101]
        FC --> VLV[Steam Control Valve FCV 101]
        VLV --> PRCS[Steam Heater Process]
    end
    PRCS -. steam flow .-> PV2
    PRCS -. heated oil .-> PV1
```

**Explanation of cascade logic:**

- The **outer (primary) loop** compares the actual outlet temperature $T_{c,out}$ with the setpoint $T_{c,out}^{sp}$ and computes a corrective signal.
- This signal becomes the **setpoint for the inner (secondary) loop**, which controls the **steam mass flow rate** $\dot{m}_s$ via the **steam control valve FCV-101**.
- The **inner loop is faster** (typically 2–5 seconds) and rejects disturbances in **steam pressure** before they propagate to the temperature.
- The **outer loop is slower** (typically 30–60 seconds) and corrects for **changes in crude inlet temperature or flow rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cascade is mandatory for steam heaters (KTU 2024 high-yield point):** Steam flow through a control valve is highly sensitive to upstream steam header pressure fluctuations (caused by other users on the same header). A single-loop temperature controller would respond sluggishly because the steam flow itself is not measured. Cascade provides **fast inner-loop rejection of pressure disturbances** and **tight temperature control** within ±1 °C.

### 4.3 Steam Trap Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SH[Steam Inlet from Shell Side] --> T1[Inlet Strainer]
    T1 --> DVC[Disc or Bellows Element]
    DVC --> CDV[Condensate Discharge Valve]
    CDV --> CO[Condensate Outlet to Drain]
    ST[Steam Trap Body] --> VNT[Air Vent at Top]
    VNT --> ARV[Thermostatic Air Release]
```

**Explanation:** The steam trap is the **unsung hero** of every steam heater. It performs three simultaneous functions:
1. **Discharges condensate** as soon as it forms, maintaining a thin, high-performance condensate film.
2. **Prevents live steam loss** — closes when steam tries to escape.
3. **Vents non-condensable gases** (air, CO₂) which would otherwise blanket the heat transfer surface and drastically reduce $U$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A failed-open steam trap wastes live steam and increases plant fuel costs by 5–15 %. A failed-closed steam trap floods the heater, reduces $U$ by up to 70 %, and can cause downstream process upset. **Always** include steam trap health in your design justification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a steam heater. List any four advantages of using steam as a heating medium in petrochemical industries.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A steam heater is a heat exchanger that uses steam — saturated or superheated — as the hot utility to transfer heat to a process fluid. The steam condenses on one side of the heat transfer surface, releasing its latent heat at a constant temperature.

**Four advantages:**
1. **High latent heat of vaporization** (~2257 kJ/kg at 1 atm), giving very high energy density per kg of steam.
2. **Isothermal heat delivery** at $T_{sat}$, which simplifies temperature control and avoids thermal stress.
3. **Wide range of achievable temperatures** (100–374 °C) by simply varying steam pressure.
4. **High condensing-side film coefficient** (5000–15000 W/m²·K), allowing compact exchanger design.
5. (Bonus) Steam is non-toxic, non-flammable, easily transportable, and inexpensive.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is the LMTD correction factor $F$ always equal to 1.0 for a steam heater? Justify with a sketch of the temperature profile.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a steam heater, steam condenses on the shell side at a **constant temperature** $T_{sat}$, while the process fluid on the tube side warms up from $T_{c,in}$ to $T_{c,out}$. The temperature profile on the hot side is therefore a **horizontal straight line**.

The LMTD correction factor $F$ depends on the parameters $P$ and $R$. Since the hot-fluid temperature does not change,

$$
R = \frac{T_{hot,in} - T_{hot,out}}{T_{cold,out} - T_{cold,in}} = \frac{0}{\Delta T_c} = 0
$$

For $R = 0$, the standard TEMA $F$-chart gives $F = 1.0$ for any number of shell or tube passes. This is the **defining property of a condenser-type heat exchanger**.

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat P&ID the working of a shell-and-tube steam heater used for crude oil preheating in a refinery. List the main instruments and describe the cascade control strategy used for outlet temperature control.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A refinery steam heater handles 80,000 kg/hr of naphtha (specific heat 2.1 kJ/kg·K) from 60 °C to 140 °C. Saturated steam is supplied at 7 bar absolute (saturation temperature 165 °C, latent heat 2065 kJ/kg). The overall heat transfer coefficient (dirty) is 450 W/m²·K. Calculate:
(i) The heat duty $Q$ in kW.
(ii) The steam consumption in kg/hr.
(iii) The LMTD in °C.
(iv) The required heat transfer area in m².

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 Marks]**

A shell-and-tube steam heater for crude preheating consists of:

**Construction:** A cylindrical shell containing a tube bundle. Crude oil flows inside the tubes (multi-pass, typically 2 or 4 tube passes), while saturated steam enters the shell, condenses on the outer tube surface, and the condensate is removed by a steam trap at the bottom.

**Working principle:** Steam gives up its latent heat while condensing isothermally at $T_{sat}$. The crude oil is heated from inlet temperature $T_{c,in}$ to the desired outlet temperature $T_{c,out}$.

**P&ID (refer to SECTION 4.1 for the full Mermaid diagram):** The main instruments are:

- **FT-101:** Orifice-type flow transmitter on the crude inlet line.
- **TT-101, TT-102:** Pt100 RTDs measuring crude inlet and outlet temperatures.
- **TE/TI/TIC-102:** Temperature indicator controller for outlet temperature — **primary (master) loop**.
- **FIC-101:** Flow indicator controller on the steam line — **secondary (slave) loop**.
- **PT-101 / PIC-101 / FCV-101:** Steam pressure transmitter, pressure controller, and the steam control valve modulating steam admission.
- **LT-101 / LIC-101 / LCV-101:** Condensate level transmitter, level controller, and condensate drain valve to maintain the trap level and avoid condensate flooding.
- **XV-101:** Emergency shutdown (ESD) block valve on the crude line.

**Cascade control logic:** The outlet temperature $T_{c,out}$ is the primary controlled variable. Its controller **TIC-102** does not directly move the steam valve; instead, it **resets the setpoint of the inner flow loop FIC-101**, which modulates the steam valve FCV-101. The inner loop is fast (rejects steam pressure disturbances), while the outer loop is slow (corrects crude inlet condition changes). This two-tier strategy keeps $T_{c,out}$ within ±1 °C of setpoint, even under significant upstream disturbances.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_c = 80{,}000$ kg/hr $= 22.222$ kg/s
- $C_{p,c} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K
- $T_{c,in} = 60$ °C, $T_{c,out} = 140$ °C
- $P_s = 7$ bar abs → $T_{sat} = 165$ °C, $\lambda_s = 2065$ kJ/kg
- $U = 450$ W/m²·K

**(i) Heat duty:**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_c \, C_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

$$
Q = 22.222 \text{ kg/s} \times 2.1 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times (140 - 60) \text{ K}
$$

$$
Q = 22.222 \times 2.1 \times 80 = 3733.3 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 3.733 \text{ MW}}
$$

**(ii) Steam consumption:**

$$
\dot{m}_s = \frac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \frac{3733.3}{2065} = 1.808 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_s = 6508 \text{ kg/hr}}
$$

**(iii) LMTD:**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{sat} - T_{c,in} = 165 - 60 = 105 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{sat} - T_{c,out} = 165 - 140 = 25 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{105 - 25}{\ln(105/25)} = \frac{80}{\ln(4.2)} = \frac{80}{1.4351}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LMTD} = 55.74 \text{ °C}}
$$

**(iv) Heat transfer area:**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{3.733 \times 10^6}{450 \times 55.74}
$$

$$
A = \frac{3.733 \times 10^6}{25{,}083}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 148.8 \text{ m}^2}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of a steam trap in a steam heater system. With the aid of a labeled diagram, explain the construction and working of (i) a **float-and-thermostatic (F&T) trap** and (ii) a **thermodynamic (TD) trap**. Compare their suitability for petrochemical steam heaters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A kerosene stream of 25,000 kg/hr is heated from 50 °C to 130 °C in a 1-2 TEMA shell-and-tube steam heater. Saturated steam at 5 bar abs ($T_{sat} = 151.86$ °C, $\lambda = 2108.5$ kJ/kg) is used. Specific heat of kerosene is 2.3 kJ/kg·K, and $U_{dirty} = 400$ W/m²·K. Determine:
(i) Heat duty in kW.
(ii) Steam rate in kg/hr.
(iii) LMTD and required area.
(iv) Number of tubes required if each tube is 25 mm OD, 22 mm ID, 4 m long.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $\dot{m}_c = 25{,}000/3600 = 6.944$ kg/s; $C_{p,c} = 2.3$ kJ/kg·K; $T_{c,in} = 50$ °C; $T_{c,out} = 130$ °C; $P_s = 5$ bar abs; $T_{sat} = 151.86$ °C; $\lambda_s = 2108.5$ kJ/kg; $U = 400$ W/m²·K; tube OD = 0.025 m, $L = 4$ m.

**(i) Heat duty:**

$$
Q = 6.944 \times 2.3 \times (130 - 50) = 6.944 \times 2.3 \times 80 = 1277.8 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 1277.8 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Steam rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_s = \frac{1277.8}{2108.5} = 0.6060 \text{ kg/s} = 2181.6 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_s = 2182 \text{ kg/hr}}
$$

**(iii) LMTD and area:**

$$
\Delta T_1 = 151.86 - 50 = 101.86 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = 151.86 - 130 = 21.86 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{101.86 - 21.86}{\ln(101.86/21.86)} = \frac{80}{\ln(4.660)} = \frac{80}{1.5397} = 51.95 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{1.2778 \times 10^6}{400 \times 51.95} = \frac{1.2778 \times 10^6}{20{,}780} = 61.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 61.5 \text{ m}^2}
$$

**(iv) Number of tubes:**

$$
A_{tube} = \pi d_o L = \pi \times 0.025 \times 4 = 0.3142 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
N_{tubes} = \frac{A}{A_{tube}} = \frac{61.5}{0.3142} = 195.7 \approx 196 \text{ tubes}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{tubes} = 196 \text{ (rounded up)}}
$$

(For a 1-2 TEMA with 196 tubes, common bundle layouts use 4 tube passes → 49 tubes per pass.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Steam Heater Problems:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to convert kg/hr → kg/s before substituting into the heat-duty equation. Always check: $Q$ in **kW** requires $\dot{m}$ in **kg/s**.
> 2. **LMTD sign error (1 mark lost):** If $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$ you have reversed which end is "1" and which is "2". This produces a negative LMTD and an impossible (negative) area.
> 3. **Wrong steam table lookup (1–2 marks lost):** Pressure on a steam table is **absolute**, not gauge. 7 bar gauge = 8 bar abs. Always add 1.013 bar.
> 4. **Forgetting $F = 1$ statement (1 mark lost):** For a steam heater, you do **not** need an $F$-chart, but examiners want the explicit justification that $R = 0$ and $F = 1$.
> 5. **Neglecting condensate management (1 mark lost in design questions):** Every KTU design answer must include a steam trap or condensate level control. Skipping this loses a full mark.
> 6. **Single-loop instead of cascade (2 marks lost):** In a control strategy question, mentioning "use a temperature controller" without specifying the master-slave cascade loses 1–2 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Steam heater** = heat exchanger using **condensing steam** as the hot utility; the steam condenses **isothermally at $T_{sat}$** on one side and transfers heat to the process fluid.
- **Why steam?** High $\lambda$ (~2000–2250 kJ/kg), isothermal delivery, tunable $T_{sat}$ (100–374 °C), high condensing film coefficient (5000–15000 W/m²·K), safe and cheap.
- **Master equation set:** $Q = \dot{m}_c C_p \Delta T = \dot{m}_s \lambda = U A \text{LMTD}$. Memorize all three forms.
- **LMTD for steam heater:** $\text{LMTD} = (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) / \ln[(T_{sat} - T_{c,in})/(T_{sat} - T_{c,out})]$.
- **Correction factor $F = 1$** always for a steam heater (condenser type, $R = 0$). No $F$-chart needed.
- **Steam consumption:** $\dot{m}_s = Q/\lambda_s$; steam pressure must be **absolute** when reading tables.
- **Control strategy:** Always use **cascade** (temperature master → flow slave) plus **condensate level control**.
- **Steam trap** is critical: removes condensate, prevents steam loss, vents non-condensables; failure mode matters.
- **Five instruments minimum** for a working steam heater: flow transmitter (crude), two temperature transmitters (inlet & outlet), steam pressure transmitter, condensate level transmitter.
- **Disturbances to design for:** Crude inlet temperature, crude flow rate, steam header pressure, fouling of tubes, steam quality ($x$).
- **Common types:** shell-and-tube (most common), plate (compact), DSI (direct injection), kettle reboiler, steam coil.
- **Material selection:** Carbon steel for shell & tubes in non-corrosive service; stainless steel (SS304/SS316) for sour or corrosive service; cupronickel for seawater-condenser variants.
- **Sustainability:** Always evaluate waste-heat recovery to preheat boiler feedwater — directly increases plant thermal efficiency and is a NEP 2020-aligned design expectation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Condensers in Petrochemical Industries

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **condenser** is a specialized heat transfer device employed in petrochemical processing units to effect a phase change from the **vapor state to the liquid state** by extracting the **latent heat of condensation** from the process fluid. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with NEP 2020 and Outcome-Based Education), a condenser is classified as a **phase-change heat exchanger (PCHX)** that operates isothermally (at constant temperature for pure components) or polythermally (over a temperature glide for multi-component mixtures) and serves as a critical separation and energy-recovery unit.

In petrochemical complexes, condensers are deployed downstream of:
- **Distillation column overheads** (to recover valuable hydrocarbon liquids)
- **Reactor effluent streams** (to recover unreacted vapors)
- **Vapor recovery units (VRUs)** (to capture fugitive emissions)
- **Steam and refrigerant cycles** (utility systems)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: A condenser is a heat exchanger that transfers latent heat from a condensing vapor to a coolant (cooling water, air, or process fluid), enabling phase transition. The fundamental energy balance is governed by $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$, where $\lambda$ is the latent heat of condensation in **kJ/kg**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine holding a hot cup of tea on a cold winter morning. The water vapor rising from the cup contacts the cooler surrounding air and condenses into tiny water droplets (you can see them as a foggy plume near your face). Nature is performing **condensation** — it is rejecting the **hidden (latent) heat** the vapor absorbed when it boiled.

A petrochemical condenser works on the same principle but in a controlled, engineered way:
- The hot hydrocarbon vapor (analogous to the tea vapor) flows into the **shell side**.
- Cold cooling water flows through the **tubes**.
- Heat crosses the metal tube wall, vapor **releases its latent heat**, and becomes liquid hydrocarbon.
- The cooling water **carries the rejected heat away**.

Think of it as a **two-lane heat toll booth**: one lane (vapor) pays a "toll" called latent heat and exits as a liquid; the other lane (cooling water) collects that toll and exits slightly warmer.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range in Petrochemical Plants | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Shell-side operating pressure | **0.05 MPa(g)** (vacuum) to **4.5 MPa(g)** | MPa |
| Condensing temperature | **-40 °C** (ethylene) to **+450 °C** (heavy aromatics) | °C |
| Overall heat transfer coefficient (U) | **300 – 1500 W/m²·K** | W/m²·K |
| Latent heat of condensation ($\lambda$) | **200 – 500 kJ/kg** for hydrocarbons | kJ/kg |
| Cooling water temperature rise | **5 – 15 °C** | °C |
| LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference) | **10 – 60 °C** typical | °C |
| Approach temperature | **3 – 10 °C** | °C |

> [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight**: The term **approach temperature** is the difference between the condensing vapor temperature and the cooling water outlet temperature. A small approach (e.g., 3 °C) indicates excellent heat exchanger design and is desired for energy efficiency — it is a frequent **KTU 2-mark/3-mark question**.

## 1.4 Classification of Condensers (KTU Taxonomic Overview)

Condensers in petrochemical service are broadly classified as:

1. **Surface Condensers (Indirect Contact)**
   - **Shell-and-Tube Condensers** (most common): TEMA Type E, F, J, X
   - **Plate Heat Exchanger Condensers**
   - **Air-Cooled Condensers (Fin-Fan)**

2. **Direct Contact Condensers**
   - **Barometric (Jet) Condensers**
   - **Spray Condensers**
   - **Quench Towers**

3. **Condensers by Condensation Mechanism**
   - **Filmwise Condensation** (most common; liquid film forms on the wall)
   - **Dropwise Condensation** (droplets form — higher U but hard to sustain)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

A condenser performs three sequential thermal duties (especially in distillation overhead service):

1. **De-superheating**: Sensible cooling of superheated vapor down to its saturation (dew) temperature.
2. **Condensation**: Phase change at (approximately) constant temperature, releasing the bulk of latent heat.
3. **Subcooling**: Further sensible cooling of the saturated liquid below its boiling point.

The total heat duty $Q_{total}$ is therefore:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{desuperheat} + Q_{condensation} + Q_{subcool}$$

$$Q_{total} = \dot{m}_v \cdot c_{p,v} \cdot (T_{v,in} - T_{sat}) + \dot{m}_v \cdot \lambda + \dot{m}_v \cdot c_{p,l} \cdot (T_{sat} - T_{l,out})$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_v$ = mass flow rate of vapor (kg/s)
- $c_{p,v}, c_{p,l}$ = specific heat capacities of vapor and liquid (kJ/kg·K)
- $T_{v,in}, T_{sat}, T_{l,out}$ = inlet vapor, saturation, and outlet liquid temperatures (°C)
- $\lambda$ = latent heat of condensation (kJ/kg)

## 2.2 Why Condensation Requires Special Heat Transfer Treatment

The presence of a liquid condensate film on the tube wall introduces an **additional thermal resistance** that dominates the overall heat transfer. The condensed film thickness grows as condensate drains down (vertical tubes) or accumulates across the tube bundle (horizontal tubes). Two classical correlations must be understood for KTU exams:

### A. Nusselt's Correlation — Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate/Tube

$$\bar{h}_{film} = 0.943 \cdot \left[ \frac{\rho_l \cdot (\rho_l - \rho_v) \cdot g \cdot \lambda \cdot k_l^3}{4 \cdot \mu_l \cdot (T_{sat} - T_w) \cdot L} \right]^{1/4}$$

### B. Nusselt's Correlation — Film Condensation on a Horizontal Tube (Bank of N tubes)

$$\bar{h}_{N,tube} = 0.725 \cdot \left[ \frac{\rho_l \cdot (\rho_l - \rho_v) \cdot g \cdot \lambda \cdot k_l^3}{\mu_l \cdot (T_{sat} - T_w) \cdot N \cdot d_o} \right]^{1/4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU**: These correlations are central to **Module 4** design problems. The vertical-tube correlation shows that $h_{film} \propto L^{-1/4}$ — meaning taller tubes are penalized with lower condensing coefficients, while horizontal-tube correlation shows $h \propto (N \cdot d_o)^{-1/4}$ — meaning deeper tube bundles suffer reduced performance. This guides the KTU design question on **why condensers are oriented horizontally in tall distillation columns**.

## 2.3 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) — The Working Engineer's Tool

The **overall heat transfer coefficient** bundles together all individual resistances:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_{film}} + R_{wall} + R_{fouling,i} + \frac{d_i}{d_o \cdot h_{tube}}$$

Engineering designers prefer using a **combined film coefficient** $h_{oc}$ referred to the outside (shell) area:

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_{o}} + \frac{d_o \cdot \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2 \cdot k_{wall}} + \frac{d_o}{d_i \cdot h_i} + R_{fouling,o} + R_{fouling,i} \cdot \frac{d_o}{d_i}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat duty (condensation only) | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ | W (with $\dot{m}$ in kg/s) |
| 2 | Total heat duty (3-zone) | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot [c_{p,v}\Delta T_{v} + \lambda + c_{p,l}\Delta T_{l}]$ | W |
| 3 | LMTD (counter-current) | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | °C or K |
| 4 | Heat exchanger area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U_o \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}$ | m² (F is correction factor) |
| 5 | Nusselt vertical | $\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l(\rho_l-\rho_v)g\lambda k_l^3}{4\mu_l(T_{sat}-T_w)L} \right]^{1/4}$ | W/m²·K |
| 6 | Nusselt horizontal N-tube | $\bar{h} = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l(\rho_l-\rho_v)g\lambda k_l^3}{\mu_l(T_{sat}-T_w)Nd_o} \right]^{1/4}$ | W/m²·K |
| 7 | Overall resistance | $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \dfrac{d_o \ln(d_o/d_i)}{2k_w} + \dfrac{d_o}{d_i h_i} + R_{fo} + R_{fi}\dfrac{d_o}{d_i}$ | m²·K/W |
| 8 | Cooling water duty | $Q = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot c_{p,cw} \cdot (T_{cw,out} - T_{cw,in})$ | W |
| 9 | Reynolds (condensate film) | $Re_{film} = \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_l}$ where $\Gamma = \dot{m}/(\pi d_o N)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Energy recovery ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{recovered}}{Q_{supplied}}$ | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In petrochemical plants, condenser design and operation directly affect:
- **Reflux flow** to the distillation column (impacts separation efficiency)
- **Product purity** (incomplete condensation allows light ends to escape)
- **Energy integration** (hot cooling water can pre-heat feed — pinching heat integration networks)
- **Carbon footprint** (poor condensation ⇒ vent losses ⇒ fugitive emissions)

The heat removed in a naphtha splitter overhead condenser can range from **5 MW to 80 MW** in a typical refinery, equivalent to the cooling demand of a small township.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Nusselt's Theory of Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate

**Starting assumptions** (Nusselt, 1916):
- Laminar film flow under gravity
- Constant wall temperature $T_w$
- Negligible vapor shear on the film surface
- Constant fluid properties
- The vapor is stationary; momentum is carried only by condensate

**Step 1 — Establish the velocity profile across the film**

Consider a vertical plate of width $W$ with condensate film of thickness $\delta(x)$ at distance $x$ from the top. The force balance on a fluid element of thickness $dy$ gives:

$$\mu_l \frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} = -(\rho_l - \rho_v) g$$

**Step 2 — Integrate the momentum equation twice**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = -\frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v) g}{\mu_l} y + C_1$$

$$u(y) = -\frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v) g}{2\mu_l} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions**

- At $y = 0$ (wall): $u = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$
- At $y = \delta$ (free surface): $\dfrac{du}{dy} = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = \dfrac{(\rho_l - \rho_v) g \delta}{\mu_l}$

Therefore:

$$u(y) = \frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v) g}{\mu_l} \left( \delta y - \frac{y^2}{2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mass flow rate per unit width**

$$\Gamma = \rho_l \int_0^\delta u \, dy = \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \delta^3}{3 \mu_l}$$

Solving for $\delta$:

$$\delta(x) = \left[ \frac{4 \mu_l \Gamma x}{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g} \right]^{1/3}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the local heat transfer coefficient**

Heat transfer at the wall by conduction through the film:

$$q'' = h_x (T_{sat} - T_w) = k_l \left. \frac{dT}{dy} \right|_{y=0}$$

Assuming a linear temperature profile across the film thickness (justified because the dominant thermal resistance is the condensate film):

$$h_x = \frac{k_l}{\delta(x)}$$

**Step 6 — Average over the plate length L**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L h_x \, dx = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \frac{k_l}{\delta(x)} dx$$

Substituting $\delta(x)$ and integrating:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{k_l}{L} \cdot \left[ \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g L}{4 \mu_l \Gamma} \right]^{1/3}$$

**Step 7 — Replace $\Gamma$ with the integrated mass flow**

The total mass condensed over length L is $\Gamma = \dfrac{Q}{W \lambda L}$, where $Q$ is the heat transferred and $W$ is the plate width.

After substitution and simplification:

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[ \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \lambda k_l^3}{\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_w) L} \right]^{1/4}$$

This is the **Nusselt correlation**, fundamental to all KTU Module 4 derivations.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Petrochemical Condenser Design

> **[KTU Typical University Exam Style — 14-mark Problem]**

**Problem Statement:**

A shell-and-tube condenser is used in a petrochemical plant to condense **n-hexane vapor** flowing at $\dot{m}_v = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$ from a distillation column. The vapor enters at **130 °C** (superheated) and exits as subcooled liquid at **75 °C**. Cooling water is available at **30 °C** and may be heated to a maximum of **45 °C**. Given:
- $c_{p,v} = 1.8 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $c_{p,l} = 2.3 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $\lambda = 332 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (at $T_{sat} = 90 °C$, the assumed saturation temperature of n-hexane at column pressure)
- Overall heat transfer coefficient $U_o = 700 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$

**Required:** Calculate (a) the total heat duty, (b) the LMTD, (c) the required heat transfer area, and (d) the cooling water flow rate.

### Solution — Part (a): Total Heat Duty

The mass flow rate is converted to SI:

$$\dot{m}_v = \frac{12{,}000}{3600} = 3.333 \text{ kg/s}$$

The three temperature zones:
- De-superheat: $130 - 90 = 40 °C$
- Condensation: at $90 °C$
- Subcool: $90 - 75 = 15 °C$

De-superheating load:

$$Q_{ds} = 3.333 \times 1.8 \times 40 = 240 \text{ kW}$$

Condensation load:

$$Q_{cond} = 3.333 \times 332 = 1107 \text{ kW}$$

Subcooling load:

$$Q_{sc} = 3.333 \times 2.3 \times 15 = 115 \text{ kW}$$

Total heat duty:

$$Q_{total} = 240 + 1107 + 115 = 1462 \text{ kW}$$

### Solution — Part (b): LMTD (Counter-current flow)

End temperatures of the two streams:
- Vapor side: $130 °C \rightarrow 75 °C$ (cools through all three zones)
- Cooling water side: $30 °C \rightarrow 45 °C$

Inlet temperature difference (vapor in vs water in):

$$\Delta T_1 = 130 - 45 = 85 °C$$

Outlet temperature difference (liquid out vs water out):

$$\Delta T_2 = 75 - 30 = 45 °C$$

Log Mean Temperature Difference:

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{85 - 45}{\ln(85/45)} = \frac{40}{\ln(1.8889)} = \frac{40}{0.6360} = 62.89 °C$$

### Solution — Part (c): Required Heat Transfer Area

Using $A = \dfrac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}$:

$$A = \frac{1462 \times 1000}{700 \times 62.89} = \frac{1{,}462{,}000}{44{,}023} = 33.21 \text{ m}^2$$

### Solution — Part (d): Cooling Water Flow Rate

Using the energy balance on the cooling water side:

$$\dot{m}_{cw} = \frac{Q_{total}}{c_{p,cw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})} = \frac{1{,}462{,}000}{4180 \times (45 - 30)} = \frac{1{,}462{,}000}{62{,}700} = 23.32 \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting to volumetric flow:

$$\dot{V}_{cw} = \frac{23.32}{1000} = 0.02332 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 84 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway**: The required area (33.21 m²) corresponds to a standard commercial shell-and-tube condenser (typically 30–40 m² for a 2-pass U-tube design with ¾" tubes, 1.5 m length). Designers add a **design margin of 15–25 %** to account for fouling and uncertainty, leading to a final specification of ≈ 40 m².

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Decisional Map for Condenser Selection

| Process Requirement | Recommended Condenser Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| High-pressure overhead (e.g., ethylene) | Horizontal shell-and-tube | Higher allowable pressure drop; compact |
| Vacuum distillation (e.g., xylene rerun) | Surface condenser with ejector system | Avoids contamination from direct contact |
| Acidic vapors (e.g., HCl recovery) | Graphite block condenser | Corrosion resistance |
| Remote location / water scarce | Air-cooled (Fin-Fan) | No water requirement; high power penalty |
| Heavy fouling service (olefin plant) | Plate heat exchanger (PHE) | Easy to clean; high U |
| Sub-ambient temperatures | Refrigerated condenser | Uses chilled brine/glycol |
| Low capital cost, dirty service | Barometric jet condenser | Simple; high water consumption |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics — Condenser System Architecture

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Petrochemical Condenser System with Instrumentation

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates the complete instrumentation, signal transmission, and control architecture of a distillation-column overhead condenser system — a standard KTU Module 4 conceptual map.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Distillation Column Overhead Vapor]
    node2[Shell Side of Condenser]
    node3[Temperature Transmitter TT100]
    node4[Pressure Transmitter PT100]
    node5[Flow Transmitter FT101]
    node6[Level Transmitter LT100 on Reflux Drum]
    node7[Cooling Water Inlet Header]
    node8[Tube Side of Condenser]
    node9[Cooling Water Outlet]
    node10[Control Valve CV101 on CW Line]
    node11[DCS Controller PID Block]
    node12[Reflux Drum]
    node13[Condensate Pump]
    node14[Two Way Splitter Reflux and Product]
    node15[Vapor to Vent or Vacuum System]
    node16[Alarm Annunciator Panel]
    node17[Recorder Data Historian]
    
    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node12
    node12 --> node13
    node13 --> node14
    node12 --> node15
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node2
    node8 --> node9
    node10 --> node8
    node3 --> node11
    node4 --> node11
    node5 --> node11
    node6 --> node11
    node11 --> node10
    node11 --> node16
    node3 --> node17
    node4 --> node17
    node5 --> node17
    node6 --> node17
```

### Reading the Flow:
- **Top flow path (red path analog)**: Vapor from the column enters the shell, condenses, drops to the **reflux drum (accumulator)**, then is split — partly returned as reflux to the column, partly sent forward as product.
- **Cooling water path (blue path analog)**: CW enters, is metered (FT101), flows through the **tubes**, and exits hot.
- **Control loop**: The **DCS PID controller** reads shell-side pressure (PT100) and adjusts **CV101** to maintain the desired condensation pressure, indirectly controlling top column temperature.
- **Auxiliary**: LT100 protects against flooding in the reflux drum; alarms and data historians provide safety and compliance.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cascade Control Architecture for a Condenser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp1[Setpoint Column Top Temperature]
    tc1[Master Controller TC Master]
    sp2[Slave Setpoint Calculated]
    pc1[Slave Controller PC Condenser Pressure]
    pt1[Pressure Transmitter on Shell]
    cv1[Control Valve on CW Bypass]
    cw1[Cooling Water Flow]
    cond1[Condenser Unit]
    
    sp1 --> tc1
    tc1 --> sp2
    sp2 --> pc1
    pc1 --> cv1
    cv1 --> cw1
    cw1 --> cond1
    cond1 --> pt1
    pt1 --> pc1
    cond1 --> tc1
```

### Cascade Logic — Why Two Loops?
- The **master (outer) loop** senses column top temperature (slow but accurate).
- The **slave (inner) loop** senses condenser shell pressure (fast, sensitive to disturbances like CW temperature drift).
- This configuration — a **KTU exam favourite** — rejects CW supply temperature disturbances far faster than a single-loop scheme.

## 4.3 Instrumentation Sensor Map (Typical Reflux Drum + Condenser)

| Tag | Service | Instrument Type | Signal | Range | Mounting |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TT100 | Vapor inlet temperature | RTD Pt100 (Class A) | 4–20 mA + HART | 0–200 °C | Thermowell on inlet nozzle |
| PT100 | Shell-side pressure | Capacitance DP cell | 4–20 mA + HART | 0–500 kPa | Top of shell |
| FT101 | Cooling water flow | Magnetic flowmeter | 4–20 mA + HART | 0–100 m³/h | CW inlet line |
| LT100 | Reflux drum level | Guided-wave radar | 4–20 mA + HART | 0–100 % | Top of drum |
| PDT101 | Differential pressure | Capacitance | 4–20 mA + HART | 0–50 kPa | Across shell |
| TT102 | Condensate outlet temperature | T/C Type J | 4–20 mA + HART | 0–150 °C | Outlet nozzle |
| QI100 | Steam quality (if reboiled) | Vibration-based | 4–20 mA + HART | 0–100 % | Steam line |
| AT100 | H₂S in vent area | Electrochemical | 4–20 mA + HART | 0–50 ppm | Near vent stack |
| CV101 | Cooling water valve | Globe valve + I/P converter | 3–15 psi pneumatic | – | CW line |
