KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]** *Explain the principle of on-off control as applied to a centrifugal pump used for sump level control. List any **three** advantages and **two** limitations.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** On-off control of a centrifugal pump is a two-position control strategy where the pump is either fully **ON (delivering rated flow)** or fully **OFF (zero flow)**, with no intermediate state. The level in the sump is monitored by a level switch or float. When the level falls below a preset **lower threshold (L)**, the controller energises the motor contactor and starts the pump. The pump runs until the level rises above the **upper threshold (H)**, after which the controller de-energises the contactor and stops the pump. A **hysteresis band** $\Delta h = H - L$ is provided to prevent rapid cycling (chattering) of the motor. **Advantages:** 1. **Simple** — requires only a level switch, contactor, and overload relay. Low capital cost. 2. **Reliable** — minimal components mean fewer failure modes; easy to maintain. 3. **No control valve** — eliminates erosion and leak points common in throttled systems. **Limitations:** 1. **Oscillatory level** — the controlled variable continuously cycles between L and H. 2. **Energy inefficient** for continuous duty — pump always runs at full rated power during ON periods. 3. **High inrush current** on every start stresses motor windings and contactor tips. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define "hysteresis band" in the context of on-off pump control. Why is it essential? Justify with a numerical illustration.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **hysteresis band** is the **deliberate gap** between the **lower threshold (L)** at which the pump starts and the **upper threshold (H)** at which the pump stops, expressed as: $$\Delta h = H - L$$ It is **essential** to prevent **chattering** (rapid on/off cycling) of the motor contactor, which would: - Overheat the motor windings due to repeated inrush currents (6–8× FLC). - Burn out the contactor tips within hours. - Cause pressure pulsations in the discharge piping. **Numerical Illustration:** If $L = 0.5$ m and $H = 2.5$ m, then $\Delta h = 2.0$ m. For a 4 m² sump and inflow 8 m³/h (pump 40 m³/h), the cycle period is 1.25 h, giving only 0.8 starts/hour — well within motor limits. Without hysteresis (i.e., L = H = 1.5 m), the pump would start every few seconds and fail in days. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **on-off control scheme for a centrifugal pump** used to maintain the level in a **crude oil storage tank** of a refinery. Clearly identify the **role of each component** in the loop. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A centrifugal pump on-off control scheme for a crude oil storage tank is shown in the block diagram in Section 4.1. The functional description is as follows: **1. Primary Sensing Element — Level Switch (LS) or Level Transmitter (LT):** A **displacer-type level switch** or **guided-wave radar level switch** is mounted on the side of the storage tank. Two setpoints are configured: a **low-level switch (LSL)** at, say, 1.0 m and a **high-level switch (LSH)** at, say, 3.0 m. The LSL is a **normally-open** contact that closes when level falls below 1.0 m, and the LSH is a **normally-closed** contact that opens when level rises above 3.0 m. **2. Controller — Relay Logic / Pump Controller:** A hard-wired relay logic panel (or a PLC with pump control function block) implements the start/stop decision. The logic is: - **Start Condition:** `LSL = 1` AND pump not running AND no fault → energise contactor `M`. - **Stop Condition:** `LSH = 0` AND pump running → de-energise `M`. **3. Output Element — Motor Contactor and Starter:** The contactor `M` (typically a 3-pole AC contactor) energises the induction motor. A **thermal overload relay (OLR)** in series with the contactor protects the motor from sustained overcurrent. A **seal-in auxiliary contact** of `M` keeps the coil energised after LSL re-opens. **4. Final Control Element — Centrifugal Pump:** The pump is a **horizontal end-suction centrifugal pump** (per API 610 standard for refinery service), driven by a **TEFC squirrel-cage induction motor** (typically IP55, Class F insulation). The pump delivers crude oil at rated flow and head when ON. **5. HMI and Annunciation:** The DCS / SCADA displays **real-time level**, **pump status** (RUN/STOP/FAULT), and **starts per day counter**. A high-high level (LSHH) alarm triggers a **second standby pump** for redundancy. **6. Local Controls:** A local **START/STOP pushbutton station** and **AUTO/MANUAL selector switch** are provided at the pump skid for maintenance and commissioning. **Control Sequence:** - **Auto mode:** PLC/relay logic controls start/stop based on level switches. - **Manual mode:** Local pushbutton controls the contactor; level switches are bypassed (used during commissioning). - **Trip conditions:** Overload trip, single-phasing, or high-high level → instant pump trip with annunciator alarm. This scheme is **fail-safe** — loss of level signal (wire break) is treated as low-level, starting the pump and preventing overflow. --- **(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers **Q = 250 m³/h** of an oil of **density 870 kg/m³** against a head of **40 m**. The pump efficiency is **75%** and the motor efficiency is **90%**. Calculate the **(i)** hydraulic power, **(ii)** shaft power, and **(iii)** motor input power. If the pump operates **on-off** for 20% of an 8000-hour year, calculate the **annual energy consumption in kWh** and compare it with a **VFD-based continuous throttling** at 20% of rated flow. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $Q = 250 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \dfrac{250}{3600} = 0.0694 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - $H = 40 \text{ m}$ - $\rho = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - $\eta_{pump} = 0.75$ - $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$ - Total operating hours = 8000 h/year - On-off: pump runs at full load for 20% of time = $0.20 \times 8000 = 1600$ h - VFD: runs at 20% of rated flow continuously for 8000 h **Step 1 — Hydraulic Power (i):** $$P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 870 \times 9.81 \times 0.0694 \times 40$$ $$P_{hyd} = 870 \times 9.81 \times 2.778$$ $$P_{hyd} = 23711.5 \text{ W} \approx 23.71 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Shaft Power (ii):** $$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{23.71}{0.75} = 31.61 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Motor Input Power at Full Load (iii):** $$P_{motor,FL} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{31.61}{0.90} = 35.13 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Annual Energy — On-Off Mode:** $$E_{ON/OFF} = P_{motor,FL} \times t_{ON} = 35.13 \times 1600 = 56208 \text{ kWh/year}$$ **Step 5 — VFD Mode at 20% Flow:** By affinity law, $P \propto Q^3$. At 20% flow (i.e., $Q_2/Q_1 = 0.20$): $$\frac{P_{VFD}}{P_{motor,FL}} = (0.20)^3 = 0.008$$ $$P_{VFD} = 35.13 \times 0.008 = 0.281 \text{ kW}$$ (Note: We assume the system head is purely friction-dominated; for static head-dominated systems the saving is less.) **Step 6 — Annual Energy — VFD Mode:** $$E_{VFD} = 0.281 \times 8000 = 2248 \text{ kWh/year}$$ **Step 7 — Comparison:** $$\Delta E = 56208 - 2248 = 53960 \text{ kWh/year}$$ $$\text{Savings \%} = \frac{53960}{56208} \times 100 = 96\%$$ **Conclusion:** For this **friction-dominated system**, VFD at 20% flow saves approximately **96% energy** compared to on-off control. However, the **VFD has a payback period of 2–4 years** depending on electricity tariff and pump size. For **intermittent small pumps**, on-off remains the most economical choice. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End Semester] **(a)** Explain the **working principle of a centrifugal pump** with reference to its **H–Q characteristic curve**. Show the **operating point** graphically and discuss how the operating point behaves under **on-off control** versus **throttling control**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working Principle:** A centrifugal pump converts the **mechanical energy of a rotating impeller** into the **pressure and kinetic energy of a fluid**. The fluid enters the impeller eye **axially**, is accelerated **radially outward** by centrifugal force, and is collected in the **volute casing** (or diffuser), where kinetic energy is converted to pressure energy. The pressurised fluid is discharged through the pump nozzle. **H–Q Characteristic Curve:** At a fixed impeller diameter and rotational speed, the pump develops a **head (H)** that **decreases monotonically** as the flow rate (Q) increases. This is the **pump characteristic curve**, typically a **downward-sloping parabola**: - At **Q = 0** (shut-off): H is at a maximum ($H_0$). - At **Q = Q_BEP** (Best Efficiency Point): pump operates at peak efficiency. - At **Q = Q_max** (run-out point): H approaches zero. **System Curve:** The piping system imposes a back-pressure called the **system head** $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^2$, an **upward-sloping parabola**. $H_{static}$ is the static elevation + pressure head, and $KQ^2$ is the friction loss. **Operating Point:** The **operating point** is the **unique intersection** of the pump curve and the system curve. At this point, the head developed by the pump exactly matches the head demanded by the system — steady-state flow. **Comparison of Control Strategies:** | Aspect | On-Off Control | Throttling Control | |--------|----------------|---------------------| | Pump curve | Fixed (rated speed) | Fixed (rated speed) | | System curve | Same | Steepens (valve adds $K_v Q^2$ loss) | | Operating point | Jumps between shut-off ($Q=0$, $H=H_0$) and rated point | Moves smoothly along pump curve | | Energy use | Wasted (pump rated power) | Wasted (valve dissipates excess head) | | Process smoothness | Oscillatory | Smooth | **On-Off Trajectory:** - When **OFF**: $Q = 0$, $H = H_0$ (top-left of the curve). - When **ON**: $Q = Q_{rated}$, $H = H_{rated}$ (right of the curve). - The actual flow **cycles** between these two extreme points as the level switch toggles. **Throttling Trajectory:** - The control valve adds an artificial resistance, **rotating the system curve** about the static-head intercept. - The operating point **slides down** the pump curve to a new, lower Q. - This is **smoother but energy-inefficient** (excess head is burnt as heat in the valve). --- **(b)** A chemical reactor cooling water pump is controlled by a **pressure switch** mounted on the discharge line. The pump starts at **2.5 bar** and stops at **3.5 bar**. The system has a constant leak of **0.5 m³/h** when the pump is OFF and the pump delivers **30 m³/h** when ON. The discharge line volume between the pressure switch and the heat exchanger is **equivalent to 0.4 m³ per bar of pressure**. Calculate the **(i)** hysteresis band, **(ii)** ON time, **(iii)** OFF time, and **(iv)** number of pump starts per hour. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Start pressure $P_{start} = 2.5 \text{ bar}$ - Stop pressure $P_{stop} = 3.5 \text{ bar}$ - Pump ON flow $Q_{pump} = 30 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ - Leak OFF flow $Q_{leak} = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ - Compliance: 0.4 m³ per bar of line volume **Step 1 — Hysteresis Band (i):** $$\Delta P = P_{stop} - P_{start} = 3.5 - 2.5 = 1.0 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent Volume per Cycle:** $$V_{cycle} = \text{Compliance} \times \Delta P = 0.4 \times 1.0 = 0.4 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 — ON Time (ii):** During ON, net filling rate = $Q_{pump} - Q_{leak} = 30 - 0.5 = 29.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ $$T_{ON} = \frac{V_{cycle}}{Q_{net,ON}} = \frac{0.4}{29.5} = 0.01356 \text{ h} = 0.813 \text{ minutes} = 48.8 \text{ seconds}$$ **Step 4 — OFF Time (iii):** During OFF, pressure decays at the leak rate: $$T_{OFF} = \frac{V_{cycle}}{Q_{leak}} = \frac{0.4}{0.5} = 0.8 \text{ h} = 48 \text{ minutes}$$ **Step 5 — Cycle Period and Starts per Hour (iv):** $$T_{cycle} = T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = 0.01356 + 0.8 = 0.8136 \text{ h} = 48.81 \text{ minutes}$$ $$S = \frac{60}{T_{cycle,min}} = \frac{60}{48.81} = 1.23 \text{ starts/hour}$$ **Conclusion:** The pump starts only **1.23 times per hour**, well within the standard motor limit of **6–15 starts/hour** for a 5–10 HP motor. The hysteresis band of 1.0 bar is **engineered adequately**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (On-Off Pump Control):** > 1. **Forgetting density of the fluid** in hydraulic power calculation. Crude oil / hydrocarbon density is **not 1000 kg/m³** — always use the given density (e.g., 870, 920, 750 kg/m³). > 2. **Confusing Q_in and Q_pump** in hysteresis derivations. During ON, the **net flow** is $(Q_{pump} - Q_{in})$, not $Q_{pump}$ alone. > 3. **Ignoring the leak/evaporation term** in OFF time. In petroleum systems, vapour losses and leakages are **non-negligible** and must be subtracted. > 4. **Forgetting units conversion** — Q in m³/h must be converted to m³/s when using the standard power formula $P = \rho g Q H$. > 5. **Misapplying affinity laws** — they hold for **kinetic pumps** (centrifugal) at **constant impeller diameter** with **variable speed**. They do not hold for positive displacement pumps. > 6. **Failing to mention hysteresis** when drawing a block diagram — examiners specifically check for this as it is the **single most important engineering safeguard**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **On-off control** is a **two-position control** (fully ON / fully OFF) — the simplest and cheapest control strategy for centrifugal pumps. - **Hysteresis band** $\Delta h = H - L$ is **mandatory** to prevent motor contactor chattering and to limit starts per hour. - Centrifugal pump **hydraulic power** $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ must be divided by $\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor}$ to get **electrical input power**. - **Operating point** of a pump is the **intersection** of the pump H–Q curve and the system resistance curve. - In on-off mode, the operating point **oscillates** between the **shut-off point** ($Q=0$) and the **rated point**. - **Affinity laws** ($Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$) are used for **VFD** analysis, not on-off, but often appear in **comparison questions**. - **Standard components**: Level/pressure switch, contactor, overload relay, seal-in auxiliary, HMI, pushbuttons. - **Cycle period** $T_{cycle} = T_{ON} + T_{OFF}$ must keep **starts per hour** within motor thermal limit (typically 6–15 starts/hr for standard motors). - **VFD saves up to 80–96% energy** in friction-dominated systems over on-off control but has higher capital cost. - **On-off is preferred** for: sump pumps, standby pumps, level interlocks, fire-water jockey pumps, lube-oil pumps. - **VFD is preferred** for: cooling tower fans, large boiler feed pumps, HVAC, process-critical flow loops. - **NPSH check** ($NPSH_A \geq NPSH_R + 1.0$ m) is **independent of control strategy** but is a mandatory design verification. - **Fail-safe design**: Loss of level signal should default to **pump ON** (overflow prevention) in storage tanks; for lube-oil consoles, default to **pump OFF** (cavitation prevention). - **Latching logic (seal-in contact)** is the key to making a momentary start signal sustain the motor running until the stop condition arrives. - **API 610** is the standard that governs centrifugal pump design in **petrochemical and refinery** services.

topics.title: Effluent and Water Treatment Control: Chemical Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effluent and Water Treatment Control: Chemical Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Oxidation in Effluent Treatment** is a physico-chemical unit operation in which a strong oxidizing agent (oxidant) is dosed into a wastewater stream to transfer electrons from target pollutant molecules (reductants) to the oxidant, thereby converting refractory, toxic, coloured, or biologically resistant organic/inorganic compounds into simpler, less harmful, or more biodegradable end-products such as CO₂, H₂O, and mineral salts.

In petrochemical effluent streams (containing phenols, mercaptans, sulfides, cyanides, hydrocarbons, and dissolved organics), chemical oxidation is deployed as either:
- **Pre-treatment** (before biological treatment) to break recalcitrant molecules
- **Post-treatment (Polishing)** to meet discharge COD/BOD/color norms
- **Tertiary treatment** for trace organics removal before reuse/recycle

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Burn Without Fire"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Washing a Greasy Pan**
> Imagine you have a greasy, oily pan (the pollutant). You cannot simply rinse it with water because the oil does not mix (refractory organics). If you pour a strong detergent that *chemically reacts* with the oil, breaking it into water-soluble soap and glycerol, the pan becomes clean without ever applying heat. **Chemical oxidation works the same way**: an oxidant (the "detergent") chemically rips electrons from stubborn pollutant molecules, transforming them into water-soluble, biodegradable, or gaseous by-products — achieving "combustion-grade cleaning" **without the actual flame**.

### 1.3 The Primary Control Parameter: ORP (Oxidation-Reduction Potential)

The single most important measured variable in a chemical oxidation control loop is the **Oxidation-Reduction Potential (ORP)**, expressed in **millivolts (mV)** versus a standard reference electrode (typically Ag/AgCl or saturated calomel).

| ORP Range (mV) | Process State | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| −400 to −200 | Strongly reducing | Anaerobic digester, sulfide-rich effluent |
| −100 to +100 | Mildly reducing | Anoxic zone, denitrification |
| +200 to +400 | Mildly oxidizing | Aeration basin, biological oxidation |
| **+400 to +800** | **Strongly oxidizing** | **Active chemical oxidation zone** |
| +800 to +1200 | Extremely oxidizing | Ozone/permanganate/ClO₂ systems |

> [!NOTE]
> In petrochemical ETP, the target ORP setpoint is typically held between **+450 mV and +700 mV** during active chemical oxidation, depending on the target pollutant class.

### 1.4 Standard Oxidants Used in Petrochemical ETP

1. **Ozone (O₃)** — strongest commercial oxidant (E° = +2.07 V)
2. **Hydrogen Peroxide (H₂O₂)** — clean, leaves only water
3. **Fenton's Reagent (H₂O₂ + Fe²⁺ catalyst)** — generates hydroxyl radical (•OH)
4. **Chlorine / Hypochlorite (Cl₂ / NaOCl)** — broad-spectrum, forms DBPs
5. **Chlorine Dioxide (ClO₂)** — selective, no trihalomethane formation
6. **Potassium Permanganate (KMnO₄)** — solid, easy to dose
7. **Oxygen / Air (O₂)** — mild, used in biological/extended aeration
8. **UV / H₂O₂ (Advanced Oxidation Process — AOP)** — photolytic •OH generation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course treats **chemical oxidation as a CONTROLLED process** — emphasis is on the *instrumentation* (ORP, pH, COD, TOC, Cl₂ residual, dissolved O₂ analyzers), *final control elements* (dosing pumps, motorized valves), and *control strategies* (feedback, feed-forward, cascade) — not on the underlying reaction chemistry.

### 1.5 Significance in the Petrochemical Context

Crude refinery, aromatics, and petrochemical complex effluents contain **recalcitrant organics** (phenols, anilines, PAHs, thiophenes) that are toxic to downstream biological reactors. Chemical oxidation:
- Converts them into **biodegradable intermediates** (organic acids, aldehydes)
- Removes **colour** (chromophoric groups get cleaved)
- Reduces **COD** (Chemical Oxygen Demand) by 30–80% per stage
- Strips **sulfide and cyanide** (detoxification)
- Enables **water reuse** within the plant (cooling tower makeup, boiler feed pre-treatment)

---

### 1.6 Key Measured Variables in the Control Loop

> [!NOTE]
> The following list represents the **instrumentation** you must know for KTU ESE — every sensor is mapped to its standard analyzer technology.

| Variable | Analyzer Technology | Typical Range (Petro ETP) |
|---|---|---|
| **pH** | Glass electrode | 2 – 14 |
| **ORP** | Platinum vs. Ag/AgCl | −1500 to +1500 mV |
| **COD** | UV-Vis absorption (on-line) or dichromate digestion | 0 – 5000 mg/L |
| **TOC** | Combustion-IR / NDIR | 0 – 2000 mg/L |
| **Dissolved O₂** | Galvanic / Polarographic membrane | 0 – 20 mg/L |
| **Residual Cl₂ / O₃** | Amperometric / colorimetric | 0 – 5 mg/L |
| **Turbidity (post)** | Nephelometric | 0 – 1000 NTU |
| **Conductivity** | 4-electrode | 0 – 50 mS/cm |
| **Flow (effluent)** | Magnetic / Coriolis | 0 – 500 m³/h |
| **Flow (oxidant dose)** | Coriolis / PD meter (low flow) | 0 – 200 L/h |

---

### 1.7 Regulatory Discharge Limits (Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Indian Standard for Inland Surface Water Discharge (IS 2490) and CPCB norms for petrochemical complexes (typically **CETP norms**):

| Parameter | Limit |
|---|---|
| **pH** | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| **COD** | ≤ 250 mg/L |
| **BOD₃** | ≤ 30 mg/L |
| **Total Suspended Solids** | ≤ 100 mg/L |
| **Oil & Grease** | ≤ 10 mg/L |
| **Phenols** | ≤ 1 mg/L |
| **Sulfides** | ≤ 2 mg/L |
| **Cyanide** | ≤ 0.2 mg/L |
| **Colour** | ≤ Platinum-Cobalt 100 Hazen units |

The **chemical oxidation stage** is the principal control point that drives the effluent's pH, COD, and phenol/sulfide content into compliance with the above matrix.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry of Oxidation–Reduction (Brief, as Required for Instrumentation Engineers)

An oxidation–reduction (redox) reaction transfers electrons between two species. Every redox pair is quantified by its **standard electrode potential (E°)** measured against the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** at **25 °C, 1 M, 1 atm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** A reaction is *spontaneous* in the oxidizing direction when the oxidant's reduction potential is **higher** than the reductant's. This is what the ORP probe senses — it is essentially a **platinum electron collector**.

**General Redox Couple:**
$$\text{Ox} + n e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$$
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$

This is the **Nernst Equation**, the single most important equation for the entire module.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Chemical Oxidation Control Loop

The instrumentation control system in a chemical oxidation tank follows this logical chain:

1. **Effluent flows in** → magnetic flowmeter (FT) sends a 4–20 mA signal to the **Distributed Control System (DCS)**.
2. **Inlet pH is measured** → pH transmitter (AIT) feeds the DCS; if pH is outside the 2–4 (Fenton) or 7–9 (ozone) window, **alkali/acid dosing is pre-adjusted** via PID loop.
3. **Inlet COD is measured** by a UV-Vis online analyzer → signal is the **feed-forward input** to the oxidant dosing controller.
4. **ORP probe (ATI) in the reactor** continuously measures the redox potential → the **feedback signal** to the PID controller.
5. **PID controller** compares ORP setpoint (e.g., +600 mV) with measured value → output (4–20 mA) drives the **dosing pump stroke / stroke frequency** (Final Control Element, FY).
6. **Residual oxidant analyzer** (e.g., amperometric O₃ sensor) measures leftover oxidant → if residual > setpoint, dosing is trimmed down to **avoid over-oxidation and wastage**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two-Layer Control Architecture:**
> - **Inner (fast) loop:** ORP → Dosing pump stroke (P-only or PI, 1–5 s response)
> - **Outer (slow) loop:** COD analyzer → ORP setpoint adjustment (cascade master, 5–15 min response)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates EVERY equation you may need for a 14-mark Part B question on Chemical Oxidation Control.** Memorize units, not just symbols.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | **Nernst equation** (ORP sensor physics) | E in V, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96485 C/mol |
| 2 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Nernst at 25 °C (log₁₀ form) | V |
| 3 | $E_{\text{obs}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{ref}}$ | Correcting ORP to SHE reference | mV or V |
| 4 | $C_{d} = \dfrac{F \times \Delta \text{COD}}{Y_{\text{ox}}}$ | **Oxidant dose rate** (stoichiometric) | kg oxidant / h |
| 5 | $\Delta \text{COD} = Q \times (S_{\text{in}} - S_{\text{out}})$ | COD load reduction | kg O₂ / day |
| 6 | $\text{COD}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{8}{n_e} \times C_{\text{org}}$ | Stoichiometric O₂ demand for organics | mg/L |
| 7 | $\text{Cl}_2\text{ dose} = 2.5 \times \Delta \text{COD} + 0.5 \times \text{NH}_3\text{-N}$ | Empirical chlorine demand (breakpoint rule) | mg/L |
| 8 | $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2\text{ dose} = \dfrac{2.125 \times \Delta \text{COD}}{0.471}$ | Fenton H₂O₂ stoichiometry (Fe²⁺:H₂O₂ = 1:5) | mg/L |
| 9 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | First-order half-life | min |
| 10 | $C_t = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$ | First-order decay (target pollutant) | mg/L |
| 11 | $G(s) = K_p\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | **PID controller** transfer function | dimensionless |
| 12 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \displaystyle\int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID control law | % controller output |
| 13 | $\tau_p \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = K_p \, u$ | First-order process model | y = process variable |
| 14 | $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -k C_A^n$ | Rate of oxidation (n-th order kinetics) | mol/(L·s) |
| 15 | $\text{ORP}_{\text{set}} = E^\circ_{\text{oxidant}} - 100 \text{ to } 200$ | Heuristic ORP setpoint rule of thumb | mV |
| 16 | $\text{O}_3\text{ transfer efficiency} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{off-gas}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Ozone contactor performance | % |
| 17 | $\text{UV dose} = I \times t$ | UV-AOP photolysis dose | mJ/cm² |
| 18 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{COD}_{\text{in}} - \text{COD}_{\text{out}}}{\text{COD}_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % |
| 19 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 1 - (1-\eta_1)(1-\eta_2)\cdots$ | Multi-stage removal (series) | dimensionless |
| 20 | $R_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{\text{moles oxidant}}{\text{moles pollutant}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{ox}}}{n_e}$ | Stoichiometric oxidant ratio | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Process Equipment and Their Instrumentation Mapping

| Equipment | Sensors Mounted | Control Variable | Final Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equalization Tank** | LT (level), pH, ORP | pH, level | Agitator VFD, NaOH/HCl pump |
| **Flash Mixer** | pH (post), FT (flow) | Effluent flow ratio | Static mixer / motorized valve |
| **CSTR / Oxidation Reactor** | ORP, pH, T, DO, Cl₂/O₃ residual | ORP setpoint, pH | Dosing pump (H₂O₂/Cl₂/O₃) |
| **Ozone Generator** | O₃ gas analyzer (UV), FT (air/O₂) | O₃ production rate | Power supply setpoint |
| **UV Reactor (AOP)** | UV intensity sensor, FT | UV lamp power | Electronic ballast |
| **Settler / DAF** | LT (sludge bed), TSS, NTU | Sludge recycle | Sludge pump, scraper drive |
| **Polishing Filter** | ΔP, FT, NTU | Backwash cycle | Backwash valves |

### 2.5 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> - **Refinery case study:** Sulfidic spent caustic from LPG/ethylene plants is wet-air oxidized at 750–950 °C, but the *post-quench pH and ORP* control loop decides whether the treated water can be sent to a biological ETP or must be recycled. A deviation of **±50 mV in ORP** can change the sulfur speciation between S²⁻, S₂O₃²⁻, and SO₄²⁻ — fundamentally altering downstream toxicity.
> - **Water-reuse in petrochemical plants:** Modern zero-liquid-discharge (ZLD) plants use **multi-stage chemical oxidation** (ClO₂ → H₂O₂/UV → O₃) with cascaded ORP, UV₂₅₄, and conductivity loops. Proper control saves **15–30% oxidant cost** per MLD treated.
> - **Process Safety:** A runaway Fenton's reaction produces explosive H₂/O₂ mixtures and exothermic surges (ΔT up to **60 °C/min** in unbuffered conditions). The **temperature transmitter (TT) on the reactor** triggers a SCRAM (emergency H₂O₂ shutoff) when T > 70 °C.

### 2.6 Control Strategy Variants (Often Asked in 14-mark Questions)

1. **Single-loop feedback (ORP-only)** — simplest, suffers from load disturbances
2. **Cascade control** — Master: COD analyzer; Slave: ORP loop
3. **Feed-forward + feedback** — Flow & inlet COD are feed-forward; ORP is feedback
4. **Ratio control** — Oxidant flow is held proportional to effluent flow: F_oxidant = K × F_effluent
5. **Selective / override control** — pH overrides ORP if pH drifts too far (Fenton requires pH ≈ 3)
6. **Model-predictive control (MPC)** — For multi-oxidant AOP trains (advanced; CL-672 level)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation for the ORP Sensor

The ORP sensor is a **platinum indicator electrode** that absorbs electrons from the most dominant redox couple in solution. The voltage it produces vs. the **Ag/AgCl reference** is given by the Nernst equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Point:** Free energy of an electrochemical cell.

**Step 1 — Gibbs Free Energy of a Redox Reaction**

For the half-reaction: $\text{Ox} + n e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$, the change in Gibbs free energy is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient $= \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ (since electrons have unit activity).

**Step 2 — Relate ΔG to Cell Potential**

The fundamental electrochemical relation is:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

Substituting in Step 1:

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for E**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert ln to log₁₀**

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the constant at 25 °C (298.15 K)**

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \approx 0.0592 \text{ V}$$

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$

This is the equation that the ORP transmitter's micro-controller uses to **display ORP in mV vs. SHE**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stoichiometric Oxidant Dose Calculation

**Given:** A petrochemical effluent with flow $Q = 100 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$, inlet COD $S_{\text{in}} = 1200 \text{ mg/L}$, desired outlet COD $S_{\text{out}} = 400 \text{ mg/L}$, and the chosen oxidant is **H₂O₂ (Fenton's)**.

**Step 1 — Compute COD removed per unit time**

$$\Delta \text{COD} = Q \times (S_{\text{in}} - S_{\text{out}})$$

$$= 100 \, \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{h}} \times (1200 - 400) \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}}$$

$$= 100 \times 10^3 \, \frac{\text{L}}{\text{h}} \times 800 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}}$$

$$= 8 \times 10^7 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{h}}$$

$$= 80 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} \text{ (of O}_2\text{ equivalent)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to H₂O₂ dose using stoichiometry**

In Fenton's oxidation, the **theoretical H₂O₂:COD ratio is 2.125:1** (mass basis), because 1 mol H₂O₂ provides 1 mol [O] equivalent (1.008 g O per 34.014 g H₂O₂), while 1 mol O₂ provides 2 mol [O] equivalent (32 g O per 32 g O₂). Hence:

$$\frac{34.014/1}{32/2} = 2.125 \quad \text{(mass H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ per mass O}_2\text{ equivalent)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute H₂O₂ dose in mg/L of effluent**

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ dose} = \frac{2.125 \times \Delta \text{COD (mg/L)}}{1}$$

For $\Delta\text{COD} = 800 \text{ mg/L}$:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ dose} = 2.125 \times 800 = 1700 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, only **50–70%** of H₂O₂ is utilized (rest decomposes catalytically). So the *practical* dose is:

$$D_{\text{practical}} = \frac{D_{\text{stoich}}}{\eta_{\text{util}}}$$

With $\eta_{\text{util}} = 0.6$:

$$D_{\text{practical}} = \frac{1700}{0.6} \approx 2833 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}}$$

**Step 4 — Fe²⁺ catalyst dose** (mass ratio Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂ = 1 : 5 for optimum •OH yield)

$$D_{\text{Fe}} = \frac{2833}{5} = 566.6 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \text{ (as FeSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O)}$$

**Step 5 — Daily consumption**

$$= 2833 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 100 \times 10^3 \, \frac{\text{L}}{\text{h}} \times 24 \, \frac{\text{h}}{\text{day}}$$

$$= 6.8 \times 10^9 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{day}} = 6800 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{day}} = 6.8 \, \frac{\text{tonnes}}{\text{day}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: First-Order Oxidation Kinetics

For a CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) used in chemical oxidation, the mass balance on pollutant $A$ is:

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = Q C_{A,\text{in}} - Q C_A - V k C_A$$

At steady state, $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = 0$:

$$Q C_{A,\text{in}} = Q C_A + V k C_A$$

$$C_{A,\text{in}} = C_A \left(1 + k \frac{V}{Q}\right) = C_A (1 + k \tau)$$

where $\tau = V/Q$ is the **hydraulic residence time**.

$$C_A = \frac{C_{A,\text{in}}}{1 + k\tau}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **If $k = 0.3 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ and $\tau = 30 \text{ min}$:**

$$C_A = \frac{C_{A,\text{in}}}{1 + 0.3 \times 30} = \frac{C_{A,\text{in}}}{10}$$

**Removal efficiency:**

$$\eta = \left(1 - \frac{1}{1 + k\tau}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{10}\right) \times 100 = 90\%$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: PID Tuning of the ORP Loop (Ziegler–Nichols Open-Loop Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **A typical petrochemical ETP reports the following open-loop step-test on the oxidant dosing pump:**
> - Process gain $K_p = 250 \, \text{mV}/(\% \text{ pump stroke})$
> - Dead time $L = 18 \, \text{s}$
> - Time constant $T = 90 \, \text{s}$

**Step 1 — Compute ultimate gain $K_u$ (Z-N formula for open-loop process with delay):**

$$K_u = \frac{T}{K_p \cdot L}$$

$$K_u = \frac{90}{250 \times 18} = 0.02$$

**Step 2 — Ultimate period (approximation):**

$$P_u \approx 4L = 72 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Ziegler–Nichols PI settings (no derivative, since ORP loop is noisy):**

$$K_c = 0.45 \, K_u = 0.009$$
$$T_i = \dfrac{P_u}{1.2} = 60 \text{ s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical refinement:** Because the ORP signal is **noisy** (platinum electrode picks up electrical interference), a **derivative term is normally disabled** in the ORP loop, and a **first-order input filter** of 5–10 s is added to prevent controller "chatter."

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: COD Removal Efficiency Across Multi-Stage Oxidation

> [!NOTE]
> **A refinery has a 3-stage chemical oxidation train:**
> - Stage 1 (ClO₂): $\eta_1 = 35\%$
> - Stage 2 (Fenton's): $\eta_2 = 60\%$
> - Stage 3 (O₃ polishing): $\eta_3 = 50\%$

**Step 1 — Inlet COD** $S_0 = 1500$ mg/L

**Step 2 — After Stage 1:**

$$S_1 = S_0 (1 - \eta_1) = 1500 \times 0.65 = 975 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — After Stage 2:**

$$S_2 = S_1 (1 - \eta_2) = 975 \times 0.40 = 390 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — After Stage 3:**

$$S_3 = S_2 (1 - \eta_3) = 390 \times 0.50 = 195 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — Overall efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = 1 - \frac{S_3}{S_0} = 1 - \frac{195}{1500} = 0.87 = 87\%$$

This meets the **CETP limit of ≤ 250 mg/L** comfortably.

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style 14-Mark Question

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A refinery ETP uses an ozone contactor (volume $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$) to oxidize phenols. The feed contains $C_{\text{in}} = 25 \text{ mg/L}$ phenol at $Q = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$. The reaction is first-order with $k = 0.05 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ w.r.t. phenol.
> **(a)** Find the outlet phenol concentration at steady state.
> **(b)** Calculate the daily ozone consumption if 4 mg O₃ are needed per mg phenol oxidized.

**Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Residence time:**

$$\tau = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{50 \text{ m}^3}{200 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}} = 0.25 \text{ h} = 15 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — CSTR steady-state equation:**

$$C_{\text{out}} = \frac{C_{\text{in}}}{1 + k\tau} = \frac{25}{1 + 0.05 \times 15} = \frac{25}{1.75} = 14.29 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Phenol removed per unit volume:**

$$\Delta C = 25 - 14.29 = 10.71 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Mass of phenol removed per day:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{phenol}} = Q \times \Delta C = 200 \times 10^3 \, \text{L/h} \times 10.71 \, \text{mg/L}$$

$$= 2.142 \times 10^6 \, \text{mg/h} = 51.41 \, \text{g/h}$$

**Step 2 — Daily mass removed:**

$$= 51.41 \times 24 = 1233.8 \, \text{g/day} = 1.234 \, \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Daily ozone consumption (stoichiometric):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{O}_3} = 4 \times 1.234 = 4.94 \, \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — With 80% transfer efficiency in the contactor:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{O}_3, \text{actual}} = \frac{4.94}{0.80} = 6.18 \, \text{kg/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $C_{\text{out}} = 14.29 \text{ mg/L}$, O₃ consumption ≈ **6.2 kg/day**.

---

### 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Oxidant Selection

| Oxidant | $E^\circ$ (V) | pH window | Best For | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| O₃ | +2.07 | 7 – 9 | Refractory organics, phenols | Off-gas scrubber needed; unstable |
| •OH (Fenton's) | +2.85 | 2.5 – 3.5 | Broad-spectrum AOP | Sludge, narrow pH |
| H₂O₂ | +1.78 | 2 – 10 | Mild oxidation | Slow without catalyst |
| Cl₂ | +1.36 | 6 – 8 | Disinfection, sulfide | THM formation |
| ClO₂ | +0.95 | 6 – 8 | Selective, no THMs | Explosive (must generate on-site) |
| KMnO₄ | +1.51 | 3 – 11 | Fe/Mn removal, phenols | MnO₂ sludge |
| O₂ (air) | +1.23 | 7 – 9 | Biological/CWAO | Slow kinetics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for the examiner:** When the question says "select an oxidant for a refinery ETP with high sulfide and phenol," the answer is almost always a **two-stage train: ClO₂/Cl₂ for sulfide → Fenton's or O₃ for phenols**.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
Define Oxidation-Reduction Potential (ORP) and explain its significance in controlling a chemical oxidation process in a petrochemical effluent treatment plant.

**Model Answer:**

ORP (Oxidation-Reduction Potential) is the **voltage (in mV) developed across a platinum indicator electrode and a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl) immersed in the solution**, indicating the solution's tendency to gain or lose electrons. In a chemical oxidation control loop, ORP is the **primary feedback variable** because:
- A target ORP setpoint (typically **+450 mV to +700 mV** for petrochemical ETP) guarantees that the oxidant concentration is sufficient to drive the redox reaction to completion.
- A rising ORP indicates excess oxidant → controller reduces dosing pump stroke.
- A falling ORP indicates oxidant depletion → controller increases stroke.
- The ORP probe responds in **1–5 seconds**, making it ideal for real-time PID control of dosing pumps.

The ORP signal essentially tells the controller **"how oxidizing"** the reactor contents are at any moment, replacing slow laboratory COD analyses.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**
List any three common chemical oxidants used in petrochemical effluent treatment and state one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Oxidant | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Ozone (O₃)** | Highest oxidation potential (+2.07 V), leaves no residue | Unstable, requires on-site generation, toxic off-gas |
| **Hydrogen Peroxide (H₂O₂)** | Clean, only water as by-product | Slow kinetics, expensive |
| **Chlorine (Cl₂ / NaOCl)** | Inexpensive, broad-spectrum | Forms carcinogenic trihalomethanes (THMs) |
| **Fenton's Reagent (H₂O₂/Fe²⁺)** | Generates •OH radical (E° = +2.85 V), best for refractory organics | Narrow pH window (2.5–3.5), generates iron sludge |

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks, students should mention **at least three** with a clear advantage-disadvantage pairing.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a cascade control system used for chemical oxidation in a petrochemical ETP, where the **master loop** is the inlet COD analyzer and the **slave loop** is the ORP controller. Label all field instruments using ISA tag conventions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An effluent stream with flow $Q = 250 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$ and inlet COD = $1500 \, \text{mg/L}$ is to be treated in a Fenton's oxidation reactor. The desired outlet COD is $400 \, \text{mg/L}$. Compute:
- (i) The stoichiometric H₂O₂ dose (in mg/L and kg/h) using the 2.125 ratio.
- (ii) The Fe²⁺ catalyst dose (Fe²⁺ : H₂O₂ = 1 : 5 by mass).
- (iii) The reactor volume if the first-order rate constant is $k = 0.04 \, \text{min}^{-1}$ (assume CSTR). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

```
                              ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                              │  MASTER LOOP (slow, 5-15 min)│
   Effluent ──►[FIT-301]──────►[AIT-101]──►[AIC-100 Master]──┐
                              │   COD Analyzer   ORP Setpt   │
                              └──────────────────────────────┘  │
                                                               │
                              ┌──────────────────────────────┐  │
                              │  SLAVE LOOP (fast, 1-5 s)    │  │
                              │                              ▼  │
                  [ATI-201 ORP Probe]──►[AIC-201 Slave PID]──►[FY-201 Dosing Pump]
                              │                              │
                              │  CSTR Chemical Oxidation Tank│
                              │       T-100                 │
                              └──────────────────────────────┘
```

**ISA Tagging Convention Used:**

| Tag | Description |
|---|---|
| **FIT-301** | Flow Indicator Transmitter (effluent) |
| **AIT-101** | Analytical Indicator Transmitter (COD) |
| **AIC-100** | Analytical Indicating Controller (Master, cascade) |
| **ATI-201** | Analytical Transmitter Indicator (ORP) |
| **AIC-201** | Analytical Indicating Controller (Slave PID) |
| **FY-201** | Final Control Element, valve/pump (oxidant dosing) |
| **T-100** | Process Vessel (reactor) |
| **LIT-101** | Level Indicator Transmitter (tank) |
| **TT-101** | Temperature Transmitter |

**Working Principle:**
1. Inlet COD (slow, ~5–15 min) drives the **master controller** AIC-100.
2. AIC-100's output is the **ORP setpoint** (e.g., 400–700 mV) sent to slave AIC-201.
3. ORP probe ATI-201 gives instantaneous feedback (1–5 s) to slave AIC-201.
4. Slave output drives the **dosing pump FY-201** stroke.
5. The cascade structure **rejects disturbances faster** than a single ORP loop.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Stoichiometric H₂O₂ dose:**

$$\Delta \text{COD} = S_{\text{in}} - S_{\text{out}} = 1500 - 400 = 1100 \, \text{mg/L}$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \text{ dose} = 2.125 \times 1100 = 2337.5 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**In kg/h:**

$$2337.5 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 250 \times 10^3 \, \frac{\text{L}}{\text{h}} = 5.84 \times 10^8 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{h}} = 584.4 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}}$$

**(ii) Fe²⁺ catalyst dose:**

$$D_{\text{Fe}} = \frac{2337.5}{5} = 467.5 \, \text{mg/L}$$

$$= 467.5 \times 250 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-6} = 116.9 \, \text{kg/h (as FeSO}_4 \cdot 7\text{H}_2\text{O, equivalent)}$$

**(iii) Reactor volume (CSTR steady state):**

$$C_{\text{out}} = \frac{C_{\text{in}}}{1 + k\tau} \implies 400 = \frac{1500}{1 + 0.04\tau}$$

$$1 + 0.04\tau = \frac{1500}{400} = 3.75$$

$$\tau = \frac{2.75}{0.04} = 68.75 \, \text{min} = 1.146 \, \text{h}$$

$$V = Q \times \tau = 250 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} \times 1.146 \, \text{h} = 286.5 \, \text{m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** H₂O₂ = 2337.5 mg/L = 584.4 kg/h; Fe²⁺ = 467.5 mg/L; V = 286.5 m³

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle, construction, and ISA tagging** of an ORP analyzer used in chemical oxidation control. Compare ORP-based control with **pH-based and residual oxidant analyzer-based** control strategies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refinery uses **chlorination** to oxidize ammonia (NH₃-N = 12 mg/L) and sulfides in an effluent. The flow is $150 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$. Using the empirical formula:
$$\text{Cl}_2 \text{ dose (mg/L)} = 2.5 \times \Delta \text{COD} + 0.5 \times \text{NH}_3\text{-N}$$
with $\Delta\text{COD} = 80 \text{ mg/L}$, calculate:
- (i) Total Cl₂ dose in mg/L and kg/h
- (ii) NaOCl (12% available Cl₂) consumption in L/day
- (iii) NaOCl cost/day if the rate is **₹ 35/L** of 12% solution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**ORP Analyzer — Construction & Working:**

An ORP analyzer has three components:
1. **Platinum indicator electrode** — a noble metal that does not participate in the reaction but transfers electrons.
2. **Reference electrode** — typically Ag/AgCl in saturated KCl (potential = +199 mV at 25 °C).
3. **High-impedance mV meter / transmitter** — amplifies the millivolt signal and converts to 4–20 mA.

When immersed in a redox-active solution, the Pt electrode absorbs electrons from the dominant couple and develops a potential vs. the reference. The reading is corrected to SHE:

$$E_{\text{SHE}} = E_{\text{measured vs Ag/AgCl}} + 199 \text{ mV (at 25 °C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ISA Tag:** **ATI-201** — Analytical Transmitter Indicator, loop 201.

**Comparison of Control Strategies:**

| Strategy | Sensor | Response Time | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ORP-based** | Pt electrode | 1–5 s | Fast, real-time | Non-specific; affected by pH |
| **pH-based** | Glass electrode | 1–3 s | Fast, stable | Indirect indicator; only works for pH-sensitive reactions |
| **Residual oxidant** | Amperometric | 10–60 s | Direct oxidant measurement | Slow, expensive, high maintenance |
| **COD-based (lab)** | Dichromate digestion | 2–4 h | Definitive | Too slow for real-time control |
| **COD-based (online UV)** | UV absorption | 5–15 min | Real-time trend | Interferences from turbidity/color |

> [!NOTE]
> **Best practice:** Use **ORP as primary feedback** and **residual oxidant as override** for oxidant economy.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Total Cl₂ dose:**

$$\text{Cl}_2 = 2.5 \times 80 + 0.5 \times 12 = 200 + 6 = 206 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**In kg/h:**

$$206 \, \frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 150 \times 10^3 \, \frac{\text{L}}{\text{h}} = 30.9 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}}$$

**(ii) NaOCl (12%) consumption in L/day:**

A 12% NaOCl solution contains 120 g Cl₂ per litre.

$$\text{Volume needed per hour} = \frac{30.9 \text{ kg/h}}{0.12 \text{ kg/L}} = 257.5 \, \text{L/h}$$

$$\text{Per day} = 257.5 \times 24 = 6180 \, \text{L/day}$$

**(iii) Cost per day:**

$$\text{Cost} = 6180 \times 35 = ₹ \, 2,16,300 \text{ per day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** Cl₂ = 206 mg/L = 30.9 kg/h; NaOCl = 6180 L/day; Cost = ₹ 2,16,300/day.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in chemical oxidation questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units in stoichiometric dose problems** — m³/h to L/h conversion is missed, leading to off-by-1000 errors. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing H₂O₂ stoichiometric and practical doses** — the theoretical ratio is 2.125, but in practice 50–70% utilization applies. Read the question carefully for "stoichiometric" vs "practical."
> 3. **Not labeling ISA tags correctly** — *FAI* is wrong; the correct ORP tag is **ATI**. Practice: First letter = **Measured Variable** (A = Analysis), Second = **Function** (T = Transmitter), Third = **Modifier** (I = Indicator), Number = **Loop number**.
> 4. **Writing chemical equations instead of control loop diagrams** — KTU Module 4 questions test *instrumentation*, not chemistry. Spend 60% of your time on the **loop diagram, signal flow, and controller tuning**; only 20% on chemistry.
> 5. **Ignoring residence time in CSTR kinetics** — Many students forget that the **hydraulic residence time τ = V/Q** governs the CSTR steady-state concentration equation. Mixing up V and Q loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning safety interlocks** — A 14-mark question on Fenton's MUST mention **temperature interlock (>70 °C), pH override (2.5–3.5), and emergency H₂O₂ shutoff** for full credit.
> 7. **Not discussing pH override control** — Fenton's pH is *very narrow*; failure to explain pH override / low-select signal selector loses marks in the cascade-control question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Chemical Oxidation**

- [x] **Chemical oxidation** transfers electrons from pollutants (reductants) to an oxidant, converting refractory organics into biodegradable or mineral end-products.
- [x] **Primary oxidants:** O₃, H₂O₂, Fenton's (H₂O₂/Fe²⁺), Cl₂/NaOCl, ClO₂, KMnO₄.
- [x] **Primary measured variable for control:** **ORP** (mV vs. Ag/AgCl reference).
- [x] **Typical ORP setpoint** in active chemical oxidation: **+450 mV to +700 mV**.
- [x] **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ (at 25 °C).
- [x] **Fenton's pH window:** 2.5 – 3.5; **Ozone:** 7 – 9; **Chlorine:** 6 – 8.
- [x] **H₂O₂ stoichiometry for COD:** 2.125 kg H₂O₂ per kg O₂-equivalent.
- [x] **Fenton Fe²⁺:H₂O₂ mass ratio (optimum):** 1 : 5.
- [x] **Chlorine dose empirical:** $\text{Cl}_2 = 2.5 \Delta\text{COD} + 0.5 \text{NH}_3\text{-N}$.
- [x] **CSTR steady-state:** $C_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}}}{1 + k\tau}$, where $\tau = V/Q$.
- [x] **Removal efficiency (series stages):** $\eta_{\text{overall}} = 1 - \prod_i (1 - \eta_i)$.
- [x] **PID controller equation:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$.
- [x] **Ziegler–Nichols PI:** $K_c = 0.45 K_u$, $T_i = P_u / 1.2$.
- [x] **Cascade control:** Outer (COD) sets ORP setpoint → Inner (ORP) controls dosing pump.
- [x] **Feed-forward control:** Effluent flow / inlet COD biases the dosing pump output.
- [x] **Safety interlocks:** High T (>70 °C), pH drift, level high, oxidant leak → SCRAM dosing pump.
- [x] **ISA tag example:** **ATI-201** = Analytical Transmitter Indicator, ORP loop 201.
- [x] **Online analyzers:** UV-Vis COD, TOC (combustion-IR), amperometric O₃/Cl₂, membrane DO.
- [x] **Discharge limits (CPCB/IS 2490):** COD ≤ 250 mg/L, BOD ≤ 30 mg/L, phenols ≤ 1 mg/L, sulfides ≤ 2 mg/L, pH 6.5–8.5.
- [x] **Multi-stage trains** are common: ClO₂ → Fenton's → O₃ (≥85% overall COD reduction achievable).
- [x] **Ozone transfer efficiency** in contactor is 80–95% with fine-bubble diffusers.
- [x] **AOP (Advanced Oxidation Process):** UV/H₂O₂, UV/O₃, Fenton's — generates •OH radical (E° = +2.85 V).

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4: EFFLUENT AND WATER TREATMENT CONTROL — CHEMICAL OXIDATION**

topics.title: Chemical Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT861 - INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemical Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Reduction** is a unit process in analytical and process chemistry in which the oxidation state of an element or compound is **decreased** through the gain of electrons, typically mediated by a **reducing agent** (a species that donates electrons). In petrochemical instrumentation, chemical reduction is widely employed as a **preparatory**, **analytical**, or **environmental control** technique for the determination of trace species, sulfur recovery, and abatement of nitrogen oxides (NOx).

Mathematically, reduction can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Oxidation State}_{\text{after}} < \text{Oxidation State}_{\text{before}}
$$

Or in the universal electrochemical convention:

$$
\text{M}^{n+} + n e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{M}
$$

where the species gains $n$ electrons and its oxidation number drops by $n$ units.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Bank Deposit" Model**
> Think of electrons as **money** and atoms as **people**. When a person receives money (electrons), their wealth (oxidation state) **decreases** in accounting terms because they now have a more negative charge. A **reducing agent** is like a wealthy donor giving away money — it becomes poorer (more positive) in the process, while the recipient becomes richer (more negative). In the petrochemical plant, certain reactions require the controlled "donation" of electrons to specific pollutants or analytes to make them detectable, removable, or benign.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Petrochemical Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Oxidation** | Loss of electrons; oxidation number increases | Combustion of hydrocarbons |
| **Reduction** | Gain of electrons; oxidation number decreases | Desulfurization, NOx abatement |
| **Reductant** | Electron-donating species | $H_2$, $CO$, $H_2S$, $Fe^{2+}$, $Sn^{2+}$ |
| **Oxidant** | Electron-accepting species | $O_2$, $Cl_2$, $KMnO_4$, $H_2O_2$ |
| **Electron Transfer (ET)** | Movement of $e^-$ from reductant to oxidant | The basis of all reduction reactions |

## 1.4 Classification of Chemical Reduction Methods

Reduction methods used in the petrochemical industry are broadly classified as follows:

1. **Wet Chemical (Volumetric) Reduction** — Uses liquid reducing agents in titration.
2. **Gaseous Catalytic Reduction** — Uses $H_2$ or $CO$ over a catalyst.
3. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** — $NO_x$ abatement with $NH_3$ or urea.
4. **Thermal/Reductive Decomposition** — Sample preparation for analyzers.
5. **Electrochemical Reduction** — Amperometric/Coulometric determination.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Reduction Occurs

The driving force for any reduction reaction is the **standard electrode potential ($E^{\circ}$)**. A species with a **more positive** $E^{\circ}$ will be reduced (gain electrons), while a species with a **more negative** $E^{\circ}$ will be oxidized (lose electrons). The spontaneity of the overall redox reaction is governed by the **Nernst equation**.

### 2.1.1 The Nernst Equation

For a half-reaction:

$$
aA + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons bB
$$

The Nernst equation gives the reduction potential $E$ as:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[B]^b}{[A]^a}
$$

Or equivalently in base-10 logarithm form (at standard conditions $T = 298$ K):

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[B]^b}{[A]^a}
$$

where:
- $E^{\circ}$ = Standard reduction potential (**Volts**)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (**K**)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96485 C/mol**
- $n$ = Number of moles of electrons transferred
- $[A]$, $[B]$ = Molar concentrations (activities)

### 2.1.2 Cell Potential

For a complete redox reaction, the overall cell EMF is:

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}
$$

A reaction is **spontaneous** if $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} > 0$.

## 2.2 Common Reducing Agents in Petrochemical Analytics

| Reductant | Half-Reaction | $E^{\circ}$ (V) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrogen gas | $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ | $0.00$ | Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) |
| Carbon monoxide | $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^-$ | $-0.51$ | Claus process, gas analysis |
| Ferrous ion | $Fe^{2+} \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + e^-$ | $+0.77$ | Volumetric analysis |
| Stannous ion | $Sn^{2+} \rightarrow Sn^{4+} + 2e^-$ | $+0.15$ | Mercury reduction in gas analysis |
| Sulfite ion | $SO_3^{2-} + H_2O \rightarrow SO_4^{2-} + 2H^+ + 2e^-$ | $-0.93$ | Oxygen scavenging |
| Ammonia | $2NH_3 \rightarrow N_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^-$ | $-0.057$ | Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) |

## 2.3 Industrial Reduction Processes in Petrochemical Plants

### 2.3.1 Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) of NOx

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SCR Reaction Chemistry (Vanadium-based catalyst):**
> 
> $$
> 4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \longrightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O
> $$
> 
> $$
> 2NO_2 + 4NH_3 + O_2 \longrightarrow 3N_2 + 6H_2O
> $$

**Operating Parameters:**
- Temperature window: **300 °C – 400 °C**
- Catalyst: $V_2O_5 / TiO_2$ (anatase)
- Space velocity: **5,000 – 20,000 h⁻¹**
- $NH_3$ / $NO_x$ ratio: **0.9 – 1.0** (stoichiometric)

### 2.3.2 Hydrodesulfurization (HDS)

A chemical reduction process where organosulfur compounds are converted to $H_2S$:

$$
R-S-R' + 2H_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Co-Mo / Ni-Mo}} R-H + R'-H + H_2S
$$

The $H_2S$ is then captured via the **Claus process**:

$$
2H_2S + 3O_2 \longrightarrow 2SO_2 + 2H_2O
$$

$$
2H_2S + SO_2 \longrightarrow 3S + 2H_2O
$$

### 2.3.3 Wet Chemical Reduction — Orsat & Gas Analysis

In the classical **Orsat apparatus**, several gas constituents are absorbed sequentially:

- $CO_2$ absorbed in **KOH**
- $O_2$ absorbed in **pyrogallol** (alkaline)
- **CO absorbed in cuprous chloride (acidic) — a reduction step** where $Cu^{2+} \rightarrow Cu^+$

$$
2Cu^{2+} + 4Cl^- + 2CO \longrightarrow 2CuCl \cdot CO + Cl_2
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$ | Nernst Equation (at 25 °C) |
| 2 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ cell potential |
| 3 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | Cell EMF |
| 4 | $W = \frac{nFM}{N_A \cdot 1000}$ | Coulometric mass of analyte (g) |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{SCR}} = \frac{[NO_x]_{\text{in}} - [NO_x]_{\text{out}}}{[NO_x]_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | SCR Efficiency |
| 6 | $R_{\text{HDS}} = \frac{[S]_{\text{feed}} - [S]_{\text{product}}}{[S]_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | HDS Conversion |
| 7 | $K_{eq} = 10^{\frac{nE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0592}}$ | Equilibrium constant from $E^{\circ}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $W$ = mass of substance deposited (g), $M$ = molecular weight (g/mol), $N_A$ = Avogadro's number, $F$ = Faraday constant, $n$ = electrons transferred, $Q$ = reaction quotient.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Environmental Compliance:** SCR units are mandated by **EPA Tier-IV** and **Bharat Stage-VI** norms to reduce $NO_x$ from refinery FCC units.
- **Product Quality:** HDS determines the **sulfur content** of diesel (ULSD: **< 10 ppm**).
- **Process Safety:** Reducing oxygen in headspace tanks prevents explosive atmospheres.
- **Analytical Measurement:** Wet-reduction methods calibrate online sulfur and mercury analyzers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation from Thermodynamic Principles

**Starting from fundamental thermodynamics:**

The Gibbs free energy change for a redox reaction is related to the maximum electrical work:

$$
\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}
$$

For non-standard conditions:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$
-nFE_{\text{cell}} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} + RT \ln Q
$$

Dividing both sides by $-nF$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

**Converting to base-10 logarithm** using $\ln x = 2.303 \log x$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q
$$

At $T = 298$ K, substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) and $F = 96485$ C/mol:

$$
\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.05916 \approx 0.0592
$$

Thus, the familiar form is:

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equilibrium Constant from Standard Potential

We know that at equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ and $Q = K_{eq}$. From:

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}
$$

and

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}
$$

Equating both:

$$
-nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -RT \ln K_{eq}
$$

Solving for $K_{eq}$:

$$
\ln K_{eq} = \frac{nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{RT}
$$

Converting to $\log_{10}$:

$$
\log K_{eq} = \frac{nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{2.303 \, RT}
$$

At 25 °C:

$$
\boxed{\log K_{eq} = \frac{n \cdot E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0592}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A positive $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ yields a $K_{eq} > 1$, meaning the reduction reaction lies far to the right (products favored).

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A petrochemical plant uses SCR to reduce $NO$ emissions. The inlet $NO_x$ concentration is **400 ppm** and outlet is **80 ppm**. The flue gas flow is **50,000 Nm³/h**. Calculate (a) the percent reduction efficiency, and (b) the mass of $NH_3$ consumed per hour (assume 100% stoichiometric usage).

### Part (a) — Efficiency Calculation

The percent reduction $\eta_{\text{SCR}}$ is:

$$
\eta_{\text{SCR}} = \frac{[NO_x]_{\text{in}} - [NO_x]_{\text{out}}}{[NO_x]_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\eta_{\text{SCR}} = \frac{400 - 80}{400} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{SCR}} = \frac{320}{400} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

### Part (b) — Mass of NH₃ Consumed

The governing reduction reaction is:

$$
4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \longrightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O
$$

**Mole ratio:** 1 mol $NO$ requires 1 mol $NH_3$.

**Step 1: Convert volumetric flow of NO from ppm to volumetric flow:**

$$
\dot{V}_{NO} = 50{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} \times 400 \times 10^{-6} = 20 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 2: Apply ideal gas law** to find molar flow at STP (1 atm, 273 K, molar volume = 22.414 Nm³/kmol):

$$
\dot{n}_{NO} = \frac{20 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}}{22.414 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kmol}} = 0.8924 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 3: Moles of NH₃ consumed** (1:1 stoichiometry):

$$
\dot{n}_{NH_3} = 0.8924 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

**Step 4: Mass flow** (Molecular weight of $NH_3$ = 17 kg/kmol):

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} = 0.8924 \times 17 = 15.17 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\eta_{\text{SCR}} = 80\%$, $\dot{m}_{NH_3} = 15.17$ kg/h.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Reduction Method

| Process Goal | Suitable Reduction Technique | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $NO_x$ removal from flue gas | **SCR with NH₃** | High conversion (95–98%), low temperature |
| Sulfur removal from naphtha | **HDS with $H_2$** | Catalytic, robust, industry-standard |
| Trace $O_2$ in gas streams | **Winkler titration / Orsat** | Wet chemical, accurate at ppm levels |
| $Hg$ vapor in gas | **Stannous chloride reduction** | Converts $Hg^{2+} \rightarrow Hg^{0}$ for AA spectroscopy |
| Online continuous $SO_2$ | **UV fluorescence after reduction** | EPA Method 30B |
| Sample prep for GC | **Reductive pyrolysis** | Converts sulfates to $H_2S$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SCR Process Flow in a Petrochemical Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Flue Gas from FCC Heater] --> B[NOx Concentration Sensor]
    B --> C{Inlet NOx > Threshold?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Inject NH3 from Storage Tank]
    C -->|No| E[Bypass Duct]
    D --> F[Mixing Chamber with Static Mixer]
    F --> G[SCR Catalyst Bed V2O5 on TiO2]
    G --> H[Reduction Reaction 4NO plus 4NH3 plus O2 to 4N2 plus 6H2O]
    H --> I[Outlet NOx Analyzer]
    I --> J{Meets Emission Norm?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Release to Stack]
    J -->|No| L[Alarm and Process Interlock]
    E --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydrodesulfurization Unit Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crude Feedstock with Organosulfur] --> B[Feed Preheater]
    B --> C[Reactor with CoMo or NiMo Catalyst]
    D[H2 Stream from Reformer] --> C
    C --> E[Hot Separator]
    E --> F[Liquid Products low sulfur]
    E --> G[Vapor Phase H2S and H2]
    G --> H[Claus Process for Sulfur Recovery]
    H --> I[Elemental Sulfur Product]
    G --> J[H2 Recycle Compressor]
    J --> D
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Wet Chemical Reduction (Orsat Gas Analysis)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Gas Inlet] --> B[Measuring Burette]
    B --> C[Pass through KOH Bulb]
    C --> D[Absorbs CO2]
    D --> E[Pass through Pyrogallol]
    E --> F[Absorbs O2]
    F --> G[Pass through Acidic CuCl Bulb]
    G --> H[Reduces and Absorbs CO]
    H --> I[Pass through Combustion Tube]
    I --> J[Burns CH4 and H2]
    J --> K[Final Residual Gas N2 Readout]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Control Loop for SCR Ammonia Injection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NOx Analyzer at Stack] --> B[Signal Conditioner 4 to 20 mA]
    B --> C[PID Controller]
    C --> D[Control Valve on NH3 Line]
    D --> E[NH3 Injection into Duct]
    E --> F[SCR Catalyst Bed]
    F --> A
    G[NOx Setpoint] --> C
    H[Feedback Signal] --> C
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Sample Pattern]**

### Question A1
**Define chemical reduction. Give two examples of reducing agents used in petrochemical industries.**

**Model Answer:**

> Chemical reduction is a process in which a chemical species **gains one or more electrons**, thereby lowering its oxidation state. A reducing agent (reductant) is the species that donates these electrons and is itself oxidized in the process.
> 
> **Examples:**
> 1. **Hydrogen gas ($H_2$)** — Used in hydrodesulfurization to convert $R-S-R'$ into $H_2S$ and hydrocarbons.
> 2. **Ammonia ($NH_3$)** — Used in Selective Catalytic Reduction to convert $NO_x$ into $N_2$ and $H_2O$.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 examples with justification)*

### Question A2
**State the Nernst equation and explain the role of $E^{\circ}$ in determining reaction spontaneity.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Nernst equation gives the electrode potential under non-standard conditions:
> 
> $$
> E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q
> $$
> 
> **Role of $E^{\circ}$:** A positive $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ indicates a spontaneous reduction reaction under standard conditions. The more positive the $E^{\circ}$ of the half-reaction, the greater its tendency to be reduced. For instance, $Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$ ($E^{\circ} = +0.34$ V) is favored over $Zn^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Zn$ ($E^{\circ} = -0.76$ V), so $Cu^{2+}$ will oxidize metallic zinc.

*(3 marks: equation + role explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model End Semester Pattern]**

### Question B — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the principle and working of **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** for $NO_x$ abatement in petrochemical flue gas streams. Discuss the chemical reactions, typical catalyst, operating temperature range, and the role of the ammonia-to-NOx ratio. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A refinery FCC unit generates flue gas with **500 ppm** $NO_x$ at a flow rate of **120,000 Nm³/h**. The SCR unit achieves **85%** efficiency. Calculate (i) the exit $NO_x$ concentration in ppm, and (ii) the hourly consumption of **30% aqueous ammonia solution** required. *(Molecular weight of $NH_3$ = 17 g/mol; density of 30% solution ≈ 0.892 g/mL.)* *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> **Principle of SCR:** Selective Catalytic Reduction is a post-combustion control technology in which $NO_x$ gases ($NO$, $NO_2$) are chemically reduced by ammonia ($NH_3$) or urea in the presence of a catalyst to produce **nitrogen and water vapor**.
> 
> **Governing Reactions:**
> 
> $$
> 4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \longrightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O
> $$
> 
> $$
> 2NO_2 + 4NH_3 + O_2 \longrightarrow 3N_2 + 6H_2O
> $$
> 
> **Catalyst:** Typically a **vanadium pentoxide ($V_2O_5$)** supported on **titanium dioxide ($TiO_2$ anatase)**, often with tungsten trioxide ($WO_3$) as a promoter to enhance thermal stability.
> 
> **Operating Temperature:** **300 °C – 400 °C.** Below 300 °C, reaction kinetics become sluggish; above 400 °C, parasitic oxidation of $NH_3$ to $NO$ occurs, reducing efficiency.
> 
> **$NH_3$ / $NO_x$ Ratio:** A molar ratio of **0.9 – 1.0** is maintained. An excess causes **ammonia slip** (unreacted $NH_3$ emitted to atmosphere), while a deficiency leaves unreacted $NO_x$.
> 
> **Instrumentation:** Online $NO_x$ analyzers (CLD or NDIR), $NH_3$ mass flow controllers, and a **closed-loop PID** maintain the setpoint ratio.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Given:** $[NO_x]_{\text{in}} = 500$ ppm, $\dot{V} = 120{,}000$ Nm³/h, $\eta = 85\%$.
> 
> **(i) Exit NOx concentration:**
> 
> $$
> [NO_x]_{\text{out}} = [NO_x]_{\text{in}} \times (1 - \eta) = 500 \times (1 - 0.85)
> $$
> 
> $$
> [NO_x]_{\text{out}} = 500 \times 0.15 = 75 \text{ ppm}
> $$
> 
> **(ii) Ammonia consumption:**
> 
> **Step 1:** Volume of $NO_x$ reduced:
> 
> $$
> \Delta \dot{V}_{NO_x} = 120{,}000 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 60 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}
> $$
> 
> **Step 2:** Molar flow of $NO_x$ reduced (at STP, molar volume = 22.414 Nm³/kmol):
> 
> $$
> \dot{n}_{NO_x} = \frac{60}{22.414} = 2.677 \text{ kmol/h}
> $$
> 
> **Step 3:** From stoichiometry (1:1 mol ratio $NO : NH_3$):
> 
> $$
> \dot{n}_{NH_3} = 2.677 \text{ kmol/h}
> $$
> 
> **Step 4:** Mass of pure $NH_3$:
> 
> $$
> \dot{m}_{NH_3,\text{pure}} = 2.677 \times 17 = 45.51 \text{ kg/h}
> $$
> 
> **Step 5:** Mass of 30% aqueous solution:
> 
> $$
> \dot{m}_{\text{solution}} = \frac{45.51}{0.30} = 151.7 \text{ kg/h}
> $$
> 
> **Step 6:** Volume of solution (density = 0.892 g/mL = 0.892 kg/L):
> 
> $$
> \dot{V}_{\text{solution}} = \frac{151.7}{0.892} = 170.1 \text{ L/h}
> $$
> 
> **Final Answers:** (i) 75 ppm, (ii) **170.1 L/h** of 30% ammonia solution.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the **Orsat apparatus** method for the analysis of flue gases. Explain how chemical reduction of carbon monoxide is achieved in the apparatus. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the **standard cell potential** and **equilibrium constant** for the redox reaction:
$$
Fe^{3+} + Sn^{2+} \longrightarrow Fe^{2+} + Sn^{4+}
$$
Given: $E^{\circ}_{Fe^{3+}/Fe^{2+}} = +0.77$ V; $E^{\circ}_{Sn^{4+}/Sn^{2+}} = +0.15$ V. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> **Orsat Apparatus:** It is a portable gas-analyzing device used to determine the volumetric composition of flue gas ($CO_2$, $O_2$, $CO$, and $N_2$ by difference). The apparatus consists of a graduated water-jacketed burette connected via a manifold to a series of absorption pipettes.
> 
> **Procedure:**
> 1. A known volume (100 mL) of flue gas is drawn into the burette.
> 2. The gas is passed into the **first pipette** containing **KOH solution** — $CO_2$ is absorbed.
> 3. The contraction in volume gives the % $CO_2$.
> 4. The residual gas is passed into the **second pipette** containing **alkaline pyrogallol** — $O_2$ is absorbed.
> 5. The next contraction gives % $O_2$.
> 6. The gas is then passed into the **third pipette** containing **acidic cuprous chloride ($Cu_2Cl_2$)** — **chemical reduction of CO occurs here**.
> 7. The contraction gives % $CO$.
> 8. The remaining gas is passed through a combustion tube with $CuO$ at 300 °C — $H_2$ and $CH_4$ burn.
> 9. The final contraction gives the sum of % $H_2$ + % $CH_4$. The residue is % $N_2$ by difference.
> 
> **Reduction Chemistry of CO Step:**
> 
> $$
> 2Cu^{2+} + 4Cl^- + 2CO \longrightarrow 2CuCl \cdot CO \text{ (complex)} + Cl_2
> $$
> 
> In this step, **$Cu^{2+}$ is reduced to $Cu^{+}$** (its oxidation state drops from +2 to +1), and the $CO$ is oxidized to $CO_2$. The $Cu^+$ forms a coordination complex with another $CO$ molecule, effectively removing it from the gas mixture.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Given reaction:**
> 
> $$
> Fe^{3+} + Sn^{2+} \longrightarrow Fe^{2+} + Sn^{4+}
> $$
> 
> **Identify half-reactions:**
> - **Reduction (cathode):** $Fe^{3+} + e^- \rightarrow Fe^{2+}$, $\quad E^{\circ}_1 = +0.77$ V
> - **Oxidation (anode):** $Sn^{2+} \rightarrow Sn^{4+} + 2e^-$, $\quad E^{\circ}_2 = +0.15$ V (as reduction)
> 
> **Standard Cell Potential:**
> 
> $$
> E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} = 0.77 - 0.15 = +0.62 \text{ V}
> $$
> 
> **Equilibrium Constant:** Since $n = 2$ electrons are transferred, using the formula:
> 
> $$
> \log K_{eq} = \frac{n \cdot E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0592}
> $$
> 
> $$
> \log K_{eq} = \frac{2 \times 0.62}{0.0592} = \frac{1.24}{0.0592} = 20.945
> $$
> 
> $$
> K_{eq} = 10^{20.945} = 8.81 \times 10^{20}
> $$
> 
> **Final Answer:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = +0.62$ V, $\quad K_{eq} \approx 8.81 \times 10^{20}$. The reaction is **highly spontaneous** in the forward direction.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign Confusion in Nernst Equation:** Forgetting that $E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ is subtracted, not added.
> 2. **Molar Volume Errors:** Using 22.4 L/mol at non-STP conditions. Always verify temperature/pressure.
> 3. **Stoichiometry Mistakes:** In SCR, students often use a 1:4 ratio by mistake instead of the correct 1:1 between $NO$ and $NH_3$.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $Nm^3$ with standard molar volume. Always state "at STP" explicitly.
> 5. **Missing Keywords in Theory:** For 7-mark questions, examiners expect keywords: *catalyst, oxidation state, standard potential, ammonia slip, by-product*. Missing these drops marks sharply.
> 6. **Diagram Labelling:** Always label the burette, pipettes, and absorption chemicals in the Orsat diagram.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Chemical Reduction**
> 
> - **Reduction = Gain of electrons = Decrease in oxidation state.**
> - **Oxidizing agent** is reduced; **Reducing agent** is oxidized.
> - **Nernst Equation** is the cornerstone; remember $0.0592/n$ at 25 °C.
> - **$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$** — negative $\Delta G$ implies spontaneous reduction.
> - **SCR Unit:** Catalyst = $V_2O_5/TiO_2$; Temperature = 300–400 °C; Use **1:1 mol ratio** of $NH_3:NO$.
> - **HDS Unit:** Catalyst = Co-Mo / Ni-Mo; converts organic sulfur to $H_2S$.
> - **Claus Process:** Recovers elemental sulfur from $H_2S$ using partial oxidation + catalytic reduction.
> - **Orsat Apparatus:** Sequential absorption — $KOH$ ($CO_2$) → Pyrogallol ($O_2$) → $Cu_2Cl_2$ ($CO$) → Combustion ($H_2$/$CH_4$).
> - **Faraday Constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol — essential for coulometric calculations.
> - **Ammonia Slip:** Unreacted $NH_3$ emitted — must be kept **< 5 ppm** for regulatory compliance.
> - **Always convert** ppm by volume: 1 ppm = 1 part per 1,000,000 in volume.
> - **Molar volume at STP** = 22.414 Nm³/kmol; at NTP = 22.4 L/mol.
> - **Keq > 1:** Reaction favors products; $Keq < 1$: Reaction favors reactants.
> - **Industrial Applications:** Sulfur recovery, $NO_x$ abatement, oxygen scavenging, and trace metal analysis.
